Before I run the cold open, I need to settle a few things with you. The opening scene depends entirely on where you want to stand in the war, so let me ask the most important question first:

**Where in the war do you want to start?**

- **Hogwarts student** — corridors, common rooms, the *Prophet* at breakfast, the war as something happening to other people's families until it isn't. Opens with a letter from home.
- **Order of the Phoenix** — active operative, late 1970s, safehouses and night work and people you care about already dying. Opens in a Brixton flat after something went wrong.
- **Ministry / Auror** — bureaucracy with teeth, the war fought through memos and warrants and the slow rot of a department being quietly compromised. Opens with an unexpected summons to Level 9.
- **Civilian** — Healer at St Mungo's, journalist, shopkeeper, brewer, midwife. The war comes through your door whether you want it or not. Opens with a customer who won't leave.

Pick one, and tell me anything else you already know about your character — name, age (18+), blood status, anything. Or just pick a vantage and I'll generate the rest and we'll fill in details as we go.

## Narrator

OOC quick answer: the main series runs roughly 1991–1998. Cho is one year ahead of Harry, so she starts Hogwarts in 1990 (born 1979). She'd be a fifth-year (15-going-on-16) during *Goblet of Fire* in 1994–95, when she dates Cedric.

So if you want to be precise about your Chang character's relationship to Cho:

- **Aunt** — a sister of one of Cho's parents. If Cho's mum is born ~1950, an older sister born ~1945 would be in her early thirties in 1977. Too old to be your peer.
- **Older cousin** — Cho's parent has a sibling who's roughly your age. That sibling's daughter would be your seventh-year peer in 1977. So she'd be Cho's **first cousin once removed** (technically), or more naturally read as a much-older cousin. Cho would be born when this character is in her early 20s. By the time Cho hits Hogwarts in 1990, this character is in her early 30s — exactly the age to be a "cool older cousin" Cho looks up to.

The cousin framing is the cleanest. The character is a Chang of the same family branch, a generation removed from Cho but recognizably her people — and there's a nice quiet possibility that, somewhere in the late 80s, she becomes the relative who tells Cho stories about Hogwarts and shapes how Cho thinks about it before she ever gets there.

Want me to go with cousin? And want to answer the three questions from my last turn so I can run the opening?

 ▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:42

The owl came at the wrong window first.

Caleb heard it before he saw it — the soft thump against the leaded glass of the next room, then the scrape of talons along the frame as the bird worked out which one of them was him. By the time he'd opened his own window the tawny was already there, leg out, business-like. Not the family owl. A school-hire from the Owl Office at King's Cross, which meant someone had paid extra to make sure this got to him before the train.

The handwriting on the envelope was his father's — but smaller than usual, written fast.

*Caleb — your mother's reading the Prophet. Don't write back to the house this week, write to me at the workshop. I'll explain. Trains run on time. Be safe. Dad.*

Below it, the Prophet headline he hadn't yet seen at breakfast: **MAGICAL FAMILY ATTACKED IN STOKE-ON-TRENT — TWO DEAD, FATHER MISSING**.

Stoke. Forty miles from the workshop. Forty miles from his mother.

The tawny shifted, waiting to see if there was a reply.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:46

Caleb sat down at the desk and pulled a half-used sheet of parchment toward him. The tawny watched.

*Dad —*

*What's going on? Why am I getting this and not the usual letter? Is Mum all right? Is the workshop all right? The Prophet on the front says Stoke and the father's missing — that's forty miles from us. Did he do it, the missing one? Or is the Prophet being the Prophet again and he's a victim too? Tell me whether I should be worried. I'll write to the workshop like you said. — C.*

He read it back, scratched out *being the Prophet again* — too flippant, his mother might see it — and replaced it with *underreporting*. Folded it. Tied it to the tawny's leg. The owl tilted its head, considered him, and was gone through the window in two pulls of its wings.

The Prophet was still on the desk. Stoke-on-Trent. **TWO DEAD, FATHER MISSING.** No names below the fold, just *the family had recently relocated from London.* That was the *Prophet's* language for *Muggle-born*, when it didn't want to say it.

His mother was a Muggle. His father was not. The workshop, on a quiet lane off the A6 north of Manchester, was where his father did his clockwork repairs — Muggle clocks, mostly, because that was the trade he'd married into; magical pieces in a back room only Caleb and his mother knew about. *Don't write to the house.* His father had never written that before. Not in seven years of school.

Downstairs, his mother's voice through the floorboards: *Caleb, the toast.* Light. Normal. She didn't know yet, then. Or she did and was being his mother.

His trunk was already by the door. The train left Euston at eleven.

OOC: Another fair catch. Let me think through this with you.

I had Caleb at home — somewhere on the A6 north of Manchester, in the family's house above or near his father's workshop, given the geography I'd already half-implied. That's a plausible place for him to be on the morning of September 1. Owl post finds people at home; the tawny crashing the wrong window first is fine for that setting.

What I didn't do is *establish* that location for you before I started running it. I wrote "Caleb heard it before he saw it" as if you already knew where "it" was, and you don't, because we haven't done the part where I tell you where you woke up this morning. That's the gap.

So — let me actually open the scene properly. Here are the two reasonable options for "where Caleb is on the morning of September 1, 1977, getting an urgent owl from his father":

**A. Home, north of Manchester.** The bedroom above his father's clock-repair workshop. Mother in the kitchen below, toast on. Trunk by the door. Three hours of cab-then-train ahead to get to Euston, then the Hogwarts Express at eleven from King's Cross. (This is what I was implicitly running.)

**B. Already on or near the platform.** The Hogwarts Express morning from a London angle — staying with a relative the night before, or already at King's Cross, the owl finding him in the bustle. Different vibe; the urgency hits with the train *right there*.

A is what I had in my head. B is also reasonable for a half-blood seventh-year. Pick one — or tell me there's a different setup you'd rather have, and I'll work with that.

And once we've settled where you are, I'll re-open the scene cleanly from the top, with the ground under your feet established before the owl arrives.

['Caleb — your mother's reading the Prophet. Don't write back to the house this week, write to me at the workshop. I'll explain. Trains run on time. Be safe. Dad.
Below it, the Prophet headline he hadn't yet seen at breakfast: MAGICAL FAMILY ATTACKED IN STOKE-ON-TRENT — TWO DEAD, FATHER MISSING.' so the father is writing me to tell me my mother is downstairs reading the prophet, and he got another copy to cut out the article, and then went to work to mail it and does not want my mother to know?]

## Narrator

OOC: You're right again. The letter as I wrote it is doing too many things at once and the logistics fall apart when you press them.

Let me think it through cleanly:

If the *Prophet* arrives at the house in the morning, your mother is down in the kitchen reading it (with you upstairs), and your father is *also* at the house at that moment — there's no plausible way he writes you a hand-delivered urgent owl from somewhere else. The "don't write to the house, write to the workshop" framing implies he's at the workshop, which is a separate location from the house. So either:

- He left for the workshop early, *before* the post arrived. Then how did he see the headline to write about it? He'd have to have his own *Prophet* delivered to the workshop — which is plausible if his trade has him reading it at work — and the owl crashing your window happened to come from the workshop, not the house. That's the version that holds together. But it requires him to have read his copy and dispatched the owl in the time between the post arriving at the workshop (~7:30ish) and the owl reaching you (~7:45) — tight but doable.

- Or he was at the house, saw your mother reading it, and instead of *talking to her*, slipped out to the workshop to write you a coded note. That's contrived. Real people would just have the conversation.

The first version works. The second doesn't. And even the first version is doing a lot of work for a moment that should be simpler than this.

The cleanest fix: ditch the "your mother's reading the Prophet" framing entirely. Whatever's pulling your father urgently is *his own* concern — something at the workshop, something he's seeing first, something he wants you to know before you board the train. Your mother in the kitchen is just your mother in the kitchen.

Want me to redo the opening with that fix? I'll re-establish where you are, write a letter from your father that actually makes sense as a thing a person would write, and let the morning unfold from there.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:38

His bedroom was at the back of the house, looking out over the small garden and, beyond the hedge, the lane that ran past the workshop two doors down. Caleb was up before the alarm — he never slept the last night, hadn't since first year — and his trunk was already by the door, packed and re-checked, the cherry wand laid across the top of his folded robes. Out the window the lane was quiet: the bakery van clattering past, a cyclist, the long grey morning light that always meant September.

His mother was downstairs. He could hear her in the kitchen, the kettle, the radio low. His father had left for the workshop before six, the way he did every weekday — out the front, down the lane, in by the side door. Caleb hadn't seen him yet today.

He was buckling the trunk's second strap when the tawny hit the window.

Wrong angle, school-hire by the look of it, irritated. He let it in. The note tied to its leg was on workshop paper — the cheap stuff his father used for receipts — folded twice and sealed with a smear of wax he hadn't bothered to stamp.

*Caleb — come round to the workshop before the cab. I want a word before you go. Don't worry your mother. — Dad.*

That was all.

Caleb turned the paper over. Nothing on the back. The tawny had already gone — no wait for a reply.

Down in the kitchen, the radio was doing the weather.

He held the paper up to his nose.

Workshop. Sawdust and the sour-clean smell of clock oil — the linseed-and-mineral mix his father used on the older movements. A trace of pipe smoke, faint, on the underside where it had sat on the bench. The wax was the cheap brown stuff from the desk drawer, no perfume to it.

Nothing else. No cologne, no cologne-not-his-father's, no smell of anyone else's hand. Just the workshop, the way the workshop always smelled, on a piece of receipt paper that had probably been waiting in the to-write pile for a week before his father grabbed it this morning.

It smelled like his father.

Down in the kitchen the radio shifted from weather to a cricket score.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:40

He took the stairs slow, the note folded in his palm. The radio came up to meet him — the announcer's voice was the unhurried Yorkshire one, the one who did the early sport before the proper newsreaders came on.

*…and at the close of play yesterday, England two hundred and forty-seven for six, with Boycott unbeaten on ninety-one. Greig will resume this morning.*

A pause. The sound of his mother setting a cup down. Then —

*…and now, the eight o'clock news in twenty minutes. Up next, today's weather across the regions.*

His mother was at the table in her dressing gown, the *Daily Mirror* open in front of her — Muggle paper, not the *Prophet*; the *Prophet* didn't come to this house — and a half-eaten triangle of toast on the plate. She looked up when the floorboard at the bottom of the stairs gave its usual creak.

"He'll get a hundred before lunch," she said, of Boycott. "If he doesn't, I'll eat your trunk." She smiled at him over the rim of her cup. "Tea's in the pot. Sit down, you've got time."

*I go and pour tea and I put milk in it, because I'm British. and say pip pip to my mother* *I sit down and eat that piece of toast hoping it was for me*

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:41

He poured. The good pot, the brown one with the chip on the lid that nobody ever replaced. Splash of milk after, the way he liked it, the way she'd taught him — milk last, in the cup, never in the pot, that was a war his mother had fought and won before he was born.

"Pip pip, mother."

"Pip pip yourself." She didn't look up from the *Mirror*, but her mouth moved at the corner. "There's marmalade if you want it."

He took the toast off her plate. She let him. She always did — there was always one for her and one for him on the same plate by accident-on-purpose, and they'd been pretending it was an accident for about a decade now.

The tea was strong. The toast was cold the way good toast is cold, with the butter set into the bread. Outside, a hedge sparrow was working the lavender. The radio gave way to the regional weather — *cloudy, brightening, a chance of showers in the Pennines later* — and his mother turned a page.

"Your father went over early," she said, mild. "He'll come round before the cab. Said he wanted to see you off himself."

She turned another page.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:42

The *Mirror* was facing her, but the angle gave him most of it upside-down across the table. He read what he could without making a thing of it. Front page: a Callaghan government story about pay restraint, a photograph of the Prime Minister at a podium. Below the fold, smaller: something about a fire at a warehouse in Birmingham, and on the right column, a strip of football news. Nothing about Stoke. Nothing wizarding-shaped, but then it wouldn't be — the *Mirror* didn't print what didn't happen as far as the *Mirror* was concerned.

She turned the page again — sport, then a weather map.

He set his cup down. "I'm going to step out briefly looking for a present for a girl. Don't worry about it. No questions. What time do I need to leave from here for the train?"

His mother looked up. The *very faintest* lift of one eyebrow — the kind that meant she had registered the sentence in full and was choosing what to do with it.

"A girl," she said, neutral.

"No questions."

"I heard you the first time, love." She glanced at the clock on the dresser. "Cab's at half past eight. You want twenty minutes either side of that, so — back here by ten past, latest. Don't be daft about it. And —" she tapped the rim of her cup, considering — "the post office shuts till nine, the chemist's open, and Pendlebury's florist won't have anything decent on a Thursday morning. If it's flowers you're after, you're better off picking them up at King's Cross. If it's not flowers, I haven't said anything."

She turned another page. The *Mirror* rustled.

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:45

He shrugged on his jacket, pocketed the note, and stepped out the front door.

The lane was cool. A milk float hummed past at the end of the row, its bottles chiming. Two doors down, the workshop's front shutters were already up — his father had been in for nearly two hours by now. Caleb went the other way.

The shop he had in mind was at the corner where the lane met the main road: a small newsagent-and-sundries that doubled as half a dozen other things — sweets, tobacco, ladies' tights, postcards of the Lake District, paperback Westerns, and a small glass case at the back where Mrs Crowther kept the things she said in a low voice were "for a present, like." Hair clasps. A few cheap brooches. Soaps wrapped in tissue paper. A handful of pens that wrote in real ink rather than biro.

The bell over the door went off as he pushed in. Mrs Crowther was at the counter with the *Daily Express* and a cigarette.

"Caleb. Train day?"

"Train day."

"You're up early for a Caleb." She folded the paper back. "Looking for something or browsing?"

"I need ladies tights... for a lady.. I will used pounds to pay for it, since we are British."

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:46

Mrs Crowther took a long look at him over the cigarette. Set the *Express* down. Pulled the cigarette out of her mouth with two fingers and tapped it on the saucer she kept by the till.

"Caleb. Love."

She let that sit for a beat.

"You're not buying ladies' tights as a present for a girl. That's not a present for a girl. That's a present for a girl's *mother*, and even then only if you've known her twenty years and she's mentioned a brand by name." She squinted at him, not unkindly. "What's the matter with you this morning, you've gone simple."

She came round the counter. Put a hand on the back of a chair like she was settling in.

"Right. Tell me about her. Age you, roughly? Older? Bookish, sporty, fussy, plain-and-pretty? You've got — what — four pound notes in your back pocket and a train at eleven, so we're working under constraints. Talk."

Behind her, the small glass case glinted in the morning light. Hair clasps. A pewter pin shaped like a sprig of heather. A green silk scarf folded into a square the size of a postcard. A fountain pen with a marbled barrel. A small bottle of something amber-coloured that might have been perfume or might have been lemon cordial; it was hard to tell from this side of the glass.

The bell hadn't rung again. They were the only two in the shop.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:47

Mrs Crowther's eyebrows went up.

"The Conway Stewart? Now *that's* a present." She unlocked the case with a small key from a chain at her waist and lifted the pen out, set it on a square of felt on the counter. The barrel was a deep marbled green and gold, the nib a steel one, not gold — but the cap had a proper clip and the body had a real heft to it when she turned it for him to see.

"Three pound ten." She watched his face. "Worth it if she's the writing sort. Worthless if she's not. — She the writing sort?"

She didn't wait for the answer. Reached under the counter and came up with a small flat box, dark green card, the right size for the pen. Lifted the lid: a velvet bed inside, and a folded slip of card for a name.

"You can put her name on the card if you want. Or leave it. Some girls like a name. Some girls don't." She lit a fresh cigarette off the stub of the old one. "Three pound ten, with the box. I'll throw in a bottle of Quink, blue-black, for the look of the thing."

The shop clock ticked. Outside, the milk float was working its way back down the lane.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:48

Mrs Crowther took the four pounds, gave him ten pence change in coppers, and slid a chewed Bic across the counter without comment.

He turned the card over on the felt and wrote it out — neat, because his handwriting was always neat, even when the contents weren't.

*Mei,*
*Would you go on a date with me? Circle one: Yes / No / Maybe*
*Love, Caleb*

He looked at it. Considered crossing out *Love*. Didn't.

Mrs Crowther read it upside-down without pretending she wasn't, then nodded once, professional, like a chemist confirming a prescription. "Mei," she said. Not a question. "All right then." She tucked the card under the velvet bed, set the pen back on top, closed the box, and slid it across in a small paper bag with the shop's name on it. Added a slim cardboard tube of Quink ink, blue-black.

"Off you trot. Train day. Tell your mum I said happy term."

The bell over the door went off behind him.

The lane was brighter now — sun catching the slate roofs across the way. Just past quarter to eight. The workshop was twenty yards down on the left, shutters up, his father somewhere inside.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:49

The workshop was twenty yards down the lane, on the left, between the cobbler's and the long blank wall of the chapel hall. The shutters were up. The sign over the door — WADDELL CLOCKS & WATCH REPAIR — had been repainted last summer in his father's careful hand, the W's slightly too tall the way they always were.

Caleb pushed the door. The brass bell did its small *ding* and the smell hit him the way it always had: linseed oil, brass polish, the faint warm dust of old movements running in the back room. The front of the shop was where customers came — a glass-topped counter, a row of pocket watches on velvet, a longcase clock by the wall that had been waiting for a part since Easter. Through the doorway behind the counter, the back room: the bench, the lamps, the small green-shaded one his father worked under.

His father was on the high stool at the bench, in his shirtsleeves, a loupe on a strap round his neck. He looked up when the bell went. The loupe was up on his forehead, which meant he had been waiting for the bell and not actually working.

"Caleb. Come through. Shut the front." A pause. "Lock it."

*I go in and shut the door*

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:49

He shut the door, turned the latch. The little brass bolt slid home with the soft click it always made.

His father waited until he heard the lock, then nodded once and went back through to the rear bench. Caleb followed.

Up close, his father looked tired. Not catastrophic-tired. The ordinary kind a man gets when he hasn't been sleeping right for a week. There was a half-finished cup of tea by his elbow and a Muggle newspaper folded on the bench — not the *Mirror*, the *Manchester Evening News* from yesterday, with something circled in pencil.

"Sit." His father gestured at the second stool. "I won't keep you. Cab's at half past."

He pushed the *Evening News* across.

The circle was around a small piece, three paragraphs, page four: a house fire on a quiet street in Salford, two dead, the husband missing. Local police treating as suspicious. The names had been printed: a couple in their forties, the wife a primary-school teacher.

"Friends of your mother's," his father said. Quiet. "From before we married. She hasn't seen the paper yet — I picked it up off the mat last night before she came down. I'll tell her tonight, after you've gone."

He took a breath. Let it out.

"They were like us, Caleb. Not the magical bit. The other bit. He's a good man. I don't think he did it."

His father looked at him over the loupe, then took it off and set it on the bench.

"That's what I mean. He's the wizard. She was the Muggle. They had a daughter at a primary near where we used to live — she'd be twelve now, thirteen. The girl was at her grandmother's the night it happened, thank God for that."

He turned the *Evening News* round so it was facing him again, and tapped the small circled paragraph.

"The police will say it was him because that's what police say when there isn't a body and the husband's missing. The *Prophet* will run a different version this morning, if it runs it at all. He didn't do it, Caleb. Neither version will be right, and your mother is going to want to ring his mother by lunchtime, and I want her to do that without me having to explain to her at the same time why we're being careful with the post for a while."

He looked up.

"You're going to school. That's the right place for you to be. I'm telling you because you're old enough and you're going to hear *something* from one direction or another and I'd rather you heard it from me first. If anyone at school asks about your mother — anyone — you say she's fine and you change the subject. Understood?"

A pause.

"And if your mother writes to you this term and the letter sounds odd, you write to me at the workshop, not back to her. All right?"

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:52

His father exhaled through his nose. Set both hands flat on the bench.

"You're a clever boy. You've always been a clever boy. All right."

He took a moment. Picked up the *Evening News*, folded it shut, set it aside.

"Yes. That's what I think. There've been three this year that I've heard of — that anyone's heard of — where the pattern's the same. Wizard with a Muggle wife, or a witch with a Muggle husband, and something happens in the night and one of them's gone and the other's dead. The *Prophet* doesn't print most of them. The Muggle papers print them as house fires or burglaries gone wrong, and the police clear it up however the police clear those things up."

He looked at the lamp. Looked back.

"Your mother is fine. She is sat at the kitchen table reading the *Daily Mirror* and she is fine. I am fine. The shop is fine. I'm not telling you we're in danger today. I'm telling you that there are people out there who think families like ours shouldn't be families, and they have started doing something about it, and I'm not having you board that train without knowing it."

A pause.

"You don't say she's all right because you're keeping a secret from anyone, Caleb. You say she's all right because there are children at that school whose parents are on the wrong side of this, and they don't all know who their parents are yet, and I don't want my wife's name in a Slytherin common room tonight. That's all."

He looked at his son properly, then. Tired eyes, but steady.

"Are we square?"

His father stared at him. Loupe in hand. Mouth slightly open.

"Atropos."

"At seven in the morning, the day you go back to school, you're standing in my workshop quoting Greek fates at me." He set the loupe down. "Your mother said this would happen if we let you read the encyclopaedias unsupervised. I told her it was character-building. I was wrong."

He pulled open the small drawer under the bench — the one with the parts trays — and rummaged for a moment. Came up with a pocket watch. Brass case, slightly tarnished, a hairline scratch across the back where someone had dropped it on a hearth in 1954. He put it on the bench between them.

"This was your grandfather's. It runs perfectly. It does nothing else. There is no charm on it. It will not save your life. If you wind it on Sundays it will keep good time and that is the entirety of what it does."

He pushed it across.

"Take it. Not because it's magic. Because it was his, and you're old enough, and because if I'm telling you the world's getting hard I'd rather you had something of his in your pocket while I do."

A pause.

"And if Atropos turns up in a Hogsmeade pub one weekend looking for you, you tell her your father said no."

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:54

His father snorted. A real laugh, the first of the morning, and then he shook his head and rubbed his eyes with the heel of his hand.

"*Innit.* God help me." He pocketed the loupe in his shirt pocket. "Off you go. Cab won't wait, and your mother will want you to sit down and have another cup before you leave or she'll never forgive me."

He came round the bench. Put a hand on the back of Caleb's neck — the brief workshop-grip of a man who didn't do hugs in shopfronts even with the door locked — and held it there a second longer than usual.

"Be safe at school. Write your mother. Write me too, if anything's odd. And —" he glanced at the small paper bag in Caleb's hand and didn't ask — "good luck with whoever she is."

He let go. Stepped back to his stool. The loupe came down over his eye and he was, abruptly, the man at the bench again.

"Go on. The bell will ring when you go out — leave the latch as you found it."

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:55

He let himself out the front. Brass bell, latch left on the inside. The lane was warmer now, the sun over the chapel-hall roof. A neighbour's cat was sat on a low wall watching him with the particular patience cats have for people on their way somewhere important.

Two doors down, the front door of the house. He kicked the threshold-bristles off his shoes — his mother's house rule, decades old — and went straight up the stairs.

The bedroom was as he'd left it: trunk by the door, satchel beside, robes folded under the wand. He slipped the pocket watch into his waistcoat pocket on the chain his father hadn't given him but which lived in the trunk for exactly this kind of moment. The pen-box went into the satchel, padded between two folded jumpers where it wouldn't get crushed. The Quink ink, upright, beside it. The note from Dad, into the inside breast pocket of his jacket. The *Evening News* page — he hesitated, then folded it small and put it in with the note.

The trunk had a strap on each end. He hefted it, carefully — heavier this year than last — and worked it down the stairs in stages, satchel over his shoulder, one bump per step. His mother was in the hall by the time he made the landing, holding the front door open for him.

"Cab in twenty," she said. "There's another cup if you want one."

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:57

She was already pouring by the time he got to the kitchen. The brown pot, the chipped lid, the cup she always gave him — the one with the cornflowers on it that had been his since he was ten. Milk last, in the cup. She knew.

She set it on the table for him without sitting down herself. Stood by the sink instead, arms folded, looking at him in the particular way mothers look at sons on the morning of a train.

"You've got that pocket watch on you."

"Yeah."

"That was your grandfather's."

"Yeah."

She nodded once. Didn't make a thing of it. Turned and ran the tap to rinse her own cup.

"You'll write."

"I'll write."

"Not just the *Mum I'm fine* one. A real one, when you've got something to say."

"I'll write a real one."

The kettle clicked off behind her. Outside, somewhere down the lane, a car door shut — not the cab, too early, but the sound of the morning getting on with itself.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:59

He drank it down — too fast, but it was still drinkable — and set the cup on the table.

She turned from the sink when he came round to her. He hugged her properly, the full-length kind that doesn't quite fit anymore because he was taller than her now and had been for two years. Kissed the top of her head. She held the back of his collar for a second the way she always did, then let go and stepped back and patted his chest twice, briskly, before she could do anything she'd be embarrassed about.

"Off with you. Don't miss the train. Don't lose your trunk. Don't fall in love with anyone unsuitable."

"I'll do my best."

"Try harder than your best."

He picked up the satchel, hauled the trunk by its end-strap into the hall, and out through the front door. The lane was bright now, the air still cool but warming. He set the trunk down at the kerb, satchel on top, and stood with his hands in his pockets to wait for the cab.

Down the lane, the workshop's shutters were still up. He didn't look back. He thought his father might be at the front window anyway.

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 08:01

He moved to the kerb proper, where a cab from Crocker's would pull up — same firm his family had used for twenty years, run by a man who knew the lane and would tap the horn once rather than ring the bell.

The trunk sat at his feet. He stood with his hands in his jacket pockets, the note from his father in one, the change from Mrs Crowther in the other. The morning had got on with itself: a woman pushing a pram on the opposite pavement, a boy on a bicycle with a paper-round bag, the milk float gone now to its next round. The chapel-hall clock chimed the quarter — he was a minute early on his own pocket watch — and a starling chattered from the gutter above the bay window.

Twenty-nine minutes till the cab.

He looked down at the trunk. Two stickers on the lid: one from first year, peeling, *C. WADDELL — RAVENCLAW —* no, that was wrong, the sticker said only his name. A second sticker from the Hogwarts Express luggage office, faded, meaningless. The lid had a fresh scuff in it from the stairs.

His mother was somewhere behind him in the kitchen window, he thought, watching from a distance she'd have called *not watching*.

*I sit down on my trunk and wait*

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 08:02

He sat. The trunk was solid, slightly cool through his trousers from the night air still in it, and the angle wasn't quite right for his back — the lid had that faint domed crown that meant you couldn't really settle on it without leaning forward. He leaned forward, elbows on his knees, and watched the lane.

The starling went on chattering. Somewhere two streets over a dog was barking at the milk float, the cheerful insistent kind of bark that meant the dog and the milkman had been doing this for years and both of them enjoyed it. The pavement opposite had gone quiet again — woman and pram round the corner, paperboy off down the next road.

He took the watch out. Wound it twice — gently, his father had been firm about that — and put it back. Felt the chain settle against the buttons of his waistcoat. Looked at his shoes.

Twenty-eight minutes.

Down the lane, the workshop bell rang once: a customer, or a delivery, he couldn't tell from here. The shutters stayed up.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 08:30

The cab — the old maroon Vauxhall with Crocker's name and number painted on the door — turned into the lane on time. Single tap on the horn. The driver was Eddie Crocker himself, the son rather than the father, who'd done Caleb's run twice now and didn't fuss about being early or late.

Eddie got out, lifted the trunk into the boot with a practiced grunt, slammed the lid, and held the rear door open.

"Train day, Caleb. King's Cross again?"

"Euston first. Then King's Cross."

"Aye, aye." Eddie went round the front. "Settle in. Bit of fog over the Pennines but it'll clear."

Caleb glanced once at the kitchen window before he got in. His mother was there, in the dressing gown still, with a hand half-raised. He waved back. She turned away from the window in the casual way that meant she was going to cry where he wasn't going to see it.

The cab pulled out. The lane fell away — bakery, chapel hall, workshop (shutters up, no figure at the window — his father had stayed at the bench, by design), the corner shop with Mrs Crowther's *Express* still folded behind the till glass — and then the main road, and then the slow grey arteries north Manchester ran on a Thursday morning. Eddie put Radio 2 on, low. The cricket was over; someone was talking about the weekend forecast.

"woulld you mind putting it on the news?"

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 08:32

"Aye, fair enough." Eddie reached for the dial without taking his eyes off the road and turned through the band — a brass band, a bit of static, somebody talking about pig prices — until he found Radio 4 and the long low chime that came in just before the half-hour pips.

*Pip. Pip. Pip. Pip. Pip. Peep.*

*"BBC news at half past eight. The Prime Minister has reaffirmed the government's commitment to the Social Contract on pay restraint, in a speech to trade union leaders last night in Brighton. Mr Callaghan said —"*

Caleb watched the lane give over to the main road, brick to brick, a wide grey corridor of shops and chimneys.

*"— in Northern Ireland, two soldiers were injured by a roadside device in County Armagh. The army has cordoned off the area and bomb-disposal officers are at the scene. — Police investigating the deaths of two people in a house fire in Salford last night have appealed for information about the whereabouts of the householder, Mr Daniel Brackley. Mr Brackley, who has not been seen since the early hours of Wednesday morning, is described as forty-one, of medium build, with brown hair. Anyone with information is asked to contact Greater Manchester Police on —"*

Eddie made a small *tch* under his breath, the kind a man makes for nobody but himself.

*"— and the weather. Cloudy across most regions, brightening from the west by midday, a chance of showers in the Pennines. The time is half past eight."*

The news handed over to a programme about gardening. Eddie didn't reach for the dial.

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 08:33

Eddie kept his eyes on the road. Took a second before he answered — long enough that Caleb thought he might not.

"That noise wasn't anything, lad." Mild. "Just — house fire and the husband's gone. You hear that twice a year on this road. Sad business."

He worked the gearstick down for a junction, looked left, looked right.

"Do I think he did it." He chewed it over, then shook his head once. "Couldn't tell you. Don't know him from Adam. Police know what they're about, mostly. Mostly." He glanced in the rearview at Caleb, then back to the road. "Knew him, did you?"

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 08:34

Eddie nodded. The kind of nod that closed a subject without making anything of closing it.

"Aye. Nasty business. Same fire and a man missing, you tend to think the worst. Police'll work it out, or they won't." He drummed the wheel with one thumb. "Sorry for your mum, knowing her."

He let it sit, then reached for the dial and turned the volume down a half-step on the gardening programme — which was now an enthusiastic woman explaining the correct way to lift dahlia tubers — until it was background noise rather than company.

The road south unspooled. Past Strangeways, past the canal bridge, the long brick streets thinning toward Piccadilly. The sun had broken properly through now and was catching the chimney pots.

"Quarter past nine for the Euston," Eddie said. "We'll be there twenty minutes early. Plenty of time."

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 08:35

Eddie's eyes flicked to the rearview, then back to the road, then back to the rearview again. His mouth worked for a second before the laugh came out — a single bark of it, surprised.

"You little —"

He shook his head, grinning now, and made a show of hunching over the wheel.

"All right, you've got me. Did it for the insurance. Husband knew too much about the dahlias." He tapped the wheel. "Christ, lad. Half past eight in the morning. — I assumed the husband, didn't I. Picked the one the radio said was missing. If the wife was missing I'd have said her. There's no science to it."

He glanced at Caleb again, fond and amused, the way men of Eddie's age looked at clever boys who'd pulled a stunt on them.

"Your dad lets you read too much. Your mum tell you that, an' all?"

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 08:35

Eddie laughed again — a softer, real one this time — and shook his head.

"Sound woman, your mum."

The cab nosed toward the curve where the road bent down toward Piccadilly station, the great blackened brick of it coming up on the left, taxis and porters and the long low queue of cars dropping off Thursday's commuters. Eddie indicated, slid into the rank, killed the engine.

"Right, lad. Here we are. Hold on a tick —" he was already out, round to the boot, the trunk hauled up onto the pavement before Caleb could finish reaching for his satchel. Eddie set it square on its end, knocked the dust off the strap, and stuck out a hand.

"Quid for the run, your dad's account, paid up. Have a good term. Don't quote any more dead Greeks at the radio."

He winked, climbed back in, and was gone into the rank traffic before Caleb could properly say goodbye.

Manchester Piccadilly was busy. Steam smell, diesel smell, the long echoing roof. Inside, the departures board would be doing its slow clatter. The Euston was at quarter past nine.

*I go look at the board for information where I am supposed to be going"

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 08:40

Inside, the station roof arched up grey and high, pigeons in the rafters, the long mechanical *clack-clack-clack* of the departures board doing its slow update. He hauled the trunk on its end-strap to a clear bit of floor in front of it and looked up.

The board cycled. Trains for Stockport, Crewe, Stoke-on-Trent (his eye caught on it, moved past it), Birmingham. Then —

**09:15 — LONDON EUSTON — Platform 4 — On time**

Calling at Stockport, Macclesfield, Stoke-on-Trent, Stafford, Rugby, Watford Junction, London Euston. Arrives Euston 12 minutes past noon. He did the maths in his head: Euston to King's Cross was a ten-minute walk if you knew where you were going, and he did. That gave him a shade under three quarters of an hour at King's Cross before the eleven o'clock — comfortable, even with a trunk to wrestle through the barrier.

A porter went past with a luggage trolley and gave him the *want a hand?* look without saying it. Caleb shook his head — he wasn't buying a porter for fifteen minutes' wait — and the man moved on.

Platform 4 was up the steps and over the bridge.

OOC pause: I've made a logistics error and I want to flag it rather than narrate around it. The 09:15 from Manchester arrives Euston at 12:12 — which is more than an hour *after* the Hogwarts Express leaves King's Cross at 11:00. Caleb cannot make that train if he's leaving Manchester this morning at 09:15.

The realistic options going back are:

1. **He'd have caught a much earlier train.** A pre-7am Manchester departure would land him at Euston around 9:30, plenty of time. Which means our morning timeline is off — he'd have left the house at maybe 6, not had a leisurely tea and a trip to the corner shop.
2. **He travelled down yesterday and stayed overnight in London** with a relative or in a guesthouse, and is going to King's Cross from there this morning. That's a clean fix and matches families who don't want to risk a same-day train.
3. **Wizarding families typically Floo or Apparate** part of the journey, especially when distance is awkward. His father could've Side-Along Apparated him to a London point, or they used a Floo connection at the workshop's back room.

The cleanest retcon, given everything we've established (workshop, Muggle mother who doesn't know about the urgency, ordinary morning), is **option 3**: there's a Floo connection in the back room of the workshop that connects to a small wizarding pub or office near King's Cross, and the cab and the Euston train are *not* what's happening — Caleb was always going to step into the workshop's back hearth at around 10:15 and come out near the platform.

That actually makes more sense of why his father wanted him at the workshop alone before he left.

I'd like to retcon the cab/Euston framing accordingly — the cab to Piccadilly never happened; Caleb's morning was longer at home, and his trip to the workshop *was* the journey. Want me to roll back to the kerb-waiting moment and re-do it as the Floo trip, or do you want to keep the cab-to-Piccadilly version and have him miss the Hogwarts Express and figure something else out?

(Per the rules, I owe you one acknowledged retcon per session and this is it.)

## User

[[OOC: is there anything interesting I could do here with the extra time that I as a character would think of or possibly want to do]]

## Narrator

OOC, sure — here are a handful of things Caleb might plausibly think to do with a few extra hours, ranging from useful to indulgent:

**Practical / story-relevant:**

1. **Detour to the wizarding side.** If we go with the Floo-from-the-workshop fix, there's no extra time at all. But if we go with option 1 (he caught an earlier Manchester train and lands in London with hours to kill), he could spend that buffer in **Diagon Alley** before going to King's Cross. Last-minute supplies, a stop at Flourish & Blotts, picking up something at the apothecary, a glance at the *Prophet* he doesn't get at home.

2. **Walk past the Salford addresses, or near them.** Risky and a bit morbid, but a clever boy who's just been told something important by his father might want to look at the lane himself — just to *see*. Wouldn't learn anything useful and might draw notice he doesn't want.

3. **Send an owl ahead.** From Diagon Alley or a public Owl Office, write to Mei (or anyone at school) and have it arrive before he does. Or write to a family friend asking after the missing man.

4. **Buy the wizarding wrapping for the pen.** The Conway Stewart in its Muggle box is fine, but he could pick up a sheet of proper wizarding paper or a slim ribbon at a wizarding stationer's that'd make the gift a little less plain.

5. **Check the *Prophet*** at a wizarding newsstand for whatever version of the Salford story they're running. His father said the *Prophet* would print a different version "if it ran it at all." He could find out which.

**Character-relevant / texture:**

6. **A proper breakfast somewhere alone.** A cafe, a bun and tea, sitting in a window watching London with the satchel at his feet. The first hour of being a man going somewhere without anyone watching. The era did that — boys travelled alone and ate alone and it counted.

7. **A bookshop dawdle.** Caleb's a clever boy. A good Muggle bookshop (Foyles isn't far from Euston) eats an hour easily.

8. **Sit on a bench in a park** with the note from his dad and read it again, properly, with no one looking over his shoulder.

**My recommendation as narrator:** option 1 (early train) + option 4-or-1 (Diagon Alley dawdle) gives the richest play. You'd get to: see London a bit, hit the wizarding world solo before school, possibly run into a classmate or two doing the same, check the *Prophet* without your dad watching, and have a quiet hour to think about Mei. It also seeds a place for the campaign to go — "the morning before the train, in Diagon" is a useful recurring beat.

Want me to retcon to early-train + Diagon morning, or keep it cleaner and go with the Floo? Either works.

## User

[[OOC: okay, I'm struggling with knowing where things are because this is all new geography. so the station I am at now has the house nearby where the people were killed?]]

## Narrator

OOC, fair question — let me lay out the geography clean.

**Manchester area (where Caleb's family lives):**
- Caleb's house and his father's workshop are somewhere north of Manchester city centre, on a residential lane.
- **Salford** (where the fire was) is the borough immediately west of Manchester city centre. From the family house, it's roughly 5–7 miles — the same metropolitan area, different neighbourhood. Close enough that "friends of your mother's from before we married" makes sense; not on the same lane.
- **Manchester Piccadilly** (the station Caleb is at right now) is the main mainline station in central Manchester, southeast of Salford. So yes — Salford is *not far* from where Caleb is now, but he's not in Salford and the cab didn't pass through it. He's in central Manchester at the train station.
- **Stoke-on-Trent** is about 40 miles south — a different town entirely. I'd thrown that into the *first* version of the morning (the headline I later replaced); the Salford fire is the one that actually concerns the family.

**Train geography:**
- Manchester Piccadilly → London Euston (mainline, ~3 hours).
- London Euston → London King's Cross (about 10 minutes' walk, two stations almost across the road from each other).
- The Hogwarts Express leaves Platform 9¾ at King's Cross at 11:00 sharp on September 1.

So the journey is: Manchester → Euston (long mainline trip) → walk over to King's Cross → Platform 9¾ → Hogwarts.

**The logistics issue I flagged:** the 09:15 from Piccadilly arrives Euston at 12:12 — *after* the Hogwarts Express has already left. So either Caleb caught a much earlier Manchester train (meaning my morning timeline at the house was wrong — he'd have left at 6am), OR the family uses a Floo connection at the workshop and the cab to Piccadilly was a wrong turn in my narration.

Does that clarify the map? And given what you now know, do you want to:

1. **Retcon to Floo from the workshop** — clean fix, no cab, no Manchester station, Caleb steps into a hearth in the workshop's back room and out at a wizarding spot near King's Cross, with no extra time to fill.
2. **Retcon to "he was on a much earlier train"** — meaning he left the house pre-dawn; the morning we played didn't quite happen. Awkward.
3. **Keep the cab and have him spend the day in London** — meaning he's actually travelling the *day before*, so it's August 31, not September 1, and he overnights in London. Reframes everything.
4. **Keep the cab and let him miss the train** — and figure out an alternative way to Hogwarts, which is its own adventure.

Option 1 is the cleanest. Option 3 gives you the most extra time to play with (a whole afternoon and evening in London + Diagon Alley + an overnight). Option 4 is the most chaotic.

Which way do you want to go?

[[floo is a little boring even if more accurate to the world. I'm going to say overnight to London. a place is already rented because they would have planned for that. assumedly maybe in Diagon Alley? so now would be me going and requesting to change my ticket to a later time in the evening to Lonon?]]

## Narrator

OOC: That's a clean fix and it actually adds texture rather than removing it. Let me walk through what we're committing to so we're aligned, then I'll roll us forward.

**The retcon:** Caleb didn't travel to Manchester this morning at all. He travelled down to London **yesterday** (Wednesday, 31 August 1977) on an evening Manchester→Euston train and is overnighting in London. The family books the same place every year — let's say a small wizarding guesthouse in Diagon Alley (the **Leaky Cauldron** is the obvious one, since it sits at the threshold between Muggle London and Diagon and a half-blood family using it makes perfect sense; or a smaller place tucked off Diagon proper, your call but Leaky is the obvious default).

**What this changes about what we played:**
- The morning at home (kitchen, tea, "pip pip", trip to Mrs Crowther's, conversation with Dad at the workshop, goodbye with Mum, the cab) — all of that **happened yesterday morning**, not today. Same content, just a day earlier. The cab took him to Piccadilly for an evening train; he arrived Euston late evening; he checked into the Leaky last night. All the items in his inventory — the pen, the watch, the note, the *Evening News* page — he's still got them.
- This morning is **Thursday, 1 September**. He's waking up in London. The Hogwarts Express leaves at 11:00 from King's Cross. Diagon Alley is right there. He has the morning to play with — three or four hours, comfortably.

**On the ticket-change question:** in this version he doesn't need to. The mainline ticket got used yesterday. He's already in London. The only ticket that matters now is his Hogwarts Express seat, which doesn't work like a Muggle ticket — you just turn up at Platform 9¾ before 11:00.

So: when you confirm the retcon, we'll pick up at the Leaky Cauldron this morning, breakfast room or Caleb's small rented

Rolling forward:

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 08:42

The ticket office at Piccadilly was at the far end of the concourse, under a brass-railed counter that ran six windows wide. Three of them were open. A queue of half a dozen people: a businessman with a folded *Times*, two women with hatboxes, an old gent with a stick, a soldier in mufti, a girl about Caleb's age with a violin case.

He joined the back of it. The line moved at the pace such lines moved — which was to say, the rate of people changing tickets in 1977 with carbon paper and a clerk who had to look things up in a printed timetable. Six minutes, by his pocket watch, before he was at the window.

The clerk was a tired-looking man in his fifties with reading glasses on the end of his nose.

"Help you, lad?"

"I've a ticket for the nine-fifteen to Euston. I'd like to swap it for an evening one, if that's possible."

The clerk took the ticket, peered at it. "Right. Evening services to Euston today —" he ran a finger down a printed sheet under the glass — "fifteen-twenty, seventeen-ten, nineteen-fifteen, twenty-one-oh-five. Last one's the night sleeper, you don't want that. Which?"

## User

"15:20 please and thank you."

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 08:49

"Right you are." The clerk took the old ticket, stamped it CANCELLED with a rubber stamp that left a faint red smudge, and pulled a fresh one from the rack. He filled it in by hand — date, train, seat number, second class — slid it under the glass with a small printed receipt.

"Fifteen-twenty to Euston, Platform Three when it's called. No reserved compartment, you'll find a seat. Refund of fourpence on the difference, here you are." Four copper pennies clinked through the brass cup at the bottom of the window. "Train arrives Euston at eighteen-twelve. Have a good trip, son."

Caleb pocketed the ticket, the receipt, the four pennies. Stepped out of the window and let the businessman behind him take the spot.

Six and a half hours to fill in Manchester before the train. The trunk at his feet was the immediate problem — he wasn't going to drag it round town all day. The Left Luggage office was over by Platform 1, sixpence a day, the green-painted sign visible from where he was standing.

## User

*I  go over to storage and put my trunk into storage paying for it, but I take my wand with me.*

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 08:53

The Left Luggage office was a green-painted hut tucked alongside the wall before Platform 1, with a hatch and a counter and a wall behind covered in numbered cubbies. A bored attendant with rolled sleeves — *PROUDFOOT* on his nameplate — looked up as Caleb hauled the trunk to the hatch.

"One trunk. Day storage, please."

"Sixpence. Fetching by six o'clock?"

"Three at the latest."

"Right." Proudfoot wrote out a paper claim ticket in duplicate, tore the carbon copy off, and slid it across. Number 47. "Don't lose that. No ticket, no trunk." He hefted it into a cubby with a practiced swing. The trunk thumped into place. The counter flap dropped.

Caleb tucked the claim ticket into the inside breast pocket where the note from his father was, slipped his hand briefly to his hip — wand still there in its inside-jacket loop — and stepped back into the concourse with just the satchel on his shoulder.

The whole station felt different unburdened. Lighter. The smell of frying from the platform cafe, the chatter of the announcement system: a train for Crewe being called now. The clock above the board read just past five to nine.

Six hours. Manchester.

Caleb paid threepence, took a seat halfway down the lower deck on the right.

The bus pulled out. Cross Street, then over the Irwell, the river dark under the bridge. Salford on the far side: terraces, mill chimneys, the long working flank of a Wednesday morning.

A woman with a string bag of vegetables sat down two rows up. The conductor moved along the aisle, calling stops in a voice that had been calling them for years. Pendleton next, then Broughton, then the Eccles New Road run.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 09:09

Heads turned. Not all of them, but enough — the woman with the string bag, an older man in a cap reading a folded *Daily Express*, a younger woman in a nurse's coat sat across the aisle.

The man with the *Express* spoke first. "The fire, you mean? Couple killed?" He tapped the paper. "Hanky Park way, what I read. Off Liverpool Street, near the old Ellor Street flats. — That right?" He half-turned to the woman with the bag.

"Round there, aye." She didn't look up from her knitting; she'd pulled it out of the bag while Caleb wasn't looking. "Off the Crescent, more like. My sister's on the Crescent, she said the police were up that way last night."

The nurse, quieter: "It was awful, what they're saying. Whole front of the house gone."

The conductor came past, glanced down at Caleb without slowing.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 09:14

He took the note out of his inside breast pocket and unfolded it on his lap, low, where the angle of the seat would shield it from the man with the *Express*.

The workshop paper was thin, a faint ruling on it from being torn out of a receipt pad. His father's handwriting — small, hurried, the *C* of *Caleb* sharper than usual.

> *Caleb — come round to the workshop before the cab. I want a word before you go. Don't worry your mother. — Dad.*

That was all of it. Twenty-one words, not counting the dash and the signature. He turned the paper over again. Nothing on the back but the faint impression of the receipt above it on the pad — a number, *£2 18s 6d*, and the half-word *clean—* before the tear.

The wax seal was the cheap brown stuff. No stamp pressed into it.

He folded the paper back along the same crease, slid it into the breast pocket. The *Evening News* page was in there too, behind it. He didn't take that out.

The bus rattled past a long brick wall, posters peeling in strips. The conductor called the next stop.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 09:15

He took the *Evening News* page out next, unfolded it the same careful way. Yesterday's date along the top: *Manchester Evening News, Tuesday, 30 August 1977.* Page 4. His father's pencil-circle around the small piece, three paragraphs, lower right.

> **HOUSE FIRE IN SALFORD — TWO DEAD**
>
> *A man and a woman were found dead in the early hours of this morning following a fire at a house on a residential street in the Salford area, police confirmed yesterday afternoon. The blaze, which broke out at around 2 a.m., destroyed the front of the property before fire crews brought it under control.*
>
> *The deceased have been named locally as Mrs Margaret Brackley, 39, a teacher at a local primary school, and a male visitor to the property. The householder, Mr Daniel Brackley, 41, has not been seen since Monday evening and police are appealing for information about his whereabouts. A spokesman for Greater Manchester Police said inquiries were ongoing.*
>
> *Neighbours described the family as quiet and well-liked. The Brackleys' daughter, who is twelve, was staying with relatives at the time of the fire.*

That was all of it. No street name. *A residential street in the Salford area.*

A *male visitor*. Not the husband. Not the husband's body and the wife's body — the wife and someone else. The husband was missing.

He folded the page along his father's crease and put it back behind the note in the breast pocket.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 09:17

The woman with the knitting smiled — the slightly wistful smile older women sometimes had for polite young men leaving on a sad errand. "Take care, love. Hope you find your friend well."

Caleb stepped off the back platform. The conductor rang the bell behind him; the bus hissed and pulled away.

He was on a wide pavement at a junction. Across the road a row of plane trees lined the start of the Crescent itself — a long curving terrace of old, soot-blackened houses, the kind that had been Salford's better address a century ago and had come down in the world since. Cars parked nose-to-tail along the kerb. The morning was bright now, properly so, gulls overhead working the wind off the river.

A woman with a shopping bag walked past him. A man on a bicycle. A milk float two streets down. Nothing visibly out of place. No police tape that he could see from this junction. He didn't know which house he was looking for.

The first person who came past was a man in his thirties with a bag of tools, walking briskly. Caleb caught him with a *sorry, mate* — the man slowed but didn't stop properly, the way working men didn't stop properly for strangers on a Wednesday morning.

"Bus said the Crescent, after the church. Is this the bit they call the Crescent itself, or is there more of it further down?"

"Aye, this is it." The man gestured ahead with his chin. "Runs all the way down to the river. You're at the top end. — Lookin' for an address?"

"A friend's place. Not sure of the number. Off the Crescent somewhere, the lady on the bus said."

"*Off* the Crescent's a lot of ground." He'd stopped now, properly. "There's the side streets — Encombe Place, Adelphi Street, the lanes back of the terrace. Which side?"

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 09:21

The workman nodded, slow.

"Aye, that'll be it. Folk talk." He shifted his tool bag to the other shoulder. "Right. Liverpool Street — back up the way you came, past the church, left at the second set of lights. You'll come into it after a few hundred yards. The bit with the flats coming down is on your right as you walk. He paused. Looked at Caleb properly for the first time — bag, jacket, the obvious not-from-round-here of him.

"You're a long way from a friend's house, lad. Watch your pockets walking through. Hanky Park's not what it was, but it's not nothing either. Go on."

He nodded once and went on his way, brisk again, off down the Crescent toward whatever job his tool bag was for.

The morning was getting warm. The walk back up to the church and over to Liverpool Street was — what he'd been told — twenty minutes.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 09:21

He set off back up the Crescent the way the bus had brought him. Past the wool shop, the butcher with two ducks hanging in the window, the Methodist church with its jumble-sale board. The pavement was uneven where roots had got under it. A woman was scrubbing her front step with a brush. A cat watched him from a windowsill.

The day *was* nice — warmer than it had been at half past eight, the sky going proper blue between the chimney pots. Gulls overhead. The smell of fresh bread from somewhere off to the left, then the smell of frying bacon from a cafe he didn't see.

He came to the church. Turned at the lights — the first set — kept on. The second set of lights was further than the workman had made it sound; a good ten minutes. He passed a junior school out for its mid-morning break, the noise of it spilling over the railings. Past a barber's with a man sitting in the chair under a sheet. Past a shuttered pub with peeling paint on the sign.

At the second set of lights he turned left.

Liverpool Street opened ahead of him: a wider road, a long view toward the river. On the right side, three streets along, he could see what the workman had meant by *the flats coming down*. Half a block of post-war low-rises in a state of demolition — one already rubble, the other two part-demolished, scaffolding and a yellow excavator parked on the broken ground. A wire fence around the lot.

A few hundred yards further on, at a corner, he saw a parked Panda car. White and blue, *Greater Manchester Police* down the side. One uniformed constable on the pavement, hands behind his back, looking at nothing in particular. Beyond him, half-hidden by the angle of a side street, what looked like the dark mouth of a fire-damaged frontage.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 09:39

He crossed Liverpool Street at the lights and walked up the far pavement, slow, hands in his pockets like a boy walking somewhere he wasn't in any hurry to get to.

The constable on the opposite kerb didn't look his way. Hands behind the back, weight on one heel. A Panda car at the kerb behind him, engine off.

Across the road, up the side street, the front of a terraced house came properly into view as Caleb drew level. Mid-row, two-up two-down, brick blackened by decades of soot and now further blackened by something faster. The front door was gone — boarded over with a sheet of plywood. The window beside it was boarded too. The bricks above the door were scorched in the long upward fan of a fire that had come out the front. Smoke had stained the wall of the house next door above the shared chimney. A scorched mattress, or part of one, leaned against the kerb in a heap of debris that hadn't been carted off yet.

A second constable stood near the boarded door, in conversation with a man in a brown raincoat — not in uniform, but the kind of brown raincoat that did the work of a uniform. CID, probably. They were not looking at the house; they were looking at a clipboard the raincoat was holding.

Two doors further down on the same side, an older woman in a housecoat stood on her step with her arms folded, watching the policemen the way people watched policemen on their own street.

Nobody had looked at Caleb.

## User

[[OOC: what is CID]

## Narrator

OOC: CID is the **Criminal Investigation Department** — the plainclothes detective branch of British police. The uniformed officers (constables in tunics, helmets, the obvious "police" look) handle ordinary patrol and immediate response. CID detectives investigate serious crimes — murders, arsons, suspicious deaths — and you can usually spot them on a scene because they're in plain clothes (often a slightly tired suit and a raincoat in this era) but they're clearly there in an official capacity, taking notes, talking to officers, examining things.

So the man in the brown raincoat with the clipboard, in 1977, is almost certainly a detective inspector or detective sergeant from Greater Manchester Police's CID, working the Brackley case. The two uniformed constables are doing scene-protection and door-knock work; the CID man is the one running the investigation.

The cultural shorthand: a uniform is "the police"; a raincoat with a notebook is "a detective." Both are coppers, but they do different jobs.

Ready to keep going whenever you are.

He crossed back over Liverpool Street and turned up the side street toward the constables. The one on the kerb watched him come — hands no longer behind his back now, dropped to his sides, attention switched on.

"Help you, lad?"

Caleb stopped a polite few yards short. "Caleb Waddell. My family are friends of the Brackleys — my mother more than me. I read the paper this morning and I came over. I don't want to be in the way. I just — the article said there was a man with Mrs Brackley who hasn't been identified yet. If you've a picture, I might recognise him. That's the only way I can think of to help."

The CID man with the clipboard had turned at the sound of Caleb's voice. Late forties, tired eyes, a moustache going grey. He came a step closer. The constable looked at him; he gave the constable a small nod that meant *I'll take this*.

"Detective Sergeant Hollis." He didn't put a hand out. "Friend of the family, you say. — How old are you, son?"

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 09:43

Hollis nodded once, slow, weighing it. The detective's small assessment — *fair enough, plausible, not what I'd have done in your shoes but plausible.*

"All right. We can do that." He flipped a page over on the clipboard, clicked his biro. "I'll give you a description, and you take it to your mother. If anything stands out, you come straight back here and ask for me by name — Detective Sergeant Hollis, Salford CID, Crescent station. Understood?"

He didn't look down at the clipboard for the description. He'd been carrying it in his head.

"White male, late thirties to mid-forties. Roughly five foot ten. Dark brown hair, going grey at the temples, worn a little long over the collar. Clean-shaven. Slim build, on the wiry side. Wearing — what was left of it — a dark suit, white shirt, no tie, brown brogues. No wedding ring. No wallet on him. No identification of any kind."

He paused.

"That ring any bells with you, Mr Waddell? Off the top — before you take it to your mother."

## User

[[OOC: does that ring any bells to me?]]

## Narrator

OOC, fair question to ask me. Let me think about what Caleb actually has to work with.

Caleb is 18, half-blood, from a Manchester family. The description Hollis gave — white man, late 30s to mid-40s, dark brown hair greying at the temples, worn a little long, clean-shaven, slim, wiry, dark suit, no tie, brown brogues, no ring, no wallet, no ID — is generic enough that it could be a thousand men. Specifically it's the kind of description that fits *most* of the men of his father's generation Caleb has ever met at a social occasion.

The things that would or wouldn't ring a bell for Caleb honestly depend on facts about your family's social circle that haven't been established yet — and I shouldn't make those up for you. What I can tell you about what Caleb has access to:

- **He knows his parents have wizarding family friends, plural, but no specific ones have been named on screen yet.** The Brackleys are the only mixed family his father has explicitly mentioned to him, and that was today.
- **The "no wedding ring, no wallet, no ID" part is the unusual bit.** A respectable man in a suit at half past one in the morning, in a friend's house, with absolutely nothing in his pockets — that's strange enough that even a clever boy without a forensic background would notice. People in 1977 Britain carry wallets. Men of that age in suits carry ID. The absence of any of it is what stands out.
- **The "no ring" might mean unmarried, or it might mean removed.** Either is interesting and Caleb would clock either possibility.
- **The clothing — dark suit, white shirt, no tie, brown brogues** — reads as a man who was either at work, or going to or from work, or coming from somewhere semi-formal. Not a labourer. Not someone in casuals. Office or office-adjacent.

So the honest answer to "does that ring any bells" is: **the description itself doesn't match anyone Caleb has a name for.** But the *details* of how the man was found — no ID, no ring, no wallet — are striking in a way Caleb might mention without claiming to recognise the person.

You can play this several ways:
1. *"No, sir, that doesn't sound like anyone I know off the top of my head."* — straight, honest, hands the description to your mother where you said it was going.
2. *Press on the no-ID detail* — "Sir, why would a friend of theirs visiting at one in the morning have nothing on him at all? Was the wallet taken?" — which signals you're paying attention and might draw more out of Hollis.
3. *Hedge* — "Nothing immediate, but I'll think about it on the walk."

Your call.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 09:45

Hollis listened all the way through. Didn't interrupt. The biro had stopped moving on the clipboard about halfway and he'd let it stop.

When Caleb was finished, the detective let a small breath out through his nose. Almost a laugh, but not quite — the recognition a tired man gives when somebody else's eighteen-year-old has just walked the route he himself walked at three in the morning, in his own head, on this same pavement.

"You read a lot of detective novels, Mr Waddell?"

He didn't wait for an answer.

"Marks on the body — that I'm not telling you. Not on a pavement, not to a friend of the family." A short pause. "What I'll say is: yes, there are marks. Yes, the door went the way you guessed. And yes, we don't think the husband did it."

He looked at Caleb properly for a moment.

"Take that home to your mother. The description, not the rest. The rest you forget I said. — One thing in return, son. Your address. Where can someone reach your mother if we need to speak to her?"

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 09:46

Hollis turned the clipboard over and clicked the biro again.

Caleb gave it: the house number, the lane, the postal district north of Manchester. The detective wrote it out in the slow careful hand of a man who'd been writing addresses on clipboards for twenty years.

"And your mother's name?"

"Eleanor Waddell."

Hollis wrote it. Read it back to himself once, silently. Underlined the postal district.

"Right." He flipped the clipboard shut. "Mr Waddell — thank you. Someone may be in touch in the next few days. If your mother recognises the man from the description, she telephones Crescent station and asks for me. If she doesn't, that's also useful to know — somebody'll come round to her either way, just routine."

He paused.

"And son. The bit you guessed at, on the pavement. Don't guess at it out loud anywhere else for a while. Not in a pub, not on a bus. Friend of the family or not."

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 09:46

Hollis looked at him for a moment. The fond exasperation of a man who'd just told a clever boy to stop being clever, and was now being asked one more clever question for the road.

"Mr Waddell."

He let it sit.

"I told you what I'm telling you. The rest you'll read in the paper or you won't." He tucked the biro into his breast pocket. "Top button I'm not telling you. Tie I'm not telling you."

A small breath.

"You've got a train to catch, I should think. Off you go. Look after your mother."

He stepped back, half-turned to the constable on the kerb, and the conversation was over without quite being ended. The constable nodded at Caleb — *go on, then* — and Hollis was already moving back toward the boarded door, clipboard at his side.

The older woman in the housecoat two doors down had not moved. She was still watching.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 09:47

Caleb sat down on the kerb opposite the boarded house, satchel on his knees, and fixed Hollis with the kind of stare a clever boy could maintain for a long time.

Hollis registered it. Did not turn round properly. Said something quiet to the constable on the kerb that Caleb couldn't hear, and went back to the clipboard with the man in the brown raincoat.

The constable wandered a few paces toward Caleb's side of the road. Stopped at the kerb. Hands behind his back again.

"You all right there, son?"

Mild. Not aggressive yet. The voice of a man who had been told to keep the boy in view without making a thing of it.

A bus went past at the top of the road. A pigeon landed in the gutter near Caleb's foot, considered him, walked off. Two doors down, the older woman in the housecoat had finally gone back inside, her front door closing with a small definite click.

▼ 09:53

Six minutes in. The constable hadn't moved on. He'd settled into a parade-rest a few yards off, watching the side street more than Caleb but not pretending Caleb wasn't there.

▼ 10:02

Fifteen minutes. Hollis came back out of the side street once, glanced over, didn't acknowledge him. Went back in.

The constable cleared his throat.

"Lad. We're going to need you to move along soon. You've had your chat."

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 10:02

The constable's mouth twitched on *Four-in-Hand Fiend*. He worked very hard not to let it become a smile and mostly succeeded. Hollis, twenty yards off, did not turn around but his shoulders went a fraction stiller in the way a man's shoulders go when he is choosing not to react.

"Off you trot, lad."

Caleb stood, brushed the back of his trousers, and went.

Liverpool Street was bright and warm now and the morning had that mid-week mid-morning look — kids back at school, the shops doing slow business, a man washing the windows of a cafe on the corner with a long-handled squeegee. He went back the way he'd come: past the half-demolished flats, past the second set of lights, past the church with its jumble-sale board, past the wool shop, the butcher with the ducks. The 33 stop was where he'd left it. He waited four minutes. The bus came.

Threepence. A seat halfway down on the right. Salford fell away behind. The Irwell. The grey arteries of the city. Manchester Piccadilly came up at the end of the route and he got off at the forecourt where Eddie had dropped him hours ago — a different version of the morning, now.

The station clock read just past ten thirty. Five hours till the Euston.

*I call my mother on a public phone that I assume is a doctor Who type deal*

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 10:35

The phone box on the forecourt was the proper kind — red, cast-iron, a small heavy door, the smell of old cigarette smoke and brass polish inside. He stepped in and the door swung shut behind him with the satisfying weight of an institution.

He fed coins. The dial whirred — *click-click-click-click* — under his finger, six digits, and the line connected with a click and a hum and then his mother's voice in his ear, slightly tinny.

"Hello?"

"Mum, it's me. Quick — am I getting through? I'm at Piccadilly, the train got moved. I won't be on the nine-fifteen, I'm on a later one. I'll be in London tonight."

A small pause on her end.

"Got moved how?"

"Engineering work. They put me on the fifteen-twenty. I'll get to the lodgings about seven. I just wanted to ring so you weren't watching the clock at noon thinking I'd be in London by then."

"All right, love. Have you eaten?"

"I'm going to."

"You'll write."

"I'll write."

The pips started — first warning that the call was running out. He'd not put enough in.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 10:36

He shoved another coin in. The pips cut off mid-stutter and the line steadied.

"Mum — listen. I went over to Salford this morning before the train. The article Dad showed me was the Brackley fire. Was that — were they your friends? Because I went and spoke to a detective. I gave him our address and your name. I'm sorry. He's going to ring you or send someone round."

The line did the small empty hiss of a long-distance connection thinking.

"You went to *Salford*."

"I'm sorry, Mum."

A pause. He could hear her breathing.

"What detective. What was his name."

"Hollis. Salford CID, he said. Crescent station."

Another pause.

"All right. — Caleb." Her voice was different now, the way it went when she was concentrating. "Tell me what he told you. Quickly."

"They've a description of the man who was with her. White, late thirties to mid-forties, five ten. Dark brown hair greying at the temples, a bit long over the collar. Slim, wiry. Dark suit, white shirt, no tie, brown brogues. No wedding ring. No wallet. No ID at all on him. Mum — does any of that sound like anyone?"

The pause this time was longer. He could hear, faintly, the radio still on in the kitchen behind her.

"Caleb."

"Mum."

"You didn't tell the detective anything that came out of your father's mouth this morning, did you."

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 10:38

"No, Mum. Honestly. I didn't bring up anything Dad said. I gave the detective the description and our address and your name and that we were friends of the family. That's it. I told him I'd take the description back to you and if you recognised the man, you'd ring."

He pressed a hand against the inside of the kiosk glass. A bus went past outside, dulled through the pane.

"I worked some of it out on my feet — about the door, about the husband — and I said it out loud. He didn't tell me anything I didn't say first. But I didn't say *Dad*, and I didn't say anything about — about the way Dad and I were talking this morning. None of that. Just neighbours-of-the-Brackleys, mother's old friends, came over because I read the paper."

A pause.

"Mum, does the description sound like anyone? I've been chewing on it for an hour."

The line hissed. He heard her cover the mouthpiece for a second — a movement, maybe to look at something, maybe to compose her face.

When she came back her voice was careful.

"Caleb. The fifteen-twenty's a long train. You'll get to London tonight and you'll go straight to the lodgings and you'll write me a proper letter from the Castle when you get there, all right? — I'll talk to your father about Mr Hollis. You don't think about it again."

A pause.

"And love — the description. I'm not going to tell you on a station phone. All right?"
