## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 10:39

A small huff of breath on the other end of the line — the closest his mother got to a laugh on a day like this.

"I'll pass that on. Word for word." A pause. "I love you. Be safe yourself. Eat something proper before the train, not just a bun."

The pips started up again, second warning.

"Go on. Hang up. Ring me from school when you're settled."

The line clicked dead before he had to decide whether to say goodbye twice.

Caleb stood in the kiosk for a moment with the receiver still in his hand. Cradled it. Pushed the heavy door open. The forecourt noise came back at him — pigeons, a porter's whistle, a black cab idling.

The station clock above the entrance read twenty to eleven. Almost five hours till the Euston.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 10:42

He crossed the forecourt to the row of food stalls along the side of the station — the proper Manchester Piccadilly cluster of a baker's barrow, a fish-and-chips van not yet open, a fruit-and-veg pitch with a man hauling crates off the back of a Bedford lorry, and a tea wagon doing brisk trade with the morning's last commuters.

The man with the lorry was the obvious one. Crates of King Edwards stacked three high on the pavement, more in the back, his stallholder son trying to set up the front display while customers picked at the apples already out. The man himself — fifties, big forearms, sweat patches on his shirt — was wrestling a sack of carrots over his shoulder and clearly behind schedule.

Caleb went over.

"Sir. Caleb Waddell. I've four and a half hours till my train and an empty pocket. Put me to work. Whatever you'd pay a casual lad for the morning, less if you like, plus a bit of dinner. I'll lift, I'll stack, I'll mind the till, I'll keep my mouth shut. — Need a hand?"

The man set the sack down on the kerb. Looked at Caleb. Looked at the lorry. Looked back at Caleb.

"Got a strong back?"

"Strong enough."

"Ten bob for the four hours and a bag of chips and an apple. You break a crate, you pay for it. — Jacket off, sleeves up. Start with them potatoes."

## User

*I work and work*

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 14:45

He worked.

The first hour was crates of King Edwards off the back of the Bedford, bent at the knees the way his father had taught him, two crates a trip onto the trestle behind the stall. The second hour was carrots and a sack of onions that split halfway and he chased onions across the pavement while the stallholder swore good-naturedly at the lorry. The third hour was minding the front of the stall — weighing, bagging, ringing up on the brass-handled till — while the man and his son ate their dinner in the cab, which was the part Caleb was secretly best at. He gave correct change. He talked to a woman about whether her plums were ripe. He did not break a single crate.

The fourth hour, his arms had gone the dull tired they went after a real morning's work and the man had stopped checking on him.

By half past two he was sat on an empty crate in the lee of the stall, sleeves rolled, hair stuck to his forehead, eating chips out of newspaper with vinegar on them. The apple was in his jacket pocket for later. The stallholder had tipped him the agreed two pounds in coin and a folded ten-shilling note that wasn't supposed to exist any more — *for the onions, lad, you didn't have to chase those, that one's mine* — and he'd waved off Caleb's protest.

The chips were very good. The pocket watch, when he dug it out, said quarter to three. The Euston was at twenty past.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 14:48

He took a wander down the row, hands in his pockets, the apple thumping faintly against his hip with every step.

The fish-and-chips van was a man and his wife, both fifty-odd. The tea wagon was an old gent with a moustache and a fag end. The bakery barrow had a teenage boy minding it. At the far end of the row, past where his stallholder's lorry was parked, there was a small flower stall set up against the station wall — buckets of late-summer asters and dahlias, a few wilting roses, sprays of something purple he didn't know the name of. Behind it stood a young woman a few years older than he was — twenty, twenty-one — in a cotton dress and a cardigan, sleeves pushed up, snipping the bottoms of stems with a pair of florist's scissors. She had an easy face, sun-browned, hair pulled back in a yellow scarf.

She caught him looking. Raised an eyebrow at him without stopping the snipping.

"After something?"

She looked him over. Took in the rolled sleeves, the dust on the forearms, the apple-shaped bulge in his jacket pocket.

"Caleb. You been humping potatoes for Mr Cheadle this morning?"

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 14:52

The bakery barrow was a four-stall sprint away. He went there at speed — not in a straight line, because he had decided on the way over that this was a serpentining errand — and arrived at the boy Trevor's counter slightly out of breath.

The barrow had: a tray of Eccles cakes, half-empty; a tray of iced buns; a tray of vanilla slices that had clearly been sat in the sun for an hour and a half; one custard tart left under a glass dome; sausage rolls that were not romantic; and, at the back behind the counter, a small box of two miniature jam tarts arranged side by side, with a sprig of pastry leaf set between them, fifteen pence the pair.

He went for the jam tarts.

"Two of those, please. The boxed ones."

Trevor handed them across in their tiny white box. "Fifteen p."

He paid. Pocketed the change. Set off back the way he'd come, serpentining, weaving past a businessman with a briefcase and a woman pulling a tartan trolley, the box held flat in front of him in both hands like an offering.

Annie was watching him come. She had got the apple from the bench in his absence and was holding it.

"Oh, *no*," she said. "What've you done."

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 14:55

Annie took the fifty-pence piece, rolled her eyes, and got to work.

She moved fast — three dahlias from the buckets, the dark red and one of the pinky-oranges and a white one, then the unloved pom-pom yellow tucked in at the back. A handful of the small white gypsophila threaded between them. She held the bunch up, considered the balance, swapped the white dahlia for the other pinky-orange, considered again, and was satisfied. A square of brown paper from a roll under the bench, folded around the stems with the practiced four-corners motion of someone who'd been doing it since she was twelve. A length of green string, two turns, a small bow.

She handed it across.

"There. — Tenpence change."

She put the coin on top of the wrapped bouquet and met his eye properly for the first time.

"Tell her she's lucky."

He pushed the tenpence back across the bench, set the box of jam tarts on top of it, and laid the bouquet on top of that.

"You're lucky."

Annie stared down at the small stack on her bench — coin, tart-box, her own bouquet returned — and then up at him, and her mouth did the thing where it tried to be cross and could not quite manage it.

"Half each. As agreed." She held the tart up between thumb and finger. "The flowers are yours and they're going on whatever train you're going on. The tenpence is mine because you are not getting out of taking your change. — Eat your tart, daft lad."

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 14:57

Annie chewed her bit of tart. Took her time about it. Watched him over the top of it.

"Smell me."

"A little."

"You absolute weirdo." She set the rest of her tart down on the lid. Considered him with her head tilted, the yellow scarf catching the afternoon light. "Yeah, all right. Quick one. And I'm absolutely going to know you did it, so don't pretend that bit's working."

She leaned an elbow on the bench, presented her cheek with the small ceremony of a woman humouring a polite madman, and waited.

He came round the bench. She smelled of cut stems and the green water in the buckets — sharp, clean, a thread of something mineral underneath — and, fainter, of soap. A talc-and-rose soap, the cheap chemist's kind. And a trace of cigarette smoke in her hair from someone else's, not hers, the kind a stallholder picked up off customers all day. Her cardigan smelled of the cardigan.

He kissed her cheek. Quick, neat, the way she'd asked for.

When he stepped back she had not moved. Her eyes were doing a small considering thing.

"That was efficient."

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 14:58

Annie didn't move for a long second. Her eyes did the same considering thing they'd done after the first kiss, but slower this time, like she was reading the back of a book before deciding to buy it.

Then she breathed out through her nose — half a laugh, half something else.

"Caleb. Mate." She picked up the rest of her jam tart, looked at it, set it back down. "You've got a girl. You've bought her a fountain pen and a bunch of dahlias and you're on a train at three to take them to her. I don't know what you've gone and convinced yourself of in the last ten minutes, but it isn't me."

Mild. Not unkind. The voice of a young woman who'd watched a lad work himself up to a romantic notion at her stall and had decided it was her job to put it down gently.

"You can have the second kiss. On the cheek, same as the first, and you take it with you, and you go and find your train, and you give those flowers to the girl they're for. — How's that for a deal?"

She held her cheek out again. Patient. The yellow scarf catching the light.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 14:59

"Twenty-one." Annie was already laughing as she said it. "And cheeky."

He came round the bench. She watched him come — eyes open, weight on her front foot, not retreating but not leaning in either. He leaned in slow. The barest scrape of his lower lip against the corner of her upper one, where the jam was. A ghost of contact. He pulled back at the same pace, eyes still open.

She blinked once. Looked at him. Touched the corner of her mouth with her thumb, checked the thumb, found a small smear of red, and laughed — properly, this time, the way she'd laughed at the *Rose* joke, an actual laugh that had to come up out of her.

"Caleb Waddell," she said. "*Tarts*."

She shook her head. Wiped her thumb on her cardigan. Reached under the bench and came up with a small notepad — the one she used for orders — tore the top sheet off the back and wrote on it. Folded it. Held it out between two fingers.

"Annie Holroyd, care of Holroyd's Florists, Stevenson Square, Manchester. — Write me a proper letter. None of this *I have a feeling about you* business. Tell me about the puzzles." A pause. "I might write back. I might not. You'll find out by whether I do."

She tucked the rest of the bouquet into the back row of her display, made the smallest adjustment to it, and looked up at him with the eyebrow back where it had started.

"Train, daft lad. Twenty past three. Go."

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 15:00

He took ten steps.

He stopped. The forecourt noise around him — pigeons, a tannoy calling a Bristol train, somebody's pram wheel squeaking — went on without him. He counted the seconds because the small pleased part of his brain that wanted to count them was loud.

Then he turned and went back, faster than he'd left.

Annie was watching him come. She had that small tilted-head consideration on her face again, the one she did with stems she was deciding about. She did not move away. She did not move toward.

He came round the bench properly this time. Took her face in both hands — careful of the scarf, careful with her — and kissed her.

A real one. Not hungry; not boyish. A held kiss, mouth softened, the tart-sweetness of jam still faintly there, the cleanness of her under it. Her hand came up after a moment and rested on the inside of his wrist. Not pulling, not pushing. Just there.

When he pulled back her eyes were closed. They opened slow.

"*Bloody hell*, Caleb."

A breath. She let go of his wrist.

"Go on. Go to your train."

She nudged him backward off her side of the bench with two fingers against his chest, gentle. Picked up her florist's scissors. Did not look at him as he went, because she would not be able to keep her face in order if she did.

The forecourt clock said three minutes past three. Platform 3 was a flight of steps and a footbridge away.

*I take one flower from the bouqet I gave her. a red dahlia* "how do I press this?"

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 15:03

Annie turned at the sound of him still being there. Saw the dahlia in his hand. Didn't say anything about him taking one back.

"Press it." She set the scissors down again. "Easy. You want a heavy book — bigger the better. A dictionary's perfect, an atlas is even better. Lay it flat between two sheets of clean paper — blotting paper if you've got it, or a folded napkin, or just plain writing paper if you haven't. Don't use newspaper, ink runs. Cut the stem short, head and a bit of stem only. Lay it flat, petals open, the way you want it to dry. Close the book. Stack a few more books on top for weight."

She glanced at the dahlia in his hand, the dark red one, the one with the most petals.

"That one'll take three weeks, maybe four. They're fat-headed, dahlias. They want time. Don't peek before two weeks or you'll undo it. — And once it's done, you slide it between the pages of something you'll keep, or you mount it with a spot of glue. That's it. Anyone tells you otherwise is showing off."

She paused.

"Don't lose it on the train."

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 15:05

Annie made a sound that was half a strangled laugh and half a *don't you dare make me cry at my own stall on a Wednesday*.

"*Engagement present.* Caleb Waddell." She put a hand over her face, briefly. "Three weeks for a dahlia. You're going to send me an *engaged* dahlia in the post in three weeks. I'm going to have to explain that to my mother. — Go on. Go. Before I climb over this bench."

She waved him off.

He walked back across the forecourt to Cheadle's stall. The man was minding the front again, his son packing crates back into the lorry. Caleb collected his trunk-claim ticket out of his satchel and handed Cheadle his half-eaten apple as a parting gift, which got a *go on, you daft sod* and a wave.

The Left Luggage office was twenty paces beyond. Proudfoot was still on, still in his rolled sleeves, still bored. He took the claim ticket, glanced at the number, lifted the trunk down out of cubby 47, and slid it across the counter without comment.

"All right, son?"

"All right."

The clock above the booking hall said eight minutes past three. Platform 3 was up the steps and over the bridge. He hauled the trunk by its end-strap, satchel over his shoulder, dahlia carefully held between two fingers of his free hand.

*I get there and wait. let me play out the train.*

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 15:11

Platform 3 was the long curving one on the far side of the bridge. He hauled the trunk down the steps a bump at a time and out into the platform's cool concrete-and-diesel air. The 15:20 to Euston was already in — a long maroon-and-grey rake of Mark IIs, the engine somewhere up at the front, doors hanging open along the length of it.

He found a second-class carriage near the middle, hauled the trunk up the small step, and worked it into the luggage rack at the end of the corridor with the help of a man already aboard who took one end without being asked. Compartments off the corridor, six seats apiece, most of them already occupied. He found one with two empty seats — an older woman by the window with a hardback library book, a man in a tweed jacket with a briefcase on his lap, an empty bench opposite. He took the corner facing forward.

Pen-box safe in the satchel on the rack overhead. Bouquet — gone, with Annie. Single red dahlia laid carefully on the inside of his folded jacket on the seat beside him, head down, where the stem wouldn't get crushed.

The platform announcer called the stops in the long unhurried voice of mid-afternoon: *Stockport, Macclesfield, Stoke-on-Trent, Stafford, Rugby, Watford Junction, London Euston.*

▼ 15:18

A whistle. The doors slammed along the rake one after another in the long staccato that meant departure was nearly. The man with the briefcase glanced at his own watch, satisfied.

▼ 15:20

A second whistle. The carriage gave the small forward jolt of a long train starting. Manchester Piccadilly slid past the window — the platforms, the parcel office, the long brick gable end with WHITBREAD'S painted across it from another decade — and then they were over the points, out across the river, into the slow grey southbound run.

The older woman turned a page. The man with the briefcase opened a *Daily Telegraph*.

*I write a letter to Annie Holroyd, care of Holroyd's Florists, Stevenson Square, Manchester . I tell her that I just left, and how excited I had been to meet my future wife. I describe the sites I see out the window. the countryside. I ask her what her favorite flower is. what she likes to do for fun. Love, Caleb*

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 15:34

He took out the Conway Stewart, uncapped it carefully, and bent over the small fold-down table at the carriage window. Got the Quink open without spilling, dipped the nib, watched the feed draw the ink up properly. Tried it on the corner of his thigh through the trousers, which everyone does and no one is supposed to. It wrote.

A page from the back of his school notebook. He smoothed it out.

> *31st August 1977, somewhere south of Stockport*
>
> *Dear Annie Holroyd, care of Holroyd's Florists, Stevenson Square, Manchester —*
>
> *I have only just left the platform and I am already writing the letter, which my mother would call premature and Mr Cheadle would probably call something rather worse. But I had said I would, and I find I am no good at sitting on a thing once I have said I will do it.*
>
> *I am, as I write this, a married man in waiting. The dahlia is on the seat beside me, head down, on top of my folded jacket, where I have already moved it twice on account of imagined draughts. I am told three weeks at minimum. I will not peek at it before the fortnight, on your authority. The engagement is going well so far. We have not yet had our first row.*
>
> *Out the window is the countryside getting on with itself. Stockport went past as a great brick viaduct over a river the colour of strong tea, and then the houses thinned out and we are now in the green bit. Cows. A canal. A man fishing on the bank of the canal who did not look up when the train went past, which I admire. Late summer everywhere — the fields gone to that pale blond they go before the harvest, hedges thick and dark, the sky one of those big high-clouded ones that looks painted on. Two rooks flying the same direction the train is going, very satisfied with themselves.*
>
> *In the compartment with me there is an older woman reading a library book whose title I cannot see, and a man in a tweed jacket with a Daily Telegraph who has not turned a page in eight minutes which I think means he is asleep behind it. They smell respectively of lavender water and pipe tobacco, neither of them as nicely as you smelled.*
>
> *Some questions, because a letter has to do something:*
>
> *— What is your favourite flower? I refuse to assume it is dahlias just because they are what you sell. I assume the man at the cheese counter does not, in fact, prefer cheese.*
>
> *— What do you do that is not the stall? Cinema, walks, Sundays out — what is a good day off, for you?*
>
> *— Is the florist's what you want, or is it what you do? Honest question. You can take the question and use it for kindling if you'd rather.*
>
> *— What are you reading, if you read?*
>
> *I will tell you about myself in the next one, in the spirit of letting the lady go first. I will say only that you have already met me at most of my best, which is a slightly worrying thing to admit on the first letter, but there it is.*
>
> *Yours, with the very faintest scrape of jam still notionally on my mouth somewhere —*
>
> *Caleb*

He looked at the signature. Considered. Got the apple out of his jacket pocket — the one he'd given Cheadle had been the half he'd already eaten; this was the whole one Annie had pressed back at him along with her tart. He'd not eaten it. He bit into it, turned the bite in his mouth, and pressed his lips down beside his name on the page — a smudge of apple and the faint sticky-red of jam still on him from her mouth. A ridiculous lip print, slightly off-shape, exactly as intended.

He looked at it. Did not regret it.

Capped the pen.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 18:12

The light had gone amber by Rugby and copper by Watford. The compartment had cycled — the older woman with the library book got off at Stafford with a small *good afternoon*; the man in tweed slept on through Rugby and woke for Watford and got off there to nobody's surprise; a young Black man in a corduroy jacket got on at Watford and read a paperback all the way down. Outside, the afternoon had done its long English thing of going gold and then grey and then the lights of the suburbs starting up one by one.

The train slid into Euston in the long curving approach, the platforms coming up alongside the windows in their lit-up evening order. Pneumatic *hiss* of the brakes. The carriages settled. Doors banging open along the rake.

Euston at six in the evening: the cathedral roof of it, the great mechanical clack of the departures board, the smell of diesel and old coffee, the rush of commuters going the other way home. Caleb hauled the trunk down off the rack — the man in corduroy steadied it for him, nodded, said *cheers* — and carried it down the platform in the river of people.

The lodgings his family had booked were in a small Bloomsbury guesthouse — Mrs Penhale's, a place his father had used for years when business took him to London — about fifteen minutes' walk through the streets behind the British Museum. He had the address in his head and a piece of paper with it written down in the satchel for safety. The Hogwarts Express was tomorrow morning out of King's Cross, ten minutes the other way. London proper was waiting outside the station entrance.

*I walk with my belongings and check in to this place*

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 18:30

The walk through Bloomsbury at this hour was the part of London that knew it was beautiful. Lit pubs spilling onto pavements, the long stuccoed terraces in the slow gold of an evening sun, leaves turning at the tops of the plane trees in the squares. He went past the British Museum's railings — the great pale columns set back behind them, the gates already shut for the day — and turned into a quieter street of tall brick guesthouses with brass numbers and bay-tree pots on the steps.

Mrs Penhale's was number 22. A small brass plaque: *PENHALE'S* in serif capitals, *BED & BREAKFAST* underneath, no pretence beyond that. He climbed the four steps, set the trunk down, and rang.

The door was opened by a small woman in her sixties in a navy cardigan, a tea-towel over one shoulder. She took one look at him — trunk, satchel, dahlia — and her face arranged itself into the recognition of a regular family's son.

"You'll be the Waddell boy. Caleb, isn't it. Your father telephoned this morning to say you'd be in this evening rather than first thing tomorrow." She held the door wider. "Come in, love. The room's the back single on the first floor — same as last time you came down with him. Supper's in twenty minutes if you want it, six o'clock sharp normally but I'll keep yours warm."

She moved the tea-towel off her shoulder and held a hand out for the trunk handle.

"Up we go. Mind the runner on the second step, it's worked loose again."

[[OOC: give me a full vision prompt for a character portrait for annie, details as specific as possible]]

## Narrator

OOC: Happy to. This is `/vision Annie` — a focused image-gen prompt of Annie, the flower-stall worker Caleb just met. Adult character, ordinary portrait. Note: I have her hair colour, eye colour, and exact build as a "you choose" since none of those got specified in scene — I'll generate plausible defaults and you can swap any of them.

**Character portrait, single subject, three-quarter length, candid environmental portrait style.**

A young Englishwoman, twenty-one years old, photographed at her flower stall on the outdoor forecourt of Manchester Piccadilly station on a warm late-summer Wednesday afternoon, around three o'clock. Natural daylight, slightly hazy, the kind of soft afternoon glow that comes through a station's open colonnade — warm side-lighting from camera-left, gentle shadow on the right side of her face. Late-1970s Manchester, period-accurate, completely unidealised.

She is of average height and build — neither slim nor heavy, just an ordinary working young woman's frame, healthy, her arms slightly sun-browned from a summer of working outdoors at the stall. Her face is open and English: a wide forehead, slightly rounded cheeks, a faint scattering of freckles across the bridge of her nose, a mouth that rests on the verge of a smile. Hazel eyes. Wavy chestnut-brown hair pulled back loosely, with a few strands escaping at the temples, and tied at the nape with a yellow cotton headscarf knotted at the back. Pierced ears, no earrings.

She is wearing a knee-length floral cotton sundress in a small print of dark red and cream on a faded olive ground — the dress soft from many washings — under a plain cream cardigan with the sleeves pushed up to her elbows. Plimsolls or simple flat lace-up shoes, lightly scuffed. No makeup beyond perhaps a trace of pink lip colour, faintly smudged at the corner of her mouth. Practical, unselfconscious; the look of a young woman who dressed for working a long day at a flower stall, not for being photographed.

She stands behind a wooden trestle bench at her stall, leaning on it with one elbow, florist's scissors set down beside her right hand. In front of her: galvanised metal buckets holding cut dahlias (dark red, pinky-orange, white, and an unloved pom-pom yellow), sprays of small white gypsophila, a few late-summer asters, a small brass-handled till, a roll of brown wrapping paper, a ball of green string, a small notepad with order pencil. The bench surface is dusted with leaf trimmings and small petals.

Behind her, the long pale brick of Manchester Piccadilly station's exterior wall, slightly out of focus — soft shapes of pigeons in the rafters, a glimpse of a station entrance, blurred figures of commuters. The depth of field is shallow; she is sharp, the background atmospheric.

Her expression: a small private amused look, eyebrow raised half a hair, the look of a young woman who has just had a slightly ridiculous and slightly sweet thing happen to her at her stall and is choosing not to make too much of it. Direct gaze just off-camera, as if at someone two paces from the lens.

**Style:** photorealistic, the visual register of a Saul Leiter or Vivian Maier candid portrait but in colour — warm, slightly grainy, gently saturated, the look of a Kodachrome photograph from 1977. Not glamour, not staged. Composition slightly off-centre, room above her head for the station roof to imply itself.

**Avoid:** modern hairstyles, modern makeup, modern fabrics, plastic textures, smooth/airbrushed skin, fantasy elements, magical iconography, contemporary phone or device, anything that breaks the 1977 working-class Manchester register.

Swap any detail — hair colour, eye colour, freckles, dress print — to whatever you'd prefer. Want me to do one for Mei next, or hold that for when Caleb actually sees her tomorrow?

Picking up where we were:

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 18:31

Mrs Penhale held the door open for him. The hall smelled of furniture polish and something cooking — a stew, beef-and-onion, the kind of smell that made a tired traveller's stomach announce itself without permission. A grandfather clock in the hall ticked the half-hour. The runner up the stairs was Turkish, worn in the middle, exactly the way Mrs Penhale had said.

She took the trunk handle and started up. He followed with the satchel and the dahlia.

The back single on the first floor: small, clean, a single iron bedstead made up with white linen, a washstand with a porcelain basin, a single window onto the lights of a back garden, a small writing desk with a lamp. A folded towel on the bed. The cleanest place to land on a long day.

"Bathroom's two doors down on your right, there's a queue in the morning so don't dawdle." Mrs Penhale set the trunk at the foot of the bed. "Supper at seven sharp — that's twenty-five minutes — in the dining room, which is the door on your left when you come down. There's two other guests tonight, a Mrs Atherton up from Salisbury and a young man called Fletcher who's something at the Museum. — Wash your hands, love. You look like you've been working."

She gave him the small approving nod of a Manchester-recognising woman to a Manchester-shaped lad and went out, pulling the door to behind her.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 18:55

The bathroom two doors down was the kind that ran hot if you were patient with the geyser and lukewarm if you were not. He was patient. The water came good after a minute and he stood under it long enough to feel the morning's potato dust and the bus and the kerb and the platform run off him.

Back in the room he changed into clean trousers, a fresh shirt, a jumper for the evening. Combed his hair with his fingers in the small mirror over the washstand. Tucked the dahlia stem-up between two folded sheets of writing paper from the desk for the trip downstairs, careful not to crush it.

The dining room was at the front, on the left. A long oak table set for three, candles not yet lit, the smell of the stew much closer now. Mrs Penhale was at the sideboard arranging cutlery.

"Mrs Penhale — sorry to interrupt. I've a flower I want to press." He held the dahlia up between two fingers. "Have you anything heavy I could borrow? A book, an atlas — and some clean paper without ink in it, blotting paper if you've got it?"

She set the cutlery down and considered him over the top of her glasses.

"A *flower*." She held a hand out for it. "Show me. — Oh, that's a proper one. Dahlia, isn't it. Three weeks at least, you know that?"

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 18:56

Mrs Penhale handed the dahlia back to him with proper care, head up, stem down.

"Well, then. Use the schoolbook for the press itself — the heaviest one you've got, lay it flat on the desk in your room. I'll fetch you blotting paper from the writing case in the parlour." She wiped her hands on her apron. "You'll want two sheets of blotting between the petals and the book, fresh ones. Put the flower flat between them, page open in the middle of the book, close it gently. Then weight on top — I've a pair of old encyclopaedias under the hall table you can borrow until you're at school. Stack them on it. Don't move it about. Three weeks, maybe four, and check it after a fortnight, no sooner."

She moved past him into the hall.

"And don't take it on the train tomorrow in your pocket like a daft thing. Keep it pressed in the book, flat in the trunk. — Supper in five minutes, Mr Waddell. Sit yourself down. I'll bring the blotting up to your room before you go up to bed."

She was halfway down the hall before he could thank her properly.

The dining room had three places set, the candles now lit, the stew on the sideboard in a covered tureen.

Mrs Atherton was already at the table — a woman in her sixties in a navy wool dress and pearls, neat grey hair, hands folded on the cloth in front of her. She gave Caleb a small polite nod when he came in. The third place was empty; Mr Fletcher, presumably, was running late.

"Mrs Atherton, this is Mr Waddell, up from Manchester for the evening. Mr Waddell — Mrs Atherton, who comes down from Salisbury once a year to see the exhibitions." Mrs Penhale set the tureen on a trivet between them. "Sit yourselves. Mr Fletcher's at the British Library again and may or may not show his face. Don't wait for him."

She lifted the lid: beef stew with carrots and pearl barley, the smell properly hitting the room now. A basket of bread. A jug of water. A small dish of pickled red cabbage.

"Help yourself, love. Plenty to go round."

Mrs Atherton waited politely for him to serve himself first, the way an older woman did with a young man at a guesthouse table.

He took the ladle and served Mrs Atherton first — *please, allow me* — a proper portion onto the warm plate, careful with the carrot and the gravy. Then his own. Then a third bowl, set ready at the empty place beside Mrs Penhale's chair at the head.

Mrs Penhale paused in the doorway with the bread basket, watched him do it, and her face did the small softening it did at any small kindness from a young person.

"Bless you, love. I usually eat after, but I'll sit with my tea while you two get going." She set the bread down, pulled out her chair, and sat — not the bowl he'd served her, but a cup of tea she'd already poured at the sideboard. "Yours'll go cold by the time I'm done in the kitchen. But that was a nice thought."

Mrs Atherton picked up her spoon.

"Manchester, Mrs Penhale said. — Visiting town, are you, Mr Waddell?"

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 19:02

Mrs Atherton's polite eyebrow went up — half at *Scotland*, half at *Botany*, all of it perfectly-mannered curiosity rather than scrutiny.

"Botany. Now there's a serious subject. My late husband took an interest in alpines — never in any organised way, just dragging me up Scottish hills to look at saxifrages." She smiled into her stew. "And yes, do show us the dahlia, if you've not put it under the encyclopaedias yet."

Mrs Penhale, behind her tea: "He's pressing it for a young lady, Mrs Atherton."

"Ah." Mrs Atherton's face did the small pleased thing older women's faces did at the sentence *young man pressing a flower for a young lady*. "Well. That is a nice piece of news for a Wednesday."

She took a piece of bread from the basket.

"What's her name, then?"

"Goodness." Mild. She set the spoon down. "How long have you and Miss Holroyd been engaged, Mr Waddell?"

Mrs Penhale, behind her tea, made a small sound that was not quite a cough.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 19:03

Mrs Atherton looked at him for a long moment over her stew. Then her face did the slow generous smile of a woman in her sixties choosing to be amused rather than scandalised, and she picked her spoon back up.

"A few hours. Well. That's a *romance*, Mr Waddell. — Go on with you. Eat your supper before it goes cold. Your future Mrs Waddell would not thank me if I let you starve on the night before term."

Mrs Penhale, finally, did laugh — short and dry, into her teacup.

"He's been like this since he came in the door, Mrs Atherton. I've already had a flower presented to me for advice."

"A *charming* young man, Mrs Penhale."

"A *cheeky* young man, Mrs Atherton."

"Well, charm and cheek often run in the same harness." Mrs Atherton tore her bread. "Eat, Mr Waddell. We'll not pry further into Miss Holroyd's prospects unless you bring her up again, in which case it's open season."

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 19:04

Mrs Atherton's spoon stopped halfway up.

"There'll be none of that at this table, love. I run a respectable house and Mrs Atherton has come down from Salisbury for the museums, not for the music hall. — Eat your stew."

"You can tell us how *clever* she is, if you like. Or how kind. Those are the two we'll have."

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 19:05

Mrs Atherton listened all the way through. Set her bread down. Considered him.

"That's a great deal more thoughtful than the opening sentence promised, Mr Waddell."

She picked her spoon back up.

"Letters as a test. There's something in that. My husband and I wrote for the better part of two years before he was demobbed — '46 to '48 — and I'll tell you, by the time he came home I knew the shape of his mind better than I knew his face. The face was a slight surprise when it turned up at Salisbury station." A small private smile. "Not an unhappy one, in the end. But the letters had done most of the work."

Mrs Penhale, behind her tea: "And the asymmetric Greek-statue business, Mrs Atherton, we are going to leave to one side."

"Quite, Mrs Penhale."

Mrs Atherton tilted her head at Caleb.

"Make it good correspondence, then. A girl who works a flower stall and reads on her Saturdays will know the difference between a clever letter and a flattering one. Don't try to be clever. Try to be honest. The clever will look after itself if you are."

She returned to her stew.

"What does Miss Holroyd read, do you know?"

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 19:06

Mrs Atherton's mouth twitched at the corner.

"A man who answers his own question with his own letter. — Well. Then you'll have an answer in return, or you won't, and either way you'll know something about her."

Mrs Penhale topped up her own tea from the pot at her elbow.

"Bread, Mr Waddell. Pass it along to Mrs Atherton, would you."

Caleb passed the basket.

The rest of the meal settled into its proper shape. Mrs Atherton talked, gently, about the Sutton Hoo gallery she meant to see again tomorrow at the Museum, and about the long train down from Salisbury with a change at Basingstoke, and about a niece in Edinburgh who had not married. Mrs Penhale produced a treacle tart from somewhere unannounced. The candles burned down a quarter-inch. Outside the window the Bloomsbury evening was full proper dark, the streetlamps on, a couple walking past on the pavement laughing about something.

By the time they pushed back from the table it was twenty past seven and Mrs Atherton was excusing herself for an early night, and Mrs Penhale was clearing dishes with the brisk efficiency of someone who had cleared a thousand dinners off this table.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 19:25

He stood, picked up his own plate and Mrs Atherton's, and followed Mrs Penhale through to the kitchen.

The kitchen was the warm working back of the house — a deep ceramic sink, a gas range with a kettle on the simmer, a wooden plate-rack on the wall, a teapot with a knitted cosy. Mrs Penhale glanced at him over her shoulder when she heard him come in.

"Oh, love. You don't have to."

"I know. I'd like to."

She considered him for a half-second — the same small assessing look she'd given him at the front door — and then turned back to the sink and rolled her sleeves up the rest of the way.

"Right. I'll wash, you dry. Tea towels in the drawer there — second one down. Plates first, glasses last. Don't break anything, your mother would never forgive me."

She ran the hot tap into the sink and squeezed in a curl of green washing-up liquid that bloomed into suds. The kettle on the range muttered to itself. Outside the kitchen window, the small garden was full dark now, a single squared light on in a window across the way.

She handed him the first plate, dripping.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 19:48

They worked through the stack — plates, then bowls, then the cutlery in a clinking handful, then the tureen and the serving spoon, then the glasses last, the way she'd said. Mrs Penhale washed at her own steady pace, neither hurrying nor pretending the company wasn't shortening the work. They didn't talk much. She mentioned that Mr Fletcher would let himself in with his own key when he eventually rolled up from the British Library, and that there'd be cold stew on the back of the range for him whenever he wanted it. She mentioned that the milkman came at half past six and not to be alarmed at the bottles. She mentioned that she'd put the blotting paper on his desk while he was at table.

By the time they were down to the last glass and the wiped sink, the kitchen clock said a quarter to eight.

"Right." She wrung the dishcloth out and draped it over the tap. "You're a good lad, Mr Waddell. Off you go up. Don't stay up too late, you've a train at eleven and the Express won't wait for any boy who's overslept his bath." A pause. "There's a postbox at the corner of the street if you want to send that letter tonight — last collection's at eight."

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 19:50

Up the stairs two at a time. The blotting paper was on the desk where she'd said — two clean fresh sheets, slightly creamy, exactly the right size.

He didn't have an envelope. He used a sheet of the writing paper from the desk drawer instead — folded the letter into thirds, then folded a fresh sheet around it as a wrapper, the way his father had once shown him with a parcel of clock-springs. Tucked the corners in. Used a smear of candle-wax from the saucer on the desk to seal the outside flap, pressed his thumb in it for the seal. Wrote the address across the front in the Conway Stewart's blue-black:

> *Miss Annie Holroyd*
> *c/o Holroyd's Florists*
> *Stevenson Square*
> *Manchester*

A return name in the top left corner, no address — *C. Waddell* — because he didn't want a Bloomsbury address on it and he'd be at school by the time it arrived. He had no stamp.

He pocketed it and went down the stairs and out the front door. The street was quiet, the lamps lit. The corner — left, as Mrs Penhale had said — was a thirty-second walk. He found the postbox: an old George VI cylinder, dark red, the collection plate at the bottom, *Last collection: 20:00 weekdays.* The pocket watch said two minutes to.

*He had no stamp.*

He dropped it in anyway. Without a stamp, the Royal Mail would deliver it postage-due — the recipient would pay a small surcharge to receive it. He could not stand on the pavement and not send it.

The flap of the postbox closed with a small definite *clack*.

He walked back to the guesthouse with his hands in his pockets.

He gave her ten. She gave him a penny back without comment.

Back up the stairs three at a time. Pen still on the desk, uncapped from earlier — he scribbled across the top-left corner of the letter, fast:

> *C. Waddell*
> *Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry*
> *Hogsmeade, Scotland*

Stuck the stamp on. Pressed it down with his thumb. Caught the candle on the way past as he turned, did not knock it over, kept going.

Down the stairs three at a time. Past Mrs Penhale, who was still in the hall and had to step aside.

"Sorry —"

"Go *on*."

Out the front door. Left at the kerb. The street was almost empty in the lamp-light. He ran — proper ran, the kind he hadn't done since he'd been a fourth-year chasing the Ravenclaw side down the corridor for the wrong reason. The pocket watch jumped against his waistcoat. The corner came up. The pillar box was where it had been an hour ago: dark red, *George VI*, the *LAST COLLECTION 20:00 WEEKDAYS* plate at the bottom.

A man in a Royal Mail van was already there with the door of the box open and a sack on the pavement. Halfway through emptying.

"Just made it," the postman said, not looking up. He held the sack open with one hand. "Drop it in here, son, save me putting it back."

Caleb dropped it in. The letter went into the sack with the rest of the evening's post and the postman tied the neck and slung it into the back of the van.

"Cheers, lad. Goodnight."

The van pulled away.

## Narrator

OOC: Both fair points, and easily fixed.

**On the wizarding mail / return address:** You're right — wizards at Hogwarts handle mail through the school owlery and the Hogsmeade Post Office, and Annie is a Muggle who can't post to "Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry." The plausible setup, given the era and Caleb's family situation:

The Waddell family — wizard father, Muggle mother — would already have a system for the mother to write to Caleb at school, since she can't put owls in the air from her kitchen. The cleanest version is: the workshop in Manchester is the family's mail relay. Letters posted to *C. Waddell, c/o Waddell Clocks, [address], Manchester* arrive normally by Royal Mail. His father then sends them on by owl (his own, or a hired one from the local Owl Office at the wizarding side) up to Hogwarts. Letters back come to the workshop and his father redirects.

So the realistic return address Caleb would have written is something like:

> *C. Waddell*
> *c/o Waddell Clocks & Watch Repair*
> *[address], Manchester*

— the workshop. Annie writes to a clock-repair shop in Manchester, his father reads the address and sends the letter on. To Annie it just looks like he gave a family business as a forwarding address while at boarding school, which is normal in 1977.

**Fixing the moment:** pretend Caleb wrote the workshop's address, not Hogwarts. Same dash up the stairs, same scramble, same successful catch of the postman. Just the address on the corner reads:

> *C. Waddell*
> *c/o Waddell Clocks & Watch Repair*
> *Manchester*

(I'm leaving the street name as a TBD I'll fill in next time the workshop comes up — I haven't committed one yet so I shouldn't fabricate one now.)

**On Annie's letters back:** locked in. Annie is a Muggle who knows nothing about magic, and any reply she sends will be to what Caleb actually wrote — questions about flowers, about her own life, about whatever he tells her about himself. She doesn't ask about boarding-school anything she has no reason to ask about. If Caleb ever wants to tell her he's a wizard, that's his decision to make in his letters, with consequences. Until then, the correspondence runs entirely on the Muggle side.

Inventory and tracker stand. The letter is in the post, address corrected. Continuing whenever you are.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:02

Back up the stairs. The desk lamp on. The two encyclopaedias from the hall table — old, navy-bound, *Pears Cyclopaedia* on one and a *Concise Oxford* on the other — already up in the room, set on the floor where Mrs Penhale had left them.

He worked carefully. Cleared the desk. Opened his Transfiguration textbook flat to a chapter near the middle — the heaviest book he had, leather-bound, properly weighty. Laid one sheet of Mrs Penhale's blotting paper flat across the open pages. Cut the dahlia stem short with the small penknife from his trunk, leaving an inch and a half of stem below the head. Laid the dahlia face-up on the blotting paper, opened the petals out with the tip of one finger so they would dry the way he wanted them to dry. Laid the second sheet of blotting on top. Closed the book gently. The closing pressed the petals flat in their first compression with the soft *whuff* a heavy book makes.

He took the closed book to his trunk, set it flat in the bottom under his folded robes, and stacked the *Pears* on top of it. The *Concise Oxford* on top of that. Wedged a folded jumper down the side so the stack wouldn't shift on the train tomorrow.

Three weeks. No peeking before two.

He shut the trunk lid.

The pocket watch said two minutes past eight. The pocket had two pounds and a penny in it, twelve Galleons and seven Sickles in the wizarding wallet at the bottom of the trunk, and a fresh evening ahead of him that the day had not yet used up.

Diagon Alley after dark was not the friendliest place — the daytime trade had mostly gone home, the smarter shops had their shutters down by seven, the pubs and the Apothecary's late counter and a few of the bookshops would still be lit. But it was here, and he was a half-blood seventh-year with an evening to himself in London the night before term, which was a thing that happened maybe once in a school career.

He went down the stairs. Mrs Penhale was in the front parlour now, the wireless on low — the long sober tone of the BBC evening news. She glanced up when he came past.

"Going out, Mr Waddell?"

"Just a walk. An hour, no more."

"Front door's not locked till eleven. — Take a key all the same." She fished one off a hook by the door and handed it across. "You don't want to be ringing the bell at half past ten with Mrs Atherton trying to sleep."

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:05

He took the key. Tucked it into the watch pocket of his waistcoat, beside the chain. Out the front door. Heard it click shut behind him. The Bloomsbury street had gone properly evening now — the streetlamps full on, the sky above the rooftops a deep blue going to black, a couple of taxis idling at the corner past the postbox he'd just used.

The Leaky Cauldron was a fifteen-minute walk south and west: down through Bloomsbury, across New Oxford Street, into the narrow tangle of Charing Cross Road. He knew the route — his father had walked him to it twice as a child, the way wizard parents walked Muggle-raised children to the threshold so the threshold would let them through later on their own.

The Muggle parts of the city looked perfectly ordinary at this hour. A pub on the corner of Great Russell Street spilling lamplight onto the pavement, the sound of someone laughing inside. The British Museum's railings on his left, the great pale gates already shut. A bus pulled up, took on three passengers, moved off. A man walking a small dog. A woman in a coat reading a *Standard* under a streetlamp.

By the time he came down the slope of Charing Cross Road, the bookshops were mostly shuttered for the night, but the pavement still had its evening crowd — theatre-goers on their way home, two students with Foyles bags. The Leaky was where it always was: a shabby narrow door between a record shop and a Greek restaurant, with the small dim sign that Muggle eyes slid over without quite resolving.

He pushed the door. The smell of the Cauldron came up to meet him — woodsmoke, old beer, pipe tobacco, something stewing in the back kitchen.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:22

Tom — the same hunched, toothless Tom he'd known since he was eleven — was at the bar polishing a glass that no amount of polishing would help. He gave Caleb a small nod of recognition without making a thing of it. Caleb crossed the taproom, went out the back door into the small walled yard, drew his wand, tapped the brick three up and two across — the sequence his father had taught him on his very first walk through, the one that Muggle-raised half-bloods learned by repetition until it was muscle memory — and stepped back as the wall began the long architectural gesture of opening.

Diagon Alley after dark was a quieter place than its daytime self. The cobbles still warm from the day. Most of the smart frontages — Madam Malkin's, Eeylops, Flourish & Blotts — already shuttered. The Apothecary's lamps still on, late counter still trading. Quality Quidditch Supplies dark. Florean Fortescue's gate down. The street was thinly populated: a witch with a basket walking briskly toward the Leaky, two men in formal robes coming out of a wine merchant's, a hooded figure crossing the alley further down without looking up.

The place you're thinking of — the proper shady one — is **Knockturn Alley**, which runs off Diagon as a narrow turning to the south, marked by an old crooked sign and a noticeable change in the cobbles. Borgin & Burkes is the famous shop down there, and there are several others a sensible person doesn't catalogue. After dark, Knockturn does not get safer.

The Diagon turning to Knockturn was about a hundred yards down on the right.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:24

He went down the alley on the right side, past the shuttered front of Quality Quidditch and a darkened tea-shop, until the cobbles took on the slight downward slope that meant the Knockturn turning was close. The sign above the corner said the name in old crooked lettering, and the pavement narrowed as it bent off Diagon proper into the lower gloom.

The lamps in Knockturn were further apart and burned with a meaner light. A woman with a covered basket crossed the mouth of the alley and didn't look up. Somewhere further down a door slammed.

Borgin and Burkes was twenty yards in, on the left. A long blackened window, most of the display behind it draped over by a velvet cloth at this hour, but a single lamp burning behind the glass and a faint movement of someone inside. The brass plate by the door read *Borgin and Burkes — Purveyors of Fine and Curious Objects — Est. 1863.* The door's open/closed plate was turned to *OPEN.*

A man came out of a doorway further down Knockturn — tall, hooded, robe too heavy for the weather — and walked past Caleb without looking at him, the wand-tip of a holstered wand briefly visible at his hip.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:25

He pushed the door. A small bell on a flat spring rang once, dull, the sound of a bell that had been dampened on purpose.

Inside, the shop was a long narrow room lit by a single oil lamp on the counter and the faint glow of a few of the objects on the shelves. The smell was strange — old wood, brass polish, dust, and underneath it something faintly metallic, like a butcher's shop after closing. The walls were crowded with cabinets: a glass case of skulls of various sizes, a row of dark bottles labelled in a hand he could not read, a long velvet-lined tray of rings, a tarnished mirror with no reflection visible from where he stood. A withered hand on a small velvet cushion under a glass dome. Something behind the counter that might have been a music box, or might not.

Behind the counter, a thin man with a long jaw and a long thin nose was bent over a ledger, writing in it with a quill. He was — Caleb knew the face from his father's vague gossip about who in Diagon was who — the proprietor. Caractacus Burke, by reputation: a man who had been in this shop since before the war, and who had reputedly sold the worst things on these shelves to the worst people who came in for them.

Burke did not look up when the bell rang. He let the silence run a beat, and then, still writing:

"Looking for something particular, young man? Or browsing."

## User

"just browsing. but perhaps there may be something you feel would be of particular interest to me, if you have a sense of these things?"

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:26

Burke set the quill down. Looked up properly for the first time.

His eyes did the long slow assessment a man like Burke did — clothes, posture, wand on the hip, the cleanness of the boy's hands, the satchel. He took perhaps four seconds about it, and they were not comfortable seconds.

"A sense of these things." He let the phrase sit between them. The corners of his mouth did not move. "I have a sense of a great many things, young man. Most of them require an actual answer to what you came in for."

He shut the ledger.

"You're seventeen, eighteen. Half-blood by your face, by your hands, by the way you walked in — too straight for somebody who comes here often, not nervous enough to be one of those who's been told not to come at all. You've been on a Muggle train today; the dust's still on your jacket. You're not one of mine and you're not one of my regulars' children." A pause. "Which means you're either lost, or you're curious, or you're shopping for someone else. Which is it?"

His hand rested on the closed ledger. He had not invited Caleb further in.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:27

Burke's eyes did a small movement — interested, faintly. The look of a shopkeeper who had just been asked the right question by the wrong customer.

"A puzzle." He let himself smile, briefly, with the corners of his mouth only. "Mr Burkes likes a puzzle." He gestured Caleb further in with two fingers. "Come and look, then. Don't touch unless I say."

He came round the counter. Slow. Unhurried. The shop creaked under him as he moved.

"There's three things in this shop that fit your description that I'd let a boy your age stand near." He stopped at a small case in the middle of the long wall. "First — this one. — A puzzle box. Cedar, brass corners, eighteenth-century French, eleven moves to the first false bottom and seventeen to the real one. The man who made it lost his wife to a mathematician, and built it as a small unkindness to that mathematician's grandson. It rewards a careful mind. It does nothing to you if you fail. It is, to be plain, the boring answer to your question."

He moved two paces along.

"Second — this. — A locked book. The lock is a riddle in mediaeval Latin and the book is in worse Latin still, and the contents are a herbalist's notes nobody has cared about for two hundred years. The reward for opening it is that you have read a herbalist's notes. The punishment is that you have spent a fortnight reading mediaeval Latin. Also boring."

He stopped.

"Third — and this is the one your question was asking about, and we both know it — I do not show to a boy I have not done business with."

He looked at Caleb steadily.

"What were you actually after, young man?"

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:28

Burke's mouth did the brief mirthless smile again.

"You can buy the book. Three Galleons, with the lock as it stands. If you crack it, the contents are yours. If you don't, you have a paperweight that judges you."

He moved back toward the counter and did not, Caleb noticed, return to the third object.

"As for the tease — " he glanced sideways at Caleb without turning his head — "I run a shop, young man. Not a museum. The things I keep behind the counter are kept there because the people who buy them have given me reason to bring them out. You haven't. You've given me a clever question and a half-blood handshake-of-the-eyes, and I have answered both with the courtesy they're worth. — The book, or not the book?"

He set both hands on the ledger. Waited.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:30

Burke counted the coins with a fingertip without taking them off the counter. Three Galleons. He nodded, not at the money, but at the way Caleb hadn't pushed them forward.

He bent under the counter and came up with the book. Small, dark leather, the lock a flat brass thing set into the front cover with a single keyhole and three concentric brass rings around it, each engraved with letters Caleb couldn't make out from where he stood. The whole object weighed, by the look of Burke's hand, about as much as a brick.

He set it on the counter on Caleb's side. Slid the coins across to his own.

"There. Yours. Don't bring it back saying you couldn't open it; that's not my problem."

He let a small pause sit.

"As for the honest answer to your question." He looked up at Caleb properly. "What would it take. — Three things, young man. First, time. You come in here twice more this year, you buy something each time, you don't bring anyone with you. Second, a recommendation. Someone whose name I trust mentions yours unprompted. Third, an honest reason. Not curiosity. *Curiosity* is what people say when they're nineteen and bored. The third thing on my counter punishes failure in ways that have, in my career, included one death and two — let us call them — long convalescences. I do not show it to boys for the *learning*. I show it to a man with a problem who has decided that thing is his answer."

He didn't blink.

"You don't have a problem yet. Come back when you do."

Caleb looked at the book, then back at Burke.
“Fair enough.”
He picked the book up carefully, testing its weight but not opening it.
“Then I suppose I’ll need to become the sort of man who comes back with a problem.”
A pause.
“For what it’s worth, I wasn’t trying to waste your evening. I just wanted to know whether there were still things in the world that demanded more than schoolwork.”
He tucked the book into his satchel.
“I’ll solve this one first.”

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:32

Burke watched him tuck the book away. Did not smile. Did not unsmile.

"Mind the lock isn't kind to forced answers." He flipped the ledger open again, picked the quill up. "You damage the rings, the riddle resets. I'd rather you didn't, but it's your book now."

He went back to writing.

The dismissal was clean and Caleb felt no offence in it. He stepped back toward the door. The dampened bell rang once on his way out.

Knockturn at half past eight had not got better in the four minutes he'd been inside. The hooded man from earlier was gone; somebody else — a stout woman with a covered birdcage that was making no birdsounds — was crossing further down. The walk back to the Diagon turning was short, and the cobbles tilted up a little under his feet as the alley climbed back to its more respectable parent.

He came out of Knockturn into Diagon proper. The lamps here were warmer, steadier. The air was lighter. The Apothecary's late counter was still trading; a man with a leather satchel was just leaving it.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:35

Slug & Jiggers Apothecary was about forty paces back up Diagon, on the same side. The late counter was the side door rather than the main shop, lit by a single hanging lamp and a sign reading *AFTER HOURS — RESTOCK & STUDENT TRADE.* The main shop was dark, but the side counter was open onto the alley with a young witch behind it, perhaps mid-twenties, in a slightly stained apron, dispatching the last few customers of the evening.

When Caleb came up she was just bagging a small stoppered jar for an older man, who paid, nodded, and went off down the alley.

She turned to Caleb, brisk but not unfriendly.

"Evening. Term-list?"

The counter behind her had the usual late-summer state of an apothecary the night before Hogwarts term: tubs of dried beetle parts, jars of various greens and browns, a stack of pre-made student kits in waxed paper, a board chalked with prices for the most-asked items — *Dragonfly Wings, dr 4s. Powdered Bicorn Horn 7s. Standard Potions Kit Yr 7 — 18s. Boomslang skin (per oz) 2g.*

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:36

She was perhaps twenty-four, slight, with dark hair pinned back off her face in a working knot, sharp clever eyes behind small round spectacles, an ink stain on the cuff of her apron, and the quick competent hands of someone who measured out beetle parts all day. Pretty in an impatient sort of way. She caught him taking her in and one of her eyebrows went up a fraction without her face committing to anything else.

"Cassia. — Seventh year tells me NEWTs in Potions, probably, given you came to the late counter. Which means you want either the Year 7 kit at eighteen sickles, which is what most of you buy, or you've got a syllabus from your professor that asks for things the kit doesn't include. — Got the syllabus?"

She didn't wait long for an answer. Reached under the counter and produced a printed sheet — *HOGWARTS — N.E.W.T. POTIONS, 7th YEAR — Recommended Independent Stock, 1977-78* — and slid it across to him.

"That's what Slughorn's been asking for. If you're not in his class, you'll need to know which Professor."

"Slughorn's mine. — Show me what's on the list."

Cassia tapped the sheet with one finger and ran down it.

"Right. Most of it's in the standard kit — your salamander blood, your ground horn, your basic dried roots, your phials. The kit's eighteen sickles and you'd be daft to buy the components piecemeal at the prices I'd have to charge you. What the kit *doesn't* cover for Slughorn this year is —" she ticked them off — "fresh Dittany, two ounces. Powdered moonstone, three drams — he's doing the Draught of Peace early. Boomslang skin, half an ounce, which is two Galleons for half-an-ounce so brace yourself. And Armadillo bile, one phial, five sickles. — Anything else on his syllabus you can pick up at school as he assigns it, but those four he wants you walking in with."

She glanced up at him.

"Standard kit plus those four — that's the whole damage. About three Galleons four sickles all in. Want it?"

Cassia took the four Galleons with a brisk satisfaction and flicked one back across the counter as change-on-account, then stopped, considered, and put it back into the till instead.

She set them on the counter beside the standard kit she was now assembling.

Useful for nothing whatsoever, but it's a good knife exercise and it teaches you that fresh ingredients don't behave the way the dried ones do. — Slughorn'll think it's funny if you bring him a bottle. He likes a student who plays with the materials."

She dropped the twist into the bag with the rest.

She gave him a small dry smile.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:40

Cassia leaned her elbow on the counter and tilted her head at him. The brisk professional was still there but a bit of the woman behind it had come up to the surface for a moment.

"Caleb. — That's a much nicer ask than the first one. And honestly, on a different night I might say yes. But I've got a man waiting for me at home with the kettle on, and I'm tired, and I've been standing in this apron since seven this morning, and the truthful answer is I want to be out of these shoes more than I want a drink even with charming company."

She tapped the counter once, friendly.

"My favourite thing to make is Wiggenweld, of all the boring answers. There's a knack to it that took me three years to get right and now I can do it half-asleep, and there's something satisfying about a potion that has only ever helped people. — Take that as your free thing for tonight, on top of the bulbs."

A small pause.

"Go on. The Cauldron does a decent shepherd's pie."

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:41

Cassia considered him for a long second. Then her mouth did the small dry quirk that wasn't quite a smile and wasn't quite not.

"All right. That's a deal I'll take." She straightened up. "Wiggenweld in five minutes flat. Stay on your side of the counter."

She moved at the brisk pace of a woman who genuinely could do this half-asleep. From under the counter: a small cauldron the size of a teacup, a glass stirring rod, a marble pestle. From the shelves behind: a jar of dried wiggentree bark, a small phial of Salamander blood, a paper twist of moondew, a sprig of dried mint.

"Right. — Three Sickles for the lot, since you're paying for the lesson and not a real brew. Pin the cauldron with your wand if it skitters, willow holds steady better than oak."

She set everything in front of her, on her side of the counter where he could watch.

"Wiggenweld's a healing potion. It wakes things up — small wounds, faints, even certain enchanted sleeps if you've brewed it right and the sleep isn't the deep kind. The trick of it is that you're not really making one potion, you're making two and joining them at the right moment, and most people get the moment wrong because they're watching the colour and not the sound."

She held the wiggentree bark up. "First half: this. Crushed, not powdered — you want it coarse enough that the salamander blood has something to wrap around. Powdered and you've made dye, not medicine."

She started crushing it with the pestle, slow demonstration pace, talking as she worked.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:43

The Galleon clinked once on the counter. Cassia glanced down at it without slowing the pestle, glanced back up at him, and her mouth did the small quirk again.

"Cheeky."

She didn't push it back. The pestle kept going for two more turns and then she set it down, swept the coarse-crushed wiggentree bark into the small cauldron with the side of her hand, and reached for the salamander blood without missing a beat.

"Right. Watch this part — this is where most people kill it." She uncorked the phial, held it tilted over the cauldron. "Three drops, slow, and you're listening, not looking. The bark is going to make a small *tssk* when each drop hits, like a wet match. If it hisses for longer than a second, you've crushed it too fine. If it doesn't hiss at all, your salamander's gone bad."

*Tssk.* First drop. A pause. *Tssk.* Second. A pause. *Tssk.* Third. Each one neat and short.

"There — that's what you want." She corked the phial. "Now it sits for the count of forty while we make the second half. — Moondew."

She pulled the paper twist of moondew toward her. Tiny pale crystals, slightly damp.

"Second half goes in cold. Moondew, mint, room-temperature water. You're making a tea, basically, and you're not heating it because the moondew loses everything it does over forty degrees. The trick is the mint goes in last and it goes in *whole* — bruise it between your fingers, don't tear it, don't crush it. You want the oils, not the leaf."

She demonstrated — a single sprig of dried mint pinched between her thumb and forefinger, twisted once, dropped into a small beaker of water with the moondew already dissolved in it.

Cassia laughed — proper laugh, surprised, the first real one she'd given up all evening — and set the beaker down before she could spill the moondew.

"A *julep version of Wiggenweld*. Caleb. — That's the most flagrant thing anyone's said to me in this shop in a year."

She shook her head, still grinning, and picked the beaker back up.

"Sixty-forty I won't take. Fifty-fifty if you actually do it and it actually works, and a written recipe with proportions and a brewing time, because if I'm going to sell a Caleb Waddell Original out of this counter I'm going to sell it consistently or not at all."

She flicked her eyes up at him.

"And it has to *work*. A julep that bounces is a Hogsmeade joke. A julep that bounces and *also* heals a sprained wrist is a product. — Mind the difference."

She finished the second half — the cold beaker with moondew and mint settling into a faint pale green — and set it beside the small cauldron with the bark and salamander blood. Glanced at the clock on the wall behind her: somewhere near twenty to nine.

"Almost forty. Watch."

Cassia's count had not stopped — she was still tracking the forty under her breath while he was talking — but she let her eyebrow do the work of replying for her.

"*Mint Mender.*" She said it flat, testing it in her mouth. "Caleb. No."

She lifted the small cauldron at the count and tilted it slowly into the cold beaker, pouring the warm bark-and-blood mixture in a thin steady stream while stirring the cold half clockwise with the glass rod. The two halves met. There was a soft sound — somewhere between a sigh and a struck match — and the colour came up: a pale, clean green, almost the colour of new lime leaves, settling in the beaker.

"Cordial doesn't lie about what it is — that one's a maybe." She set the rod down. "*Curative* I won't put on a label unless we're licensed and we're not. A bouncing mint syrup that says *curative* on the bottle is how the Ministry shuts your shop down."

She picked up the beaker, held it to the lamp. The green was even. She gave a small approving nod to herself and set it down again.

"Working name only: 'Caleb's Folly.' Until you prove it heals. Then we can argue about the rest."

She glanced at the clock.

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:46

Cassia snorted.

"That is the worst pitch for a healing potion I have ever heard. — *You forget you hurt.* Caleb. That's a problem with most cocktails, not a feature." She was already starting to clear the counter, sweeping the moondew twist back into its jar, the salamander phial back to its shelf. "If you're putting alcohol in it, the proportions go to hell anyway. Moondew binds with alcohol differently than it does with water. You'll have to redo the whole second half. — Which is your problem, Mr Folly, not mine."

She set the small green beaker aside. Looked at it for a moment. Then, with a small efficient gesture, picked it up, corked it with a stopper from a tray of stoppers under the counter, and slid it across to him.

"For your trouble. Don't drink it — it's sat on the counter and it's not properly settled. But take it. Compare yours against it when you brew. That's how you'll know what 'right' looks like."

A pause. The lamp behind her hissed faintly.

"Send me the recipe when you've got it. *Properly* written, with weights and times. Not a love letter."

Cassia made a small dignified sound that was definitely not a laugh — she would not give him the satisfaction — and went back to wiping down her counter without looking up.

"Goodnight, Caleb."

He went serpentine away down Diagon — three paces left, three paces right, dodging an imaginary defender — until he passed the dark front of Quality Quidditch and was out of her line of sight, at which point he straightened up and walked like an ordinary person.

The alley was emptier now. The Apothecary's lamp went out behind him as he turned the corner — Cassia closing up properly. Florean Fortescue's gate, Madam Malkin's, the long shuttered fronts. The Leaky's back archway came up.

Inside, the taproom was its evening self: a few tables of locals, two wizards hunched over a chessboard, the fire low. Tom was still behind the bar. The smell of shepherd's pie was, in fact, coming from somewhere.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:56

He took a small table near the fire — settled the satchel on the chair beside him, hung his jacket on the back of his own. Tom came over.

"Shepherd's pie, please. And a mint julep, if you do them."

Tom blinked at him. The slow blink of a publican who had been asked for stranger things in his career, but not many.

"Mint julep. Don't get many of those in here, son. — I can do you bourbon, fresh mint, sugar, ice. Crushed ice'll be from the cellar. Three sickles, give or take." He squinted at Caleb. "You eighteen?"

"Eighteen."

"Right." A small nod. "Pie's eight sickles. Five minutes."

He went off to the kitchen.

The taproom settled around Caleb. The chess players muttered over a queen sacrifice. The fire popped. He set the small corked phial of demonstration Wiggenweld on the table in front of him, beside the place where his glass would land — clear pale green in the firelight, properly settled now, looking like nothing more dangerous than a bottle of cordial.

*I start sniffing it and sticking my tongue in it, trying to get a handle on knowing what this version, the normal version is like.*

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 20:57

He uncorked the phial. Held it under his nose first.

The smell was — green. Properly green, the way crushed leaves smell, but underneath it something cleaner: a mineral note like wet stone, and a faint warm sweetness from the salamander blood that hadn't fully announced itself in the brewing but had settled into the cooled potion as a low rounded thing. The mint was there but not on top. It sat back. The whole thing smelled like a thing that had been *finished*, the way a soup is finished — every component present, none of them shouting.

He touched the cork-stopper to the tip of his tongue. A small bright sharpness — the moondew, he thought, that was the moondew talking — and underneath, the cool herbal cleanness of the wiggentree, and the faint mineral weight of the salamander. The mint came last on the tongue, after the swallow, the way Cassia had said it would when she dropped it in whole.

It tasted like a healing potion ought to taste: like nothing you would crave, but like something your body would say *yes* to if you were sick.

He recorked the phial. Set it back beside his place.

Tom came back with the shepherd's pie — proper, the kind with crisped potato on top — and a tall glass of pale crushed-ice julep, mint sprigs already bruised against the side of the glass, sugar visible on the rim.

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 31 August 1977 — 21:00

He paid Tom — eight sickles for the pie, three for the julep — and Tom gave him a small *cheers, lad* and went back to the bar.

The julep first, on its own. He lifted the glass, smelled the bruised mint coming off the top in the cold rising vapour from the crushed ice, the bourbon underneath it sweet and grassy. Sipped. Cold burn of the spirit, the sugar on the rim, the mint clean and bright at the back. Good drink. Properly made.

He set it down. Took up a fork to the pie — first bite, hot, the gravy under the crisp top exactly as it should be — and chewed slowly while he thought about what he was about to do.

A small approach: the recipe wanted moondew, not a moondew-already-bound. The Wiggenweld in the phial was finished, *settled*, every component locked into the others. Adding it to a julep wouldn't be making the julep medicinal. It would be testing whether finished Wiggenweld stayed integrated when you put it in alcohol. Cassia had told him moondew bound differently with alcohol. The phial was a reference sample for what *right* tasted like — and the only way to know what alcohol did to it was to put a drop of it in a glass of bourbon and find out.

He tipped the phial. One drop into the rim of the julep, where he could see it. Stirred with the small mint sprig.

The drop went into the julep clean — no fizzing, no colour change visible against the pale green-on-amber. He sipped. The julep was still the julep: bourbon, mint, sugar. The Wiggenweld was — gone, almost. A faint herbal note that hadn't been there before, possibly. A barely-there cleaner green underneath the mint. If he hadn't known what to listen for, he'd have called it just a slightly fresher mint.

He sat back. Took a longer pull of the pie. Watched the glass.

After a minute, the julep didn't *do* anything. It didn't bounce. It didn't fizz. It didn't separate. It just sat there, looking like a julep that had had a drop of cordial added to it.

Which — if Cassia was right — meant the moondew in the Wiggenweld had been bound by the alcohol on contact, and was no longer doing whatever Wiggenweld's moondew did. The healing was, presumably, gone. What was left was a julep with a faint herbal undertone.

He took another small sip. Pulled out the back of his school notebook and uncapped the Conway Stewart.

> *31 Aug, the Leaky, table by the fire — first experiment.*
> *One drop finished Wiggenweld in 1 mint julep (bourbon, sugar, ice, mint).*
> *Visual: no change. No fizz, no colour shift, no separation.*
> *Smell: mint slightly cleaner. Herbal undertone faint.*
> *Taste: julep more or less unchanged. A trace of green at the swallow.*
> *Hypothesis: alcohol binds the moondew on contact, neutralising the healing component. The other elements are inert in the dose. Conclusion: cannot add finished Wiggenweld to alcohol and retain function. Must brew the second half differently — moondew in a non-alcoholic carrier, joined to the alcohol after the bind.*

He read it back. Capped the pen. Ate more pie.
