## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:36

Cassia's mouth did the small *don't laugh in front of Mr Pewsey* thing.

"Six hundred and sixty-three years old, if you bought it in this shop and Burke wasn't lying about the date. — Back. Now."

She herded him through the front of the shop, past the long counter with the cash register and the boards of dried this-and-that, past two aisles of standing shelves — copper cauldrons, scales, mortars-and-pestles in graduated sizes — to the back room. A wide oak workbench, a tall stool, an open ledger, two cups of tea (one half-drunk, hers; one untouched, Pewsey's). The door behind them stayed open — Pewsey's rule.

"Show me. Quietly. He'll listen even if he pretends he isn't."

He set the locked book on the bench. Walked her through it: bought from Burke last night, *Magister Cuthbertus de Lindesia, AD 1314*, the front-cover lock with the three concentric brass rings and the medieval Latin riddle, the answer being VIS. The herbal entries that filled most of the volume. The seventeen interleaved pages every seventeen leaves apart, each beginning with a verb of perception in one of nine specific places, each numbered, each containing a single larger word — and he read her the seventeen words in order from his notebook. The second lock he hadn't seen at first, in the spine, which had released only after he set the front rings to NEX. The hidden quire that had come free from the back binding then. Cuthbert's account of the young woman in 1311 who had walked the path involuntarily through visions and named the seventeen things and gone silent at *silentium* and remained alive. Cuthbert's warning. The four petitioners.

While he talked he opened the lock — V-I-S, click — and showed her the front coming free. Closed it. Opened it again at N-E-X — and the spine clicked, and he lifted the hidden quire out of the back, and laid it open on the bench beside the rest.

Her finger traced down his transcription of the seventeen words — *VIS, UMBRA, NOMEN, SANGUIS, CINIS, VOX, SIGILLUM, SOMNIUM, CARO, NUMERUS, NIHIL, STELLA, AURUM, OS, LIMEN, SILENTIUM, FINIS* — twice, slowly. She did not say anything for a moment.

She looked up at him.

"Caleb."

She kept her voice low. The door behind them was still open and Pewsey was definitely still in earshot.

"Where did you say you bought this."

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:43

Cassia let him talk. Did not interrupt. When he was done she pulled the small wooden stool out from under the bench, sat down on it, and put one elbow on the bench beside the open quire.

"Caleb." She kept her voice low. "Look at me."

He did.

"I am twenty-four years old, I work in a shop, and I am four years out of school. — That book is not a herbal." She tapped the open quire with a fingernail without looking down at it. "Or rather — yes, it is, on the front of it. The herbal entries are real. I can read enough Latin from school to see they're real medieval recipes. Nothing surprising. *That part* of it I would happily look at."

She did look down at the quire then. Read a line of it. Looked back up.

"This part. The hidden part. *This* —" another fingertip on the page — "is not an adventure. I don't know what it is. I genuinely don't. But I know enough to know I shouldn't, on a Thursday morning before my shop opens, sit down with a customer I met yesterday and decide we're going to *find the path*."

Behind them, in the front of the shop, Pewsey turned a page of something audibly. The reminder.

"Listen to me." She kept her voice to him alone now. "I'm not telling you to throw it away. I'm telling you that what you have is older and stranger than you're treating it. People who walked things like that and called them *paths* — sometimes they came back, and sometimes they came back wrong, and sometimes they didn't come back at all. That's not a Slug-and-Jiggers warning, that's just — what people who study old things will tell you. — Show this to a librarian at school. Show it to a Defence professor, even. *Don't* show it to your mates in the common room and don't try the first word on yourself in your dormitory."

A short pause.

"I'll happily look at the herbal entries. The dittany doses, the salves. A working potioneer's job, that's me. — I will not help you find the path. — Are we square?"

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:44

Cassia listened all the way through. Mouth flat, considering, not warming and not cooling. When he was done she let the silence run for a second longer than was comfortable.

"Caleb."

She tapped the bench once, lightly.

"You are very good at making something sound reasonable. I noticed it last night and I'm noticing it again now."

A pause.

"I'll look at the herbal entries with you. The Latin, the recipes, the medieval pharmacopoeia — yes. That's a real thing two people who like potions can do across the post and I'd enjoy it. Send me transcriptions of the herbal pages, two or three at a time, in a letter. I'll write back with what I think they correspond to or don't. We can build a working translation together over the autumn." She glanced down at the open quire. "The hidden pages — no. Not on parchment, not by owl, not in this shop. I'll be honest with you: I don't want any record of me having looked at them, and I don't want copies of them in my flat. — Including in your letters to me. The herbal you can send. The hidden bit you do not."

Another pause. She softened slightly.

"And — your enthusiasm yesterday wasn't creepy, it was just a lot. — But yes. We can correspond about the herbal. Send me the first batch of pages when you've settled into school. I'll look forward to it."

She sat back on the stool.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:47

He stopped at the threshold of the back room. The small twitch of *kiss her properly before you go* came up; he registered it without acting on it. She was watching him — calm, neither inviting nor warning, a face he wouldn't be able to read better than that without practice. He stepped through into the shop proper.

Pewsey was at the long counter, a clipboard in his hand, ticking down a list of jars on the shelf behind him.

"Mr Pewsey, sorry to bother you twice. Quick question." Caleb pulled the open book from his satchel — only the herbal entries showing, the hidden quire safely back in the binding — and held it open to the title leaf where Cuthbert's name and date were written. "Bought this off Burke last night. *Cuthbertus de Lindesia, anno Domini MCCCXIV.* Have you come across him? Was he anyone — herbalist of note, recorded somewhere — or just a parish doctor whose notebook nobody threw out?"

Pewsey looked at the title leaf. Considered. Set the clipboard down.

"Lindesia. — Lindsey, that'd be. Lincolnshire, north of the Wolds." He squinted. "Cuthbert. *Cuthbert.* — Doesn't ring a bell as a herbalist of any standing, lad. Most of them in that period are catalogued — there's a *Compendium Magistrorum Medii Aevi* that lists every named magical practitioner in Britain from 1100 to 1500, and if your man was anyone of consequence he'd be in there. Brownson's keeps a copy. Four doors down."

He looked at Caleb properly for the first time.

"Mind. The ones who *aren't* in the *Compendium* are a more interesting set than the ones who are. — The famous ones got famous because they were average enough not to get into trouble. The ones who didn't make it into the lists are sometimes more worth a look. Sometimes they're nothing. Sometimes they're not." A pause. "Brownson opens at ten. Old Brownson himself will know more than the Compendium will, if you ask him civilly. — Now, off with you. Cassia's got an inventory to finish and you're a distraction."

"Two hours of stocking, in the back, away from Miss Burbage. — Dust me down the high shelf in the dry-store, wipe each jar with the cloth that's hanging on the hook, put each one back exactly where it came from. Don't move anything. Don't look at anything. Don't ask Miss Burbage questions about her work while I'm not in the room." He paused. "I'll feed you a cup of tea at half past eight. — That suit?"

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 07:50

Pewsey did not dignify that with a reply. He pointed two fingers toward the back of the shop and then to the right, off the back-room doorway, into a narrow side passage Caleb hadn't noticed last night. The dry-store.

It was a long shallow room — barely wider than the shelf-runs on either side — lit by a single overhead lamp and a high transom window that let a slab of morning light in across the upper rows. The shelves ran from waist-height to nine feet up, four runs deep on each wall, and every shelf was packed with stoppered glass jars in graduated sizes. Each jar was labelled in copperplate on a small ivory tag tied to the neck with green string. *Dried Asphodel, gr.4. Pickled Murtlap, gr.2. Powdered Re'em horn, gr.3. Shavings, Dittany Stem, gr.1. Newt Eyes, fresh, gr.1, decanted weekly.* Etcetera. Two thousand jars. Possibly more.

A wooden stepladder leaned against the right-hand wall. A small bucket on the floor with a soft cloth over the rim, faintly damp, the kind of cloth a clean-and-dust shop kept for jar-work specifically. A second cloth, dry, on a hook by the door.

Pewsey appeared at the doorway behind him.

"High shelf. Top run, both walls. Damp cloth, then dry cloth. *Don't shift the labels.* Don't open any jar. If you find a jar that's hairline-cracked, you set it on the bench by the door without comment and I'll deal with it. — Two hours. Tea at half past."

He went out. Closed the door of the dry-store behind him. Not locked, not slammed — closed.

Caleb hung his jacket on the hook beside the dry cloth, rolled up his sleeves, set the satchel down out of the way, took the damp cloth, took the stepladder, and started at the far end of the right-hand wall on the top shelf.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 08:02

He grumbled at himself in a low under-breath all the way down the right-hand wall — *Pewsey. Lindsey. Cuthbert. Cassia. Fag-papers. Eighteen years old and dusting jars at five past eight on a school morning* — and worked properly through it.

The work was the kind of work where the grumbling helped. Damp cloth around the jar — wipe the shoulder, the body, the base, the underside of the lid. Set down. Dry cloth, same circuit. Set the jar back on its mark — every shelf had a faint ring of dust where the jar had stood, which was its address. Move along. Next jar. *Pickled Salamander Tongues, gr.3.* Next. *Powdered Boomslang Skin, gr.2.* Next.

The dry-store was, in fact, properly dusty up there — a fine pale-grey film on every shoulder, the kind that came of a shop where the high shelves got dusted twice a year if they got dusted at all. The cloth darkened fast. He shook it out into the bucket every fifth jar and went on.

By twenty past eight he had finished the right-hand wall's top run — twenty-three jars — and was halfway across the left-hand wall's. The transom window was throwing its light a foot lower as the morning came on. From beyond the door, the muffled small sounds of the shop: the front door's bell as Pewsey opened up shutters, a *good morning, Mr Pewsey* from a delivery man, the rustle of paper, Cassia's voice asking after a missed dragonfly invoice. The shop's normal Thursday morning.

He kept going. Eyes on the jar, hands working steady. The grumbling had quieted into the low concentrated nothing of a clever boy doing routine work properly because he had said he would.

OOC: Yeah — Pewsey said he'd bring Caleb a cup of tea at half past eight, so we're about six minutes off. Let me run that.

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 08:30

The door of the dry-store opened. Pewsey stood in it with a chipped white mug in one hand and a small plate with a single biscuit on it in the other.

"Half past."

He set the mug on the bench by the door — strong tea, milk, no sugar visible, the way an apothecary served tea to a working man on the assumption you'd ask if you wanted otherwise — and the biscuit beside it. A digestive. Not a fancy biscuit; a working biscuit.

He glanced up at the shelves, the work Caleb had got through, the cloth in his hand, the cloth in the bucket. Did not say anything about it. Did not say *good*. Did not say *no good*. Just a small almost-imperceptible nod that was more than nothing.

"Five minutes for the tea. — Don't get crumbs in the jars."

He went out. Door closed.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 08:31

He took the mug. Stood at the bench by the door rather than sitting on it, which would have meant moving the bucket. The tea was good — properly strong, properly hot, the milk just right. He worked through it in efficient sips. The biscuit went in three.

Five minutes was enough for what it was. He set the empty mug and the empty plate on the bench, brushed the crumbs off his palm into the bucket rather than the floor, picked up the damp cloth, and went back up the stepladder.

The left-hand top run was the rest of his morning. He started where he'd left off — *Asphodel root, dried, gr.2.* Wipe. Set down. Dry. Set back. *Belladonna leaves, dried, gr.1.* Wipe. Set down. Dry. Set back. *Crushed Beetle Eyes, gr.4.* The grumbling had run down. The work had its rhythm now. By a quarter to nine he was halfway across; by ten past, three-quarters; by half past nine he was at the last three jars on the wall and the cloth had darkened twice over.

The transom window had the proper morning light in it. The shop beyond the door had filled with its working sound — customers, the bell, Pewsey at the counter explaining something patiently to somebody, Cassia's voice answering a question about scales.

He set the last jar back on its mark — *Powdered Wormwood, gr.2.* The two top runs were done. The shelves looked, to his eye, properly clean, the labels undisturbed.

He shook the cloth out into the bucket one last time, climbed down off the ladder, and stretched his back.

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 09:33

He folded the damp cloth over the rim of the bucket, hung the dry cloth back on its hook, picked up his mug and plate, put his jacket back on, slung the satchel over his shoulder. Out into the back room — the workbench was empty now, Cassia somewhere in the front of the shop with a customer by the sound of it — and through into the main shop.

Pewsey was at the counter, weighing out something brown into a small twist of paper for an older witch who was waiting with a coin purse open. He glanced up at Caleb without stopping the weigh.

"Done?"

"Both top runs. Cracked nothing, moved nothing, shifted no labels."

The older witch glanced at Caleb without comment, took her twist of brown stuff, paid, and went out with the bell.

"I'm a bit early on Brownson's. — Anything else needs doing in the next twenty minutes? Sweep, lift, anything you'd hand a pair of arms?"

Pewsey looked at him. The same considering pause as before.

"You did the dust honestly, I'll give you that. — No. I've enough to do that's mine, and you're a customer, not staff. The deal was two hours. Two hours. Off you go." He paused. "Come back when you've got a sixth-year cousin needs his kit and tell him to ask for me. — And mind Brownson with that book of yours. He'll be more interested than I was, and not necessarily for the same reasons."

She was at the standing scales by the window, weighing something pale and powdery into a paper twist for an older wizard in a green coat. She looked up, caught the awkward wave, did the small registering tilt of her head he was already starting to know — *yes, I see you* — and one corner of her mouth went up half a hair without disturbing the customer.

That was all of it.

He ducked his head, tucked his chin into his collar, and went out the door. The bell rang once behind him.

Diagon Alley properly mid-morning now. Shops mostly open or in the act of opening, the breakfast trade replaced by the morning's regular customers, witches with shopping baskets, two boys in robes who looked like fifth-years comparing potion-kit prices outside Slug & Jiggers' front window. Brownson's, four doors down on the same side, had its sign hanging — *BROWNSON & SON, BOOKSELLERS — Specialists in Antiquarian, Magical, and Historical Volumes* — but the shutters were still down and a small printed card in the door said *OPEN AT TEN.*

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 09:35

He sat on the low stone step in front of Brownson's window. Hands in his pockets. Satchel on his lap.

Diagon at this hour was the wizarding world's working morning. A witch came past with a small terrier on a lead. Two boys in scarves came out of Eeylops with an owl in a cage between them — a barn owl, indignant, the boys arguing about whose turn it was to carry. An older man with a long white beard the wrong shape for Dumbledore's, in plain travelling robes, walked past reading a folded *Prophet* and not looking where he was going. Florean Fortescue's was opening up across the way; the proprietor in his pink-and-orange apron rolled his front canopy down on its handle and put out the day's chalkboard.

Caleb watched the alley happen.

He took the apple out of his jacket pocket. Looked at it. Polished it on his sleeve. Took a bite.

Twenty-three minutes.

▼ 09:50

A witch in working robes came up the alley with a heavy ring of keys, unlocked the door of the bookshop, glanced at Caleb without comment, and went inside. A minute later he could hear the scrape of the shutters being unbolted from the inside. A few minutes after that, a thin tall woman of perhaps fifty appeared at the door — *Mrs Brownson*, presumably — and lifted the small *OPEN AT TEN* card down.

"You can come in if you've a mind, lad. We're early today."

## Narrator

OOC: Good question. We never settled it during character setup so there's no commit either way.

Standard Hogwarts students may bring an owl, a cat, or a toad. By 1977 the toads are out of fashion (mostly an early-20th-century thing), so it's owl-or-cat for most students, with rats becoming a more common alternative. Owls are useful — they're the school's mail system, but a personal owl gives you mail privacy. Cats are a companion. Some students don't bring an animal at all.

For Caleb specifically — half-blood, Manchester family, wizard father with his own working life — there are a few clean options:

- **Family owl.** The Waddell household has an owl that lives at home and handles the family's wizarding mail. Caleb does *not* bring it to school; it stays with his father at the workshop, which is also why his father's note yesterday came by school-hire tawny rather than the family bird. This is the most realistic working-class wizarding family setup. Caleb would borrow the family owl when home, send letters via the school owlery when at school.

- **Personal owl at school.** Caleb has his own owl that lives in the Hogwarts Owlery during term and at home in summer. Possible but slightly more affluent than the family I've been writing — owls cost money and feed.

- **No animal.** Plenty of seventh-years have outgrown wanting one. Some never did. Perfectly normal.

- **A cat.** Always possible. Different vibe — a cat suggests someone who likes a presence in the dorm room rather than a working tool.

Given everything established (workshop family, half-blood, Manchester, careful with money — bought a £3.10s pen and a 40p bouquet on his Muggle pocket money, didn't haggle but isn't loaded), I'd default to the **family owl at home** option. Caleb has known the bird his whole life, knows its name, can borrow it when on holiday, doesn't have one with him at school. Mail goes through the Hogwarts owlery during term.

If you'd rather he had a personal animal at school, two options that fit:
- A small owl he was given as a NEWT-year present (an Athene, a barn owl, a little screech owl). Practical, low-maintenance, his.
- A cat. Slightly more "this is my companion" — useful if you want a presence in scenes.

Pick one and I'll commit it. The family-owl-at-home version is the lightest-touch and won't add inventory burden; the personal owl at school version gives you a small story hook (his bird, his trips up to the owlery to send letters); the cat version gives you a mute fond presence in dormitory scenes.

What suits Caleb best?

Picking up at Brownson's:

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 09:50

He stood up from the step, brushed his trousers down, and went in.

The shop was — small, but somehow not small. The front room ran perhaps fifteen feet deep, but the shelves climbed high enough that every wall was a hatched wall of book-spines, and a brass rolling ladder went up into the gloom of an upper shelf-tier that disappeared into a balcony Caleb couldn't quite see the back of. The smell was the proper old-bookshop smell: leather, foxed paper, the faint dryness of vellum, a hint of beeswax from the shelves themselves. A single tabby cat was curled on a stack of catalogues at the front counter and did not lift its head when he came in.

Mrs Brownson — the thin tall woman in working robes — was already back at the counter, pulling an account-ledger toward her. The witch with the keys had vanished further into the shop.

"Antiquarian, magical, or historical, lad? — Or all three at once, which is the most fun." She didn't look up.

## Narrator

OOC: Good. Let me commit her to the database first and then we'll handle Brownson's. The owl: small, possibly the runt of her clutch, slightly lopsided in some way (a wing that doesn't quite fold flush, an off-centre tuft, one ear-tuft taller than the other), clever, devious, Caleb's. Already at the Hogwarts Owlery when he arrives.

Names — I'll skew older, settled, a name a Manchester half-blood family would have given. Names that aren't punny but have weight:

**English-traditional:**
- *Vesper* — evening, the evening star. Has the right slightly-old feel.
- *Beatrice* / *Bea* — old-fashioned, dignified.
- *Wren* — small, clever, the wren is England's smallest bird and traditionally cunning ("the king of birds"). Possibly perfect for a runt-owl who's smarter than her size.
- *Mabel* — proper-old-fashioned, working-class English name; an owl named Mabel is a particular kind of joke.
- *Hettie* / *Hester* — same register, slightly literary.

**Latin/learned:**
- *Strix* — Latin for owl, also for the bird of ill omen. Dramatic, a bit dark.
- *Minerva* — owl of wisdom; obvious but classic.
- *Pallas* — same family, less obvious.

**Slightly stranger:**
- *Tuesday* — for a bird that arrived on a Tuesday. Quiet small joke.
- *Margaret* / *Greta* — name a serious creature seriously.
- *Solomon* — a wise name for a small clever owl.
- *Atropos* — given Caleb's father-joke yesterday morning about Greek fates. A small inside reference. Atropos cuts the thread, which is a heavy name for a small owl, but it's the kind of name a clever 11-year-old who'd just read Greek mythology might have given the bird he was given to take to school. It would have stuck.

**My favourite for Caleb's character:**

***Atropos*** — because it's an inside-joke between him and his dad that started yesterday morning ("if Atropos turns up in a Hogsmeade pub one weekend, you tell her your father said no"), it ties back to Caleb being the kind of clever boy who reads encyclopaedias unsupervised, and a small lopsided clever runt owl named for the fate who cuts threads is exactly the kind of thing Caleb would have thought was funny at eleven and still thinks is funny at eighteen. It also retroactively makes his father's joke even funnier — *Atropos* turning up in a Hogsmeade pub *would* be Caleb's owl finding him at the Three Broomsticks.

Or **Wren**, if you'd rather not anchor the owl to yesterday morning's bit and want her to feel like her own thing.

Pick one (or another from the list, or one of your own) and I'll commit her.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 09:52

Mrs Brownson glanced up from the ledger.

"Athena." A small approving nod, like the name was sound. "Right. — Who's the man?"

He set the book on the counter, opened to the title leaf. Cuthbert's name and date in the medieval cursive, dark ink on slightly yellowed vellum.

"*Magister Cuthbertus de Lindesia, anno Domini MCCCXIV.* I bought this off Burke last night. A herbalist's notebook by the look of the contents. Mr Pewsey at Slug & Jiggers said you'd have a *Compendium Magistrorum* that'd tell me whether he's a known figure or just a parish doctor. He wasn't sure either way. He said the ones who *aren't* in the Compendium are sometimes more interesting than the ones who are."

Mrs Brownson tilted her head and looked at the book. Did not touch it. Read the title leaf from where she stood.

"Cuthbert of Lindsey. — Hm. Not a name I have on hand. — *Compendium Magistrorum Medii Aevi*, yes, we keep that on the reference shelf. Three volumes, alphabetical by Christian name, so your man's in volume one. Free to consult; you can't take it from the shop." She glanced sideways at him. "Mr Pewsey told you the right thing about the Compendium, mind. It's a roll of the *credentialed*. The ones licensed by the medieval magical chapter-houses or admitted to the Wizards' Council. A man practising in a Lincolnshire parish in 1314 might be in it or might not, depending on whether he ever bothered with the chapter-house at York. — Volume one's at the front of the reference shelf, by the long table. — Help yourself."

She nodded toward the back of the shop, where Caleb could now see, past the standing shelves, a small reading table with three or four leather chairs and a low brass lamp.

"Anything else, you ask. I'm here all morning. — And the cat is Bartleby and he doesn't like being moved, so work around him."

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 09:53

Mrs Brownson made a small sound that might have been a *hm* and might have been the start of a warning, and then closed her mouth. She set the ledger down to watch.

Bartleby was the proper kind of bookshop tabby — heavy, dignified, half-lidded, settled on a stack of last spring's catalogues at the front counter the way a bishop settles on a throne he will not, in this lifetime, be lifted out of. He did not look up as Caleb came over.

Caleb crouched. Made a slow loose fist. Held it up — not pushed, just *available* — about six inches from the cat's nose. Stayed still.

Bartleby's eyes opened a fraction more. Considered. Made the small assessment a senior cat made when offered the fist of a stranger. Reached out the tip of his nose, very deliberately, and touched it once to Caleb's knuckles. Sniffed twice. Considered again. Then — and this was the test, which Caleb had passed before with his mother's cousin's cat in Stockport without ever quite knowing why — turned his head and rubbed the side of his jaw against Caleb's knuckles, transferring scent from his cheek-glands onto Caleb's hand: *you are now part of the furniture.*

Caleb opened his hand. Scratched, gently, behind the left ear, then at the soft place where the jaw met the neck. Bartleby's eyes closed properly. A low rolling purr started up — the kind that wasn't pleasure exactly so much as a slow public statement of *the human is acceptable, leave us alone.*

Mrs Brownson, behind the counter, said, "*Hm.*"

This time it was the proper kind of *hm.* Caleb couldn't read whether it was *well done* or *nobody else gets that out of him.* Possibly both.

*I pet him until he has the cat 'I'm over it' and then I do that spell to summon the book because I think I'm allowed to do that here but I don't if I'm not*

## Narrator

OOC: Quick check before I run that beat.

A few flags worth surfacing:

**On the spell.** I assume you mean *Accio* (the Summoning Charm). That's a real-world fourth-year charm — Slughorn-era Hogwarts curriculum — and a seventh-year would have it. So Caleb is in technical bounds.

**On using it in a shop.** This is where it gets a little dicey:

- Wizard shops generally tolerate magic done by adult wizards on the premises, but using a Summoning Charm to fetch a book off a reference shelf is the kind of thing that would draw a pointed look from a bookseller. *Accio* on antique books is a way to damage them — they fly through the air, hit a person or shelf, drop. It's a bit like using a vacuum cleaner near a 14th-century manuscript. Mrs Brownson would not be thrilled.
- It's a working bookshop, not a library you read in. There's a deference owed to the place.
- The volume isn't far — Mrs Brownson said *back of the shop, at the long table.* Caleb could just walk over and pick it up. Casting a Summoning Charm in this context reads as showing off, or as laziness, in a way an older bookseller would clock.

**On "if I'm not allowed."** A respectable bookshop wouldn't have a posted rule against magic — it'd just be a thing you don't do, like running a hand along glassware in a museum. The way Caleb would find out he isn't supposed to is by getting a sharp word from Mrs Brownson and possibly being asked to leave.

**Three honest options:**

1. **Skip the spell, just walk to the back and fetch the book.** Quietest, most appropriate, makes Mrs Brownson a future ally rather than wary.
2. **Ask first.** *"Mrs Brownson, do you mind if I summon it?"* — gives her the chance to say no, and reads as polite rather than presumptuous. She probably says no, but you've asked properly.
3. **Cast it anyway.** Risks a sharp word and earning yourself a quiet mark in her ledger as the kind of customer she keeps an eye on. Would be in character for a clever boy who's been pleased with himself all morning, but it costs goodwill.

Which? (Or tell me if I've misread the spell you meant.)

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 09:55

He gave Bartleby one final scritch under the chin — the cat's eyes were closed and the purr had downshifted to the low contented rumble of a creature who was about to remember that it had been planning to nap anyway — and stood up.

The reading table at the back of the shop was where she'd said. The reference shelf above it ran a full wall, the spines in the dignified leather of working scholarly volumes. *Compendium Magistrorum Medii Aevi* was easy to find — three matched volumes in dark green calfskin, *Vol. I (A — F)* on the spine of the first. He took it down carefully, sat in one of the leather chairs at the long table, set the volume on the green baize, and opened it.

The Compendium was indexed alphabetically by Christian name, as Mrs Brownson had said. He turned to *C*. The pages were dense — small two-column type, roughly twenty entries per page, names and dates and a brief paragraph on each. *Cadwallader, Iolo (fl. 1240–1278). Caedmon de Whitby (fl. 1093–1140). Caractacus of Arundel...* Down through the C's. Past *Cuthbert de Aspley.* Past *Cuthbert of Furness Abbey.* Past *Cuthbert of Hexham.*

He kept going.

*Cuthbert of Lindesia.* Two lines of entry. Smaller than most.

> *CUTHBERTUS DE LINDESIA, fl. 1295–1320. Lincolnshire herbalist; mentioned in the rolls of the York Chapter House (1297, 1304). No surviving works listed. See also: PEREGRINI INFAMIAE, infra, vol. III appendix.*

Caleb read it twice.

He closed Volume I. Looked at the spine of Volume III on the shelf. *Vol. III (R — Z, with appendices.)*

## User

*I look up Pellegrino Infirmary*

## Narrator

OOC: Small note — *PEREGRINI INFAMIAE* is the cross-reference, not *Pellegrino Infirmary*. They sound similar in English but aren't related; *Peregrini Infamiae* is a Latin phrase meaning roughly *the wanderers of infamy* or *those marked with infamy who travelled*. So that's what Caleb's looking up. Continuing:

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 09:58

He pulled Volume III down off the shelf. *(R — Z, with appendices.)* Heavier than Volume I — the appendices added weight. He set it on the baize, found the contents leaf at the front, and ran a finger down the appendix listings.

*Appendix A: Posthumous Adjustments and Corrections, 1850–1972.*
*Appendix B: Suppressed Entries (with date of suppression).*
*Appendix C: Peregrini Infamiae.*
*Appendix D: Index of Cross-References.*

He turned to Appendix C.

The appendix was prefaced with a paragraph in the Compendium's careful editorial voice. He read it through.

> *Peregrini Infamiae — "The Wanderers of Infamy" — is the term given by the Wizards' Council of York (in session at Beverley, 1331) to a small set of British magical practitioners of the late thirteenth and early fourteenth centuries whose work was deemed, after long deliberation by the Council, to fall outside the boundaries of legitimate magical practice as the Council understood them. The Council declined to call these figures heretics, considering the term to belong properly to the Church and to lie outside the Council's jurisdiction. Instead it described them as "wanderers" — magicians who had walked, in some sense not always specified, beyond the discipline of their training — and recorded their names in this appendix as a caution to subsequent practitioners.*
>
> *The list is not a list of the prosecuted, the convicted, or the censured. It is a list of those whose work the Council judged to be sufficiently strange that the practitioners themselves, in the Council's view, had become subjects of study rather than practitioners of magic. Most figures in this appendix did not, in fact, suffer any sanction in their lifetimes. Some lived long and quiet lives. The notation reflects the Council's retrospective assessment, made in many cases decades after the practitioner's death.*

Below the preface: a list. Twenty-three names, alphabetised, each with a brief paragraph. Caleb's eye went straight to the *C*'s.

> *CUTHBERTUS DE LINDESIA (q.v., Vol. I). Herbalist, Lincolnshire. Listed for his attendance, in the years 1311–1313, upon a young woman of his parish who experienced a series of seventeen visions, the content of which Cuthbert recorded in writing. The woman did not survive the seventeenth vision in any condition recognised by Cuthbert's contemporaries as life. Cuthbert himself, by 1314, was reported by his associates to have undergone a perceptible change in manner, and the Council subsequently judged that he had, in some sense, walked the same path the woman had walked, though deliberately and to a different end. Cuthbert refused all questioning on the matter and continued his practice as a parish herbalist until his death in 1320. The Council notes the case as the foundational instance of what later figures (q.v. RUDIBARTUS DE STAINFORD, RICHARD OF KIRKHAM, MARGERY THE LITTLE OF LACOCK) attempted to replicate, in some cases with effects similar to Cuthbert's, in others not. The path itself, in so far as the Council was able to ascertain its content, is recorded in the supplementary roll appended to this appendix (see Appendix C, Roll II).*

Caleb read it twice. The phrase *did not survive the seventeenth vision in any condition recognised by Cuthbert's contemporaries as life* sat on the page in the Compendium's calm Latin-derived bureaucratic prose, and he read it three times more.

He looked at the contents listing for the appendix. *Roll II — The Path of the Seventeen.* Two leaves further on. He turned to it.

The supplementary roll began on a fresh page. The heading: *ROLL II — THE PATH OF THE SEVENTEEN, AS RECORDED FROM CUTHBERT'S ACCOUNT.*

The page below it was blank.

A small note at the foot of the page, in modern editorial type rather than the medieval voice:

> *Editor's note (1934): The supplementary roll has been removed from this volume on the recommendation of the standing committee of the Department of Magical Education and the Wizengamot's office on the regulation of historical knowledge. The roll is held in restricted archive at the Department of Mysteries and is available for consultation only by the holders of a research warrant duly issued by both bodies. A summary of the path's content, suitable for the general reader, has been considered and rejected on three separate occasions (1856, 1901, 1934).*

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 10:04

He set Volume III open at the appendix and pulled Volume I back across the baize beside it. Took out his notebook.

The four names Cuthbert had listed in the hidden quire — the petitioners who'd come asking him for the path. He'd copied them down last night without translating. He turned to the page in his notebook and read them off:

> *Rudibartus de Stainford*
> *Ricardus de Kirkham*
> *Margaria Parva de Lacock*
> *Iohannes Capellanus de Sleaforde*

The first three matched the appendix exactly. *Rudibartus de Stainford. Richard of Kirkham. Margery the Little of Lacock.* Cuthbert had named, in his hidden quire, the same three people the Wizards' Council later listed as the figures who attempted to walk the path. The Council had judged Cuthbert the *foundational* case and these three the *replicators.*

Cuthbert had refused them. They had walked it anyway. They had got onto the official list.

The fourth — *Iohannes Capellanus de Sleaforde,* John the Chaplain of Sleaford — was not in the appendix. Caleb checked the *I*'s and *J*'s of the *Peregrini* list twice. Not there. He went back to Volume I, the main alphabetical listing, and looked under both *Iohannes* and *Johannes.* Several Johns. None of them a chaplain of Sleaford. None of them dated to the 1310s.

So three of the four had walked it. The fourth had not — or had walked it and avoided notice — or had abandoned the attempt — or had died before he tried — or had simply been forgotten by history because his attempt produced nothing the Council noticed.

He read each of the three's appendix entries — Rudibartus, Richard, Margery. The Compendium gave each a short paragraph. *Rudibartus de Stainford* (a minor wizard of the West Riding, attempted the path circa 1320, judged by the Council to have *walked partially and stopped*, lived a further twenty years in retirement, no further notable practice). *Richard of Kirkham* (a Cumbrian; attempted circa 1325, judged to have *walked it differently than Cuthbert and reached an undocumented end-state*, disappeared from his parish in 1328, fate unknown, the Council marked him *missing rather than deceased* in 1331). *Margery the Little of Lacock* (a Wiltshire wisewoman; attempted circa 1330, *walked it to its conclusion*, recorded by neighbours as undergoing a profound change, lived in apparent good health and continued practice until 1372, when she died of unrelated old-age illness; her own surviving notes — a small commonplace book — are *held in the Lacock parish chest, presumed lost during the Reformation*).

So three different outcomes. One stopped halfway and lived quietly. One reached an end-state nobody could describe and vanished. One reached an end-state and lived a long good life.

Margery's outcome was the only one that read as *worked.*

Caleb made his notes. Closed both Compendium volumes. Slid them carefully back to their places on the shelf.

The pocket watch said quarter past ten.

If he was going to fetch his trunk from Mrs Penhale's and make King's Cross by ten thirty for the eleven o'clock, he had to leave the alley now.

*I ask what this is and what this means as I walk toward the exit* 'surviving notes — a small commonplace book — are held in the Lacock parish chest, presumed lost during the Reformation).'

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 10:16

He came back up to the front counter. Mrs Brownson was helping a customer — an older man with a list — and the second witch (the one with the keys) was now at the till. He waited politely until the customer was paid up and on his way.

"Mrs Brownson — last quick question, then I'm out of your hair. The appendix said one of these old practitioners had a *commonplace book*, *held in the Lacock parish chest, presumed lost during the Reformation.* What does that mean exactly? — Is that a real bit of jargon I should understand?"

Mrs Brownson set down the parcel she was tying.

"Three things in one sentence." She held up three fingers. "*Commonplace book* — that's a personal notebook. People kept them all through the medieval and early-modern period. Recipes, prayers, snippets they liked, copies of letters, household accounts, charms. Personal miscellany, the kind a working herbalist or midwife would keep beside her bed and write into for forty years. They're often the most useful single source we have on a person's actual practice, because the publicly-published works of the period are usually what people *wanted* to be remembered for, and the commonplace book is what they were actually doing.

"*Parish chest* — every English parish kept a locked wooden chest in the church, where the parish records lived. Baptism, marriage, burial, churchwardens' accounts. Sometimes valuables. Sometimes private papers a family didn't want lost — a will, a deed, a notebook the family had decided was too important to keep at home where it might burn. A commonplace book *held in the Lacock parish chest* means the family — or Margery herself, before she died — had decided to deposit it with the church for safekeeping rather than passing it down to a relative.

"*Presumed lost during the Reformation* — the third bit. Henry the Eighth dissolved the monasteries in 1536 to 1541. *Lacock Abbey was a Cistercian house, dissolved in 1539.* During the dissolution and through the rest of the Reformation, parish chests across England were emptied or destroyed in stages — partly by the crown's commissioners, partly by Protestant zealots stripping out anything that smelled of Catholic ritual, partly by accident. What got into a parish chest before the dissolution and isn't accounted for in a later inventory is described as *presumed lost during the Reformation* — meaning it was probably destroyed, but nobody can prove it was."

She gave a small wry smile.

"In other words: a useful working notebook by a 14th-c. wisewoman of Lacock, deposited with her parish church for safekeeping, almost certainly destroyed in the 1530s or 40s when Lacock came down. Almost certainly. — Why d'you ask?"

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 10:18

Mrs Brownson's face did a small careful thing — not quite a fall, not quite a closing, but the look of a working bookseller who had just been told something more than she expected to be told before lunch on a Thursday.

She glanced past Caleb at the front of the shop — the door, the alley beyond it, the witch behind the till who was politely pretending she was not listening. She lowered her voice without making a thing of lowering it.

"Mr Waddell. — That's *that* book."

She didn't ask. She *knew*.

"Burke had that book on his shelf for — what, eight years? Nine. I tried to buy it off him in '73 to put it under glass in my own back room and have a proper look at it without selling it on, and he wouldn't take what I offered." A small breath. "He must have decided to move it to the front of his window when somebody walked in who looked like they'd ask the right question. — Apparently that was you."

She did not touch the book. He hadn't taken it back out — it was in the satchel, where it had been since he left Slug & Jiggers. But she was looking at the satchel.

"Listen to me carefully, because I am about to be more forthcoming than is good for either of us."

She glanced at her counter-witch. Came round the front of the counter so she was on Caleb's side of it. Lowered her voice further.

"The supplementary roll was removed from the Compendium in 1934, as the editor's note says. *Officially* it lives in the restricted archive at the Department of Mysteries. *Unofficially* — and I am telling you this because you are eighteen years old, going to school today, and the alternative is that you leave my shop wondering — the Department holds it because they have not been able to verify it. Cuthbert's path, as he wrote it down, has been challenged on the grounds that what he recorded was a young woman's involuntary visions, not a working magical sequence. The committee that took the appendix out of public circulation in 1934 was split on whether to suppress it or print it. They suppressed it because they could not agree on whether it was real. They are still arguing about it, as far as I know."

A beat.

"Margery's commonplace book is — was — the only other recorded primary source. If it survives anywhere, it would settle the argument. It is *almost certainly* destroyed. *Almost certainly* is what we say in this trade when we mean *we have not actually checked*. The Lacock parish records from the 1530s are held in the Wiltshire archives, with a small wizarding subset deposited at the Cotswold Branch of the Magical Records Office at Cirencester. I have not personally been to Cirencester to check what's there from Lacock specifically. I do not know anyone who has."

She looked at him properly.

"Mr Waddell. You are at Hogwarts today. You have NEWTs to sit, and a school to be at, and a life that is not yet committed to a fourteenth-century footnote. You are not at this moment in a position to take the train to Cirencester. — You also bought this book off the worst man on Diagon, and the worst man on Diagon let you have it. That is a thing I do not yet know what to do with. — Will you keep it safe?"

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 10:21

Mrs Brownson opened her mouth, closed it, and did not deny it.

A small dignified *go on with you* gesture toward the door.

"Hogsmeade weekends, Mr Waddell. The first is in October. — Don't pet my apprentice."

He was already halfway out the door. Bartleby did not look up. The bell rang.

Diagon Alley properly mid-morning was busy now and he had to thread past it. Down past Slug & Jiggers — he did not stop, but he glanced through the window and caught Cassia in the same standing-scales pose, weighing for another customer; she did not look up — past the bookshop, past Florean Fortescue's, past the shutters of the smaller daytime trades. The Leaky's back archway. The taproom briefly, the smell of late-breakfast cooking, Tom at the bar. Out the front.

Charing Cross Road into the Bloomsbury return — north and east, fast, the satchel banging at his hip. Past the British Museum. Down the lane to Mrs Penhale's. He let himself in with — no, the key was hers, he'd given it back. He rang the bell.

She opened on the second ring.

"You're cutting it fine, love."

"I know — sorry — train at eleven."

"Trunk's where you left it." She stepped back. He grabbed the trunk by its end-strap from the umbrella stand. "*Go.* Don't be polite. I'll wave you off from the step."

He went. Out the front door. Down the steps with the trunk thumping behind him, satchel over the shoulder, jacket flying open. North on the lane, right at the corner, up through the streets toward Euston Road. The trunk was heavy and the pavement was uneven and twice he had to slow for the corner of a kerb, but he did not stop.

Onto Euston Road. King's Cross was the great brick palace on the right.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 10:43

He went *vrooming* through the concourse with a trunk that was decidedly not vrooming-shaped — past the ticket queues, past the *Daily Mail* stand, past a startled woman with a hatbox who said *well, really* — and threaded between Platforms 9 and 10. The brick pillar between them. The brass plate that read *9 / 10*. He did not slow down. He did not glance round to see if Muggles were watching, which was the rookie mistake, but he had been doing this since he was eleven and he knew the trick: lean into the wall like you mean it and the wall has the courtesy to be elsewhere.

The wall was elsewhere.

He came out the other side onto Platform 9¾.

The Express was sat in its full late-Edwardian splendour — the great red engine, the *HOGWARTS EXPRESS* nameplate gleaming, steam curling lazily from the funnel and from beneath the carriages, the long red rake stretching down the length of the platform. The platform itself was *full*. Trunks. Owls. Cats. Parents hugging children. Children hugging parents. A first-year somewhere ahead of him crying. A pair of fifth-years arguing about whether something was *funny enough* to do. The smell of coal smoke and the faint warm hay of small magical creatures. The clock at the end of the platform: *10:43.*

He got there with seventeen minutes to spare.

He hauled the trunk down the platform, scanning for a carriage with room. The compartments toward the front were already full — the early arrivers always took those — so he worked his way down toward the middle and back. Halfway down, a door stood open onto a compartment with two seats free.

Through the open door he could see, with her back to the corridor, a girl with a long dark plait, in a Ravenclaw-coloured scarf, reading a book.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 10:44

The plait did not turn round.

A small considering pause from the compartment.

Then she did turn — slowly, the way she turned when she had decided that a thing was happening and she was choosing to be present for it — and Mei Chang looked at him over the back of the bench seat.

She was — Mei. Same calm dark eyes behind the rectangular glasses, same precise plait, same Ravenclaw scarf wound twice. She'd grown half an inch over the summer, or held herself half an inch differently; he couldn't tell which. Her face did the thing where her mouth did not change but her eyes did the small slow registering — *yes, I see you, hello, you have run for this train and you are pink in the face* — and one corner of her mouth moved a fraction.

"Caleb."

She did not say *MEIIII*. She did not stand up. She set her book down on her lap, page-down to keep her place, and watched him in the doorway with the particular composed Mei-ness that was the first thing he ever loved about her and also the thing that had kept him from speaking for two years.

"You're going to want to come in before you take someone's eye out with that trunk."

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 10:45

He set the trunk down inside the compartment with a soft thump, hauled it up onto the luggage rack with effort — Mei reached up to steady the far end without comment, two of her fingers on the corner where the strap was — and got the satchel down on the empty bench opposite hers. He sat. Pulled out the small white shop-bag from Mrs Crowther's. Unwrapped the green card box from inside it. Set the box on his knee.

"Don't open it yet. — Or do. I haven't decided which is worse."

He held it out across the gap between the benches.

The compartment was a six-seat Edwardian: red plush, brass fittings, the window already grimed with the morning's coal smoke. Outside on the platform a witch was hugging her son for the third time and her son was Looking At The Ceiling. The clock at the end of the platform read forty-five past.

Mei took the box. Considered it. Looked at him over the top of it.

"You haven't decided which is worse." She set the book down on the seat beside her. "All right." She lifted the lid.

Inside the box: the Conway Stewart, marbled green-and-gold, slightly the heft of an actual fountain pen against the velvet bed. The small folded card tucked under the velvet. She lifted the pen first, turned it once between her fingers — testing the weight the way she tested everything, the small Mei-assessment — and set it back. Lifted the card. Unfolded it.

Read.

Read it again.

Did not say anything for a beat.

Then her face did something he had not seen on it in two years. The composure stayed, but underneath it something softened — the corners of her mouth did not lift, exactly, but the tension at them released.

"Caleb."

She did not look up from the card.

"Have you a quill on you. I want to circle one."

He hugged her — quick, slightly off-angle because of the bench between them, satchel-strap getting in the way once and being shrugged off. She did not stiffen. She did not, exactly, hug back; she let him hug her with the particular small steady acceptance of a person who had been waiting some time to be hugged by him and was choosing to receive it the way she received most things, with her whole calm attention and not a fuss.

He pulled back. Sat down opposite her properly. Did the *be cool* under his breath. Did the *plenty of options* under his breath. Did the *just sit there and smile.*

Smiled.

Mei watched all three whispers with the small composed amusement of a girl who could lipread well enough to follow a one-man performance happening eighteen inches from her face. She tucked a piece of hair that had come loose from her plait back behind her ear. Looked at him over her glasses.

"You have *plenty of options.*"

She did not say it as a question.

"Caleb. — Sit there and smile. That bit you got right."

The compartment door, which he had not closed when he came in, slid further open as a small fourth-year head poked itself in — *anyone in here? — oh, sorry —* and was gone again as fast as it had come.

The platform clock outside the window read eleven minus thirteen. Steam was getting up in the engine somewhere ahead of them; Caleb could feel the carriage's faint settling sigh as the boilers came to pressure. Two prefects were walking the platform.

He stood up, made a small ceremony of crossing the gap, and sat down beside her — careful of the box on her lap, the pen, the book underneath the box — and stared at the ceiling of the compartment as though he had a great interest in red plush upholstery.

His arm went over her shoulders by degrees: first along the seat-back behind her, casual; then a slow descent to actually rest on her shoulders. The whole sequence took perhaps four seconds and was visibly choreographed.

Mei did not look at the ceiling. She looked at him doing the ceiling thing. The corner of her mouth did the half-millimetre move.

She picked her book back up. Found her place. Did not, however, open it yet.

"Caleb."

"Mei."

"I am going to read in a minute. — But you may sit there and smile at the ceiling for as long as you like."

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 10:50

Mei made a small huff that was not, technically, a laugh.

"Caleb. *Read.*"

She did not look at him, but she tilted the book a fraction his way so he could in fact read along if he wanted to. The page was open at a chapter on *Sympathetic Resonance in Spoken Charms* — dense, the kind of seventh-year prose that read slowly even when you were paying attention.

He leaned. Closed his eyes.

She read. Turned a page after a few minutes, careful not to jostle him. The compartment door opened twice more in the next minutes — different fourth-year, then a Hufflepuff Caleb didn't know — and both times Mei looked up, said *full, sorry* in her quiet voice, and the door slid back. By 10:55 the platform outside the window was the proper chaos of the last five minutes — a small shoving cluster of trunks at the boarding doors, a witch running for a porter, parents kissing children one final unnecessary time. The boilers settled into their working hiss.

By 10:58 the platform was thinning. The whistle blew somewhere at the front. Doors began to slam down the rake.

By 11:00 the train gave its small forward lurch.

Caleb did not feel it. He had been asleep for perhaps four minutes. The adrenaline of the last day — the morning at the workshop, the bus to Salford, the kerb opposite the Brackley house, Annie at the stall, Borgin & Burkes, the locked book at the desk in Mrs Penhale's, the run to the post box, the early morning at Slug & Jiggers, the stocking the high shelves at Pewsey's, Brownson's and the appendix and the editor's note, the run back for the trunk, the platform — had collected behind his eyes and dropped him neatly the moment his shoulder met hers.

Mei read on.

The Hogwarts Express slid out of King's Cross. London came up alongside the windows and slid backward. Up through the brick suburbs. Over the river. North.

Mei let him sleep. She read through to the end of the chapter on Sympathetic Resonance, turned the page on the next, read half of that, and then — at the point where her own shoulder had begun to go faintly numb under the weight of his head — she made a small considered movement, lifted his head off her shoulder by an inch with the side of her own, and let him settle gently against the corner of the bench-back instead, where he could go on sleeping without her arm asleep.

He slept on. The Express was somewhere north of Watford by then, into proper countryside, the long fields sliding past in the gentle late-morning English haze.

About an hour later — just after half past twelve — Mei reached over and put a finger to his shoulder. A single light prod.

"Caleb."

He surfaced slowly. Eyes still closed. Made a small noise.

"You said an hour or two. It has been an hour and a half. The trolley will be along in a few minutes if I have not miscounted, and you said you wanted to talk."

He opened his eyes.

The compartment was exactly as he had fallen asleep in it. The window now had proper countryside out of it — a long view of stubble fields, a farmhouse, a herd of cows looking up at the train with the long stupid stare cows gave passing trains. The light was bright and yellow. Mei was looking at him with the same composed attention she'd looked at him with at half past ten, only now there was a small fold of book-paper under her thumb to mark her place.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 12:36

He sat up properly, rubbed his face, and reached for the satchel.

"This — I want to talk to you about. Mostly because it has been keeping me awake and I do not want to be the only person in the world thinking about it." He set the locked book on the small table between the windows. "And — incidentally, before I forget — this is *deeply* Ravenclaw of me. I want it noted. I have spent a Wednesday evening on a Latin lock."

Mei set her bookmark in. Closed her book. Looked at the locked book on the table the way she looked at a problem set.

"Noted. Show me."

He walked her through it. Slowly, this time — Mei was Mei and he wanted her to actually understand it — and he left nothing out.

Yesterday afternoon, Borgin & Burkes; the conversation with Caractacus Burke; the locked book at three Galleons. The medieval Latin riddle on the front cover lock and the three concentric brass rings. The herbalist Magister Cuthbertus de Lindesia, AD 1314. His own slow translation of *TRES SUM. PRIMVS HOMINI VITAM DAT…* and the working out of the answer — the false starts (*MAD, AMO, NOX*), the realisation that a herbalist's three-letter word for *the thing that gives, takes pain, and defers death* would be **VIS**, the vital force. The lock opening. The herbal entries: a routine 14th-c. pharmacopoeia. The seventeen interleaved pages every seventeen leaves apart, each beginning with a verb of perception (*vidi, audivi, sensi, inveni*) in one of nine specific places, each numbered *primus* through *septimusdecimus,* each containing a single larger word at its centre — and he read her the seventeen words in order from his notebook:

> *VIS. UMBRA. NOMEN. SANGUIS. CINIS. VOX. SIGILLUM. SOMNIUM. CARO. NUMERUS. NIHIL. STELLA. AURUM. OS. LIMEN. SILENTIUM. FINIS.*

The hidden second lock in the spine. The seventeenth word — *FINIS* — being on the *outside* of the path, Cuthbert's own word for the unknown. *NEX* as the second cipher, the proper Latin for *death*. The hidden quire that came free from the back binding. The young woman of Cuthbert's parish in 1311 — seventeen visions across one season, in nine places, naming the seventeen things in the order recorded. *She did not die. She did not heal. She remained.* Cuthbert's warning. His list of four petitioners. His refusal to teach them.

Then this morning. Slug & Jiggers. Pewsey's lead to the Compendium. Brownson's. *Cuthbertus de Lindesia,* listed in Volume I as *Lincolnshire herbalist; mentioned in the rolls of the York Chapter House,* with no surviving works listed and a cross-reference: *PEREGRINI INFAMIAE.* Volume III, Appendix C. The Wanderers of Infamy. The Wizards' Council of York's 1331 list of practitioners who had walked beyond their training. Cuthbert as the *foundational case.* Three named successors. *Rudibartus de Stainford* (walked partially, lived quietly). *Richard of Kirkham* (walked it differently, vanished 1328, fate unknown, marked missing not deceased). *Margery the Little of Lacock* (walked it to its conclusion, lived a long good life until 1372, died of unrelated old age).

The fourth name on Cuthbert's list — *Iohannes Capellanus de Sleaforde,* John the Chaplain of Sleaford — not in the Compendium. Not in either alphabetical listing or the Wanderers' appendix.

The supplementary roll. *Roll II — The Path of the Seventeen.* The page blank. The 1934 editor's note: removed from circulation by joint recommendation of Magical Education and the Wizengamot, held in restricted archive at the Department of Mysteries, accessible by joint research warrant only. A summary considered and rejected three times — 1856, 1901, 1934.

Mrs Brownson at the counter. *That book.* She had tried to buy it off Burke in 1973. The 1934 committee had been split. The reason the path had been suppressed was not that it was dangerous but that it was contested — half the committee thought it was Cuthbert's record of a young woman's involuntary visions, half thought it was a working magical sequence, and they had not been able to agree. *They are still arguing about it, as far as I know.* Margery's commonplace book — *almost certainly* lost in the dissolution of Lacock Abbey in 1539, *almost certainly* meaning *we have not actually checked.* Possibly held, if anywhere, at the Cotswold Branch of the Magical Records Office at Cirencester.

He laid it all out. Showed her the lock. Set it to V-I-S, the front clicking open. Closed it. Set it to N-E-X, the spine clicking. Lifted the hidden quire out of the back binding and laid the four pages flat on the table.

He did not read her the quire's text out loud. He let her look at it.

She read the first paragraph. Then the second. Then she went back to the first and read it again, more slowly, the way she did when she wanted to be sure of the Latin.

She did not say anything for nearly a minute.

Then she looked up.

"Caleb. — Where is this book *now?*"

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 12:48

He glanced down at the book, mildly confused.

"...Right here?"

Then her question caught up with him. The book *itself* — yes, between his hands. The book she'd asked about — no, she meant Margery's. The commonplace book. *Where is it now?*

"Oh — Margery's. — Almost certainly destroyed in 1539 with the dissolution of Lacock Abbey. *Almost certainly* — Mrs Brownson said that's what the trade says when they mean *we haven't actually checked.* The Lacock parish records from the 1530s went two places: the Wiltshire archives — Muggle — and a small wizarding subset that ended up at the Cotswold Branch of the Magical Records Office at Cirencester. Mrs Brownson hasn't been to Cirencester to check what's there from Lacock. She doesn't know anyone who has."

He let that sit.

"So: probably gone. Possibly not. Nobody's looked."

He leaned over and rubbed the tip of his nose lightly against the side of her cheek — once, gently, the small affectionate friction of an Eskimo kiss — and pulled back.

Mei's face did the small exact thing it did when something happened she had not predicted. Her eyes closed for a half-second. Opened. The corner of her mouth went up the half-millimetre.

"Caleb."

She did not move away. She did not, however, kiss him back yet — they were on a train, the corridor was full of fourth-years, and Mei was Mei.

"You are *terrible.*" Quiet. Not unkind. "And I am trying to think about a fourteenth-century manuscript, and you are — *very distracting.*"

She picked up the open quire again, by way of recommitting her attention to it. But her free hand, after a moment, came across and rested very lightly on his where it was resting on his knee. Just the back of her fingers across his knuckles. Then she went on reading.

He turned his hand under hers — palm up — and folded his fingers over the back of hers with the deliberate concentration of a man performing surgery on a tiny mechanical part. He stared at the joined hands. He did not move them. He committed.

Mei looked up from the quire after a moment. Looked at the hands. Looked at his face — the studious furrow, the absolute stillness — and her composure cracked, briefly, into the small huff of a laugh she rarely allowed herself in public spaces. She put her free hand over her mouth to keep it from escaping further.

"Caleb."

"Mm."

"You are *holding my hand.*"

"Mm."

"Very seriously."

"Mm."

She gave up on the quire. Closed it gently, tucked it back into the hidden quire-slot in the binding's spine, slid the brass front-cover rings off VIS so the front lock re-engaged, and pushed the locked book to the side of the small table.

She turned slightly toward him on the bench. Her hand stayed in his.

"I am going to read later. — Tell me something about your summer that I do not already know. Something small."

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 12:51

Mei's eyes closed for a beat at *Wasian babies.* The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth went up properly this time — almost a smile, suppressed.

"*Wasian.*" Flat. "Caleb."

"*Almost a hundred and thirty-five pounds*, Mei."

"You weigh as much as a small calf. Congratulations. — Don't get hot and bothered, he says, like I'm — *Caleb.*"

But she'd let him lean in to whisper the third bit. Her head was tilted close to his, her plait against his cheek, the quiet of his voice in her ear close enough that she did not have to do anything to hear him — and what he said dropped neatly into that quiet as a different thing entirely.

She did not pull back fast. She did not pull back at all, in fact. Her face — the side of it Caleb could see, with his mouth still near her ear — went very still.

After a beat she sat back from him properly. Looked at him.

"*Investigated a murder.*"

She did not say it loud. She kept her voice the level of conversation among friends in a busy compartment — a level you could not be overheard at unless someone was leaning toward the door — and she did not let her face change.

"Caleb. — What."

Mei's face did — nothing visible. She had been Mei in this compartment for three quarters of an hour and she was not going to break that stillness now, not on a train where the corridor was full of fourth-years and the door was not closed.

She did, however, look at him — properly, with the full Mei-attention — and her hand around his tightened by a fraction.

She was right; it was the kind of thing a Slytherin in the corridor outside could hear and remember and use later. She didn't say that part out loud. She didn't have to.

"Tell me — quietly — what happened. From the start. From the beginning."

A pause.

"And you should know, before you start, that my parents run an import shop on the Manchester Muggle side. So whatever this pattern is, you are not the only one in this compartment with skin in it."

Picking up:

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 12:54

He told her properly. Voice low, the corridor noise as cover — the trolley was somewhere down the carriage now, the rattle of it audible — and the train itself doing the work of muffling them. The whole of yesterday morning, condensed.

His father at the workshop, before his mother saw the *Mirror.* The *Manchester Evening News* page four with its three-paragraph piece. The Brackleys: Margaret, dead, Muggle, primary-school teacher. Daniel, missing, wizard. The unidentified male visitor, also dead. The daughter at her grandmother's. His father's pattern: three this year. *People who think families like ours shouldn't be families.*

His decision to go to Salford on the bus before the train. The bus passengers and Liverpool Street and the Crescent. DS Hollis. Caleb giving Hollis his real address and his mother's name, for which his mother had not been pleased on the telephone and had not, on a public line, named the man she might have recognised.

The official description of the male deceased: white, late thirties to mid-forties, five ten, dark brown hair greying at the temples, slim, wiry, dark suit, white shirt, no tie, brown brogues, no wedding ring, no wallet, no ID. Caleb's deductions on the pavement: door forced inward, deceased had marks, police did not suspect Daniel Brackley. Hollis confirming, informally, all three. Hollis warning him not to repeat the deductions in public.

He told her his mother had said she'd talk to his father about it, and that Caleb did not yet know whether his mother had recognised the man.

He did not whisper the next part.

"Mei. Your parents and mine should know each other. I am not joking when I say that. If this is a *pattern*, then the people the pattern targets are people in households that look like ours — and *yours*. If our parents are not exchanging news, somebody is going to hear about something happening to one set and not the other in time to do anything about it. — I would like our families to meet. Not for any reason about you and me. For — the practical reason."

He let that sit a beat.

"And. Mei. I do not want you or your family to be hurt. I do not want my mother to be hurt. I am eighteen years old and I do not know how to make any of that not happen, but I think people knowing each other is part of how you start. — I am not playing at this."

Mei was quiet for a moment.

She did not look at him. She looked at the table — at the locked book, at the small bottle of ink — and her thumb moved once across the back of his hand where it was resting on his knee.

When she answered her voice was lower than it had been so far, and steadier.

"All right." A breath. "Yes. Our parents should meet. — My father is the Muggle. My mother is the witch. The shop is in his name; she does the books. They live above it. We have not had anyone come round for them in any way that makes me think they are *next* — but I have been worrying about it for nearly a year and I have not had anyone to say that to."

She glanced up at him.

"Write your parents tonight. I will write mine. We can pick a Saturday in the autumn — Hogsmeade weekends I cannot do, but a Sunday when one of us is allowed home for the weekend, or a school break. — Manchester is not large. They can meet at a tea-shop one of my father's customers runs near Piccadilly station. Neutral, public, easy to leave."

A pause.

"And, Caleb. — The Wasian grandbabies."

The corner of her mouth did something small.

"I will allow that to remain on the table."

Down the corridor, the trolley wheels rattled audibly closer. A voice — *anything off the trolley, dears* — at a compartment two doors up.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 13:03

The trolley reached their compartment a couple of minutes later. The trolley witch was the same plump cheerful woman who'd done this run for as long as Caleb had been on the train — *anything off the trolley, dears?* — and the trolley itself was the proper magical-confectionery overload: Chocolate Frogs in piles, Bertie Bott's, Pumpkin Pasties, Cauldron Cakes, Liquorice Wands, Fizzing Whizzbees, the bottom shelf of pumpkin juices and butterbeers.

"Four Chocolate Frogs, please."

"Eightpence each, three Sickles for the four."

He paid her three Sickles and pocketed the change of nothing. The four small pentagonal blue boxes, each with the gold *CHOCOLATE FROGS* wordmark and the small darting silhouette of the frog itself, came across into his hand.

He gave Mei two. Kept two for himself.

The trolley trundled on. *Anything off the trolley, dears?*

Mei turned one of the boxes over in her fingers, considered it, glanced at him with the small Mei-amusement, and set both her boxes carefully on the table beside the locked herbal book. She did not, immediately, open them.

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 13:04

He thumbed the catch on the corner of the box and the small magical seal gave with the tiny *crack* it always gave. The frog inside was already alert — they always were, the moment the box opened — and it did the small considering pause that meant it was about to leap.

He caught it with the fingers of his other hand, neat, before it could clear the table. Bit the head off. Properly: the way you ate a Chocolate Frog. The frog stilled in his hand and went back to being chocolate.

The card was tucked into the lid the way the cards always were. He pulled it out and turned it over.

> *MERLIN*
>
> *(c. 5th century — c. 6th century)*
>
> *The most famous wizard of all time, Merlin was the wizard adviser to the legendary King Arthur of Camelot. Sometimes called Merlinus Ambrosius or, in Welsh, Myrddin Emrys, Merlin pioneered the use of magic for the protection of ordinary people, and the Order of Merlin (the highest civil decoration in the wizarding world) is named in his honour.*
>
> *Card #1 of the Famous Wizards series.*

The portrait moved, faintly — Merlin in the foreground inclining his head, the long beard, the high blue robes, an indistinct landscape behind him. Caleb had it already, of course; everyone had Merlin a dozen times over.

He set the card on the table between him and Mei. Set the second unopened box beside it, in his pocket for later — no, on the table, where he wouldn't forget it. Ate the rest of the first frog in two bites.

Her thumb moved once across the back of his hand. Her plait had come slightly loose where it had been pressed against his cheek when he was sleeping; a few hairs were out at the temple.

The compartment was quiet. The corridor was quiet. The chatter from the next compartment had settled into the lower hum of fourth-years who were getting on with their Chocolate Frogs and gossip.

He sat. She sat. The train ran on.

Another pause.

"That is what I would want."

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 13:22

She seemed satisfied with the answer. *That sounds nice* was, in her register, a confirmation rather than a closing — but she was happy to let it sit, and she settled back against his shoulder properly with her hand still in his.

The train ran on through the afternoon.

There was a small interruption around half past one — Mei's seventh-year prefect badge meant she was due in the prefects' carriage for a meeting at quarter to two, and at twenty to she squeezed his hand, gathered the locked book carefully into her lap, and considered.

"Caleb. — May I take this with me? Not to show. I would like to read the herbal entries on my own for a half hour. I read Latin faster on my own than aloud."

She would bring it back. She wouldn't open the spine lock without him; that wasn't a question. She wanted forty minutes with the front-of-book herbal in her own quiet.

He gave her the book. She took it carefully into her satchel, gave him a small *back in forty* glance, and slid the compartment door open. Out. Down the corridor toward the prefects' carriage.

He had the compartment to himself.

The window was the long Midlands countryside now, gone past Stafford and into the open run toward Crewe and the north. The light coming in was good. He put both feet up on the bench opposite, leaned back, and considered what he had to do with forty minutes.

OOC: A few things Caleb plausibly wants to do with this gap. Tell me which (or invent your own):

1. **Write to your parents.** A proper letter. Mei's "write home tonight" plan plus the practical question of getting parents to meet. Has the materials in his satchel — pen, ink, paper, stamps, envelopes.
2. **Write to Annie.** Second letter, properly thought-out, since the first one was a hasty train-of-thought job sent unstamped and run-to-the-postbox. He owes her better.
3. **Write to Cassia.** First contact letter — establishing the herbal correspondence she agreed to. Promise of transcribed pages once he's at the castle.
4. **Sit and look out the window.** Train-doze, daydream about Mei.
5. **Reread his notes from yesterday and last night** — the experiment notes, the lock-puzzle work, the appendix transcriptions from Brownson's. Get them organised before they go cold.

Or some combination. Forty minutes of train is enough for two of these comfortably or three if he's quick.

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 13:40

He stood up. Took the locked book off the table. Put it behind his back with both hands. Tilted his head up and to the right and stared at the ceiling of the compartment with the air of a man who had no idea what was happening, no idea whatsoever.

Mei looked at him in the doorway. The corner of her mouth did the thing.

"*Caleb.*"

She glanced down the corridor — empty in their direction, fourth-years at the far end — and looked back at him.

"What do you get."

She thought about it for half a second. Then, very deliberately, she stepped half a pace forward, into him — close enough that the front of her jumper was an inch from his — went up on her toes the small distance she needed to, and kissed him on the mouth.

Once, briefly. Closed-mouth, careful, the kind of kiss that was a kiss and not a question. The whole of it took perhaps two seconds.

She pulled back the inch. Her glasses had not come off but they had moved a fraction. She pushed them back up her nose with her index finger.

"That. — Now hand it over, please."

She held out her hand.

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 13:41

Mei took the book from him carefully, two-handed, and tucked it into her own satchel under the flap. Then she looked up at him properly and her face did something small that he was not, yet, sure he would ever stop seeing.

"Caleb." Quiet. "It is the first."

A pause.

"For the second one — yes, the dark conversation made the first one feel right. I have been wanting to since you sat down, and I would not have done it because of *that*. I did it because of *you*. — The journal was a useful prop."

She did her plait back over her shoulder with one hand, pushed her glasses up her nose again, and stepped back from him into the corridor.

"Forty minutes. Don't get up to anything elaborate."

The compartment door slid shut behind her. Through the glass he watched her turn left up the corridor toward the prefects' carriage at the front of the train, the locked book safe in the satchel under her elbow.

He sat back down.

The compartment was very quiet. The window was bright with afternoon. The train ran on.
