*I touch all over the diadem, the bust the jewel pressing pushing pulling, especially the diadem*

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 21:35

He set both hands on the marble.

The diadem first. He ran his fingertips along the slim gold-tinted circlet — but it was carved marble, painted gold, and his fingers found nothing but the cool smooth shape of the stone. The central jewel above her forehead. He pressed it with his thumb. He pressed it harder. He tried to push it sideways. He tried to twist it. He pressed at the band of her brow, the place where the diadem met her hair, the small curl of stone behind her ear where the band disappeared.

Marble. Solid marble.

He moved to the bust itself. The shoulders. The base of her neck. The carved folds of her robes where they fell into the pedestal. The pedestal itself — the four sides, the corners, the bronze plaque (*foundress of this house*), the incised motto running round (*wit beyond measure*). He pushed the plaque. He tried to slide it. He ran his fingers along every join he could find between the bust and the pedestal — and there was no join, the whole thing was carved from one block.

He stepped back.

Carved 1881. Solid marble. No mechanism. No catch. No hidden compartment.

The bust looked at him with the same slight not-quite-smile she had been giving the room since 1881 and her diadem was, on this closer inspection, not different from the Grey Lady's in any way he could pin down — it had simply been the trick of the light from the lamp behind him.

A second-year going past the alcove with a book under her arm gave him a slightly puzzled look at finding a seventh-year manhandling the bust, and went on without comment.

## User

"Lumos"

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 21:36

His wand was in his pocket. He drew it — the springy cherry, the familiar weight of the unicorn-hair core — and gave it the small flick.

"*Lumos.*"

The tip lit. A clean steady white-gold light, brighter than the common-room lamps, pooled in a small circle on the marble in front of him.

He raised it to the bust.

In the directed light Rowena's marble face came up sharper than it had under the ambient lamps. The slight dust of three centuries in the carved folds of her hair. A hairline crack in the lower lip he had not noticed. The diadem above her brow held its central jewel — and the jewel, in the wand-light, was unmistakably *not* a marble carving painted gold but a small *real* faceted stone set into the marble. Pale blue. Possibly a sapphire. Properly set, with the small bevelled edges around it. The light caught a single small spark of refraction inside it.

The motto round the pedestal in the wand-light was clean. Bronze plaque clean. The carved folds of her robes — clean. The slight unevenness of the pedestal's base where the floor was a fraction off level — visible. The whole bust solid, joinless, carved from a single block. No seams revealed in better light.

But the jewel was real. He had not registered that under the lamps.

## User

*I tried to nudge it so see if it move on the uneven floor*

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 21:37

He set his free hand against the side of the pedestal and pushed. Gently at first — testing — and then with proper weight against the marble, the kind of push you would use to nudge a heavy piece of furniture by a quarter inch on an uneven floor.

The pedestal did not move.

He shifted his stance and tried the other direction. Then forward. Then back.

Nothing.

Whatever was holding the pedestal level on the slightly uneven floor was holding it firmly — either by sheer weight (the marble was heavy, and a 1881 commission of this size would weigh several hundred pounds), or by a fixing he could not see, or by a charm. He could not tell. The pedestal was either bolted to the floor or charmed in place or simply too heavy for his shoulder to budge.

The bust looked down at him with the slight not-quite-smile.

The Lumos held steady on his wandtip. The pale blue stone in the diadem caught the light and threw a faint speckle of refraction onto the back wall of the alcove behind the pedestal — a small wandering spot of paler light that moved when his wand moved.

## User

*I hold my wand tip directly to it and try to guide along the light to see if it crosses any unusual points in the room or comes accross any objects specifically.*

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 21:39

He brought the wandtip close to the pale blue stone — almost touching, the small white-gold pool of light directly on the facet — and the speckle of refraction on the back wall of the alcove sharpened into a clearer shape: a small bright dot, off-centred, the stone's principal beam.

He held the wand still. Watched the dot.

It sat on the back wall of the alcove, two-thirds of the way up, slightly to the left of centre. Plain stone behind. Nothing on the wall there but the wall itself — no plaque, no mark, no decoration.

He moved the wand. Slowly. The dot moved with it, sliding across the stone of the back wall as the angle of his light into the facet shifted. He guided it left, then right, then up the wall toward the ceiling, then down toward the floor of the alcove.

The dot found the back-wall floor, climbed up the side wall, crossed the carved edge of the alcove's stone moulding — nothing there reacted to it — and traced along the side wall and out into the curve of the common room proper. Once it caught for a moment on the brass curve of a wall sconce and seemed to flare slightly brighter, but that was the brass reflecting back, not the dot itself doing anything.

He brought the wand back to direct the dot at the diadem itself — at the stone — at his own wandtip — at his own face — at the bust's marble eyes — at the carved motto round the pedestal — at the bronze plaque.

Nothing reacted. No catch, no click, no opening. No latent enchantment lighting up. No second stone responding from elsewhere in the room.

It was, as far as Caleb's careful directed Lumos could tell, just a small set sapphire in a marble bust catching wand-light and throwing a refraction-spot like any faceted stone would.

He let his wand-arm relax. The light stayed on. The dot vanished from the wall as the angle shifted.

## User

*fineeee.* *I go to bed*

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 21:42

He cancelled the Lumos with a small flick. The wandtip went out. The alcove returned to its ordinary lamplit dim.

He gave the bust one last look — Rowena's slight not-quite-smile, the small set sapphire catching the ambient lamplight now as ordinary glint — and went across the common room toward the staircase up to the seventh-year boys' dormitory.

Mei caught his eye from the eagle door, where she was finally finishing with the last of her prefect duties — *going up,* his small shrug said, and her small nod said *I'll be a few minutes more.*

The staircase up was familiar. Six years of climbing it. The seventh-year boys' dormitory was at the top — a circular room with five four-poster beds, the trunks already at the foot of each, the curtains drawn back, a fire just laid in the small grate. Lin was already in his pyjamas at his bed reading. Two of the other seventh-year boys were further along, one asleep already, one writing in a notebook.

Caleb's bed was the one nearest the window. His trunk at its foot, exactly where he had left it on the platform that morning. He went to it, knelt, opened it. Pulled out the herbal-book wrapped in his jumper and slid it under his pillow without making a thing of it. Pyjamas. The two sealed letters from his jacket pocket — for Cassia, for his parents — went onto the small bedside table to drop in the common-room mail box on his way down to breakfast. The dahlia in its press he left in the trunk.

Lin glanced up from his book. "*Crumpet in the lorry,* mate. — Sleep well."

Caleb gave him the small *go on* gesture. Got into pyjamas. Got into bed. Drew the curtains halfway. The pillow was cool. The mattress was the same mattress he had slept on for six previous Septembers.

Outside the dormitory window the Highland night was full dark and the stars were properly out.

He closed his eyes.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 1 September 1977 — 22:15

He swung out of bed quietly — bare feet on the cold stone floor — picked up the two sealed letters from the bedside table, and padded down the dormitory staircase in his pyjamas.

The common room was thinner now. A few seventh-years still up by the fire. A fifth-year reading at one of the tables. Mei had not yet come up — *or* had come up and was already in the girls' dormitories. He could not tell from the angle.

The mail box was a small brass-bound wooden chest beside the eagle door, with a slot in the top. *RAVENCLAW HOUSE OUTGOING POST. EMPTIED DAILY.* He dropped both letters in. The small papery sound of them landing on the day's other letters at the bottom.

He went back up. Into bed. Pulled the curtains the rest of the way.

The dormitory was quiet except for the sound of Lin turning a page once.

He closed his eyes again. The herbal under his pillow. The dark outside. The first day of seventh year, properly done.

He slept.

OOC: Committed. Twelve commits flushed for the day. Letters marked in-transit. No replies due yet (Annie's window is 1-2 weeks from 31 Aug → earliest 7 Sept; parents 2 Sept dispatch + ~5 days → ~7 Sept; Cassia 2 Sept dispatch + ~7 days → ~9 Sept).

Now — waking up. First day of seventh year proper.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 07:18

The dormitory was grey with morning. Highland September light, cool and clean, coming in through the high arched window above his bed. The fire had gone out properly in the night and the room was the proper sharp temperature of early autumn at altitude.

Caleb came up out of sleep slowly. The pillow under his cheek had a small hard rectangular shape under it that took him a beat to remember — *the herbal* — and the memory came back of yesterday in proper order: Salford, Bloomsbury, Diagon, the train, Mei, the feast, the common room, the bust. The day itself, when he laid it out, was absurd in its density. He had done a week's worth of living between Tuesday morning and this morning.

Lin was already up. His bed was made. Through the open dormitory door Caleb could hear the proper morning sounds of the tower — water in the bathroom across the landing, somebody on the stairs, the quiet under-thrum of the common room two floors below starting to wake up.

The two other 7th-year boys in the dormitory were stirring. One — a fair-haired boy Caleb knew vaguely as *Greg* — was sitting up rubbing his eyes. The other had pulled his curtains shut and was clearly intending to sleep through breakfast.

Outside the window the grounds were visible in long morning light. The lake gleaming. The path down to the greenhouses. A single owl going somewhere with intent.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 07:21

He swung out of bed, pulled school robes on over his pyjamas — proper dressing could wait — wand in his back pocket, feet shoved into shoes with the laces undone, and went down the dormitory stairs at speed.

The common room was thinly populated. Three early-risers at the tables. The fire being relit by an elf or by magic or by both. Out the eagle door before anyone said anything to him.

He took the moving staircases at the brisk lope of someone who had not yet thought about why he was hurrying. Down through the school. Past the Great Hall — the doors were open, breakfast was being laid, a few early students were already eating — and out through the side door that gave onto the grounds.

The morning hit him properly. Cold. The air had the sharp clean quality of altitude in early September, the dew still on the grass, the lake lying flat and silver-grey in the long light. The path down to the greenhouses was wet and dark with dew, and his shoes left a clear track of darker prints on the path as he ran down it.

The owl he had seen from the window was already nowhere — owls flew faster than he could run, and faster than he could even see them go — but the *direction* the owl had been going was the greenhouse cluster on the slope below the castle, where the morning sun had not yet reached. Six greenhouses in two rows, glass panes opaque with overnight condensation, the doors mostly shut.

The first greenhouse — Greenhouse One, the introductory one for first-years — was quiet. The second the same. At the third he saw movement.

A figure was inside. Tall — properly tall — bent slightly to a workbench, sleeves rolled, a watering can in one hand. The pane of glass was misted over but the silhouette was unmistakable: Professor Sprout, the Herbology mistress, doing whatever she did before breakfast on the first day of term.

The owl was on the roof of Greenhouse Three, on the wrought-iron ridge, preening. A school owl by the look — tawny, large, ordinary. Either delivering, or having delivered, or simply visiting the greenhouse for some owl reason of its own.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 07:24

He pushed the greenhouse door open. The warm wet earth-and-leaf smell hit him — the proper Herbology-greenhouse smell, twenty years of plants and compost and damp glass — and the air inside was a good ten degrees warmer than the air outside.

Professor Sprout looked up from her workbench. Stout, ruddy-cheeked, her hair in its perpetual escaping-bun, a smudge of compost on her cheekbone, sleeves rolled to her elbows over forearms tanned from decades of greenhouse light. She set the watering can down.

"*Caleb Waddell.* — Goodness." She took him in: the robes-over-pyjamas, the undone laces, the slight breathlessness from the run. The corner of her mouth tucked. "Of course I have a moment. The mandrakes are not going anywhere. — What''s on your mind so early on the first day?"

She pulled a stool out from under the bench with one foot — it scraped on the slate floor — and gestured at it.

"Sit. Or stand, if standing suits. Sprouts before breakfast is informal. — Talk to me."

## User

"I was hoping to talk to you about the potential for part time work. as you know, beyond the magical properties in this type of botany, I just enjoy the biology of it all. I was wondering if you could get the school to fund  some hours of the week for a part time assistant... that sounds intense when really I just mean some hours of the week if you could use help with upkeep of anything or had a specific project in mind? something you have wanted to do but haven't had the time for? my only other major interest right now was magical animals but this seemed more likely to be stable."

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 07:26

Sprout listened with her arms folded loosely over her apron, head tilted, the compost-smudge on her cheekbone catching the cool morning light. She let him finish the whole thing without interrupting — even the part about magical animals — and then she nodded once.

"All right. — That''s a serious ask seriously made, on the first morning of seventh year, in pyjamas, before you''ve had your breakfast. I''m going to take it seriously."

She tucked a wisp of hair back into the escaping bun.

"The school doesn''t have a formal *part-time assistant* line for greenhouses. — But. — There is something called the *Herbology grounds-keeping fund,* which Professor Dumbledore lets me draw on for casual student help during the year. It pays a knut per hour for routine work and a sickle per hour for the more skilled jobs. It is not meant to *fund a job* in the usual sense — it is meant to let me say *yes* when a student turns up wanting to spend their weekends doing real work."

She picked the watering can back up.

"I have two projects on the go I have not had time for. One is repotting and recataloguing the Greenhouse Five reserve stock, which is *behind by a year* and not getting any less behind. The other is a propagation project I am running on my own time — Bouncing Bulbs from a Yorkshire variant — that I am keeping in Greenhouse Six and need someone to help me check daily. The first is mindless and useful. The second is interesting and I would not let *most* seventh-years near it. — You I would think about."

A pause. She looked at him properly.

"Tell me first, though — what are your NEWTs? Have you got Herbology among them?"

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 07:27

Sprout's eyebrows went up.

"*Five NEWTs* including Herbology *and* Care of Magical Creatures *and* Alchemy *and* Potions. — Caleb. That is — that is a *biology and pharmacopoeia track,* almost word-for-word." She set the watering can down again, properly this time. "You are doing what I would call a Healer's preparation without the Healing — or a master-herbalist's preparation, or a magizoologist who plans to know what they are putting in their potions. — Yes. *Yes.* If you turn up with that NEWT load and you have got energy left after, I would be very glad of the help."

She looked him up and down once more. Took in the seriousness underneath the pyjamas.

"Here is what I will do. — Come and see me on Sunday morning, properly dressed, after breakfast. I will give you the Greenhouse Five tour and we will see whether the reserve-stock recatalogue is the right starting place. If you are still standing after a Sunday morning, we will talk about the Bouncing Bulbs. — Pay you a knut an hour to start, sickle once you are doing the real work. *Cap of six hours a week,* because you have five NEWTs and they will *eat you alive* if I let you."

A pause.

"And on the magical-animals front. — Talk to Hagrid. He''s the Keeper of Keys and Grounds and he handles the school''s creature population — the thestrals, the owls, the nifflers in the lower paddock, the school cats. He does not have a formal *assistant* role either, but if you turn up at his hut on a Saturday morning with the same energy you brought here, he will find work for you. He is good. — *Different* from me, but good. I expect you''d like him."

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 07:30

He gave her a quick grin and went out of Greenhouse Three the way he'd come in — back into the cold morning, the dew-damp path, the shoes still flapping at the laces.

Hagrid's hut was on the far edge of the grounds, the other side of the lake, at the eaves of the Forbidden Forest. The path round the lake was the long way; the path across the lawn was the short. He took the short, robes flapping behind him, the ridiculous robes-over-pyjamas combination giving him the appearance of a seventh-year on some kind of lost errand.

The hut came up. Round, low, smoke coming from the chimney already — Hagrid was up before most of the school — and the great pumpkin-patch beside it heavy with this year's crop. Fang the boarhound was tied up outside on a long rope, dozing. The big oaken door was shut.

He went up. Knocked. Tried to make the knock the right level — loud enough that Hagrid would hear, soft enough that he wouldn't startle Fang.

A long pause inside, and then the door opened.

Hagrid filled the doorway. Properly filled it — six-foot-eleven, twice as wide as a normal man, the great brown beard, the long brown hair, the small black eyes that were warmer than the rest of him suggested. He was in shirtsleeves and braces and a moleskin waistcoat, holding a cup of tea the size of a small cauldron.

"Mornin'." He took Caleb in. The pyjama-trousers were visible below the robe-hem. The undone laces. "Tha''s — Caleb, isn'' it? Caleb Waddell. Ravenclaw. Seventh-year. — Yer a bit unde''dressed fer breakfast in the Hall, lad. Did yeh get lost?"

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 07:34

Hagrid let out a great bellowing laugh — the kind of laugh that startled Fang properly awake on his rope and then settled him again — and pushed the door wide open.

"*A wizard is never lost.* — Caleb Waddell. Yeh sound like Dumbledore on a tired day. — *Get in,* lad, yer freezin'. There''s tea on."

He stepped back into the one-room hut. The inside was warm and smoky and absolutely full of things — copper pots hanging from the rafters, a great iron kettle on the hearth, a side of bacon hanging in a cloth, a sideboard with several jars of things Caleb did not recognise, an enormous wooden table with two chairs that were Hagrid-sized and one stool that was a normal-person size, evidently kept for visitors. Fang flopped back down outside.

Hagrid pulled the stool out for him with one finger.

"*Care fer Magical Creatures NEWT.* Good. — Yeh know wha''? Tha''s good. Kettleburn''s a fine teacher but he''s seventy-three an'' he''s losin'' fingers — three since I''ve known ''im — an'' he could use a hand on the practicals more than the school''ll ever budget him fer. Yeh tell him yer NEWT-track an'' he''ll have yeh helpin'' him within the fortnight."

He poured Caleb a cup of tea from the great kettle. The cup was the same size as Hagrid''s own — which meant it held about a pint of tea — and Hagrid set it in front of him without commentary.

"As fer me. — *Yes,* I could use help. *No,* the school don''t pay fer it as a job, but the school does pay me a small somethin'' fer student helpers fer specific work, an'' I share it. Sickle a Sat''day fer a half-day. Two sickles if yeh stay through dinner. — But yeh don''t come ter me fer the *coin,* Caleb. Yeh come ter me ''cause yeh want ter learn animals. The coin''s incidental."

He sat down opposite at the great table. The chair creaked.

"*The forest.* — Tha''s a different conversation, an'' I''ll tell yeh now: yeh don''t go in the forest with me on yer firs'' Sat''day. Yeh come ter me ''ere on a Sat''day mornin'', yeh do whatever I''ve got that needs doin'' — feedin'' the thestrals, cleanin'' out the owl droppings on the roof o'' the Owlery, helpin'' with the school nifflers in the lower paddock — an'' yeh prove ter me yer the kind o'' lad who don''t flinch. *Then* we talk about the forest. — Sound fair?"

He took a long pull of his own enormous tea and looked at Caleb over the rim with the small black eyes.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 07:37

Hagrid choked on his tea — laughing — and waved an enormous hand to indicate Caleb should go ahead and try.

Caleb stepped out of the hut. Fang lifted his great head off his paws and considered Caleb with the long-suffering expression of a boarhound being made to engage with morning enthusiasm. The grounds were quiet — early-morning quiet, the lake lying flat, smoke from the chimneys of the castle rising in straight lines up the cold air, the Owlery a small distant tower visible at the eastern corner of the castle.

He cupped his hands round his mouth, faced the Owlery, and bellowed:

"*AAAATHHHHHEEEEENNNNNAAAA!*"

The shout went out across the lawn. Echoed faintly off the castle wall. A cluster of crows in the trees beyond the pumpkin patch took off, croaking. Fang raised his head an inch further and lowered it again, evidently deciding none of it concerned him.

For a moment — nothing.

Then, from the direction of the Owlery, a small dot appeared against the pale grey sky. A small dot getting larger. Fast.

The dot resolved into Athena — small, lopsided, the runt screech-owl with the off-balance left wing that had never bothered her — coming straight in across the lawn at top owl-speed with the look of a small bird who had clearly decided that her boy was finally calling her and she was going to make him work to catch her.

She did the Athena-thing she had done since she was a fledgling: she came in fast, *pulled up* at the last possible moment, and tried to land on his head rather than his shoulder. He caught her on the forearm at the last second — old habit, an arm raised reflexively — and she clipped his ear with her wing on the way past, scolded him with a small cross *kweee,* and dug her talons into his sleeve to balance.

Hagrid had come out of the hut and was watching the whole thing with both eyebrows up.

"*Tha''s* yer owl?"

He came over. Bent down to Athena's level — which was, for Hagrid, a substantial bend — and made a small soft clicking sound at her with his tongue. Athena turned her head ninety degrees and considered him with one yellow eye. The other yellow eye — independently — watched Caleb's face for whether this was a friend or a person to be scolded.

"Found 'er, did yeh?" Hagrid said. "She''s the runt of a screech clutch. Look at the left wing — never quite straightened in the joint. *That* is why she landed crooked, lad, an'' tha''s why she always will. — She''s a beautiful little thing. — *Ahn ahn,* she trusts yeh proper. Tha''s a good owl."

He straightened. Smiled — the proper great Hagrid smile.

"Saturday. Bring her. We''ll set her loose in the lower paddock an'' see if she makes friends with the school owls or scolds ''em all. — Either way, I want ter see her flyin' her own way."

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 07:40

Athena's small head turned the full ninety degrees again to look at Caleb at the *covered ear* — her yellow eye narrowing in the small offended way she did when she felt she was being managed — and then she registered the *sweetheart* and the warmth of his voice and went back to looking pleased with herself.

He extended his arm out toward Hagrid's. Athena considered the offered ramp with the deliberation of a small bird who took her diplomatic visits seriously. She edged sideways down Caleb's forearm with a precise sidestep — one foot, then the other — and made the short hop across to Hagrid's enormous wrist, which she landed on with a small adjustment for the unfamiliar grip surface. Hagrid's wrist was approximately the size of a fence-post; she settled on it and craned her head up to look at him properly.

Hagrid's face did the thing it did with animals — went still and soft, the small black eyes warm — and he said, very quietly:

"*Hullo, sweetheart.*"

He did not move his arm. He did not reach to touch her. He waited. Athena considered him for a long beat. Then she sidestepped two paces along his wrist toward his hand, leaned forward, and very gently nipped the knuckle of his enormous index finger — the small precise *I am evaluating you* nip of an owl whose verdict was being formed.

Hagrid did not flinch. He did not even blink.

"All righ', then," he said, just as quietly. "All righ'."

Athena did the small mantle of her wings that meant *acceptable* and sidestepped back down toward Caleb's arm without further comment.

Hagrid looked at Caleb over her.

"Yer right, lad. — Nobody''s tellin' that one anythin'."

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 07:42

Hagrid nodded. "*Sat''day mornin'.* Eight o''clock. — An'' bring tha'' little ''un with yeh."

Caleb thanked him again, gave Fang a quick scratch behind one enormous ear on the way past — Fang made the small *uhf* sound of a dog accepting service — and walked the path round the lake toward the south shore, Athena settled on his forearm.

The lake at twenty to eight on a September morning was lying still. Slate-grey, faintly steaming where the cold water met the slightly warmer air, the surface marked here and there by a fish coming up after something or by a drifting leaf. The far shore was lost in a thin ground-mist. The mountains beyond were the proper Highland purple-grey. The sun was up but not yet over the eastern hills; the light came indirect, soft, the way September morning light came at altitude.

He found a flat-topped stone near the water's edge and sat. The pyjama-trousers under his robe meant he was warmer than he ought to have been; the cold off the lake was real but he was insulated against it.

Athena, on his forearm, considered the lake for a beat. Then she sidestepped to his shoulder, paused, and launched.

She did the Athena-thing: she lifted off light, banked left, and made a small surveying circle out over the water, low — wingtips almost grazing the surface — and then climbed in a tight spiral to about thirty feet, where she hung on the morning thermals and watched. Her crooked left wing did its compensating thing in the air the way it always did; she flew with a very slight list to starboard that she had been correcting for since fledging.

He let her go. Did not call her. Did not gesture. The point was that she could be a small wild thing for a few minutes without him.

She made three big lazy circles. Then she stooped — not at anything, just for the joy of stooping — and pulled up six feet over the water with a small *kweee* of self-satisfaction. She came back to him in her own time, four minutes later, and landed on the stone beside him rather than on his arm. She walked the small distance to his thigh on her crooked little legs and pressed her body against his hip.

He scratched the back of her head with one knuckle, the way she liked. She closed her eyes.

The lake was very quiet. The mist on the far shore was beginning to lift in tatters.

He sat with her for the rest of the twenty minutes. Did not think about much. Watched the lake.

When the time was up he said, quietly, against her small head: "I have to go to breakfast, sweetheart. — I love you. — I'll see you later. Off you go."

She gave him a long look — yellow eye, the one good eye, the off-balance one squinted as it always was when she was thinking — and lifted off without ceremony. The lazy climbing curve back toward the Owlery, the small lopsided silhouette diminishing across the lake into the morning sky.

He stood. Brushed dew off the back of his robe. Started the walk back up to the castle.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:08

He came up the slope from the lake, shoes still flapping at the laces, the pyjama-trousers visible under the robes if anyone bothered to look. The Entrance Hall was busier now — students coming down from their towers in twos and threes — and the doors of the Great Hall stood open on the warm noise of breakfast.

Inside: the four long tables under the candles, the staff at the high table mostly in already, the air full of bacon and toast and porridge and the small mounting laughter of a school that had had one good night's sleep and was now surfacing.

He stood in the doorway and looked.

The Ravenclaw table. Mei was about a third of the way down, on his side, plait over her shoulder, in proper school robes now, eating porridge slowly with one hand and reading something on the table beside her plate with the other. The space across from her was empty. Lin was further up the table near the seventh-year cluster, talking with two boys Caleb knew vaguely — the *Greg* from the dormitory and another. Davina was at the far end with two fifth-year prefects, doing what looked like coordinating something on a clipboard. Esme was with three Ravenclaw fourth-year girls halfway down — the Ravenclaw friend Caleb did not know, plus two others — and not looking at the door.

Across the hall the Gryffindor table had filled out. Hess Ardwick — the Hufflepuff he had known for years from prefect-coordination meetings as Mei's Hufflepuff opposite-number — was visible at the Hufflepuff table, eating with her usual unhurried calm. At the Slytherin table, fewer faces, but he caught the deliberate not-looking of Cass Selwyn, who was sitting alone at one end with a copy of the *Daily Prophet* open in front of her like a wall.

The high table: Dumbledore at his centre seat, McGonagall to his right, Slughorn already mid-anecdote at somebody, Flitwick on his cushions, Sprout — newly returned from Greenhouse Three by the look of her clean apron — settling in with a plate. Kettleburn was at the far end of the table, the Care of Magical Creatures professor, looking exactly as advertised: very old, very weather-beaten, currently in the process of trying to butter a piece of toast with what looked like a four-and-a-half-fingered hand.

## Narrator

OOC: Quick honest answer.

Per KNOWLEDGE_1, Cass Selwyn is described as a *peer-mate friend with the user despite expectations* — meaning the friendship is established as a feature of the cast file, but whether it's *active* in your specific playthrough hasn't been declared yet. We have not seen Cass on screen with Caleb at any point in our 36 hours of play. She wasn't in the Caleb-Mei-Lin train compartment dynamic; she wasn't at the feast in any committed beat; she's just appeared in the database as a project-scope row, not yet activated for this playthrough.

So the honest answer is: **she could be your friend, but it's not committed yet.** I see her at the Slytherin table because the Hall is full of everyone, and she's a peer-mate cohort character who'd plausibly be there. Whether Caleb has the kind of relationship with her where he'd cross the hall and sit down at the Slytherin table — versus the kind where they nod at each other in corridors and have a couple of in-class moments together — is yours to decide.

Two ways this can go:

1. **Yes, established friend.** Caleb and Cass have been friends since lower years — quiet, cross-house, slightly improbable, the kind of friendship that confuses both their families. Mei knows about it. Cass has been at his table in the library before. He could walk over to her right now and sit down without it being a thing in either direction. (This activates the Cass thread — her Mark/Selwyn-pressure plot becomes available as a depth-track.)

2. **No, just acquaintance.** Caleb knows Cass by name and face, has had a conversation or two over six years, but they aren't friends in any operational sense. He'd nod at her if their eyes met; he wouldn't go sit with her. (Cass stays as a project-scope row, dormant in this playthrough.)

3. **Something in between.** Lower-level friendliness — they've shared a study session in the library once or twice, sat at the same table when groups were forming, nothing more. Could go either direction depending on what you do this year.

Which would you like? I'll commit it as a playthrough fact going forward.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:09

He went straight across the Hall — past the Gryffindor table, past the bench-end of the Hufflepuff table, the pyjama-trousers visible under his robes, the laces still flapping — and pulled out the bench beside Cass at the end of the Slytherin table.

The *Daily Prophet* lowered an inch.

Cass looked at him over the top of it. Pure-blood face, dark hair pulled back severely the way she wore it on first mornings, the small sharp mouth that did most of her communication. She took in the pyjamas, the laces, the dew still on the back of his robe, and her eyebrow went up by a fraction.

"*Caleb Waddell.* — You are at my table. In your nightclothes. — Did you wander."

Around them, the nearer Slytherins — three fifth-years, two sixth-years — registered Caleb at the Slytherin table and registered which Slytherin he had sat down beside, and went back to their breakfast with the deliberate not-looking of students who had decided this was Cass's problem.

She folded the *Prophet* down properly. Set it on the bench between them. The headline visible: *MINISTRY ANNOUNCES NEW DEFENSIVE SPELL TRAINING FOR APPROVED CIVILIAN PERSONNEL.* Below the fold something Caleb could not read at this angle.

"All right." Cass shifted to make room properly. The faintly cynical mouth did the small *fond* version it did when she was being given a friend in spite of her better judgement. "Sit. Eat. Tell me you have a reason."

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:10

He turned his head sharply toward the three fifth-year Slytherins across the table — the ones who had registered him and made a point of looking studiously at their plates — and gave them the wide-eyed *boo* face of a seventh-year who had decided to be exactly as ridiculous as the situation called for.

"*Boo.*"

The middle one — small, dark-haired, holding a piece of toast — flinched properly. The boy on his left snorted into his porridge. The girl on his right rolled her eyes at all three of them and went back to her tea with the dignity of a fifth-year who had decided she was not part of this.

Cass, beside him, did not look up. She picked up her teacup with the small composed Slytherin face she used when she was deciding whether to dignify a thing with engagement.

"*Caleb.*" Mild. "You cannot come to my table in pyjamas and *boo* the children."

A pause. The corner of her mouth did the half-millimetre thing.

"*Effective,* though."

She poured tea into the empty cup at his place — without asking — and pushed the milk jug toward him.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:11

He sidearm-hugged her — the brief one-armed squeeze of a friend who was not going to make a thing of it — and Cass tolerated it with the small sigh of a girl who had been receiving Caleb-Waddell-side-hugs on and off for six years and had decided long ago not to fight them.

"*Lowling.*" She poured herself more tea. "You are a Ravenclaw seventh-year, Caleb. — You are *exactly* the kind of person who comes to my table in pyjamas. The Slytherins are accustomed."

She set the pot down.

"Athena is well? — Good. — *Herbology and animals.* That is — very you. Sprout will love you. Hagrid will *adopt* you. — I would say be careful with Kettleburn, he loses fingers, but you have already worked that out yourself, I imagine."

A pause. The cynical mouth softened a fraction.

"As for me. — *Fine.*"

She said it in the small Slytherin register that meant *not actually fine, but I am going to say I am fine because we are at the breakfast table on the first morning and there are fifth-years in earshot.* Her eyes flicked sideways to Caleb's, briefly, and did the small *I will tell you properly when we are not here* thing.

"Summer was — the usual. My mother wrote often. The house was warm. Ferenc came home for August." She picked up her teacup. "I survived."

She let *Ferenc came home for August* sit on the table between them without elaborating. Drank.

## Narrator

OOC: Honest answer — yes, you would know *of* Ferenc, and probably know him by face from a few specific moments, but not well.

Ferenc finished Hogwarts three years ahead of Caleb and Cass — he was a Slytherin seventh-year when Caleb and Cass were fourth-years. So Caleb would have:

- **Seen him around the school for three years** as an upper-year Slytherin. The kind of older student a fourth-year recognizes by face and surname but doesn't actually talk to.
- **Encountered him by extension of being Cass's friend.** A handful of moments — Ferenc passing through the library when Caleb was at Cass's table, Ferenc collecting Cass at the Hogsmeade carriage line, that sort of thing. Caleb would have been introduced once or twice by Cass herself, and Ferenc would have been *politely cool* — pure-blood-Slytherin-elder-brother register, slightly distant, never quite hostile.
- **Known what the Selwyns are** without being told outright. Six years in a wizarding school teaches you which families are which. Caleb knows Cass's family is on the wrong side of the war in the abstract, watched Ferenc go from upper-year-Slytherin to *post-Hogwarts and gone somewhere unspecified,* and knows the *Prophet* has run a couple of items about the Selwyns over the years. He has not, as of right now in scene, been told that Ferenc is a confirmed Death Eater recruiter — that's KNOWLEDGE_4 content that surfaces at higher DEPTH, almost certainly through Cass herself when she's ready.

So: knows Ferenc enough to recognize the name and place a face, knows he's older and Slytherin and out of school, knows the family is *that family.* Doesn't know the specific recruitment fact — that comes when Cass tells him, or when something else surfaces it.

The line *Ferenc came home for August* lands on Caleb as: *Cass's older brother, who is exactly the sort of older brother Cass has, was at the Selwyn house all month.* He can read between those lines as much as his own knowledge of the family lets him — which is *enough to know that's not nothing,* not *enough to know exactly what it means.*

Continuing whenever.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:12

He pulled the *Daily Prophet* across the bench toward him, leaving it open on the table between them so Cass could still read it from her side.

The headline, top of the front page: **MINISTRY ANNOUNCES NEW DEFENSIVE SPELL TRAINING FOR APPROVED CIVILIAN PERSONNEL**

The lede underneath, in smaller type:

*The Ministry of Magic announced yesterday a new programme of defensive spell instruction available to specified civilian witches and wizards, on application and subject to approval by the Department of Magical Law Enforcement. The programme, which begins on the first of October, will offer instruction in a curriculum of "intermediate defensive charms appropriate to private citizens of standing." Approval criteria, the Ministry said, will be "rigorous." Applications open today.*

Below the fold, smaller items: a piece about a Quidditch league fixture being moved, a notice from Gringotts about updated vault charges, a small column at the foot of the page headed **OBITUARIES.**

The obituaries column had three names. The first two were elderly — a witch in Cornwall, a wizard in Edinburgh — both with the polite *peacefully, at home* phrasing. The third was shorter and read:

*MARGARET BRACKLEY, of Salford. Suddenly. Survived by her daughter. Funeral arrangements private.*

Three lines. No mention of Daniel. No mention of fire. No mention of the unidentified male visitor.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:13

Cass looked at the obituary line where Caleb's finger was still resting beside it.

She did not, immediately, ask him how he knew. She read the three lines properly — the *suddenly*, the *survived by her daughter*, the *funeral arrangements private* — and her face did the small Slytherin-composure she did when she had decided to engage with a thing rather than slide past it.

"*Suddenly* in the *Prophet's* obituary register means *the cause of death is something the family did not want to print.* — Cancer is not *suddenly.* Heart attack is *suddenly.* Murder is *suddenly.* House fire is *suddenly.* — *Suddenly* covers the whole range from natural-but-quick to *we are not telling you what happened.*"

She picked her teacup back up. Did not drink from it.

"What do you know about Margaret Brackley, Caleb."

The question was quiet. The fifth-years across the table were not paying attention. The Hall around them was loud enough to swallow the conversation.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:14

Cass went still.

Properly still — the Slytherin-composure stillness she did when she registered that a conversation had just moved from *small talk* to *something else,* and the fifth-years across the table were now an actively hostile audience even though they were not paying attention. She did not look around. She did not lower her voice further; lowering further would have signalled to anyone watching that the conversation had become important.

She picked up a piece of toast that she had not been planning to eat. Buttered it slowly. Set it down.

"*Caleb.*" Mild. Conversational register, for the room. The eyes were not mild. "I read the *Prophet* in the morning. *House fires* have been a recurring item in it. There were two in July — one in Devon, one in Northumberland — that I noticed because the *Prophet* did not, particularly. — *House fire* was the first guess my brain offered for *suddenly* in a 1977 obituary. — That is what I meant."

A pause.

"It is interesting that *you* found it interesting, though."

She picked the toast up. Took a small bite. Chewed. Did not look at him.

"We will talk." Even quieter, almost not aloud. "Not here. — *Where do you have first lesson.*"

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:14

Cass set the toast down.

She looked at Caleb properly for the first time since he had pointed at the obituary. The Slytherin-composure was still on but underneath it her eyes had gone the kind of sharp she had been doing the *fifty-percent of her attention is elsewhere* version of all morning.

"*Yes.*" Quiet. "That is — yes. — You are right. — I should not have said *house fires* with that confidence."

A pause. She glanced once at the fifth-years across the table; still not listening, still pretending not to be listening.

"What I read in the *Prophet* was — *small items.* Not headlines. Not even properly *articles.* — *Property loss in Devon, family relocated.* — *Northumberland house damaged by accidental magic, no injuries reported.* Three or four lines each, deep in the home-news pages, sandwiched between cattle markets. — I noticed because the phrasing was wrong. *Property loss* and *accidental magic* are not phrases the *Prophet* uses for ordinary house fires. They are phrases the *Prophet* uses when the editorial decision is *do not say what this was.*"

She set her teacup down.

"And I drew an inference, Caleb. — *I am not* — I am not where you are. I am at the *something is wrong* stage, not the *I know what is wrong* stage. — *House fire* was the wrong word. I should have said — I do not know what the right word is. — *Property loss in suspicious circumstances.* *Disappearances dressed as accidents.* — *Something.*"

A pause. She picked up the *Prophet* and folded it closed. Set it on the bench between them, face down.

"You knew about Margaret Brackley before the *Prophet* printed her name. — That is what I am taking from this. — *Where do you have first lesson, Caleb.*"

Cass's face did one specific thing.

She had been doing the *something is wrong* face all morning, which was a Slytherin-composure face under wartime composure under family pressure. Caleb's three slurred syllables landed under all of those layers and her face did the *I have just recognised the actual word for what I have been not-saying* face, which was a different face entirely.

She picked up the *Prophet* again. Folded it once more — into quarters this time, a smaller shape she could cover with her hand — and put her hand flat on top of it.

"Caleb."

Conversational register. The fifth-years across had not heard him. Caleb's slurring had been quiet enough to land just on her side of the bench.

"You are *very specifically* not going to say that word at this table. — Or any table. — Or any common room, classroom, or staircase, between now and the next time we are properly alone."

A pause. The corner of her mouth did the half-millimetre but it was not amused; it was the small Slytherin-grimace that meant *I have been thinking the same word for months and I am not allowed to say it either.*

"*Where is your first lesson.*"

Third time of asking. She was not letting it go.

Cass set her teacup down. She had read the paper before he sat down — she had been reading it as a wall when he came in — and his *mmm* did not require her to look at it again. She did, however, glance at the page where his eyes were and her own eyes went briefly to the obituary column.

Her face did not change. The cynical mouth did not move. But she folded the *Prophet* a fraction more carefully than she had unfolded it, and her tea was steady going to her mouth in a way that meant she was steadying it deliberately.

"*Salford.*" Quiet. "Did you know her?"

She said it the way Cass said real questions — flat, in case the answer was hard to give.

Cass nodded once. Did not push. Took another small sip of tea.

"All right." Quiet. "*Suddenly.*" The word, said in her flat register, was not commentary; it was the small click of a clever person registering a *Prophet* phrasing that meant something other than its surface.

She did not say anything else about it. She picked up the paper, folded it the rest of the way closed, and slid it back across the bench toward him in case he wanted to keep reading.

Across the Hall, at the staff table, Dumbledore was passing a piece of toast to McGonagall in the small domestic way he sometimes did. Slughorn's voice carried briefly — *"...and I said to him, *no,* my boy, you cannot put dittany in a Pepper-Up..."* — and was lost again in the general noise.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:14

Cass's teacup paused on its way down to the saucer.

She set it down. Carefully. Both hands flat on the table for a beat — pure-blood Slytherin composure, the kind a girl learned from being raised in a house where her face was always going to be observed by someone — and then she turned her head a fraction toward him, eyes only.

"*Caleb.*"

Quiet. The faintly cynical mouth had gone serious.

"Tell me you mean *the day after the Manchester papers reported it,* and not — what that sentence sounded like."

She did not look around the Hall. She did not need to. Her own composure was the cover; if anyone glanced over they saw two old friends having tea at the breakfast table and one of them was reading an obituary.

## Narrator

OOC: Cass was reading Caleb's sentence — *I went to her house to see how she had been murdered* — and asking him to clarify what *went to her house* actually meant.

She heard two possible registers in it:

1. **Light register, after the fact:** Caleb walked past the Brackley house in Salford the day after Manchester papers covered it, looked at the boarded-up house from the pavement out of curiosity, registered the damage, and went home. Public news, citizen rubbernecking, no involvement.

2. **Heavier register, closer to the event:** Caleb actually got himself to the scene while it was a scene — talked to police, examined evidence, *investigated* the murder. Which would mean Caleb is doing something more direct than reading the *Prophet* and feeling concerned.

The second is much closer to what Caleb actually did (he went *the morning after,* spoke to DS Hollis at the boarded house, worked out aloud how the door was forced, got himself onto the police's radar with his real name and address). Cass is asking him to tell her it was the lighter version, because the heavier version is — for a 17-year-old wizarding student in 1977 — a serious thing to be doing, and she would have specific reactions to that.

So her line was a clarifying ask, not a fishing one. *Tell me this was the casual version of what you said, and not the version your sentence sounded like.* If the answer is *no, the heavier version,* she will adjust her register accordingly and probably want to talk about it.

Cleanly back to scene. Cass is waiting for Caleb's clarification.

"*You asked the police.*" Quiet. "Caleb. You asked the *Muggle* police, in *Manchester,* about a *Brackley* murder."

She said it not as criticism but as confirmation, the way Cass said the second half of a sentence aloud to make sure she had it right.

Single word. The Cass register. Asking the actual question.

Cass's eyes flicked up to his properly. The composed face stayed but something behind it shifted — registered being met where she was rather than being deflected.

She took a small breath in.

"*Fair.*"

She set the toast down. Both hands flat on the table again.

"What I want from this conversation is — *I want to know how much you know.* That is the first thing. — The second thing is — I want to know whether you came and sat at my table this morning *because we are friends and you wanted to see me,* or *because you are working out who knows what, and I am on the list.*"

A beat.

"You can answer in either order. — I will not be offended by the second."

She picked the teacup up. Did not drink. Held it as something to hold.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:17

Cass set the teacup down properly. The Slytherin composure stayed but it was working harder now — the kind of composure that was a held thing, not a resting state.

She did not, immediately, respond to the *if you are using that and reporting on me, so be it.* She did not respond to *I'm not debating telling you about anything.* She heard them and let them sit.

What she responded to was the last sentence.

"*That just happened and you don't know what to do with it.*" Very quiet. "Caleb."

A breath.

"All right. — I will tell you what to do with it. — *Nothing.* You do nothing with it. — That is the answer." She picked her toast back up; set it back down. "I should not have said it. *Ferenc came home for August* was — a slip. The kind of thing I would have said to you in fourth year and not thought twice about, because my brother was just my brother then and the worst thing he was was *insufferable.* — He is a different thing now. And I should not have given you that piece of information at the breakfast table on the first morning of seventh year while you are sitting in pyjamas reading the obituary of a woman you went to look at the boarded-up house of."

A pause. She looked at him properly.

"I came down to breakfast this morning intending to read the *Prophet* until someone made me stop. — You are the someone. — I am — *glad* it is you, Caleb. But you came over with information of your own, and I came down with information of my own, and we have now both put a small piece of it on the table without meaning to. — *I am going to retract mine.* You can keep yours. — *We are friends.* I am not — *reporting on you.* That is — not a thing that is happening. — But the rest of it is — *please let it be Friday morning.*"

She picked her tea back up. Drank, properly this time, the small unsteady mouthful of a girl who had decided to put the conversation back in its box for the moment.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:18

Cass went very still.

She set the teacup down without drinking from it. The Slytherin composure that had been working hard since *Ferenc came home for August* was now working harder. Her eyes flicked once — small, fast — to the fifth-years across the table, who were three feet away and entirely capable of hearing if Caleb's voice rose, and then back to him.

Her voice dropped to the level she had been using for the *why.*

"*Caleb.*"

A breath.

"You have just said a sentence at the breakfast table that — *if* it had been overheard — would have *ended you.* Not — not in some abstract future way. — Today. By dinner. The *Prophet* would have it by Sunday and you would not be at this school by next week. — *Lower your voice.*"

Another breath. She picked up the teapot — purely for something to do with her hands — and topped up his cup that did not need topping up.

"I — *understand* what you are saying. I am not — *disagreeing* with the analysis. — But you cannot say it. *You cannot say it.* Not at my table, not at Mei's table, not in your common room, not to Lin Wan, not to anyone. The thing you are describing is — *that is what they are recruiting on.* — That sentence is the *first sentence in their sales pitch,* Caleb. — Do you understand what I am telling you."

Her mouth was tight. Her composure was held.

"You are angry. — You are *right* to be angry. — But you are eighteen, you are at school, and you have just told a friend at the breakfast table that you have decided the answer to the problem is *permanent.* — That is not — Caleb. — *That is not a Ravenclaw sentence.* — Please. — *Please.* — Lower your voice. Eat. — We will talk about this somewhere else."

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:19

Cass's mouth tightened.

She did not answer immediately. She picked up her toast — for the third time, finally took a small bite of it, chewed without tasting, swallowed — and used the chewing as a beat to compose what she said next.

"*That.*" Quiet. "*That is the question that gets you the answer you do not want.*"

She set the toast down.

"You know what house. — You have been at this school six years. You have sat in classes with the people whose families talk like that. — *I have sat in those classes longer than you have, Caleb.* I sat next to a boy in second year who — at twelve — said the Muggles who killed his great-uncle in the war should have been *put down like dogs.* He is — *out of school now.* He is — *out doing things.* — That sentence you just said is the *register* he has been speaking in for six years."

A breath.

"The house is Slytherin. — *Yes.* — But it is *not the whole house,* and you know that, and you said the sentence to *make me say the word.* — *Don''t.*"

She looked at him properly. The composure was thinning at the edges now.

"I am sitting at this table in this house *trying* not to be that. — Some of us are. — Do not — make me defend the rest of us at breakfast. — Please."

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:21

Cass listened. The composure stayed up but her eyes did not leave his face. The toast went untouched.

When he was done she did not, immediately, answer. She took the small breath of a Slytherin who was choosing carefully between three or four possible responses and discarding the wrong ones.

"*The difference is wanting to and being forced to.*" Quiet. "Caleb. — That is the sentence every one of them has said to themselves on the way in."

She did not say it as a gotcha. She said it flat. The way a person told another person something true that they did not want to hear.

"I am not — *equating you with them.* I know the difference between you and them. I have known the difference between you and them for six years. — *They* know it too. *That is what they exploit.* — They take a clever angry kind person who has been *pushed* — *and the war is pushing you, Caleb, this morning, in pyjamas at the breakfast table* — and they hand him the *cold hard logic* you just said out loud, and he uses it, and *then* he is one of them, and he does not get to come back."

A breath. She looked down at her plate.

"I am not asking you to *not defend yourself.* — *Defend yourself.* Defend Mei. Defend your mother. — Learn the spells they are not going to teach you in the *approved civilian programme.* Be ready. — But the sentence you said was not *I will defend myself.* The sentence was *the only logical answer is to end them.* — Those are different sentences. — *Hear the difference.*"

She looked back up. The held composure was now visibly tired.

"I am — sorry. I am not — *trying* to lecture you. — But you are the only person at this table I would say any of this to, and you have just put yourself somewhere I have watched my brother go. — Please be careful with the words you pick. Even with me. — *Especially* with me."

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:23

Cass did not look away.

She sat with the question. The composed face did not move, but it had gone — for a moment — from *held* to *honest,* which was a different and rarer Cass-face, and she let it be what it was rather than tucking it away.

"I do not have a solution, Caleb." Quiet. "Nobody does. — That is the *actual* answer. — Not *yet.* Maybe not in time."

She let that sit between them.

"What I have is — *people who are working on it.* — There are people in this castle, and people outside it, who *are* trying to do something more than the *approved civilian programme.* — *Real* people. — Not freelancers. *Not* people deciding the answer is *permanent* over breakfast on the first morning of seventh year because the *Prophet* ran a soft headline. — People who have been at this for years. People who are *organising.* People who *do* know how to defend, and how to find, and how to stop."

A breath.

"I am not — *recruiting* you to them. — I do not have the credentials to. I am not one of them. — But I am telling you they exist, because what you are describing — *me alone, against this, until I am too slow* — is not the only shape the choice has. — There is *another* shape. It involves *not being alone,* and *not deciding by yourself,* and *not over breakfast.* — That is what I have."

She picked up her teacup. Held it again. Did not drink.

"And — Caleb. — *I am not them.* You said it. I believe you. — But I am sitting here at this table because *my* family is the other version of the question you just asked. *Their* version is — *the world is broken, the only logical answer is to clean it.* — And the answer they reach is *also* permanent. — Two people at this table, both reaching *permanent,* from opposite directions. — That is what I am — frightened of."

I'm not out hunting. so as long as that doesn't happen, then there won't be an issue to begin with."

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:25

Cass let the *unbecoming* land. Did not flinch from it.

She nodded, once, slowly.

"*Fair.* — *Smile and take it* is not what I meant. If it sounded like that, I said it badly. I am — not very good at this, the first morning back, before the porridge has gone cold. — I will try again."

A breath. She set the teacup down.

"You are right that *not hunting* is a real difference. — *I take that.* What I was trying to say was — there is a thing that happens to people who decide the answer in the abstract. They prepare for it. They sharpen — *something* — in their head, the part that does the deciding. And the prepared thing makes it easier to use when the moment comes, and *it makes it easier to find moments.* — That is the part I was warning you about. — Not the act. The *getting ready* for the act, alone, on a Friday morning, with no one to check you against."

She looked at her plate. Looked back up.

"You are not alone. — That is the only thing I had to give you that was not *smile and take it.* — *You are not alone.* Mei. Lin. — *Me,* if you want me. — And — what I said about people working on it — I will not bring it up again at this table, but the fact that they exist is not nothing. — I am sorry for the lecture register. — Eat your porridge. — Please."

She picked her toast back up. Took a proper bite this time. Looked tireder than she had looked when he sat down — the cost of the conversation visible at the corners of her mouth — but the held composure had gone soft into something more honest.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:26

Cass — for the first time since he had sat down — *laughed.*

A small one. Single huff, half-surprise, half-relief. The Slytherin composure cracked at the edge in a way that meant she had needed the break and Caleb had given it to her in the register only a six-year friend would have dared.

"*Caleb.*" Mild. The cynical mouth was back, properly, recovered. "You cannot — *bite at me* — at the breakfast table. The fifth-years are already terrified."

She took a sip of tea. Properly this time.

"*Yes,* if you want me. — As a *friend,* you absolute Ravenclaw idiot. — I am — taken. As you well know. As you have known for nine months. — *Don't.*"

But the said-it-warmly register was on, and the toast went into her mouth in proper bites now, and the held thing in her shoulders had let down by an inch.

The fifth-year Slytherin who had been *boo*'d earlier was looking pointedly at his plate.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 2 September 1977 — 08:27

Cass's tea paused halfway to her mouth.

She set the cup down — slowly, with the small Slytherin precision she used when she was deciding whether what had just been said was a joke or a thing to take literally — and looked at Caleb with both eyebrows up properly for the first time that morning.

"*A third.*" Flat. "Caleb. — *No.*"

A pause. The cynical mouth did the small *Caleb-being-Caleb* fold.

"I am — *taken.* I just told you I am taken. — Nine months. — Catch up."

She picked the toast back up. Bit. Chewed.

"And — *by letter.* — Yes. *Please.* — Caleb. — *Please by letter.* I do not need this conversation continued at the Slytherin table tomorrow with a fifth-year listening. — Send me the letter. I will write back. I will send it back saying *no* in seventeen different registers, and you will treasure it as a piece of correspondence, and we will move on with our lives."

But she said *Caleb-being-Caleb* in the register that meant the morning had recovered properly and the friendship was back on its feet, and her shoulders had let down the rest of the way at last.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:28

Cass was in the middle of a sip of tea when the cheek-kiss landed, and she gave him the *Caleb you absolute idiot* sideways look over the rim of the cup without lowering it.

"*Smoking hot relations.*" Flat, after she swallowed. "I am writing the letter, Caleb. — *Seventeen registers.* — I will start it tonight. — *Go.*"

She made the small shooing gesture with two fingers — the affectionate *off you go before you make it worse* gesture — and went back to her toast properly.

He stood. Stepped back over the bench. Gave the fifth-year Slytherin who had been *boo*'d a small friendly nod on the way past — the boy looked relieved — and crossed the Hall toward the Ravenclaw table.

Mei was about a third of the way down on his side of the table, plait over her shoulder, just finishing her porridge. The space across from her was still empty. She had been reading something at her elbow throughout the breakfast — Caleb registered, on approach, that it was the locked herbal — and she looked up when his shadow reached her.

She took in: the pyjama-trousers visible under his robe, the laces still undone, the dew on the back of his robe-hem, the colour in his cheeks from the morning air, and the small fact that she had just watched him cross the Hall from the Slytherin table where Cass Selwyn was now alone with a piece of toast and looking — Mei could see from this angle — *fond and tired.*

Mei put her spoon down. Closed the book on a slip of paper. Both elbows on the table.

"*Caleb Waddell.*"

A beat.

"Sit down. — *Talk.*"

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:29

Mei did not, immediately, react.

She picked up her spoon. Looked at the porridge in her bowl. Looked at Caleb. Set the spoon down.

"*Cass came on to you about the threesome.*" Flat. "Caleb. — Cass Selwyn. — Has been with Marcus Bletchley since *Christmas of last year.* — Who you and I both ran into at the Boxing Day Ravenclaw-alumni thing where she introduced him to you *by name* and he asked after your father''s shop. — *Caleb.*"

She picked the spoon back up. Took a small bite of porridge. Chewed. Swallowed.

"You are an *absolute liar* and I am going to enjoy hearing the actual version of this conversation. — *Sit. Down.*"

The corner of her mouth was doing the half-millimetre thing in spite of itself. She slid the locked book sideways to make room for his plate. Did not, however, take her eyes off him.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:30

He went — back across the Hall at a half-jog, the laces still flapping — leaned over Cass's bench, said *Mei says she's in when you are!* in a stage whisper that was meant to be private but absolutely was not, and was already moving away by the time Cass's face caught up with the sentence.

He saw, over his shoulder, Cass close her eyes for a long beat and put her forehead briefly against her own hand. The fifth-year Slytherin who had been *boo*'d earlier was now staring fixedly at his porridge with the expression of a boy who had heard exactly what had just been said and was going to spend the rest of breakfast pretending he had not.

He came back to the Ravenclaw table, swung over the bench beside Mei, and dropped into the seat with the air of a man returning from a productive errand.

"Consulting work. Okay. — I'm back and present."

Mei looked at him.

She looked at her porridge.

She looked at him again.

She did not, immediately, say anything. The corner of her mouth was doing the half-millimetre thing properly now. She picked up her spoon, took a bite of porridge, chewed it slowly, and swallowed it, and then — without looking at him — said:

"*Caleb.*"

Pause.

"What you just did to that girl. — *At eight thirty in the morning.* — On her *first day back.* — *In her own house.* — *In front of a fifth-year.*"

Pause.

"I love you. — But — *Cass is going to kill you.*"

She took another bite of porridge.

"*Eat.*"

*I take her hand and kiss it* *eat eat*

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:31

He took her hand off the table — the small careful pull of her fingers from where they had been resting on the cloth — and turned it over. Kissed the back of her knuckles. Once. Set the hand down again gently.

Mei did not move her hand back. It stayed where he had set it, on the cloth between them, fingers loose. Her cheeks had gone — for a beat — slightly more colour than her composure liked, and she dealt with this by taking a very deliberate bite of porridge and looking at her bowl.

"*Caleb.*"

The Mei-warmth was on, full, the dry register temporarily not on top.

She reached for the porridge ladle on the table and served him a bowl without comment. Pushed it toward him. Pushed the milk jug. Pushed the small dish of brown sugar.

He ate. The porridge was hot and salty and proper Scottish porridge, which the Hogwarts kitchens did not pretend was anything else. He put brown sugar on it because he was going to. Mei, beside him, ate her own and did not pull her hand away even when the spoon required two hands; she just lifted her free hand briefly and put it back.

Across the Hall, Cass had — Caleb saw on a small glance — finished her toast, picked up her bag, and was leaving the Hall with the upright posture of a Slytherin who had decided to go elsewhere before anyone else came over to her table. The fifth-year who had heard the *threesome* line was visibly going to live with it for the rest of his life.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:33

Mei's spoon paused.

She finished the bite she was on. Set the spoon down. Turned to him on the bench properly — both knees angled toward him now, the small composed Mei-precision — and looked at him with the level expression of a girl who had decided this sentence was not going to be allowed to pass.

"*No.*"

Quiet. Flat. The Mei-register that ended a thing.

"You do not — get to say that. Not at the breakfast table, not as a joke between the *Cass* sentence and the porridge. — *Better than you deserve* is — that is not a thing that is true, Caleb, and *you* saying it out loud to *me* makes it harder for me to disagree without sounding like I am performing."

A breath. She picked up her tea.

"I am here because I want to be. I am — *with you* — because I want to be. — There is no *deserve* on this bench. — *Eat your porridge,* and *do not say that again.*"

She drank her tea. Did not look at him for a moment, and then did, sideways, with the small fond exasperation that meant she had said the serious thing and was now letting it go.

"*Honestly.*"

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:35

Mei nodded once, slowly.

"All right. — That tracks." Quiet. "Cass has — never been the *I am sympathetic to what Ferenc does* version of a Selwyn. She has been the *I am quietly not going to be him* version, since at least fifth year, when she stopped sitting with the Avery girls and started sitting at the end of her own table. — She was worried about *you.* That is — *consistent with her.*"

A breath.

"What did she say. — Roughly. — I do not need verbatim. — Just — *what is the thing she told you that I should know about.*"

She had moved her hand back to his — the small fold of her fingers over his on the bench between them — without making a thing of the gesture. Her eyes did not leave his face.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:36

Mei did not lecture.

She squeezed his hand once. Picked her spoon back up with the other. Looked at her porridge.

"All right." Quiet. "*Ferenc visited and Cass mentioned it solemnly* is — yes. That is information. — *Suddenly* in the obituary is — yes. That is also information. — Cass does not slip on either of those by accident." A small breath. "She gave you something. — She is — *with us,* in some shape. — Not in the circle. But not against it."

She took a bite of porridge. Chewed. Swallowed.

"I will not lecture. — But I am going to say *one* thing about the wand-wave-and-disappear sentence, and then I will let it go, and I will not bring it up again unless you do."

She turned her head toward him. The Mei-register, level.

"You and I are *together* now. — That was not true yesterday morning and it is true today. — *If you become a person who decides things alone in advance,* I lose access to the deciding. *That is what frightens me.* — Not the act. Not the *would I do it if it came to it,* because *I would too,* Caleb. — The *deciding alone, in advance, before there is a moment to decide in.* — *That* is what Cass was saying. I think she was saying it well. — And I am saying it once. — *Don't.*"

Her hand stayed in his.

"That is all. — *Eat.*"

She turned back to her porridge.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:37

Mei's spoon stopped.

She set it down. Both hands flat on the table. She did not, immediately, answer — she took the small Mei-pause that meant she had heard Caleb's question properly and was considering whether to push back or to let him have it.

She let him have it.

"*No.*" Quiet. Flat. "I do not want a council and a vote. — If someone has a wand on me, I want you to *put them on the floor.* — I do not want you to write to my family. I do not want you to consult anyone. I want you to *act.* — *That is not what either of us was saying.*"

A breath.

"*Cass* was talking about the *moment before the moment.* So was I. — The *deciding in advance,* alone, on a Friday morning, that the answer to a problem you have not yet been *in* is *permanent.* — That is the thing. — *In* the moment, when there is a wand on someone you love, *you do whatever it takes.* That is — *not the conversation we were having.*"

She picked her spoon back up.

"You are not — wrong about *attack.* — You are not wrong about *defence.* — You and Cass and I are *all* on the same page about *if it happens.* — The page we are arguing on is *what you do with your head between now and then.* That is — different. — And I think you are conflating them on purpose because you are angry, which is *fair* — but it is also *the conflation that makes the rhetoric work.*"

She took the bite she had been about to take.

"That is — the second sentence I said I would not say. — I lied. — It is now the *only* sentence on the matter. — *Eat.*"
