## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:38

Mei left her hand where it was. Ate her porridge with her free hand. The Hall ran on around them — the bench filling out further down their table with younger Ravenclaws coming down late, the Slytherin table where Cass had been now with the fifth-years filling in the vacated end, the Hufflepuff table laughing about something at the far side, Slughorn telling another anecdote at the staff table.

The mail came in.

The morning post owls came through the high windows in their proper great rush — a hundred, two hundred birds streaming in across the Hall, dropping letters and parcels onto students' plates, banking back out the same windows. Athena was not among them, which made sense — she had been at the Owlery and had then come to Caleb at the lake; she would not be working the morning post round today.

But the morning post brought one thing for Caleb. A folded copy of the *Manchester Evening News* — the next day's, *Thursday's,* which had not been on the platform yesterday morning when his father had handed him the page. It landed in front of his porridge bowl with a small slap. No accompanying note. The paper was folded to page four. The same page his father had folded yesterday's to.

The headline this time, slightly larger, slightly further down the page:

**SECOND DEATH IN SALFORD HOUSE FIRE — POLICE NOW LINKING TWO INCIDENTS**

## User

*read it with Mei*

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:39

He slid the paper across so Mei could read with him. She set her spoon down properly and bent her head to it, her plait sliding off her shoulder onto the page.

The article ran below the headline in plain *Manchester Evening News* prose:

*Police investigating the death of Mrs Margaret Brackley at her Liverpool Street, Salford home on Tuesday have today confirmed a second fatality at the property, a man whose identity has not yet been released pending notification of family. Detective Sergeant Adam Hollis of Salford CID told this paper yesterday afternoon that the two deaths are now being treated as connected and that an investigation is "active and ongoing." He declined to comment on whether Mr Daniel Brackley, the deceased woman's husband and the property's joint owner, is being sought, saying only that "we want to speak to Mr Brackley as a matter of urgency." A small fire damage report at the property is being treated as incidental and not the cause of either death. — Anyone with information is asked to contact Salford CID on the number listed below.*

A small column at the side carried a black-and-white photograph: Liverpool Street, the boarded house, the same house Caleb had stood opposite on Wednesday morning. The boards were still up. A single uniformed officer was visible at the far end of the street.

Below the article, his father had drawn — in pencil, very small, in the bottom margin of the page — a single short note:

*C — they are not pretending it''s a fire. proud of you for going. don''t go again. write your mum. — Dad*

Mei had finished reading a beat after Caleb. She did not, immediately, look up. Her eyes were on the photograph.

"*Damage report incidental.*" Quiet. "*Not the cause of either death.* — Caleb. They are saying it on the record now."

She did look up. The morning Mei-warmth was still there but underneath it the clever-eye was on properly.

"Your dad is — *good.* — *Don''t go again. Write your mum.* — Both of those are right."

## User

[[is any of this supposed to be new information?]]

## Narrator

OOC: Honest answer — mostly no, but two pieces are new.

**Already known:**
- Margaret Brackley dead, Daniel Brackley missing — known from the *Manchester Evening News* page Caleb's dad gave him on the platform Wednesday morning.
- A second male body at the house, identity withheld — known from the same conversation; Caleb worked out at the scene with DS Hollis that the second body had identifying marks Daniel didn't have, which is how Hollis informally confirmed it wasn't Daniel.
- Police want to speak to Daniel Brackley — implicit from the Wednesday conversation; now public on the record.

**New:**
1. **The fire was incidental, not the cause of death.** This is genuinely new on the public record. Yesterday's coverage was treating it as a *house fire.* Today's *Evening News* is saying *a small fire damage report at the property is being treated as incidental and not the cause of either death* — meaning the police have publicly admitted the fire was cover, and the deaths were caused by something else. That's a real escalation in what the *Muggle press* is being told to print.
2. **They are now linking the two deaths.** Yesterday's reporting treated Margaret's death as the story and the second man as an unidentified additional body. Today is *connecting* them in print.

So the article is mostly confirmation of things Caleb already worked out at the scene Wednesday morning — but the **public record** has now caught up to what Caleb knew privately, which is itself information about how the case is being handled. Hollis is *not* burying it; he's letting the *Evening News* print *connected* and *not the cause of either death,* which means he's choosing to be honest with the press despite whatever pressure is or isn't on him.

The *real* new thing is what the article *doesn't* say: no mention of the wizarding angle, no mention of *suspect,* no mention of the Brackley family connections, no naming of the second man. The Muggle police are being honest about what they can be honest about, but the wizarding side of the case is still being kept off the page entirely.

Plus the marginal note from Caleb's dad — *proud of you for going, don't go again, write your mum* — which is real new content from his father.

Cleanly back to scene. Caleb and Mei have just finished reading.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:41

Mei refolded the *Evening News* and slid it back to him. She picked her spoon up. Took one more bite of porridge — finishing the bowl — and chewed it slowly, looking past him at nothing in particular while she thought.

"Today." She set the spoon down. "Charms first thing — Flitwick wants the seventh-years for his welcome-back lecture, which is mostly the timetable and whether we are going for NEWT-O or NEWT-E. — Then Potions before lunch, Slughorn, who will talk for an hour about who got Outstanding in their OWLs and pretend he is talking about something else. — Lunch. Library afternoon — I want to start the next thirty pages of the herbal before I lose the thread of the first thirty, and I want to write a *very brief* letter to my father about your father''s proposal so that *my* father at least knows the proposal exists when *your* father''s letter arrives. — Prefects'' meeting at six. Dinner. — *I am free between four and six, and after dinner.*"

A pause. The corner of her mouth did the thing.

"Why. — Have you got a plan, Mr Waddell."

## User

[[I just assumed today would be different. you brought us to school on a Thursday evening to go to sleep and wake up for a Friday. so is that one day of classes? or do they start Monday??]]

## Narrator

OOC: Honest answer — canonically Hogwarts term begins with classes the day after the Welcoming Feast, regardless of what day of the week 1 September falls on. The Welcoming Feast is the *evening of* arrival; classes begin the *following morning.* So 1 Sept 1977 was Thursday → arrival → feast → bed; 2 Sept 1977 is Friday → first day of classes, full timetable.

It feels strange because Friday-as-first-day-of-school is not a thing in Muggle Britain (or anywhere), but Hogwarts runs on its own calendar and the feast-then-classes structure doesn't shift by weekday. So yes — Mei has a real day of classes today, the Saturday and Sunday that follow are the first weekend of term (Saturday is when Caleb goes to Hagrid's at 8am with Athena; Sunday is Sprout's Greenhouse Five tour after breakfast).

So the day is real. Mei has Charms and Potions. Caleb presumably has a similar timetable; we haven't laid out his exact NEWT timetable but his five subjects (Care of Magical Creatures, Alchemy, Potions, Herbology, DADA) will have their first lectures spread across Friday + Monday onward. Likely he and Mei share Potions (both 7th-year NEWT cohort) and possibly DADA.

Continuing whenever — Mei has just asked if Caleb has a plan for the 4-6 window or the after-dinner window.

## Narrator

OOC: Yes, good idea — let me think it through and propose. Schedule is information that everyone (including me) benefits from having locked.

**Caleb's NEWT subjects (5):** Care of Magical Creatures, Alchemy, Potions, Herbology, Defence Against the Dark Arts.

**The 7th-year cohort I'm tracking:**
- **Mei** (Ravenclaw, prefect) — we haven't named her NEWTs yet. I'd suggest Potions, Herbology, Charms, Arithmancy, Ancient Runes — a research/scholarly track that fits her register. She'd share **Potions and Herbology** with Caleb.
- **Lin** (Ravenclaw, Beater) — Healing track per his profile: Potions, Herbology, Charms, Transfiguration, DADA. Shares **Potions, Herbology, DADA** with Caleb.
- **Davina** (Ravenclaw, prefect) — Mei's prefect counterpart. Suggest: Charms, Transfiguration, Arithmancy, Ancient Runes, Defence — a Ministry-track. Shares **DADA** with Caleb.
- **Cass** (Slytherin) — not committed. Suggest: Potions, Charms, Transfiguration, DADA, Arithmancy — pure-blood family standard. Shares **Potions, DADA** with Caleb.
- **Toby Greaves** (Gryffindor, Beater) — DADA-strong per profile. Suggest: DADA, Charms, Transfiguration, Care of Magical Creatures, Herbology. Shares **DADA, Care, Herbology** with Caleb.
- **Rory Macnair** — house-flexible per profile. Whatever subjects you'd like.
- **Owen Reeve** — house-flexible per profile. Same.
- **Liri Penrose** (Ravenclaw, Muggle-born observer) — Charms or Arithmancy track.

**Standard Hogwarts timetable structure (canon):**
- Five class days, Mon-Fri
- Two periods morning (9-10:30, 10:45-12:15), lunch, two periods afternoon (1:15-2:45, 3-4:30)
- Some doubles (90 min straight)
- Astronomy is Wednesday at midnight (separate)
- Classes meet 2-3 times per week each

**Proposed timetable for Caleb:**

| | Mon | Tue | Wed | Thu | Fri |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 9:00-10:30 | DADA | Potions | Herbology | DADA | **Charms-equivalent (free)** |
| 10:45-12:15 | Alchemy | Care of MC | Potions | Alchemy | Potions |
| LUNCH | | | | | |
| 1:15-2:45 | Herbology | Free | Care of MC | Free | DADA |
| 3:00-4:30 | Free | Herbology | Alchemy | Care of MC | Free |

That's: Potions ×3, Herbology ×3, DADA ×3, Care of MC ×3, Alchemy ×3 — fifteen periods, normal NEWT load. Free periods Mon afternoon, Tue 1:15, Thu 1:15, Fri 9 and 3 (the schedule canonically gives 7th-years free periods because they're not taking the full ~9 subjects of lower years).

**Friday 2 Sept (today) for Caleb:** free period 9-10:30 (he's having breakfast), Potions 10:45-12:15 with Slughorn (matches Mei!), lunch, DADA 1:15-2:45 with Henshaw, free 3-4:30.

That gives the 4-6pm window Mei mentioned as genuinely free for him too (since his classes end at 2:45 today), so they could meet up before her prefects' meeting.

**Two questions for you:**

1. **Does this schedule shape work?** I can adjust — different subjects in different slots, different free-period arrangements. The constraint is just *5 subjects × 3 periods/week + free periods.*

2. **Do you want me to nail down the other 7th-years' NEWTs?** If yes, the suggestions above are starting points; you can adjust. If not, I'll only commit them when they appear in scenes.

If you say *yes go,* I'll commit the schedule plus Mei's NEWTs to the database so it's stable for the rest of the campaign.

- **Care of Magical Creatures** (Kettleburn): Toby, Owen, Hess + Caleb
- **Alchemy**: Liri + Caleb (small class)

OOC: Locked. Two notable consequences worth flagging:

1. **Alchemy is just Caleb and Liri.** Two NEWT students in a small, quiet class. That's an unusually intimate setting for someone Caleb has known for years but doesn't have particular history with — and given Liri's role per KNOWLEDGE_1, that proximity is going to matter. Three times a week, a small classroom, two students.

2. **Caleb's Friday today:** he has a free period right now (9-10:30, while breakfast is winding down), then **Potions with Slughorn at 10:45** alongside Mei, Lin, Cass, Rory, Owen, and Hess — six of his cohort plus him in one room, on the first day. Then lunch. Then **DADA at 1:15** with Henshaw. Then free 3-4:30. So Mei's *4-6 free* window matches Caleb's afternoon free perfectly.

Cleanly back to scene — Caleb just asked Mei what she's doing today and she answered with her timetable + free 4-6 + after dinner.

She picked her spoon up. Took the last bite of her porridge. Set the spoon down properly in the empty bowl.

"Four o''clock. The alcove. — *Bring your wand* and the *Lumos* you used last night, because I want to see what you saw before I look at it with my own. — And — Caleb. — *That is the third date you have asked me on in eighteen hours.* Coffee at the Hog''s Hood, Cirencester, and now Rowena''s diadem at four. — I am, for the record, going on all three."

The corner of her mouth was doing the half-millimetre thing properly now.

She glanced past him at the staff table — Slughorn was making the small *we are about to begin* gesture to his end of the room — and reached for her satchel.

"Welcome-back lecture in twenty minutes. — Walk me to Charms?"

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:44

Mei's hand folded into his without comment as they stepped out of the Hall. Her satchel over her opposite shoulder, locked book inside.

"*Up in the girls' area.*" Mild. "Caleb. — *No.* — There is no *certain time.* The staircase charm is on twenty-four hours a day for the duration of the school year. Hogwarts has had a thousand years to work out which of the founders'' provisions are relaxed and which are not, and *that one* is not. — Davina was not joking last night."

She squeezed his hand once. Released it briefly to wave at a fourth-year going past in the other direction, took it back.

"The boys'' staircase has *no* such charm in either direction. — *I* can come up to your dormitory whenever I like. — Which is — historically — *not balanced,* and which is *also* not changing in our lifetimes. — *Sorry.*"

The corridor ran on around them, students streaming both ways toward first-period classes. The Charms classroom was on the third floor; they had a few minutes.

Mei stopped at the foot of the staircase. Looked at her pocket-watch — small, silver, on a chain in her robe pocket — and then at him.

The Mei-pause. Reassessing.

"*Fifteen minutes.*" Quiet. The corner of her mouth did the thing properly. "All right. — *Brief.*"

She did not lead him anywhere — she let him pick. The corridor at the foot of the third-floor staircase had a small recess two doors down, behind a tapestry that had been there for as long as either of them had been at Hogwarts, where the wall was set back about three feet and the tapestry made a soft curtain across the front of it. Standard Hogwarts kissing-recess. Every prefect knew about it. Mei had walked past it for six years without using it once.

She glanced at the recess. Glanced at him.

"*That one.* — *Five minutes.*"

## User

"7" *I go with her*

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 08:46

Mei rolled her eyes — fond, brief — and let him lead her the two doors down to the tapestry.

The recess was as it had been for six years. The tapestry was a hunting scene that had gone faded with age, the edges fraying, the figures on it long since unable to be made out properly. The stone behind it was cool. The space inside was dim and quiet and smelled faintly of dust and old wool.

He stepped in first. Held the tapestry up. Mei followed, ducking slightly under his arm. The tapestry fell back into place behind her, and the corridor noise dropped to a muffled background.

She set her satchel down at her feet. Looked up at him in the dim. The morning had been a lot — Cass, the *Evening News,* the porridge, the timetable — and the last twenty minutes had been an emotional running gauntlet.

He could see all of it on her face.

He cupped her jaw with both hands. Took the seven minutes properly.

The first kiss was soft and slow and reset something. The second was hers — she pressed up onto the balls of her feet and found his mouth properly, and her hand came up to the back of his neck, and the kiss was *Mei is here* in the way the train compartment had been *Mei is here.* Her plait slid forward over her shoulder; he did not catch it this time, just let it fall against his arm. The third kiss was long and unhurried and ended with her forehead against his with both their breaths coming a fraction faster than ordinary corridor breath.

After a beat she said, very quiet:

"*Charms.*"

But she did not move.

He reached past her, found her satchel by feel, and lifted it onto her shoulder. Kissed her once more on the corner of the mouth. Held the tapestry up.

She stepped out. Adjusted her robe. Smoothed her plait. Took a small breath in.

The corridor was unchanged. A second-year went past with a stack of books. The tapestry settled back into its wall. They walked the rest of the way to the Charms staircase with her hand in his, two minutes early.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 10:43

The dungeon was the proper Slughorn dungeon — long, low-ceilinged, great cauldrons on the worktables, ingredient jars along the walls catching the lamplight. A handful of seventh-year NEWT students stood around in clusters waiting for the door to open. Mei was already there — she had come down from Charms — and Lin beside her. Cass had come in the other way and was at one of the worktables with Rory Macnair beside her, currently saying something quiet that made Cass snort. Hess Ardwick was at the next worktable, plait neat, talking to Owen Reeve.

Owen looked up when Caleb came in. The small genuine smile, the lifted hand in greeting — easy, friendly, the way he had been since they had been twelve and shared a Herbology bench in second year, when he had quietly handed Caleb the right pair of dragon-hide gloves before Sprout could reprimand him for picking up the wrong ones. Owen had been steady-presence ever since. The kind of friend Caleb did not see every day but always saw on the days that mattered.

## Narrator

OOC: Let me re-look at the room with seating in mind.

The dungeon has — call it six worktables, two students per table is the Slughorn standard for NEWT Potions (you brew in pairs).

Configuration as of Caleb walking in:

- **Cass + Rory** at one table — already paired.
- **Hess + Owen** at the next — already paired.
- **Mei + Lin** standing together — they haven't claimed a table yet, but they're a likely pair if they sit together.

That leaves three or four worktables empty plus whoever else turns up (the class is 7 students with Caleb included, so we have an odd number — three pairs + one trio, or three pairs + Caleb solo at the seventh seat).

So the open options are:

1. **Sit with Mei** — claim a table together. The obvious choice. Lin probably partners with someone else (likely Owen or one of the others — Lin is house-mate to Mei and Caleb, but with Mei taken he'd float to whoever's free).
2. **Sit with Lin** — bro-partner, leave Mei to find another partner. She'd probably end up with whoever Lin would've, which is fine but a slightly weird choice on a first day where you and your girlfriend just got together.
3. **Sit alone at a table for now** — claim one and see who comes in late and pairs with you.
4. **Approach Cass and Rory's table** — already paired, can't easily join.
5. **Approach Hess and Owen's table** — same.

The realistic Caleb-and-Mei move is #1 unless you specifically want different.

What does Caleb do?

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 10:44

He set both phials on the corner of their worktable.

The Wiggenweld first — bright clean green, the small label in Cassia's looping script: *Wiggenweld — 30 Aug 77 — C.B.* — and beside it the Bouncing Bulb syrup, translucent jade in its corked glass, currently doing its small periodic hop against the inside of the phial — a soft up-and-down every three seconds or so, springy and intentional.

Mei glanced at the two phials. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth.

"*Show-and-tell.*" Quiet. "Caleb. — On the *first day.* — You have brought *two.*"

She did not, however, tell him to put them away.

The dungeon door banged. Slughorn came in in his proper start-of-term green velvet, the smell of him preceding him by three feet — pipe tobacco, crystallised pineapple, the very faint top-note of something brewed. He scanned the room as he walked to the front, registering students by face. His eyes passed over Mei and on, passed over Caleb, registered the *two phials* on the worktable, and stopped properly — both eyebrows up.

The Wiggenweld he registered first; the Bouncing Bulb syrup he registered a beat after, and his whole face changed in the small Slughorn way that meant he had just been handed something more interesting than he had expected on the first morning of seventh year.

He went to the chalkboard with the sweep of a man who had decided to deliver his welcome-back lecture more enthusiastically than usual, and turned.

"*Seventh-year NEWT Potions, my dears. Welcome back.* — *I am very pleased to see you.* — Now —"

He glanced at Caleb's worktable once more on the way to picking up his chalk, and his mouth had a small private pleased curve.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 10:45

Slughorn turned to the room with the chalk in one hand and the demonstration ladle in the other, and beamed.

"*Welcome to NEWT Potions.* — Six of you have made it this far, and if memory serves, there were *eleven* of you in fifth-year Slughorn lectures, and the four who decided not to take the NEWT have, I am sure, *gone on to perfectly respectable lives* doing things that do not involve them setting their eyebrows on fire. — *We do not miss them.*"

A small ripple of laughter from the front row. Hess Ardwick smiled into her notebook.

"*This year* you will be brewing four Outstanding-grade potions and submitting one of them as your independent project. The rest of the time you will be assisting one another, *making mistakes,* and *cleaning up after yourselves,* which is a skill the Ministry pretends to value but in fact does not. — *I* value it. — *Clean up after yourselves.*"

He set the ladle down. The chalk went up to the board and he wrote, in his swooping hand:

*NEWT POTIONS 1977-78 — INDEPENDENT PROJECTS — DUE BY EASTER*

He turned back to the room. His eyes went to Caleb's worktable. The Wiggenweld phial. The hopping syrup beside it.

"And — *Mr Waddell.*"

The Slughorn-pleased eyebrow.

"You have brought your homework. — *On the first day.* — *Before I have even said good morning to you.* — Tell me what they are, dear boy. Stand up, hold them up, talk us through. *The class will benefit.*"

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 10:46

He stood. Picked up the Wiggenweld first.

"This one isn't mine, sir. It's a reference phial — Cassia Burbage at Slug & Jiggers brewed it for me on Wednesday evening so I could see what the finished thing was supposed to taste and smell and look like. — She told me I could keep it for working from."

He held the Bouncing Bulb syrup up beside it. The little hop, gentle, against the inside of the glass.

"This one is mine. I made it on Wednesday night in the kitchen of the bed-and-breakfast I was staying at in Bloomsbury — Cassia gave me the bulbs and the recipe and told me you'd think it was funny if I brought you a phial. — Pin the sprouts with the flat of the knife so they don't jump while you cut, chop fine, steep ten minutes in warm sugar-water. — I do not know if it has any effect if you drink it. I have not been brave enough to try."

He set them both back down.

"The independent project I had in mind, sir — provisionally — is to see whether alcohol works as the solvent in a healing potion the way water does. Whether you can make a working Wiggenweld with the alcohol carrying the bind instead of the water. — It would, conveniently, also have mint in it, because the alcohol in question is a bourbon julep — Cassia and I have provisionally named it *Caleb's Folly* until I can prove it heals, at which point we will argue about the rest of the name."

A pause.

"I tested a drop of finished Wiggenweld in a julep at the Leaky on Wednesday night. The healing component appeared to be neutralised on contact — the alcohol bound the moondew before it could do its work. So the answer is *not* finished-Wiggenweld-plus-julep. The next experiment is to brew the second half of a Wiggenweld in a non-alcoholic carrier and join it to the alcohol after the bind. — Which I have not done yet."

He sat down.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 10:48

The dungeon was very quiet for a beat.

Slughorn's mouth had been hanging open by a fraction since *neutralised on contact.* His chalk had stopped moving. The eyebrow that had gone up at the phials had not come back down.

"*Caleb Waddell.*"

He set the chalk down on the demonstration cauldron's lip. Came round the front of his desk. Crossed half the dungeon to Caleb's worktable in the slow appreciative walk of a man who had been expecting to give a welcome-back lecture and had just been handed something else to do entirely.

He picked up the Bouncing Bulb syrup phial. Held it to the lamp. Tapped it once with a fingernail. The syrup hopped enthusiastically. He smiled.

"*Bouncing Bulb syrup* on the *first day* of NEWT Potions, brewed in a *Bloomsbury* kitchen, on the kitchen *gas hob,* by a seventh-year who had the *Cassia Burbage recipe* in his head — *clean colour,* *no scum,* *good viscosity.* — Excellent."

He set the phial back down. Picked up the Wiggenweld. Considered it.

"And — your *Folly.*" Quiet, the Slughorn-pleased register. "*Mr Waddell,* you have just told this class — on the *first day* — that you intend to spend the year trying to brew a *bourbon-based Wiggenweld variant* and that you have *already done a control experiment at the Leaky Cauldron.* — *And* identified the *moondew bind problem.* — *And* proposed a *staged carrier* as the next step."

He set the Wiggenweld down beside the syrup.

"*Yes.* — *Provisionally yes,* on the project. — I will want a written proposal by *the end of next week,* with proper experimental controls, an accurate cost ledger for ingredients, and a *witness* — Miss Chang or Miss Burbage or *me,* if Miss Burbage is too distant — for any tasting trial of any version of *Caleb''s Folly* until we know what it *does.* — Because *Mr Waddell,* — *bourbon-bound moondew* could do *several* things, and not all of them are *heal a sprained wrist.* — *Are we agreed.*"

He held Caleb's eye properly. The pleased register was still on, but underneath it the actual Potions Master was on too — the man who had been teaching this for thirty-odd years and had seen students kill themselves with stupider experiments than this.

## User

"Yes, but to clarify. The intention is the observation of alohol as the solvent rather than water. the bourbon or rum is incidental. that is for flavor or variety, but the alteration to the effects is the intent and purpose."

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 10:51

Slughorn's eyebrows went up properly this time.

"*Alcohol as the solvent rather than water.*" He repeated it slowly. The pleased register shifted into something more interested still. "*Mr Waddell.* — That is — *not the project I thought you had just pitched.* — That is a *substantially* more ambitious one."

He picked up his chalk again. Tapped it against the demonstration cauldron's rim.

"Water is the *unmarked default* in every potion brewed in this castle. Every recipe in your textbook *assumes* water. Every binding behaviour, every reaction kinetic, every *temperature* is calibrated to water. — You are proposing to swap it out. — *That is not a tweak. That is a foundational change.*"

He set the chalk down again.

"The *flavour* of bourbon-versus-rum-versus-anything-else is incidental to your hypothesis. *Yes.* — Understood. — But — Mr Waddell — *every* alcohol is a different solvent. Bourbon is not rum is not gin is not vodka is not absinthe. They have different proofs, different congeners, different polarities. — If you are testing *alcohol-as-solvent,* you are not testing one thing. You are testing *a class of solvents,* and you will need to be very disciplined about which one you are working with at any given moment, *because the experiment cannot tell you which result came from which property of which spirit unless you control for it.*"

A pause. He looked at Caleb properly.

"This is a *master''s-track* question, Caleb. — Not a NEWT-project question. — *I am not telling you not to try it.* I am telling you the proposal you bring me at the end of next week needs to *acknowledge* that you are doing master''s-track work in a NEWT classroom, and *narrow* it accordingly. Pick *one* spirit. *One.* For the year. — Establish whether *any* alcohol can carry *any* potion bind. — *Then,* Easter and beyond, you broaden. — Are *we* agreed on that revised version."

He let the question sit with the appropriate respect for the answer.

## User

"Yes, sir. Ethanol. Pure. Clean. Still will write it."

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 10:52

Slughorn nodded slowly. The pleased register came back properly.

"*Ethanol. Pure.*" He said it with the small approving Slughorn note that meant *that is the right answer.* "*Sensible.* — A controlled solvent with a known proof, no congeners to muddy the data, available at a chemist's-supply or by Vanishing-extraction from any reasonable spirit. — *Yes.* — That is a *NEWT-feasible* version of the question, Mr Waddell. *Well-narrowed.*"

He picked his chalk back up.

"Proposal end of next week. *Witten.* I will tell you in advance — I will want to see your *control protocol* most of all. The hypothesis is *can ethanol carry a potion bind* and the experiment that answers it is going to involve brewing the *same potion* twice — once with water, once with ethanol — and comparing. — Pick a *small* potion to start with. *Not* Wiggenweld — too many components, too many bindings, you will not be able to tell what failed. Pick something with *two or three* ingredients. — Cure for Boils, perhaps. *Three* ingredients. Easy to brew. Easy to test."

He patted the corner of Caleb's worktable once with the flat of his hand.

"*Excellent.* — Sit down, Mr Waddell. The rest of you — back to *me* — and the *welcome-back lecture* I had prepared, which I suspect will now feel quite pedestrian by comparison. *Proper* class begins on Wednesday with the first NEWT brew, which is the *Draught of Peace,* a potion *none of you* will get right on the first try and I shall enjoy *all of it.*"

He went back to the chalkboard. The class settled.

Mei, beside Caleb, did not look at him directly — she was facing the front, the proper attentive Mei-prefect-front-facing — but under the worktable her hand found his knee for a beat and squeezed once. Approving.

Behind them, Lin made a small *nice work, mate* sound under his breath.

Across the dungeon at the third-row right table, Cass had been watching the whole exchange with her chin on her hand. The faintly cynical mouth was — for the moment — fully on the *fond* setting.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 10:54

Caleb made the gesture openly across the desk — *me-Mei-Cass-me-Mei-Cass*, eyebrows up, the inquiring head-tilt — directed across the dungeon at Cass, with Mei beside him watching it happen.

Mei's prefect-facing-front composure broke immediately. She did not even try. She put her hand over her face for a beat and her shoulders shook properly with a contained laugh.

"*Caleb.*"

She said it from behind her hand. Did not lower the hand for another beat. When she did, the dry composure was already trying to reassemble itself, and she shook her head once at him with the small *you are an absolute idiot, do continue* register that meant she was, in fact, watching Cass's face for the response with interest.

Across the dungeon, Cass had clocked the full gesture from the start and was watching it complete with her chin on her hand. The faintly cynical mouth had gone serious in the precise Slytherin way that meant she was about to make a decision visible across thirty feet of dungeon.

She held Caleb's eye. Held it. Held it.

Then — slowly, very precisely — she shook her head. *Once.* Mouthed the single syllable: *No.*

A beat.

Then, with the same precision, she lifted her right hand to her mouth, kissed the tips of two fingers, and pointed the kiss across the dungeon — not at Caleb. At *Mei.*

Mei caught it. Made a small noise — half-laugh, half-something-else — and gave Cass a tiny dry nod across the dungeon that was *I see you, thank you, no.*

Slughorn was still at the chalkboard, mid-sentence about NEWT-O grading, and had not noticed any of it.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 10:55

He reached across the worktable and took Mei's hand. Laced his fingers through hers. Set their joined hands on the bench between them.

Mei did not pull away. She looked at the joined hands for a beat, then at him, with the soft Mei-warmth that had been there in the tapestry alcove an hour earlier and was not, she had decided, going to leave her face for the rest of Potions. The corner of her mouth was doing the thing.

She did not say anything. She just held his hand and turned, properly, to face Slughorn at the front of the room with the prefect attention she had been holding onto until thirty seconds ago.

Caleb did the same. Eyes front. Hand in hers under the bench between them.

Slughorn was at the chalkboard, drawing — without looking — the same graph he drew every year about NEWT-O vs NEWT-E grading distributions, a curve of expected outcomes against effort invested, with the Slughorn-asterisk in the bottom-left for *Outstanding work, occasional, not predictable.* He was talking through it with the easy fluency of a man who had given the lecture thirty-six times.

"*Now.* — As I was saying before Mr Waddell distracted us all *very satisfactorily.* — A NEWT in Potions runs O-E-A-P-D, and the marker that comes down from the Ministry will tell you ..."

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 11:18

The lecture ran on. Slughorn worked through the NEWT-O graph, the year's structure, the four-Outstanding-grade-potions plus the independent project, the cleanup expectations, and the rules about brewing outside class hours (forbidden in the dormitories, allowed in the Potions classroom on Saturday afternoons under his supervision *with* a written request *in advance,* not on the day, *do not turn up at his door at noon on a Saturday with an idea, Mr Waddell, you specifically*).

A small ripple of laughter at that, the warm kind. Caleb gave Slughorn a small acknowledging nod from his bench.

The students took notes. Mei wrote with her free hand without releasing his, which Caleb registered as both impressive and pointed. Lin's quill scratched behind them. Cass at her bench was taking notes with the small precise pure-blood-Slytherin handwriting she had had since fourth year.

Slughorn finished the graph at twelve o'clock with the small flourish that ended every welcome-back lecture he had ever given.

"*That is your year. Tuck in. Brew thoughtfully. Clean up after yourselves.* — *Mr Waddell, your written proposal by next Friday.* — *Off you go.*"

The dungeon stood up in the small bench-scrape rush of the end of a class. Slughorn was already crossing the room toward Caleb's table — clearly intending to handle the phials personally before Caleb left — and Mei squeezed his hand once and released it.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 12:01

Slughorn arrived at Caleb's worktable with the slow appreciative walk he had crossed the dungeon with earlier. He picked up the Bouncing Bulb syrup phial. Tapped it once, watched the small enthusiastic hop. Set it back down.

"*Keep that one, dear boy. Show it to Sprout if you have not. — She will be pleased.*"

He picked up the Wiggenweld phial. Held it to the lamp. Turned it once.

"*Cassia Burbage.*" He said the name with the small Slughorn-recall of a man who logged everyone he had ever taught. "*Slug & Jiggers. Half-blood. Outstanding in Potions. Year of '73. — Tell her I said hello, Mr Waddell, and that I am glad to see she is putting good work into hands that will use it.*"

He set the Wiggenweld back down beside the syrup.

"*Next Friday. Written. Cure for Boils. Pure ethanol.* — *Off you go. — I have a Charms class to be late for in fifteen seconds.*"

He patted the worktable once and was gone — out the door in his proper start-of-term green velvet, leaving the smell of pipe tobacco and crystallised pineapple behind him.

The dungeon emptied around them. Mei was already gathering her satchel; Lin was checking his timetable; Cass had not, conspicuously, looked at Caleb on the way past — but she had gone the long way around the dungeon to the door, which took her past Caleb's worktable, and she had said *"Letter this evening, Caleb"* under her breath without breaking stride.

She was gone before he could answer.

Mei nodded — *yes, lunch* — slung her satchel, and they came out of the dungeon together, Lin a step behind. The corridor up from the dungeons was the proper end-of-period crush: students streaming up toward the Great Hall, the smell of food coming down to meet them.

The Hall was its lunch self. Bowls of stew, cottage pie, plates of bread, jugs of pumpkin juice. The four tables filling out steadily as classes let out. Lin peeled off toward a clutch of his Quidditch friends further down the Ravenclaw bench; Caleb and Mei sat at the middle of the Ravenclaw table, opposite each other this time, satchels on the bench beside them.

Lunch was lunch. Cottage pie. Mei eating with the slightly more relaxed pace of someone who had survived first-period Charms and the welcome-back-Slughorn-show. Caleb registered, briefly, that he was hungry — the porridge had been a long way back — and ate.

A passing Hufflepuff sixth-year said *welcome back* to Mei in a friendly way; Mei said it back. A first-year Ravenclaw further down the table dropped a bowl with a clatter and was told *it's all right* by half the bench at once. Davina came in late from Charms, sat with two prefects further along, gave Caleb a small dry nod across the room.

By twelve to one Mei was finishing her tea and Caleb had eaten enough.

"DADA at one fifteen?"

"DADA at one fifteen. — *Henshaw*. Floor three, the long classroom on the west side. *Don't* sit at the back; he calls on the back." She was already standing.

She gave him a small *go on* squeeze of the hand on the way past — no kiss in the Hall — and went off toward the library to start her thirty pages of herbal.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 13:13

The Defence classroom was on the third floor, west side, the long one with the wide arched windows looking out over the grounds. He arrived two minutes before the bell. The door was open.

Inside: a long room with desks in rows of two, facing a teacher's desk and a chalkboard at the front. Tall windows down the right side, light flooding in. Wall-mounted display cases between the windows held curio items — a hinkypunk lantern, a glass jar of what was probably grindylow scales, a framed scroll Caleb did not have time to read on his way in. The walls were painted a pale grey that caught the September light cleanly.

Six other NEWT-track DADA students from his cohort were already there, with two minutes still to spare:

- **Lin**, second row left, satchel on the desk, the empty seat beside him deliberately kept open — *that one's yours, mate* gesture across the room.
- **Davina**, second row right (across the aisle from Lin), notebook out already, glasses on properly.
- **Cass**, third row left, alone, the empty seat beside her conspicuous. Not looking up.
- **Toby Greaves**, third row right with **Rory Macnair** beside him — Toby talking, Rory listening with the small dry attention he gave most things.
- The **front row** was empty entirely.

Open seats: beside Lin (second row left), beside Davina (second row right), beside Cass (third row left), the entire front row. The back two rows visible behind Toby and Rory's row.

Mei's *don't sit at the back* line was still in his ear.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 13:14

He went down the centre aisle, past Lin's saved seat — Lin gave him a small *huh, all right* eyebrow that meant nothing critical — and pulled out the chair beside Cass at third row left.

Cass did not look up immediately. She was reading the front of her own notebook — already opened to a fresh page, *Defence Against the Dark Arts, NEWT, 1977-78* in her precise pure-blood-Slytherin hand at the top — and she let him sit. Pulled the empty inkwell on his side of the desk closer to the centre between them so they could share. Filled it from her own bottle without asking.

"*Caleb.*" Quiet, dry, not surprised. "You are at my desk again."

She closed her notebook on her finger. The faintly cynical mouth was on its working register, the lecture-from-breakfast filed away.

"You did *not* have to do that. — But — thank you."

She did not, however, thank him in any way that would have looked across the room as a thanks. She just opened her notebook back up and wrote the date at the top of the page in her precise hand, and shifted her satchel to make room for his on the empty side of the desk.

Across the aisle Davina had clocked Caleb's seat choice. The half-millimetre at the corner of *her* mouth went up by a fraction. She did not turn her head.

Behind them, Lin's *huh, all right* had become a *fair enough* in his head, audible only by the small *fwf* of him reaching into his satchel for his own quill without commentary.

The classroom door at the front opened with a sharp *click,* and a man came in.

Henshaw — Caleb's first sight of him — was younger than Slughorn by a long way and older than Caleb by enough that Caleb registered the gap. Mid-forties. Tall, lean, dark hair gone iron-grey at the temples and cropped close. A small scar that ran from the corner of his left eye into his hairline. Robes plain, dark grey, no academic flourish. A wand in a forearm holster Caleb caught a flash of as he moved his arm. He carried no books. He set a single piece of paper on the teacher's desk and turned to the room.

The talking in the room stopped on its own.

"*Defence Against the Dark Arts.* Seventh-year NEWT. — I am Professor Henshaw. — You will not need a textbook for this class. You will need a wand, a notebook, and the willingness to be wrong out loud, which I have observed seventh-years are bad at. — *We will work on it.*"

He picked the paper up. Glanced at it. Set it back down.

"Six of you. — *Mr Macnair.* — *Mr Greaves.* — *Miss Selwyn.* — *Miss* — *Davina, isn't it. Yes. Davina.* — *Mr Wan.* — *Mr Waddell.*"

He read each name without ceremony, eyes flicking up briefly to register the face. When he got to Caleb his eyes paused for a half-second longer than they had on anyone else, registering something. Moved on.

"*Good.*"

He came round the front of the desk. Sat on the corner of it. The forearm holster was visible again briefly.

"This is the year you will write in your career documents that you took NEWT Defence. — *I do not care about that.* I care about whether you survive the first six months after you leave this castle. — *Some of you,* statistically, will not. — *That is the conversation we are going to have for the rest of the year.*"

He let his hand drift behind Cass — under the cover of the desk, between her back and the back of the chair — and ran his fingertips lightly down the length of her spine through her robes. The small tickling scritch of an old friend who had decided, two minutes into the year's most serious-feeling lecture, that the appropriate thing to do was annoy his Slytherin friend.

Cass did not move.

The pure-blood-Slytherin composure she had been raised in — the one her mother had drilled into her since she was six, *do not let your face do anything in public your face does not need to do* — held perfectly. Her quill kept moving. The notebook page kept filling. She did not flinch. She did not turn her head. She did not, even, change her breathing in any way that would have given Henshaw or anyone else a thing to register.

She did, however, move her *foot.*

The toe of her right shoe came down, slowly, deliberately, on top of his under the desk. Not hard. Not stomping. Just — *resting* on his foot with the weight of a girl who was telegraphing, *I am going to grind this in if you do not stop, and I am not going to look at you while I do it.*

Her quill kept moving. Her face did not change. Her foot stayed on his.

Henshaw, at the front, was still on his opening line — *some of you, statistically, will not* — and had not noticed any of it.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 13:17

He kept his hand where it was for one more beat — fingertips on the small knot at the top of her right shoulderblade, the one she had been carrying since at least the breakfast table — and dropped his voice to the level only she could hear, under cover of Henshaw's opening monologue.

"Nobody can see. Are you really upset? — I'll quit if you actually are."

He worked the knot with two fingertips. Slow, not lingering, the practical *I am trying to fix the thing* press of a man who had spent the summer hauling crates off a Bedford and knew where shoulder muscles tied themselves up.

Cass did not, immediately, answer. Her quill kept moving. Her foot stayed lightly on his.

After a beat — without looking at him, without breaking the line of her face for Henshaw — she said, equally low:

"Not upset. — *Stop anyway.*"

The foot pressed a fraction more deliberately on his.

"You are at *my* desk in *my* class on the *first day,* with a *Marcus* who will hear about this from *somebody* before dinner if you do not let me sit through Henshaw with my dignity intact. — *Hand off.*"

A pause. The faintly cynical mouth did the small fond version under the composure.

"*Thank you,* though. — Knot was real."

He took his hand back. Folded both arms loosely on the desk in front of him in the proper attentive seventh-year posture. Cass did not move her foot off his — left it there as the small *registered, friend, no offence taken* contact — and went on writing.

Henshaw, at the front, had reached the end of his opening preamble.

"*Right.* — *Ten things you do not know about defending yourself, that you are going to know by Christmas.*"

He turned to the chalkboard.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 13:18

Henshaw wrote on the chalkboard, in fast clean handwriting:

*1. The most dangerous spell is the one you do not see coming.*

He underlined it.

"That sounds like a tag-line on a cheap defensive-spell pamphlet. — *It is not.* — It is the *single* fact that determines whether a duel is over in three seconds or in three minutes, and whether *you* walked away from it. — *Most* of you, in this room, have spent six years learning to *cast* spells. — You have spent *almost no time* learning to *see* them coming. We are going to fix that this year. — *That is most of what we are going to do.*"

He turned back to the room. The scar at the corner of his eye caught the light.

"Second thing."

He wrote:

*2. The wand-pause is half a second longer than you think it is.*

A small pause. He looked at the room properly.

"What I mean by *the wand-pause* is the gap between when a wizard *decides* to cast a spell and when the spell *actually emerges from the wand.* In the textbook it is treated as instantaneous. — *It is not.* It is on average — for a competent NEWT-level student — about half a second. *Yours,* presently, is closer to a full second. The duellists at the Aurors' office work it down to about a fifth of a second. — *Voldemort,* in the duels he has fought publicly, appears to have it down to about a tenth. — *That difference is why he is winning.*"

He set the chalk down.

"You will not be brought to a tenth of a second this year. You will, if you work, get to about a third. *That is enough to keep most of you alive against most of what is out there.*"

The classroom was silent. Cass's quill, beside Caleb, had stopped.

Cass did not respond visibly. She did not need to. The foot stayed on his.

After a moment her shoulder, where his head had touched it, pressed back against his arm by a fraction. The small *registered, friend, we are fine* contact. She kept writing.

Henshaw, at the front, was on the third thing.

*3. The killing curse cannot be blocked.*

He did not underline this one.

"You will hear, in pubs, that a strong enough Shield Charm will turn it. — That is *not true.* The killing curse passes through every Shield Charm yet recorded. You can *dodge* it. You can *put something between you and it that is not a charm but a physical object.* You cannot *block* it. — *Anyone telling you otherwise* either does not know what they are talking about, or wants you dead in a particular way."

A pause. He set the chalk down for the first time properly.

"I am going to tell you, as your professor on the first day of NEWT Defence in 1977, that *some* of you in this room will see that curse used in the next five years, and *some* of you will see it used at people you love. — *I am not being dramatic.* I am being accurate. — The first thing I want you to know about it is that *you cannot block it,* and the second thing I want you to know about it is that you *can* — most of the time — *be somewhere else when it arrives.* — *Speed* and *terrain* are what we are going to spend October on."

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 13:25

Henshaw picked the chalk back up.

*4. The first three seconds of any encounter decide it.*

He did not elaborate immediately. He let the line sit on the board.

"You have three seconds. — From the moment you become aware that you are in a fight. *Three seconds* to do *one* of three things. — You can put a wall between yourself and the threat. You can put a *spell* between yourself and the threat that will buy you the next three seconds to do something else. Or you can *act first* — *go,* hard, and end it before the other person can ready theirs. — *Whichever* of the three. *You will choose one in three seconds, or one will be chosen for you.*"

He turned to face them properly.

"This year I will train you to make that choice in less than two. Some of you, in time, in less than one. — *That is what the wand-pause work is for.*"

He wrote the fifth one beside it.

*5. The instinct to freeze is the most common cause of death in a defensive encounter.*

He did not say anything to it for a long beat. The room read the line in silence.

"This is the one most of you are going to lose, the first time you are tested. — *That is normal.* — *I lost it the first time I was tested.* — But it is also the one we can train hardest, because it is — paradoxically — the most *trainable* of the human responses to threat. *We freeze because we have not pre-decided.* In this class you will *pre-decide* until your hands move before your conscious brain does."

Cass, beside Caleb, was writing. Her shoulder had relaxed by a fraction against his arm where it had been tense an hour ago.

Henshaw wrote the sixth on the board.

*6. Most threats are not duels.*

He underlined this one twice.

"You will, in books and bad films, see Defence portrayed as two wizards facing each other across a clear floor and shouting spells. — *That is not what most threats look like.* Most threats look like *someone breaking into the place you sleep.* They look like *being followed home.* They look like *a stranger at your door who is too friendly.* They look like *a known person who has been turned and is now in your kitchen.* — *None of these are duels.* — Most of them happen in the *first three seconds we just talked about.* Most of them end in those three seconds whether the defender does anything or not."

He set the chalk down properly.

"The duelling drills we will do this term are *useful.* They train your wand-pause and your reactions. — But the *defensive* curriculum, after Christmas, is going to look like *coming up the stairs to a room that may or may not be safe* and *deciding what you do at the door.* That is the real shape of what is going to be asked of you. — *Most of you* — *most of you* — will face that situation, *not* a duel."

A pause. He looked across the six of them.

"Questions, before we start drills."

Cass, beside Caleb, did not raise her hand. Her quill was steady on her notebook. The foot on his stayed.

The room went still in the small specific way a room went still when one student had said a thing the rest of the class did not have the context for. Lin, behind, made no sound. Davina, across the aisle, set her quill down without looking up. Cass, beside him, had gone — Caleb felt rather than saw — completely motionless. Toby, in the third row right, was looking between Caleb and Henshaw with an expression that was not yet *what* but was on its way there. Rory, beside Toby, had not moved a muscle, which was its own response.

Henshaw, at the front, did not answer immediately.

He looked at Caleb properly. The half-second pause his eyes had done on Caleb's name during the register was on his face again — recognition, or near-recognition, of a particular kind of question.

"*Yes,* Mr Waddell." Quiet. "They do. — When the people who would stop a clearer kind of attack are *not present,* and the people who would investigate *are persuadable* — *yes.* They look like house fires. They look like *suddenly.* They look like *peacefully, at home,* in the obituary column of the *Daily Prophet.*"

A pause. He did not look away.

"This is the kind of question that does not appear in the textbook. — I am glad you asked it. — *Stay after class.*"

He turned back to the board.

"*Right. — Drills.* Stand up. Move the desks back against the windows. We are going to spend the rest of the period working on Stage One of the wand-pause exercise, which is *recognising your own.*"

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 13:34

The class stood. Desks scraped back against the wall under the windows. Henshaw moved his own desk to the side of the room with a small flick of his wand, and the long classroom became a long open floor with the chalkboard at one end and the windows down one side.

"*Stand in a line. Wands out. Facing me.*"

The six of them lined up. Cass next to Caleb on his left, Davina on her left, then Lin, then Rory, then Toby at the far end. Wands out. Henshaw walked the length of the line once, looking at each of them in turn.

"This drill is the simplest one we will do all year. — *I am going to call out a spell.* You will cast it at the wall behind you, *not at me,* the moment you hear it. — *I am going to be timing your wand-pause.* — You will be appalled at the result. — *Good.*"

He stepped back to the chalkboard end of the room. Folded his arms. Watched them.

"*Lumos.*"

Six wand-tips lit, raggedly, across half a second of staggered timing. Henshaw nodded once.

"*Nox.*"

Six tips went out, faster this time. Better.

"*Lumos.*"

Better still. Caleb felt his own hand start to *anticipate* — the small Ravenclaw-cleverness of pattern recognition — and Henshaw, somehow, registered it from the other end of the room.

"*Mr Waddell. — Do not anticipate. The point is the gap between hearing and casting, not your guess at the next call. — Anticipating is what gets you killed. — Reset.*"

A pause.

"*Expelliarmus.*"

Six red flashes, ragged again. The wall took them without comment.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 13:42

Henshaw worked them through it. Twenty minutes of *Lumos, Nox, Expelliarmus, Stupefy* called in unpredictable orders, sometimes two in fast succession, sometimes one with a long silence first. The classroom wall took spell after spell with the patience of a Hogwarts surface that had been hexed at for centuries and was not going to start complaining.

By twenty to two the line was tireder, and Henshaw called *halt* and walked the length of them, naming approximate times.

"*Mr Waddell. About nine-tenths of a second average. Standard for a NEWT-track student in his first lesson.* — *Mr Wan. Eight-tenths.* — *Miss Selwyn. Six-and-a-half tenths.* — *Mr Macnair. About a second.* — *Miss Davina. Eight-tenths.* — *Mr Greaves. Eleven-tenths.*"

Toby, at the end of the line, made a small *hh* sound of professional offence.

"*That is not a criticism, Mr Greaves. That is a baseline. — You will be at six-tenths by Christmas. All of you.* — Wands away. *Class dismissed.*"

The line broke up. Desks scraped back into rows under the supervision of Henshaw's wand. Cass, beside Caleb, was already gathering her satchel — quick, precise — and she gave him a small look as she shouldered it.

"*Letter tonight,* Caleb. Henshaw asked you to stay. — Go." She paused half a beat. "*And.* — Six-and-a-half-tenths. *Just so we're clear.*"

The faintly cynical mouth was on its dry friendly register. She went.

Lin clapped Caleb's shoulder on the way past — *whatever this is, mate, see you at dinner* — and Davina gave him a small nod of *we'll talk* on her way out. Toby and Rory left together, Toby still shaking his head at his eleven-tenths. The classroom was empty except for Caleb and Henshaw within a minute.

Henshaw was at his desk, putting his single piece of paper into a leather folder. He did not look up as Caleb approached.

"*Mr Waddell. Sit, please.*"

He gestured at the chair opposite his desk. The wand was, briefly, visible in the forearm holster as he moved his sleeve.

"*Sorry.*"

He looked at Caleb properly. The scar at the corner of his eye in the afternoon light from the windows.

"You asked a question in there that came out of *somewhere specific.* I would like you to tell me where. — At your pace, in your words. Not for a report. Not for a file. *I am asking* because the question matters and I need to know what you have. — *Your father.* Mother. Friend at school. Newspaper. Diagon. — *Where.*"

He waited. He did not fill the silence. The afternoon light came in through the long windows and caught on the dust above his desk.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 13:50

He kept it short. Hollis. The Brackleys. His father's count of three in the north-west. Lin's two in the Birmingham area — no name on Lin, just *a friend with family there.* Mei's family. His own. The pattern: mixed-marriage households, wife the witch, classed in the local press as house fires, closed fast. The *Prophet* running near-zero coverage. *Suddenly* in this morning's obituary column. The *Manchester Evening News* cover his father had marked-up and posted to him, which arrived at breakfast, naming the deaths *connected* and the fire *incidental.*

He did not name Cass, or Davina, or Esme. He named Mei, his father, and *a friend in the year above with family in Birmingham* for Lin. He did not name DS Hollis by name — he said *the Salford CID detective on the case.*

Henshaw listened without taking notes. His hands stayed folded on the desk. When Caleb finished he sat for a long beat, looking past Caleb at a point on the wall.

Then he looked back.

"*Mr Waddell.*"

Quiet.

"Five known incidents, three regions, one summer. — *Yes.* — That is — *consistent* with what I have been hearing through other channels."

A small breath.

"You did *good work* on the Salford morning. — Your father is right that you should not go again. — *I will not lecture you.* — What I will tell you is this: there are people in this castle, and outside it, who have been tracking this *exact* pattern since — *for a longer time than the Prophet would let you guess.* — You found it on your own this summer, which is — *exceptional* for a seventh-year. — But you are *not* alone in having found it. *Do not act* as if you are. — *Do not* organise on it. Do not investigate further. Do not make lists you carry on your person. — Your job, this autumn, is to *learn defence.* — *Mine* is to keep you in shape to be useful when you finish school in June."

A pause. The scar caught the light.

"I am going to ask you to *trust me* for the next ten months. — I cannot tell you everything I know. *Some* of what I know is mine to keep and not yours to ask. — I *can* tell you that the question you asked in class today is *the right one,* and the answer is *yes,* and the people I work for *also* think it is yes. — *Speed and terrain,* Mr Waddell. *October.* — *Get good at the wand-pause.*"

He stood. The audience was over.

"You have lunch missed by now. The kitchens off the Hufflepuff stair will give you a plate if you knock and ask politely. — *Off you go.*"

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 13:55

Henshaw paused at the corner of his desk. He had been expecting Caleb to leave; the question stopped him.

He sat back down on the edge of the desk.

"*Practice partner.* — Yes. — That is the right instinct. *Solo* drills get you to a third of a second on the wand-pause; *pair* drills get you everything else. Reading another wizard's eyes, their shoulders, the way their wand-arm cocks before the cast. — *None* of which you can train alone."

A pause. He considered the question properly.

"*The woods* — *no.* Not the Forbidden Forest. The school does not allow it and the things in it are not safe even for two NEWT-track seventh-years dueling each other. — *The Room of Requirement,* if you can find it, will give you a duelling space. Most of you will spend the year not finding it; some of you will. — *Failing that:* the unused fourth-floor classroom on the east corridor. It has been vacant for three years. Filch does not check it. *Do not tell me you are using it.* I will *not* know you are using it. — *Lock the door.* Cast a *Muffliato* on the walls at the level you have learned by then. *Bring* a third party as a witness to start; an injury without a witness is a story you cannot tell."

He held up a finger.

"For *spells* — pair drills *do not use* offensive curses. Even at low power. *Stinging hexes* and *trip jinxes* and the *Knockback Jinx* at low intent. *Rictusempra,* if you both want to laugh — it teaches dodge-and-recovery. — The block we drill is not a block but a *step.* Move three feet to the left or three feet to the right *the moment you see the wand cock.* The shield charm comes second, after you have moved. — *Most* of what duelling looks like in real life is *not standing where the spell is going to be.*"

He pushed off the desk. Crossed to the window.

"For your specific situation — *you are 0.9 seconds today.* Your partner — *whoever you pick* — is somewhere similar. — Drill *Lumos and Nox* between you for the first two weeks, calling for each other, until your reaction-times are matched. *Then* move to *Expelliarmus* with each other''s hands as targets. *Then,* if you are still alive and still at it, the stinging hexes."

He turned back.

"*One more thing.*"

The scar caught the light again. He looked at Caleb properly.

"*Pick a partner you would not mind seeing hurt, by accident, by you.* — Not because you intend to. *Because at some point in this year somebody you drill with is going to misjudge a stinging hex and put it where it should not have gone, and you are going to do the same to them.* — Pick someone *steady.* Someone *who will not make it about that.* Your *girlfriend* is a tempting choice. — *I would not.*"

The medieval-history section was on the far side of the library, second-to-last aisle on the right.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 14:04

He went to Madam Pince's desk first.

She was at her chair behind the great oak counter, sorting returned books into a wheeled trolley by the small precise system she had used for as long as Caleb had been at the school. Severe black robes. Hair scraped back tighter than was comfortable. The thin face with the sharp nose, the sharp eyes behind half-moon spectacles she did not actually need but wore for effect.

She looked up. Recognised him. Did not, immediately, smile — Madam Pince did not — but her face did the small *Caleb Waddell, seventh-year, Ravenclaw, has not damaged a book in six years* register that was, for her, the highest available register of approval.

"*Mr Waddell.*" Quiet. "What are we looking for."

He kept his voice at the proper library register.

"Madam Pince. — I am looking for help locating something rather specific. Three or four threads I'd like to follow at the same time. — A herbalist of early-fourteenth-century Lincolnshire — Cuthbertus de Lindesia. A parish in his diocese, probably small, probably in the Lindsey region. Four petitioners who came to him, if any are findable: Rudibartus de Stainford. Richard of Kirkham. Margery the Little of Lacock. Iohannes Capellanus de Sleaforde. — And, last, a thing called the Path."

He said the last word at the same conversational level as the others, and watched her face.

Madam Pince's quill paused on the sorting-list.

She did not look up immediately. She set the quill down very precisely. Pushed the half-moon spectacles up the bridge of her nose. Then looked at him — the sharp eyes properly on his — and held his gaze for a longer beat than he had expected.

"*Mr Waddell.*" Quieter than she had been. "*The first three threads* I can help you with. The Hogwarts library has a perfectly serviceable medieval section, and I will show you how to use the parish-register index, which most students do not know is maintained on the south aisle. — *The fourth thread* I would ask you to be careful about saying out loud at the desk."

A pause.

"Where did you hear it."

She did not say *the Path is a thing.* She did not say *the Path is not a thing.* She asked where he had heard it. The sharp eyes did not leave his face.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 14:06

Madam Pince absorbed *a Burke book* with the small tightening at the corner of her mouth that meant she had not been surprised by *the Path* but was now properly attentive.

She closed the sorting-list. Came round the side of the counter — Madam Pince almost never came round the side of the counter — and lowered her voice to a register Caleb had not heard her use before.

"Mr Waddell. Walk with me to the parish-register index. — Slowly, so it looks like a routine reference question. — Do *not* take the book out of your satchel here."

She moved without waiting. He followed at a casual student-pace, satchel on his shoulder, two steps behind. Down the central aisle, past the medieval-history section, to the far south-west corner of the library where the indexes were kept — three tall narrow cabinets with shallow drawers, the parish-register one labelled in faded copperplate.

She pulled a drawer for show. Stood in front of it. Spoke at the level of the drawer.

"What I know about *the word* you used at my desk." Quiet. "I know it is a word with a small canonical literature, all of it suppressed or restricted. I know the original roll was withdrawn from publication in 1934. I know the Department of Mysteries holds the only authorised copy. I know that *students* who have asked me about it in the last twenty-eight years number *three,* including yourself, and that the previous two were both seventh-years who came to me with private books."

A pause.

"I do *not* know whether the thing the word names is real. I do not know what walking it does. I do not have, in this library, a *single* volume that addresses it directly — Hogwarts'' library was instructed in 1934 to hold nothing on the subject and Madam Mortlake — my predecessor — *complied.* — *I do not have books for you on the fourth thread.*"

She slid the drawer open, made a small show of looking through index-cards, and shut it again.

"What I *can* do — for the first three threads — is locate Cuthbertus and his Lindsey parish in the medieval-magical section, and run the four petitioners through the parish-register index, *which will tell us if any of them are buried in churchyards still on the wizarding registers.* — *That* gives you grave-locations, parish records, and the names of any modern parsons who would let a polite seventh-year look at their books."

A breath. The sharp eyes, on his face again.

"As for what *I* would do, were I you, on the fourth thread. — *I would ask Mrs Brownson on Diagon, on the first Hogsmeade weekend.* She is the one. She has handled, in her career, perhaps half a dozen books that touched the subject. — *I am, in this matter, the wrong librarian.*"

A small pause.

"Now — you said *Burke.* — Caleb. — *Burke does not sell what he does not understand.* — If he sold you the book, he sold you the *book.* Not the puzzle. — Be aware of that."

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 14:09

He pulled the Lincolnshire drawer.

The cards were yellowed, the older ones brown, all in different copperplate hands by different librarians across centuries. He found the entries in alphabetical order by parish and pulled the ones he wanted onto the small reading shelf attached to the side of the cabinet.

**LINDSEY (LINCS): St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh, parson''s register held to 1547 (extant); transferred to Lincoln diocese 1547. Last Hogwarts contact: 1923 — query about a 14th-c. herbalist, no follow-up filed.** A small note in the margin in a different hand: *Cuthbertus de Lindesia, herbalist, fl. 1310-1318.*

**STAINFORD (LINCS): hamlet near Lincoln, parson''s register held to 1492; lost to fire 1493. No surviving wizarding register.** A marginal note: *Rudibartus de Stainford, fl. early 14th c.; burial site uncertain.*

**KIRKHAM (LANCS): parson''s register extant from 1287, held continuously to present. Last Hogwarts contact: 1962 — routine.** No marginal note for Ricardus.

**LACOCK (WILTS): parson''s register lost in dissolution 1539, but Lacock Abbey papers in possession of Sir John Talbot 1539; some passed to Cotswold Branch of Magical Records Office, Cirencester, 1841.** Marginal: *Margery the Little of Lacock, d. 1372; commonplace book uncertain — possibly with the Talbot papers, possibly destroyed.*

**SLEAFORD (LINCS): parson''s register held to 1544 (extant), 1545 onwards lost.** No marginal note for Iohannes Capellanus.

He read the cards twice. Did not, per Pince's instruction, take them. Made small mental notes — St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh was the parish, the 1923 Hogwarts query was a thread he had not known existed, and Margery's commonplace book *might* be at Cirencester after all.

When Madam Pince came back twelve minutes later — empty-handed, which was a tell — he closed the drawers and gave her a small thank-you nod and went down the central aisle to find Mei.

She was at her south-aisle table where he had seen her, locked herbal open, notes-paper to one side. She looked up at his approach and the half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth went up by a fraction.

He pulled out the chair opposite hers — properly opposite this time, no seat-confusion — and sat. Did not, immediately, say anything. The library quiet was the right register. Mei was working; he was not going to interrupt.
