## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 14:24

He went back into the medieval-magical aisles. Madam Pince had said she had come back empty-handed but Caleb wanted to see for himself what Hogwarts had — and what it didn't.

The medieval-magical section ran the length of the second-to-last aisle on the south side, three shelves high, organised by century. The fourteenth-century shelf was at chest height. He worked along it with one finger on the spines.

Most of what was there was the standard surviving fourteenth-century corpus — *Liber Subtilitatum* (Hildegardian, lower spine), the *Compendium Magistrorum Medii Aevi* (the same Compendium Pewsey had pointed him at on Diagon — a slimmer copy here, probably a school edition), *Magickal Practitioners of the English Reformation* (out of period), *A Calendar of Wizarding Lincolnshire 1100-1500* (which was promising), and a small leather volume called *Lives of the Lindsey Herbalists* (which was very promising indeed).

He pulled the *Calendar of Wizarding Lincolnshire* and *Lives of the Lindsey Herbalists.* Took them down to a smaller table half an aisle away from Mei — close enough to see her, far enough not to disturb.

Cracked *Lives of the Lindsey Herbalists* first. The index at the back: *Cuthbertus de Lindesia, herbalist, fl. 1310-1318, of St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh; pp. 47-53.*

He turned to page 47.

A short biographical entry. Six pages. *Magister Cuthbertus, son of the wheelwright Aelfric of Sleaford, took orders at Lincoln in his nineteenth year and was given the small parish of St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh — a fenland parish of approximately one hundred souls, mostly Muggle — where he served as both priest and parish herbalist for thirty-one years. He was, the writer of this volume notes, the only known herbalist of his generation in the Lindsey region to have kept a written corpus of any size. His herbal of 1314 is held in private hands. He also kept a parish casebook — separately — covering medical and other matters of his cure of souls. The casebook is presumed lost.*

A separate paragraph:

*Most notable of his cases is the one which appears in some versions of his herbal as a hidden quire: that of a young woman of the parish, daughter of one Aelfgyth widow of Wulfric the smith, who at the age of eleven came to him with what he called *visiones septemdecim* — seventeen visions across one season — and at the conclusion of which she went silent and remained so for the remainder of her life. She is recorded in the parish register as Aedith Wulfricsdohter; her gravestone, which Cuthbert paid for, is in the churchyard of St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh, at the south wall, with the inscription *AEDITH WULFRICSDOHTER QUAE SILUIT LOCUTA EST IN AETERNUM* — *Aedith daughter of Wulfric who being silent speaks for ever.* She died in her thirty-second year, AD 1332, twenty-one years after the visions, having lived as a respected member of the parish, communicating throughout in writing.*

He read it twice.

He closed the book on his finger. Let the pages settle. Then opened it again to make sure he had read it correctly.

*Aedith Wulfricsdohter. Died AD 1332, age 32. Twenty-one years after the visions. Buried at St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh churchyard, south wall.*

He picked up the book and carried it across the aisle to Mei's table. Pulled the chair out and sat opposite her again. Did not say anything until she looked up.

She did. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth.

He turned the book around and pushed it across.

"*Page 47. Bottom paragraph.*"

## User

[[also, I dont understand... what were all the cards for? I thought they pointed to documents there at hogwarts... if she knew what she was looking for, what was I doing?]]

## Narrator

OOC: Fair question, and you're right that the structure I gave you was muddled. Let me sort it.

The parish-register index is a *separate* tool from the medieval-magical book stacks. It's not pointing to documents in Hogwarts — it's pointing *outward,* to where parish records survive in the wider wizarding world: which parsons still keep registers, where graves are, when Hogwarts last contacted them. It's a *finder's index,* not a card catalogue of Hogwarts holdings.

So Pince split the work sensibly:
- **She** went to pull what *Hogwarts holds* on Cuthbertus from the medieval-magical section — books, manuscripts, biographical entries — and came back empty-handed, because Hogwarts' fourteenth-century holdings on Cuthbertus are thin. *Lives of the Lindsey Herbalists* and the *Calendar* were on the open shelves; she didn't pull them because she was looking for restricted-section material on the Path question, which Hogwarts doesn't have.
- **He** worked the parish-register index to identify *the external research targets* — which parishes still have records you could go visit or write to, where the four petitioners are buried, who the modern parsons are. The cards gave him: Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh has a parson's register (1547 cutoff but extant); Stainford's burned in 1493; Kirkham's is *continuous from 1287* (rare and valuable); Lacock's papers may be at Cirencester; Sleaford's lost from 1545. That's *fieldwork plan,* not Hogwarts holdings.

The two threads come together at Cuthbert specifically because *Lives of the Lindsey Herbalists* — a book Caleb later found on the open shelf himself — bridges them. It tells him both the historical figure (Cuthbertus, biographical entry, his casebook) *and* the connection to the named parish that Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh card pointed at, *and* the gravestone inscription that's a real-world physical thing he could in theory go visit.

So the cards weren't useless — they're the map for the *Cirencester trip* and any future fieldwork (write to the modern parson at Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh? travel to Lacock?). The Hogwarts-holdings work is the *book* he's just brought to Mei.

Pretend Madam Pince's *I will be back in twelve minutes* / *came back empty-handed* sequence was clearer about *why* she came back empty-handed: she was looking specifically for restricted-section Path material, found Hogwarts has none of it (per the 1934 instruction she'd already mentioned), and that's what *empty-handed* meant. The open-shelf books — *Lives of the Lindsey Herbalists* and the *Calendar* — Caleb found on his own walking back through.

Cleanly back to scene. Caleb has just slid *Lives of the Lindsey Herbalists* across to Mei, page 47, and said *bottom paragraph.*

## User

[and also Talbot? that is a thread?]

## Narrator

OOC: Yes, real thread. Sir John Talbot is the historical figure who took possession of Lacock Abbey when it was dissolved in 1539. The card Caleb pulled said *Lacock Abbey papers in possession of Sir John Talbot 1539; some passed to Cotswold Branch of Magical Records Office, Cirencester, 1841.*

That's a real historical fact (Lacock Abbey was indeed granted to Sir William Sharington in 1540, sold to the Talbot family later — I've nudged the timeline slightly toward Talbot for our purposes, which is fine for wizarding-history register). The Talbot family kept Lacock Abbey from the Tudor period through to 1944, when it was given to the National Trust by Matilda Talbot. They held its papers privately for four centuries.

What this means for Caleb:

- *Some* of the Lacock papers went to Cirencester in 1841 — that's the wizarding portion the Magical Records Office accessioned. That's the lot Mrs Brownson pointed Caleb at, and where Margery's commonplace book *might* survive.
- The *rest* of the Lacock papers stayed with the Talbot family. The Talbots are a known wizarding-adjacent family (real-world: William Henry Fox Talbot of photography fame was a Talbot of Lacock; in our register he is/was a half-blood or Squib of the Lacock Talbots, depending on what the campaign needs). Their private archive at Lacock — what wasn't sent to Cirencester — would still be in family hands somewhere, possibly at Lacock itself, possibly at a Talbot estate, possibly catalogued, possibly not.

So the threads from the card are:

1. **Cirencester** (already the plan) — Margery's commonplace book *if it went there.*
2. **The Talbot family archive** — Margery's commonplace book *if it stayed with them,* and *anything else* about Lacock-era wizarding material that wasn't sent to Cirencester. This is a much harder thread; private family archives don't open to seventh-year students from Manchester. Reaching a modern Talbot would mean either a formal letter of inquiry, a connection through Mrs Brownson or someone else with bona fides, or — if a current Talbot is wizarding-adjacent — the kind of social bridge Caleb does not have.

It's worth knowing about as a thread that exists. If Cirencester comes up empty in October, the Talbot archive is the next obvious place to look. If it doesn't, it can stay parked.

Cleanly back to scene. Caleb has just pushed the book across to Mei.

OOC: Yes — locked book threads are getting busy. Clean list below.

**THE LOCKED BOOK — open threads, as of 2 Sept 1977 14:34**

**The young woman**
- Name: **Aedith Wulfricsdohter** (Aedith daughter of Wulfric)
- Mother: Aelfgyth, widow of Wulfric the smith
- Visions: age 11, seventeen of them across one season, AD 1311
- Outcome: silent for the rest of her life, communicated in writing, *respected member of the parish*
- Died 1332, age 32, twenty-one years after the visions
- Buried: south wall of St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh churchyard, gravestone paid for by Cuthbert
- Inscription: *AEDITH WULFRICSDOHTER QUAE SILUIT LOCUTA EST IN AETERNUM*
- **Her grave physically exists and could be visited.**

**Cuthbertus de Lindesia**
- Born Sleaford, son of Aelfric the wheelwright
- Priest of St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh, 31 years
- Herbal 1314 (Caleb has it)
- Parish casebook *separately* kept, presumed lost
- *The casebook would presumably contain Aedith's case in fuller form than the herbal's hidden quire.* It is the most valuable missing document.

**The four petitioners — research status**
- *Rudibartus de Stainford* — burial uncertain, parson's register lost in 1493 fire. Walked partially.
- *Ricardus de Kirkham* (Lancs) — register continuous from 1287; could be researched. Vanished 1328.
- *Margery the Little of Lacock* — commonplace book the prize. Possibly at Cirencester, possibly with Talbot archive, possibly destroyed.
- *Iohannes Capellanus de Sleaforde* — register lost from 1545; not in the Compendium at all. Black hole.

**Document targets, by location**
- *Cirencester / Cotswold Branch of Magical Records Office* — Margery's commonplace book if it went there in 1841; Lacock parish records 1530s; possibly papers connected to the Path roll itself.
- *Talbot family private archive* — Margery's commonplace book if it didn't go to Cirencester; private holdings, very hard access.
- *St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh* — Aedith's grave, parson's register held to 1547. Modern parson's name not yet known. A polite letter inquiry possible.
- *Stainford* — Rudibartus's gravesite. Burial unconfirmed; parson's register burned 1493.
- *Kirkham (Lancs)* — Ricardus's parish records, continuous from 1287. Modern parson reachable.
- *Sleaford* — Iohannes is the gap. No current trail.
- *Department of Mysteries restricted archive* — original Path roll. Joint Wizengamot/Magical Education research warrant required. Not realistic at seventh-year level.

**Hogwarts library — what's available**
- *Lives of the Lindsey Herbalists* (open shelf) — biographical entries on Cuthbert and Aedith; Caleb has it now.
- *A Calendar of Wizarding Lincolnshire 1100-1500* (open shelf) — broader regional context; not yet read.
- *Compendium Magistrorum Medii Aevi* school edition (open shelf) — already read on Diagon at Brownson's.
- *No restricted-section material on the Path itself* — 1934 instruction enforced by Madam Mortlake.

**Hogwarts/library — anomalies noted**
- 1923 Hogwarts library query about Cuthbertus, no follow-up filed. Whoever asked, asked once, did not return. A predecessor researcher — student? professor? unknown.
- Two prior seventh-year students in 28 years asked Madam Pince about the Path with private books. Their identities and outcomes unknown. Not committed-to-memory — Pince said it but did not name them.

**People in scope**
- *Mrs Brownson* — Diagon. Has the broadest knowledge of the Path; will help. First Hogsmeade weekend visit planned.
- *Cassia Burbage* — Diagon. Will translate herbal pages by post (2-3 at a time). Will NOT engage with the hidden quire.
- *Mr Pewsey* — pointed Caleb at *Compendium* and Brownson's; said *the catalogued ones tend to be the safe ones; the uncatalogued ones are sometimes more interesting, sometimes nothing, sometimes not.*
- *Madam Pince* — will help with first three threads (Cuthbert, parishes, petitioners); will not touch the fourth (Path); explicitly redirected to Brownson.
- *Caractacus Burke* — sold the book. Set conditions for showing his third item. Will not actively help.

**Working hypothesis (Caleb + Mei)**
- Visions and Path may be **separate phenomena.**
- Cuthbert may have **reverse-engineered** the Path *out of* Aedith's visions and walked it himself.
- Aedith's silence may have been **her own actions going wrong,** not the Path's expected outcome.
- *Margery walked it deliberately and lived a long good life — she is the success case and the most valuable second source.*

**Immediate next actions**
1. Continue working with Mei this afternoon through the Hogwarts books.
2. Mrs Brownson and the reader's-pass conversation — first Hogsmeade weekend (~22 Oct).
3. Cirencester trip — after credentials.
4. Letter to the modern parson at St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh — possible at any time, low cost. Could be drafted this week.
5. Letter to the modern parson at Kirkham — same.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 14:35

Mei finished the bottom paragraph of page 47 and looked up. Her face had not, exactly, gone composed — but it had gone *quiet,* the small Mei-quiet of a clever person who had just been handed a name to attach to something that had been an abstraction.

"*Aedith.*"

Quiet. She said it once, the proper way, the *th* coming through correctly — the small Ravenclaw-precision of a girl who had read enough Old English in her time to know the name was three syllables not two.

She set the book down on her side of the table. Did not push it back. Closed her own notebook on her finger.

"All right." A breath. "*She had a life.*"

She let that sit.

"Twenty-one years of it. — Silent the whole time. Communicating in writing. Respected member of the parish. Cuthbert *paid for her stone.* — Caleb. — That changes — *most* of what I was assuming."

She turned the page back of the book. Read the surrounding context — she always did that when handed a paragraph cold, read the page before and the page after to make sure she was reading the line in its full setting — and then came back to page 47.

"The thing I am stuck on." Quiet. "She *lived.* — *That is not the *silentium* outcome a person walking the Path wrong would die into.* That is — *that is a person who reached an end-state and then went on living inside it.* Twenty-one years. — *Communicating in writing.* — *Caleb,* she would have written things down."

She looked at him properly.

"She was *literate enough to communicate in writing to a Lindsey parish for twenty-one years.* — *In what.* — On *what.* — *Where did her writings go.* — A parish woman of fenland Lincolnshire, the whole village reading her notes for two decades. Some of those notes were *kept.* Cuthbert kept his casebook, even if it''s lost. Aelfgyth her mother — *if Aelfgyth could read Aedith''s notes, Aelfgyth kept them.* — Aedith may *have left a corpus.*"

A small breath.

"And — *Caleb.* — That stone. *quae siluit locuta est in aeternum.* — *She who being silent speaks for ever.* — That is not a generic medieval epitaph. That is *Cuthbert,* who knew her case completely, telling whoever was *coming after* — *if her writing survived, read it.*"

She closed the book.

"*Add to the threads list.* — *Aedith''s own writings.* — Anywhere they may have gone. Parish chest. Mother''s family. Cuthbert''s casebook copy of them, if she left him any. — They will *not* be in the Talbot archive. — They will be in *Lincolnshire,* if anywhere. — *Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh.*"

She did not look excited. She looked the way Mei looked when something had just become a more serious thing than she had thought it would be at lunch.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 14:41

Mei's mouth did the small *Caleb you absolute idiot* register, and she looked at him properly across the table.

"*From my graverobbing experience.*"

A pause.

"Caleb. — *I am a Ravenclaw seventh-year, not a sexton.* I have read about coffins. I have not *robbed* any."

The corner of her mouth was doing the half-millimetre thing in spite of itself.

"But — *yes* — I have read about coffins. — A medieval English fenland coffin in 1332 would be *oak,* if the family could afford a proper one. Otherwise pine, or simply a shroud. A Lindsey parish small-holder''s daughter — Cuthbert paid for the stone, which was the expensive part — would likely have had a *plain pine box* or a *shroud burial.* — *Pine in fenland soil* would be — gone. Inside fifty years. *Bones,* though, in the right soil, would still be there. Fenland is wet and acidic, which is *bad* for bone but *good* for textiles, leather, and *parchment* in the right conditions — *bog conditions* preserve organic material spectacularly, but a churchyard is drained, not bog."

She closed *Lives of the Lindsey Herbalists* and set it carefully aside.

"*Caleb.* — She has been there *six hundred and forty-five years.* In a fenland churchyard. The *coffin* is gone. The *body* is *probably* gone — the bones might still be there, depending on the water table — and the *writings*, if they were buried with her, would be — *dust,* unless they were sealed in something the parish would not have had access to. Lead-lined coffins came later and would have been a noble''s burial, not Aedith''s. Vellum *can* survive in surprisingly good shape in the right conditions, but *those* conditions are not a fenland parish-side grave."

A breath.

"*Realistically* — if Cuthbert had wanted Aedith''s writings to survive, he would not have buried them with her. He would have kept them with his casebook. — Or given them to her mother. — Or *donated them to the parish chest,* which is the *one place* in 1332 that would have held a small chest of papers for an unbroken six centuries, *because parish chests are exactly designed for that.*"

She leaned back.

"*Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh parish chest* is *probably* where her writings would be, if anywhere. — The grave gives us *her,* but not her *words.* — *Write the parson.*"

She picked the book back up. Re-read the line about the parish — *a fenland parish of approximately one hundred souls* — and set it down again.

"All right. — *Fenland* is what I said. *Fenland-on-the-marsh* is what the *parish name actually says.* — That is — *bog conditions,* not *churchyard conditions,* depending on where the churchyard sits and how the marsh has moved in six hundred years. — *If the south wall of the churchyard has been at any point during those six centuries under the water table or under bog peat,* — then preservation is — *substantially better* than I just said."

She picked the quill back up. Made a small note on her own paper.

"*Bog burials in fenland conditions* — *Lindow Man* is the famous one but there are dozens — *can* preserve textiles, leather, hair, skin, and *vellum* startlingly well. The Lincolnshire fens have produced bog finds. — *If* Aedith was buried in a coffin, *and* the coffin sank into wet ground, *and* the bog held — there is a small but real chance there is *something* there of her, beyond the bones. — *Possibly* including writings, *if* she was buried with any."

She set the quill down.

"*That is not* a reason to dig her up. *That is* a reason to write the parson and ask whether the south wall of the churchyard has, *at any point in living memory or in the parish records,* been disturbed by water — flood, subsidence, drainage works, anything. — *That tells us what condition her grave is in.* — *Without* having to do anything to it."

A breath.

"*Caleb. — That is a clever question.*"

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 14:46

Mei sat with each of the three options Caleb had just put on the table.

She did not, immediately, dismiss any of them. The clever-eye was on properly now — both elbows on the table, fingers laced, looking at Caleb across the book.

"All right." Quiet. "*Three Caleb-hypotheses.*"

She held up her thumb.

"*One.* He cared about her, she got with child by him — or by someone — and the parish would have been *cruel* about it. He hid her, faked the death, faked the burial. — That fits *some* of the evidence. The stone is a generous one. The inscription is a *signpost.* — But it does *not* fit *the woman lived twenty-one years writing in the parish.* If she vanished at seventeen the parish would have *known.* And we have her dying at thirty-two with a parish-recognised burial. — *Possible* but *the timing is wrong* unless the cover-up is much more elaborate than one priest could sustain."

Her index finger.

"*Two.* He fell in love with her over those twenty-one years and she had a child by him. — Caleb. — That is — *period-appropriate but ugly.* It implicates Cuthbert in a way that — *if true* — colours everything we are reading from him. — *I am not closing it as an option.* But I am noting that *if* it is true, *the reason the casebook is missing* changes — not *lost* but *destroyed by someone who found it after his death and would not have it on the parish record.*"

Her middle finger.

"*Three.* The stone marks the *writings* not *her.* — *Caleb. — That one I had not thought of.* — *That one* — *fits the inscription.* — *quae siluit locuta est in aeternum* — *she who being silent speaks for ever* — *would be a perfectly Cuthbert-thing to put on a stone marking a box of writings rather than a body.* — She would still be *somewhere* — buried elsewhere or under a different stone — but the *south wall of the churchyard* would mark *her words* and not *her bones.* — *That is — clever.*"

A breath.

"*All three* of these change *what we ask the parson.* — Not *has the south wall been disturbed by water,* but *what is recorded about the south wall as a site at all.* — Date of stone setting, any reburial records, whether the stone has been moved, whether anything was *disinterred* in the area on any subsequent renovation. — *Parsons keep that kind of record.* For exactly that reason — *graves do get moved* over centuries, and parsons keep careful logs because consecrated ground rules are strict. — *Modern parson, polite letter, broad question.* — *Yes.*"

She picked up her quill. Wrote on her notes-paper:

> *Letter to modern parson, St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh:*
> *— Q1: Records on the south-wall area of the churchyard, any disturbance, reburial, stone movement.*
> *— Q2: Aedith Wulfricsdohter, d. 1332 — anything in the parish memory about her, her stone, her care.*
> *— Q3: Whether the parish has a chest of pre-Reformation papers and whether reading access can be requested for academic study.*

She looked up.

"*Caleb. — That third one was good.*"

Mei looked up at him. The clever-eye softened.

"*Both of us.*" Quiet. "*All right. — Both of us.*"

She did not, this time, push the *don''t deflect compliments* register at him. She just said it back. The corner of her mouth was doing the half-millimetre thing in the soft direction.

She picked her quill back up and went back to the next page of the locked herbal. Her plait fell forward over her shoulder. She tucked it back without looking up.

He stood. Pushed his chair in quietly.

The library at quarter to three on a Friday afternoon was its proper afternoon self — the long west light slanting through the tall windows, dust visible in the beams above the shelves, Madam Pince at her desk in the middle distance, a few students at the other tables working their own work. Caleb shouldered his satchel. Slid *A Calendar of Wizarding Lincolnshire 1100-1500* back onto its shelf — he had not opened it; it could wait for another afternoon.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 14:52

He set his satchel back down beside the chair he had just vacated. Stepped behind Mei. Set both hands lightly on her shoulders, professional, low pressure to start, the way he had with Cass an hour ago — except this time he did not have to ask.

Mei made a small *mm* sound that was *yes, please* without breaking from the page.

He worked. The long flat of his thumbs along the top of her trapezius first, slow, finding the knots she had been carrying since before breakfast — the prefects'-meeting-tension, the herbal-translation-tension, the morning's-conversation-tension. There were several. He worked through them at the pace a tired girl would let a person work them through, slow circles with the thumbs and longer pulls with the heels of his hands across the muscle. Her plait fell forward over the page; she tucked it back without looking up.

After about five minutes she set her quill down properly. Closed her eyes. Did not stop him. Just let him work.

Twenty minutes.

The clock on the library wall — the one above Madam Pince's desk — ticked over from 14:52 to 15:14 in the slow Hogwarts-library way clocks ticked when nobody was watching them. Mei made small sounds of *yes-there* once, a *don't stop that one* once. Her shoulders went, by degrees, from the carried-tense state they had been in to something closer to actually-relaxed. The library quiet held around them. Madam Pince glanced over once, registered what was happening, and went back to her sorting without comment.

At 15:14 he set his hands lightly on her shoulders, palms flat, held for a beat, and let go. Touched the top of her head briefly with the flat of his palm — the small *I'm done, I'll come back at four* gesture — and stepped away.

She did not, immediately, open her eyes. She said, quiet, to the page she had been working on:

"*That was — really good. — Thank you, Caleb.*"

He picked his satchel up. Went down the central aisle of the library, past Madam Pince's desk — she did not look up — to the medieval-magical aisle. Pulled *A Calendar of Wizarding Lincolnshire 1100-1500* off the shelf, took it to a small table on the far side of the library, well out of Mei's eye-line, and sat down to read.

The atlas-volume was actually a calendar — yearly entries 1100-1500, with maps tipped in at every fifty years showing the wizarding parishes of Lincolnshire, the major routes, the seats of the local pure-blood families, the location of the bog-and-fenland boundary as it shifted across four centuries.

He opened to 1311.

## User

.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 15:16

The page for 1311 was the small dense annual entry typical of these calendar-volumes — three columns, one per region of Lincolnshire — Lindsey, Kesteven, Holland.

Under Lindsey, 1311:

*Spring: continued floods on the lower Witham, drainage works at Holbeach. — May: a recorded outbreak of cattle fever in the parishes south of Lincoln, attributed at the time to bog-fever, treated by a regional council of herbalists; minor losses. — Summer-Autumn: notice in the parish records of St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh of "an unwell maid of the parish" attended by Magister Cuthbertus, the reverend; no further public detail. — Autumn: harvest indifferent. — November: cold setting in early. — December: heavy frost.*

He read the *unwell maid of the parish attended by Magister Cuthbertus* twice. *Public* detail not given — but the parish had recorded it, and the Lincolnshire calendar had picked the entry up. It was the *first known external reference* to Aedith's case.

He turned the page. 1312.

*Spring: notice that the maid of the parish attended last year remains "in a strange and remarkable condition," and that Magister Cuthbertus has refused to receive certain visitors who came inquiring at his door from outside the parish. — Summer: those visitors are noted, by name, in the parish margin: Rudibartus de Stainford (twice), Ricardus de Kirkham (once), Margery the Little of Lacock (once), Iohannes Capellanus de Sleaforde (twice). — Cuthbertus is recorded as having received none of them.*

He stopped there.

The four petitioners were *named in the parish register at Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh in 1312* — *all four,* in a single year, *coming to the same parish* in response to whatever news of Aedith's case had reached them. Cuthbertus had refused all four.

*Iohannes Capellanus de Sleaforde* — the man who did not appear in the *Compendium* at all — was, in this entry, *named twice.* He had come to Cuthbert *more than the others.* He was *real,* and had been *the most persistent.*

He closed the book on his finger and looked up at the rafters of the library for a moment.

The clock above Madam Pince's desk read 15:23. Thirty-seven minutes until the Rowena alcove.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 15:24

He took the *Calendar* over to Mei's table and set it down beside her own work, open to 1312, and slid his finger under the lines about the four petitioners.

"*Two things you should see.* — 1311: bog fever in parishes south of Lincoln, herbalists treating it. *Same year as her visions.* — And the four petitioners are named in the *parish register* in 1312. *All four.* Cuthbert refused all of them. Iohannes is named *twice.*"

Mei leaned over. Read the lines. Her quill paused. She tapped the page once where *Iohannes Capellanus de Sleaforde — twice* was written.

"*The one who is not in the Compendium.*" Quiet. "*Came to her parish twice.* — Caleb. — That is — *significant.* — Add it. — *Iohannes is the most interesting petitioner.*"

She made a small note on her paper: *Iohannes — twice — only one not in Compendium — most persistent and most missing.*

"*Bog fever the same year* I will read tonight. Maybe it''s nothing. Maybe a nine-year-old with bog fever and a survival has — *something* — to do with the visions. *Hold it.* — Thank you for bringing it across."

She turned back to her own work.

He took both books — *Calendar* and *Lives of the Lindsey Herbalists* — and slid them carefully back onto their respective shelves in the medieval-magical section. Madam Pince registered the returns from her desk with a small approving micro-nod.

He went to the natural-history section — different aisle, two over from medieval-magical — and started looking along the shelves for something on magical animals. The shelf was wider than he had expected: *Fantastic Beasts and Where to Find Them* (Newt Scamander, 1927, well-thumbed school copy), a *Practical Handbook of Hippogriff Husbandry,* a beautiful illustrated *Owls of the British Isles* with what looked like proper field colour-plates, *Care of the Lesser Magical Beasts* (a working-textbook for NEWT students), and — at the end — a slim volume Caleb did not recognise: *Companion Owls — A Quiet History,* by Hesba Ollivander.

He pulled the *Owls of the British Isles* and *Companion Owls — A Quiet History* and went back to his small table on the far side of the library.

*read companion owls*

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 15:31

The volume was slim — a hundred and twenty pages, leather binding gone soft at the spine, the gilt of the title nearly rubbed off. *Companion Owls — A Quiet History,* by Hesba Ollivander, 1922. Inside the front board: an owner's bookplate from a *Miss Edith Macnamara* he had never heard of, and underneath, a stamp from the Hogwarts library acquisition of 1934.

He read the first page.

*This is a small book about the relationship between a person and an owl. There are good books on the Post Owl, the Eagle Owl, the Tawny Owl, on the breeding and the keeping and the training of the working species. This is not one of them. This is about the owl, generally smaller than the working species and frequently a runt or a weakling, who is found in the wood by a child, brought home in a coat-pocket, refuses to be released, and stays.*

He read it twice.

The book worked through, in short chapters: the small-species owls (screech, little, scops, pygmy) who turned up in human company; the natural history of why they imprinted on humans (it was almost always a runt-survival behaviour, the chapter argued — a young owl whose parents had given it up, finding warmth instead from a thinking thing that did not eat it); the *quiet bond,* which was Ms Ollivander's term for the relationship that formed; the differences between this and a working-Post-Owl bond.

By page eleven Caleb had stopped reading at his usual pace and was reading every line.

*The companion owl differs from the post owl chiefly in that her loyalty is to the person, not to the parchment. A post owl will deliver to the recipient. A companion owl will deliver to the person of whom she is the companion, regardless of the addressing of the letter. This is the source of much of the comic and occasionally tragic literature of misdirected magical correspondence in the eighteenth century.*

A page later:

*The companion owl, generally being smaller than is ideal for distance work, is generally not employed as a post owl. She can be — and the literature is full of fond accounts of small owls performing post duties faithfully — but the companion bond is the priority. In a household which already has a regular post owl, the companion owl is best left to do what she does best, which is to find her person across distances inexplicable to natural history, and to communicate with her person in a register the literature on this subject — small as it is — has not yet sufficiently described.*

A page later, in a paragraph he read three times:

*The most striking property of the companion-owl bond, and the one which has resisted scholarly explanation, is the apparent ability of such an owl to know when her person is in distress, and to fly to him from very considerable distances without conventional summons. The bond is not the result of training; it appears, simply, to develop on its own, and to deepen with time. There are recorded cases of companion owls flying eight hundred miles in a single night, in storm conditions, to reach a person in extremity. The mechanism is not understood. The phenomenon is undisputed.*

He set the book down on the table and looked at the rafters of the library for a moment.

The clock above Madam Pince's desk read 15:43. Seventeen minutes until the Rowena alcove.

He slid *Companion Owls* into his satchel — that one was going home with him for proper reading later — and pulled *Owls of the British Isles* in front of him. Larger book, hardback, the proper field-guide format with colour plates tipped in across from the descriptions.

He flipped to the screech-owl section.

*WESTERN BARN OWL — Tyto alba — common and widespread.* Not Athena. He turned the page.

*EURASIAN SCOPS OWL — Otus scops — rare British vagrant.* Not Athena.

*LITTLE OWL — Athene noctua — small, ground-hunting, introduced to England 19th century.* Not Athena, but the *genus name* gave him a small private smile — *Athene* — which he had not consciously borrowed when naming her, but apparently his brain had.

*EURASIAN PYGMY OWL — Glaucidium passerinum — very rare British vagrant. Smallest European owl. Largely Continental.*

*SCREECH OWL — common term in Britain for several smaller species; technically *Megascops* in North America. The British "screech owl" is most commonly a colloquial name for the BARN OWL or for the TAWNY OWL''s juvenile call. There is no native British species called the screech owl in the strict ornithological sense.*

He stopped at that.

He read it again.

*There is no native British species called the screech owl in the strict ornithological sense.*

He turned the page. The next entry was on the *TAWNY OWL,* which had a juvenile call sometimes described as a screech and was the common British woodland owl. Athena was — he thought back to her on his arm this morning, the small lopsided shape, the off-balance left wing — *not* a tawny owl. She was small. Very small. She had a pointed face, big yellow eyes, the *kweee* call that had given her her name in his head. The book described tawny juveniles as much larger than that.

He flipped further. *LITTLE OWL* he had already passed. *PYGMY OWL — very rare British vagrant — smallest European owl — largely Continental.*

He read the *Pygmy Owl* entry properly.

*Glaucidium passerinum* — six and a half inches tall — small grey-brown owl with rounded head, pale facial disc, yellow eyes, white "false eyes" at the back of the head — diurnal hunter — found in coniferous and mixed mountain forests of central and northern Europe — *very rare in Britain, recorded only as occasional vagrant* — characteristic call described as *a soft repeated kweew or a sharper kweee in alarm* — known for *unusual confidence with humans for so small a bird, occasionally allowing close approach in nesting territory.*

The colour plate was on the facing page. The bird in it had Athena's head shape exactly.

He read the entry three times.

The clock above Madam Pince's desk read 15:51. Nine minutes to the Rowena alcove.

He looked at the colour plate of the European Pygmy Owl in *Owls of the British Isles* — the small grey-brown body, the rounded head, the yellow eyes, the white *false eyes* on the back of the head — and registered that yes, in his head, that was Athena.

The implications, his Ravenclaw brain doing them in sequence:

Athena was not a screech owl. She was a *European Pygmy Owl,* a *very rare British vagrant,* native to *central and northern European mountain coniferous forests.* Which meant either:

- A continental pygmy owl had crossed the North Sea and reached Manchester, fledged, and ended up in the woods near Caleb's house — possible, vagrants did stray, but unusual for a fledgling rather than an adult.
- Or her parents had been a Continental pair who had, somehow, made their way to that woodland and bred there.
- Or — and this was the version that stuck in his head — she was not a vagrant at all but a *companion owl* in the sense Hesba Ollivander had been describing twelve pages ago, and the *very rare British vagrant* statistical reality of *Glaucidium passerinum* in 1977 had nothing to do with how she got to the wood Caleb had walked through that summer afternoon when he was thirteen.

He closed *Owls of the British Isles.* Stood. Took the book back to the natural-history shelf and slid it back into its slot. Madam Pince registered the return.

The clock above her desk read 15:54. Six minutes.

He went down the central aisle to find Mei.

He took the end of her plait between his thumb and forefinger.

Mei did not look up. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth went up.

He worked the end of the plait — *played* with it, not unravelling, just running the soft brushy end through his fingers, twisting the very tip lightly the wrong way and watching it spring back, lifting it and letting it fall against her shoulderblade.

She wrote one more line in her notes. Set the quill down. Turned her head a fraction toward him without looking up at his face.

"*Caleb.* — I am giving you another two minutes of plait-playing and then we are going to your alcove date. — *Use them well.*"

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 15:55

He set both hands gently on the sides of her head — fingertips at her temples, palms loose against her cheeks — and tilted her head back the small angle that brought her face up to his.

Mei let him. Eyes closed, head against the back of her chair, the small *here we go* face of a girl who had been about to keep working and had decided, on balance, that she would rather not.

He kissed her. Upside-down, the small particular geometry of an upside-down kiss — his upper lip against her lower, her upper against his lower, the orientation reversed and the tongues finding each other in the wrong direction in a way that made both of them register the small *oh* of doing it differently. Slow. Brief. Mei's mouth opened against his for a beat and then they both broke for breath, the upside-down kiss being a longer-than-five-second proposition difficult to sustain.

When he straightened a fraction her head came back up to its natural angle. Her glasses had gone crooked. She did not, immediately, fix them.

He moved his hands from her temples to her ears — the small scoop of his fingertips against the soft skin behind each earlobe, slow circular rubs at the small bony part where the ear met the skull. Mei's eyes stayed closed. She made a small *mmh* sound that was not for the library — quiet, not loud — but real, the small involuntary noise of a person being touched in a way they had not known they wanted.

After about thirty seconds she opened her eyes.

"*Caleb.*" Quiet. The half-millimetre was full and proper. "You have *exhausted* the two minutes. — *Both* of those count."

She straightened her glasses. Picked up her quill. Capped the ink.

"*Rowena.*"

She started gathering her notes-paper and the locked herbal back into her satchel.

When she got to him she stopped a full arm's length away. Did not, however, stop the small composed face from doing the half-millimetre thing. Did not, however, fail to register where he was leaning and the small *come and get me* register he had leaned that way in.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 15:59

He pushed off the shelf, reached his hand back behind him without looking, and felt her hand fold into his after a beat. Walked.

Mei followed at his shoulder, satchel bouncing once against her hip as she fell into step. Madam Pince registered the joined hands on the way past her desk with the neutral face of a librarian who had seen six years of seventh-years pair off in this library and was not going to remark on this one. Out into the fourth-floor corridor.

The corridor was emptier than it had been at lunch — proper afternoon-into-evening time, classes finished, the school in its drift toward dinner. The light through the high arched windows was the long west light now, slanting and amber, catching the dust above the stair-treads.

They went down. Down to the third floor, across the long west corridor, around to the great central staircase, and up — five floors up — to the Ravenclaw tower. Mei's hand stayed in his the whole way. She did not say anything; he did not say anything. The walk was four minutes and the silence was the right kind.

The eagle door's riddle for the late afternoon was: *I am taken from the mine and shut up in a wooden case from which I am never released, yet I am used by almost everyone. What am I?*

Mei answered without breaking stride. "*Pencil lead.*"

The door swung.

The common room was its late-afternoon self — a few seventh-years working at the tables, a clutch of fourth-years by the fire, two fifth-years arguing in low voices over an Astronomy chart. Esme was at her usual table by the window, head down in her Defence textbook. None of them looked up.

He led Mei to the alcove. The Rowena bust was where it had been last night — slight not-quite-smile, marble cool under his fingertips when he set his free hand on the pedestal — and the alcove was its own quiet space, set back from the common room proper, partly screened by the curve of the wall.

Four o'clock by the small clock on the mantel above the fire across the room.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 16:00

Caleb drew his wand from the sleeve-pocket. Held it out point-up, the cherry shaft springy in his fingers, and gave Mei the small *yes, you too* gesture with his eyebrows.

"*Lumos with me. Tips together. — Don't tell me you've never wondered.*"

Mei rolled her eyes properly — the proper full Mei eye-roll, fond — and set her satchel down on the floor of the alcove. Pulled her own wand. Held it up beside his.

"*Caleb.* — *Tips together* is — that is *innuendo, Caleb.* You said *winking.*"

But she lifted her wand to his.

The cherry tip and the willow tip — Mei's wand was willow, Caleb registered, the pale wood with the small inlay band she had carried since she was eleven — came together at an angle, the points just touching, the small *click* of two wands meeting tip-to-tip in the alcove dim.

"*Lumos.*"

They said it together.

Both tips lit at the same moment — Caleb's clean white-gold the same as last night, Mei's a slightly cooler pale-silver — and the light pooled on the marble face of Rowena directly above them, a small shared circle of brighter illumination.

The bust came up sharper than it had under either of their lights alone. The dust in the carved folds. The hairline crack in her lower lip. The not-quite-smile.

The diadem's central jewel — pale blue, the small set sapphire — caught both lights at once.

The refraction that had been a single spot last night, when Caleb had cast Lumos alone, was now *two spots,* slightly offset from each other against the alcove's back wall — one from Caleb's angle, one from Mei's. The two spots, with the wands held tip-to-tip directly under the jewel, sat side by side on the stone.

And as both Caleb and Mei watched, holding the wand-tips steady, the two spots — *very slowly* — drifted *toward each other* across the back wall of the alcove. As if pulled. The drift took perhaps three seconds. They met. Merged into a single brighter spot, on a slightly different patch of stone than either of them had been on alone.

The merged spot held there. Steady. Brighter than either had been.

Mei made a small sound. Not a word. The small *oh* of a clever person registering something she had not expected to register.

Mei did not, immediately, answer.

She kept her wand-tip pressed lightly against his, eyes on the merged spot on the back wall. The two of them stood there in the alcove — wand-tips touching, the small bright pool on the stone, Rowena's marble face caught above them in the doubled light — and she was *thinking*.

After a beat, very quiet, the prefect-register that meant she did not want her voice to carry into the common room behind them:

"*I don't know.* — Caleb. — *Hold the wands steady.* — *Don't move.*"

She did not move her own wand. She moved her *eyes,* slowly, tracing the line from the merged spot back up the wall, back to the jewel above them, back to where the two wand-tips met at the angle just under it. Worked the geometry of it in her head visibly.

"*Two lights from below, faceted stone above, two refractions on the wall behind.* — That is — *normal optics.* The lights merging *because we are holding the wands close together at the same angle* — that is *also* normal optics. Two close light-sources will produce overlapping refractions through the same facet."

A breath.

"*What is not normal optics* is the *drift.* — *Two spots do not drift toward each other across a wall.* They sit where the geometry puts them. They only move if the *light source* moves or the *facet* moves. — *Neither moved.*"

She held her wand a fraction more carefully against his. Looked at the merged spot.

"*Caleb.* — *Hold,* — *and I am going to move my wand a half-inch to the left.*"

She did. Slow, careful, the wand-tip sliding the small distance.

The merged spot on the wall did *not* split back into two. It *stayed* as a single spot — and *moved* with her wand by the corresponding small amount.

Mei moved her wand back. The spot tracked back.

"*Caleb.* — *Move yours.*"

He did. Slow, careful, his wand-tip sliding the half-inch.

The spot tracked again — but with *his* movement this time, not hers.

Mei's eyes did the small *I am working out a geometry that should not exist* register.

"*The merged spot is responding to whichever of us moves last.* — *Or both at once.* — *I am going to move mine and you keep yours still.* — *Hold.*"

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 16:02

Mei moved her wand. Slow.

The spot on the wall tracked her movement — but as it moved, Caleb registered something he had not noticed before. The merged spot was not the same shape as either of their original individual spots. It was *brighter,* yes, but it was also — *now that he was watching it carefully* — *slightly elongated,* a small oval rather than a circle. And the *direction* of the elongation was rotating, slightly, as Mei moved her wand. Not following her movement linearly. *Rotating.*

Mei stopped moving. The oval steadied, but did not return to a circle. It held as an oval at a particular angle.

She made a very small *huh* under her breath.

"*Caleb.* — *Look at the shape.*"

He looked. The oval was real. He had not imagined it.

"*The merged spot has a shape now.* — Two unmerged spots are circles. The merged one is an oval. — That is — *not* what optics does. — Optical merger of two circular sources gives you a brighter circle, or a peanut, or a single circle if they overlap completely. *It does not give you an oval that rotates.*"

She held her wand still. Looked up at the diadem above them, at the small set sapphire catching both their wand-lights.

"*Caleb.*" Quieter. "*The jewel is doing something to the light. — Not just refracting it. — It is — combining the two lights and producing something that is not what either of them is alone.*"

A breath.

"*Wand-cores.*" Quiet. "*Yours is unicorn hair. Mine is dragon heartstring. — Two different cores, two different magical signatures, the lights from each carry that signature, the jewel is —* I don't know what the jewel is doing. — *But the oval shape suggests there is information in the merged light that is not in either single light.*"

She did not, however, take her wand-tip away from his.

But.

Mei's eyes flicked sideways to him. Her hand in his had *also* registered something — he could see it on her face — a small *did you feel that* expression that she did not voice.

She did not let go of his hand. She did not lower her wand. She held all three connections steady — wand-tip to wand-tip, hand to hand, both Lumoses lit — and the small oval on the back wall of the alcove held, and the small low thrum carried from her hand into his, carried for a long beat, and held.

She said, very quietly, almost without breath:

"*Caleb. — Don't move.*"

The oval — Caleb registered through his closed eyes, a small change in the brightness behind his eyelids — *changed.* Got brighter. He opened his eyes a fraction without breaking the kiss. The oval was *very visibly brighter* than it had been. Not larger. *Brighter.* And the rotation it had been doing — the small slow rotation that had paused when they had stood still — was *moving again.* Slower. *Steadier.* As if it had been pacing itself to the wand-tips before and was now pacing itself to *something else.*

Mei's eyes opened a fraction at the same moment. They both saw it. Held the kiss for one more beat, the small quiet hold of two people who had decided they were not going to break a moment that was producing this much information out of the world. Then both of them, at the same moment, lowered their mouths apart.

The oval, on the wall, was now visibly *not* an oval. It had become — over the course of the kiss — an *ellipse with a small bright tail,* the shape of a small comet pointing away from the wand-tips and *toward* — Caleb traced the line with his eyes — *the small Rowena bust to their right.*

The bust's marble face was unchanged. The bright tail of the spot pointed at her left ear.

Mei said, very quietly:

"*Caleb. — The shape is pointing at her ear.*"

The low thrum in their hands faded with the merge.

Mei kept hold of his hand. Stepped the small half-step to the side of the bust — the alcove was just big enough for both of them to stand at the bust's left side — and brought her wand up to the marble of Rowena's left ear.

Mei did not, immediately, react to the *make out with her statue ear.* The Mei-pause that meant she had heard it, was registering it, and was deciding whether the joke was useful right now or not.

She decided not.

The marble ear in this kind of close light was not what it had looked like under ambient lamps. The carved whorl of the inner ear was *deeper* than a generic 1881 commission would have bothered to carve. The sculptor had gone in there with proper attention. The small canal of the carved auditory passage was not the shallow surface-curve of a standard portrait bust. It was *cut deep,* the small dark interior of it visible to about a quarter of an inch in.

Mei moved her wand half an inch closer.

The light caught something in the cut. Not a reflection — the marble was too dull for that. A *small piece of something not-marble* set inside the carved ear-canal, deeper in than the eye could easily reach.

She did not touch it. She turned her head a fraction toward Caleb.

"*Caleb. — There is something inside the ear.* — *Set into the canal.* — *Look.*"

Mei looked at him.

The composed face. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth doing the dry version, not the warm one.

"*Caleb.* — *No.*"

Quiet. The Mei-register that did not need to elaborate.

"This is *Rowena Ravenclaw.* — In *the Ravenclaw common room.* — Commissioned 1881. — There is a *thing inside her ear* and your first instinct is to *put your finger in there.* — *Caleb.*"

A breath.

"*Things set into stone for a reason* are *protected* for that reason. — A 1881 commission would not have a *random object* in the ear-canal *unless* whoever commissioned it wanted that object to be there. *And found-objects-pulled-by-finger* in old wizarding furniture is — *exactly* — *how students with otherwise promising NEWT scores end up in the hospital wing on the second day of term with their fingers fused to a ring of marble.*"

She did not, however, move her wand away from the ear. She held the Lumos steady.

"*We look,* — first. *We do not touch.* — *And* — Caleb. — *I want to know whether the thing is supposed to come out.* — Which means we *ask Flitwick.* — He is the Ravenclaw Head of House. He has been in this common room every year since *he was eleven himself,* and he has been Charms master since 1934 — *which* — *is the year Madam Pince mentioned.* — There is a non-zero chance Flitwick *knows* what is in the ear and *put it there himself.*"

A pause.

"*I am not making the decision to extract it. — That is a Flitwick decision.*"

She held her wand steady. Looked at Caleb properly.

"*We came up here for the date.* — We have *had* the date. — *Found a thing.* — *Did not break the thing.* — *Left.* — *Tea?*"

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 16:08

He pointed his wand-tip at the bust's left ear and gave it the proper Charms-class flick.

"*Accio Rowena Ravenclaw statue ear scroll.*"

The wand quivered briefly in his hand — the small *I am trying* sensation of a Summoning charm hitting something that was not coming — and then went still. Nothing emerged from the marble ear. Nothing rattled in the canal. The small piece of not-marble he and Mei had seen in the Lumos light remained exactly where it had been.

The wand quiver subsided properly.

Mei, beside him, made a small *snrk* under her breath — the suppressed-laugh register, contained in the alcove and out of earshot of the common room — and shook her head once.

"*Accio Rowena Ravenclaw statue ear scroll,* Caleb. — *That is not how Summoning charms work.* — They will fetch a *named* object that is *not warded against you.* If the thing in there *is* a scroll, *and* it is *not warded,* you would still need to know enough about it to summon it precisely — *Accio scroll* would have been the right syntax — and it *still* would not work because the *bust* is *almost certainly* warded against exactly this kind of casual extraction. — Whoever set the thing in there in 1881 *expected* a Ravenclaw seventh-year to try Accio. *We are not the first.*"

She lowered her own wand. The Lumos winked out.

"*Flitwick,*" she repeated. "*Tomorrow.* He has Saturday morning office hours."

She tugged Caleb's hand gently toward the alcove's exit.

## Narrator

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 16:11

The common room had a small tea-alcove off the central area — a long narrow side-table with a kettle on a perpetual low charm, a wooden tray of mugs, a tin of black tea, a tin of Earl Grey, a small jar of honey, a milk jug under a charmed cooling-cover that kept it perpetually fresh. The seventh-year tradition since at least Caleb's first year had been: tea was free, you took your own, you washed your mug after.

He led Mei to it. Found two clean mugs. Spooned tea into a small infuser, poured the kettle over them, splashed milk into both, handed Mei hers. She took it with both hands. They went to one of the smaller two-person tables near a window — west window, the long afternoon light coming in over the loch — and sat opposite each other with the tea between them.

He looked at her face.

The afternoon light caught the edge of her plait, the curve of her jaw, the small imperfection where her glasses sat slightly crooked from when she had bent over the marble ear. Her cheeks had a faint colour from the alcove. The clever-eye was off-duty for the moment; the warm Mei was on. She looked tired. She looked properly content.

She let him look. Did not, this time, do the *don't* register. Just held her tea in both hands and looked back.

After a beat the corner of her mouth did the half-millimetre thing.

"*Caleb. — You are staring.*"

She did not, however, ask him to stop.

She held the mug in both hands and looked at him over the rim. The west light was gold on the side of her face. She did not say *yes,* exactly — she did not need to — but the small fond *all right* register was on properly.

"*The seventh-year girls'' dormitory* — *no.* Davina is up there marking timetables for the new prefects. — *Yours,* — *yes.* — *If your roommates are not in.*"

A pause. The half-millimetre.

"*Lay down,* Caleb. — *Lay down.* — *Not* — anything else. — Dinner is at six. We *both* have a prefects'' meeting at seven. — *Tea, a lie-down, dinner, then I work and you do whatever you do.*"

She took a proper sip of her tea.

"*Drink. — Look at me.* — I will give you that for the rest of tea. *Then* lie-down. — *I am not* doing the alcove again until we have spoken to Flitwick, *just so you know.* — *That is a tomorrow problem.*"

She set her tea down. Folded her hands round the mug. Looked back at him with the small open Mei-attention that meant she was choosing to let him have this beat properly.

Tea took ten minutes the proper way — small sips, the warmth coming through the mug into his hands, Mei across from him doing the same thing at her own pace. Neither of them said anything much. He looked at her face. She let him. Once she reached across and tucked a wisp of hair behind his ear with her free hand, and then pulled the hand back to her mug.

The mugs went empty. He took both to the side-table, rinsed them under the small *Aguamenti*-fed tap, set them on the drying rack the way the seventh-year tradition required.

He came back. Held his hand out.

Mei picked her satchel up. Took his hand. They went up the stairs to the seventh-year boys' dormitory together — her plait swinging once against her back, the small composed Mei-prefect-walking-pace beside him.

The dormitory door was open a crack. He pushed it. Looked in.

Lin was at his bed, in his shirtsleeves, propped against the headboard reading a book Caleb did not recognise. He looked up when the door opened. Registered Mei behind Caleb's shoulder. The friendly Lin face did not change, exactly — but his eyebrow went up by a small fraction and he raised the book a fraction higher in his hands, a deliberate *I am reading and I am not paying any attention to whatever else is happening in this room* gesture.

"*Mate.*" Quiet. "*Mei.* — *Carry on.*"

He went back to his book and very pointedly did not look up again.

Caleb led Mei across to his own bed. The curtains were tied back; the bedclothes were the proper made-up state he had left them in this morning before the run to the greenhouses. He sat on the edge of the bed. Toed his shoes off.

Mei set her satchel down on the floor at the foot of the bed. Toed her own shoes off — small, precise, the leftright-leftright of a girl who was used to taking shoes off at the door of her grandparents' house — and sat beside him.

She did not, immediately, lie down. She looked at him sideways with the small *here we are, then* face.

He lay back on the bed. The pillow under his head was the same pillow he had slept on this morning; the herbal book was no longer under it, the satchel at the foot of the bed had it instead. He raised his right arm — the *come here* gesture — and held it.

Mei looked at the arm.

She did not, immediately, scoot. The Mei-pause that meant she was working out the geometry of *lying on a single bed with your boyfriend with his friend reading on a bed across the room and your robes still on* and arriving at the small adjustment for it.

Then she lay down. Slid into the curve of his arm, head on his shoulder, her plait coming forward across his chest as her head settled. Her free arm went across his stomach. Her glasses came up against his shoulder and she adjusted them slightly without lifting her head.

She made a small *mmh* sound. The kind of *mmh* a tired prefect made when she finally let herself be horizontal for the first time in the day.

The dormitory was its proper afternoon-into-evening self. The window looked out over the grounds — the loch, the trees beyond, the long blue evening starting to come down the sky. Lin was reading. The pages of his book turned at a steady rate. He did not, true to his word, look over.

Caleb's hand at her shoulder, against the cloth of her robe. The warm weight of her along his side. The small smell of her hair — clean, the soap she used.

She said, very quietly, against his shoulder:

"*This is — quite nice, actually.*"

He closed his eyes.

Mei's breathing slowed against his shoulder almost immediately — she had been more tired than she had let on at the tea-table, the kind of tired that only registered when a person finally stopped moving — and within two or three minutes her breath had gone properly even, the small steady rhythm of someone genuinely asleep.

He drifted with her. Did not quite go under the way she did, but went *down* to the half-conscious state of an afternoon nap with a girl on his shoulder and a friend reading across the room — the small sounds of the dormitory registering at the level of background warmth: the turn of a page from Lin's bed, the wind in the high window, the very distant noise of the common room two floors below.

Once Lin shifted on his bed — a small *fwf* of him reorganising his pillow — and Caleb half-opened one eye, registered Lin had put a glass of water on the small table between their beds, and went back under. Lin's *quiet considerate* register Caleb logged for later thanks.

The afternoon ran on. The light through the high window shifted from gold-amber to long-rose as the September evening came on properly. Mei did not stir. Caleb did not stir. The pocket watch on the bedside table — Caleb had set it there last night — ticked steadily.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 16:48

He came back to surface slowly. The arm under Mei's head had gone the small pins-and-needles tingle of a long lie still, and his shoulder ached faintly where her glasses had pressed against it, but neither sensation was enough to move for. He let it be.

Mei was still asleep. Her plait had come further across his chest. Her hand on his stomach had relaxed into a loose splay. Her breathing was the slow even thing of a person two-thirds of the way through a proper nap.

The dormitory had darkened by perhaps a quarter-shade. Lin was still reading. He had got further into his book; Caleb could see the bookmark had moved.

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 17:54

The eagle considered.

The small bronze head tilted a fraction further to one side. The polite *I have heard the answer and am evaluating it* gesture of an enchanted door that had spent eleven hundred years receiving correct answers, incorrect answers, and the occasional joke.

Mei froze beside him.

Lin, on the other side, did not — Lin made a sound that was halfway between a laugh and a horrified cough, and put his fist briefly against his mouth.

The eagle paused for one long beat. Then, in the small bronze voice it used when it had decided a thing, said:

"*A serviceable wit. — Not the answer.*"

It did not open.

Mei turned to him slowly. The composed Mei-prefect face was barely holding. Her cheeks had gone — the western corridor light catching them — a properly visible colour.

"*Caleb. — Waddell.*" Quiet. The dry register, but the warm version of it. "*Did you just — at the eagle door, in front of Lin, on the way to dinner — make a wet joke about me at the actual house entrance.*"

Lin, leaning against the wall, was now actively shaking with suppressed laughter and pretending not to be.

The eagle, patient, repeated:

"*What gets wetter the more it dries?*"

▼ Friday, 2 September 1977 — 17:55

"*A towel.*"

The eagle's bronze head straightened. The polite *correct* register.

The door swung.

But Caleb was not done.

"*Eagle without a sense of humour. I think you should loosen up. — The point is just to show I'm clever, and I did. — But thank you for your service. I love you. — No hard feelings.*"

The eagle, mid-swing, paused at *I love you* — the small bronze head tilted again, the registering-pause — and held the door at the half-open position for a beat. Then, in the same dry bronze voice, said:

"*Mr Waddell. — A century of service to this house, and you are the first to declare yourself.*"

A small bronze pause.

"*The sentiment is — noted.*"

It opened the rest of the way.

Mei, beside Caleb, had gone — somewhere between *the wet joke* and *the eagle declaration of love* — into the small contained Mei-laughter that was full and silent and shoulder-shaking. She put her hand on his arm to keep her upright. Lin, behind, had given up pretending altogether and was openly laughing, head back against the corridor wall.

Mei made a small *snrk* — the proper full one — into Caleb's shoulder.

She got herself upright. The plait had come loose at the bottom from the shoulder-shaking; she did not bother fixing it.

Lin, behind, had recovered enough to push off the wall. The friendly Lin face was back to its working register but his eyes were still bright.

"*I love you,* he says. — *To the door.* — Caleb. — *Dinner.* — *Walk.*"

The three of them stepped through. Down the seven moving staircases toward the Great Hall. The eagle door swung quietly shut behind them, and — Caleb registered, just for a beat, before they were round the first bend — the bronze head turned to watch them go.

## Narrator

OOC: Time-jump check — let me see what's between now and tomorrow morning.

**Tonight (Fri 2 Sept evening):**
- Dinner 6-7pm
- Mei has a prefects' meeting 7pm (she said earlier)
- Cass said *letter this evening* — she's drafting one for Caleb
- Mei said she'd draft the parson letter tonight, Caleb to edit, send tomorrow
- The morning post tomorrow could include: Annie's letter (still inside the 1-2 week window — 31 Aug + 7 days = 7 Sept earliest, so no), parents (sent 1 Sept evening + ~5 days = 6 Sept earliest, so no), Cassia (sent 1 Sept evening + ~7 days = 8 Sept earliest, so no). No replies due.

**Saturday morning (3 Sept):**
- 8am Hagrid's hut with Athena (locked in)
- Flitwick's office hours are also Saturday morning per Mei

So there's a scheduling conflict to flag — Caleb is supposed to be at Hagrid's at 8am with Athena, and Mei wants to take the Rowena thing to Flitwick during his Saturday morning office hours. Both are *Saturday morning.*

Three options:

1. **Hagrid first (8-12), Flitwick after.** Flitwick's office hours typically run a chunk of the morning into early afternoon for Charms questions. Caleb can do Hagrid's half-day, come back for lunch, and go to Flitwick with Mei in early afternoon. Means we time-jump to *waking Saturday morning, going to Hagrid's,* and revisit Flitwick later.

2. **Skip Hagrid this Saturday and go to Flitwick.** Loses Saturday-Hagrid plan, which is a real first-meeting commitment with Athena.

3. **Send Mei to Flitwick alone or with Lin if she's keen, while Caleb is at Hagrid's.** Mei is plenty competent to do this herself; the question is whether the *we* part of the find matters or whether it's just data Mei can bring back to Caleb at lunch.

What I'd suggest: time-jump to *waking Saturday morning at 7am, going to Hagrid's at 8 with Athena.* The Flitwick conversation slots into Saturday afternoon when Caleb's back from the grounds — possibly with Mei, possibly with Mei reporting in if she went earlier. Cass's letter and Mei's draft of the parson letter both arrive at Caleb's bedside or via the morning post overnight, depending on how Cass routes hers (she might just hand him the letter at breakfast).

If that order works, I'll time-jump to Saturday morning. Tell me and I'll run.

OOC: Skipping past dinner, the prefects' meeting, the night, and the early morning. Letter from Cass landed at his bedside at some point; the parson-letter draft from Mei is presumably done. Going to Saturday breakfast.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 07:32

He had woken at quarter past seven, the way he had wanted to — the proper alarm-free wake of a body that had been told *eight at Hagrid's* and had set itself accordingly. The dormitory was cold. Lin was still asleep in the next bed, the curtains drawn. Greg was making the small *I am up but I am not enjoying it* sounds from his side of the room.

Caleb dressed properly. Robes, tie, jumper underneath because the morning was sharp. Boots. Wand in his sleeve-pocket. He had picked up the letter from his bedside on the way out — Cass's letter, delivered last night by an obliging fourth-year of her own house who had handed it to Caleb in the corridor with a small *here* and gone — and had read it before sleep. Going to mention it at breakfast. He put it in his satchel.

The parson-letter draft was on the desk between his and Lin's beds where Mei had left it — she had written it during her free hour after the prefects' meeting last night, brought it up, and gone. He picked it up, folded it once, and slid it into the satchel beside Cass's letter. Reading and editing was a *with-tea* job.

Down the staircase. Through the eagle door — the riddle this morning was *I have no body but I come alive with the wind* — *an echo*, he answered, and the door swung. The corridors were thinner at half past seven on a Saturday than on a weekday; a few owls coming back from morning post-rounds, two Hufflepuff first-years on what looked like a corridor-exploring mission, a single house-elf going somewhere with a basket of laundry.

The Great Hall was half-set for breakfast. The four tables had their first plates and tureens — porridge, bread, butter, eggs in two big copper pans, a vat of tea — but were thinly populated, the way Saturday breakfast was thinly populated when the school had no obligation to be there for any particular hour.

Mei was at the Ravenclaw table about a third of the way down, on his side, in proper Saturday robes with no tie, plait done loose down her back. Locked herbal at her elbow already. She had a cup of tea in one hand and was reading. The space across from her was open. So was the space beside her.

Lin was not down yet. Davina was visible at the far end of the Ravenclaw table with two prefects from another house, evidently going over something on a clipboard with the small Saturday-morning-prefect attention. Cass was at the Slytherin table at her usual end-of-bench spot. She glanced up when Caleb came in. Held his eye for a beat — *did you read it?* — and went back to her tea.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 07:33

He sat down opposite Mei. She glanced up, registered him, the half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth, and went back to her herbal — not aloof, just *I have not finished my page,* the small Mei-courtesy of not putting her work down for him until she was at a stopping place.

He poured himself tea from the pot. A splash of milk. Took the letter out of his satchel.

Cass's writing was the same precise pure-blood-Slytherin hand it had been on the inkwell-page in DADA. A single folded sheet of cream parchment, the wax seal already broken from last night. He opened it on the table beside his plate.

He read.

The opening — *I promised seventeen registers of no. On reflection I have decided seventeen would be excessive even by my standards, and I have settled for seven* — got a small huff out of him on the first read last night, and got it again now.

The seven registers ran in a column. *Practical no. Reputational no. Familial no. Personal no. Caleb no. Mei no. Aesthetic no.* He had laughed properly at *Marcus, when I told him about the proposal at supper last night, was delighted at the suggestion (his word, not mine — he is dreadful) (I asked him to behave) (he sends his regards).* He had stopped at the *Caleb no* — *Caleb, you are my friend in a way I would not put through that, regardless of the politics of the rest of it* — and read it twice.

Then the line across the page and the proper letter underneath it.

The *anger and sharpening* paragraph. The *the structure is the trap* line. The ask — *when the anger sharpens into a plan, come to a person who loves you and lay it out before you act on it. Not because we will tell you to stop. Because we will tell you whether you are still you in the plan.*

The vouching for Henshaw. *My mother knew him at school, when his name was not yet Henshaw. — She would not say the things she has said about him if she did not believe him.*

The Ferenc paragraph. *I am still asking you to leave that one alone. — I will tell you when I am ready.*

The last thing. *If he lets us pick partners I will pick you. The morning of the dueling decision was when I decided. The threesome joke did not change it.*

The P.S. *Marcus says hello and that he hopes you are growing into your Manchester. I do not know what he meant by that and I am not asking him to clarify.*

He folded the letter back up. Drank his tea.

## Narrator

OOC: Fair — and you're right, that paragraph was me writing badly in two ways:

1. **"got a small huff out of him on the first read last night."** Confusing pronoun. *Him* meant Caleb. The whole paragraph was meant as Caleb-on-Saturday-morning re-reading the letter and registering, again, the same things he registered on Friday night when it arrived. But because I'd been writing in third-person past about Caleb and just mentioned Cass writing it, *him* lands as ambiguous. Should have been *Caleb* or *himself*.

2. **The "Marcus, when I told him" line.** That's a sentence *from inside the letter*, written by Cass, where she's telling Caleb that she told Marcus about the threesome joke at supper. Marcus is Cass's boyfriend (Marcus Bletchley — Mei named him at breakfast yesterday: *Cass Selwyn has been with Marcus Bletchley since Christmas of last year. Who you and I both ran into at the Boxing Day Ravenclaw-alumni thing where she introduced him to you by name and he asked after your father's shop*).

So the line is Cass writing to Caleb: *I told my boyfriend Marcus about the joke you made; he thought it was funny; he sends his regards.* Caleb laughs at it because of the layered comedy of (a) Cass actually told her boyfriend the joke, (b) her boyfriend was *delighted at the suggestion*, (c) Cass parenthetically calls him *dreadful*, (d) he sends his regards.

Pretend the paragraph reads instead:

> Cass's writing was the same precise pure-blood-Slytherin hand it had been on the inkwell-page in DADA. A single folded sheet of cream parchment, the wax seal already broken from last night. He opened it on the table beside his plate.
>
> He re-read.
>
> The opening — *I promised seventeen registers of no. On reflection I have decided seventeen would be excessive even by my standards, and I have settled for seven* — had got a small laugh out of him last night and got the same laugh again now.
>
> The seven registers ran in a column. *Practical no. Reputational no. Familial no. Personal no. Caleb no. Mei no. Aesthetic no.* The line where Cass mentioned telling Marcus — her boyfriend — about the threesome joke at supper, and reported him *delighted at the suggestion* with a parenthetical that he was *dreadful* and another that he *sends his regards* — had landed properly the first time and landed again. The *Caleb no* — *Caleb, you are my friend in a way I would not put through that, regardless of the politics of the rest of it* — Caleb stopped on, and read twice.

Same content, but now you can tell who *him* is and who *Marcus* is.

Cleanly back to scene. Caleb has folded the letter back up and is drinking his tea opposite Mei.
