*I edit Mei's letter*

## Narrator

OOC: Need to write Mei's draft of the parson letter first so Caleb has something concrete to edit. Drafting now in keeping with what we agreed at the library: it's a polite Hogwarts seventh-year reading-academic-history-for-NEWT-Ancient-Runes-context register, asking the modern parson at St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh about (1) the south-wall area's records, (2) parish memory of Aedith, (3) access to the pre-Reformation parish chest. Mei would write it in her precise hand, formal, polite, brief.

**Mei's draft of the parson letter** (on a single sheet of school parchment, in her clear precise hand):

*The Reverend [parson's name to be filled in],*
*The Vicarage,*
*St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh,*
*Lincolnshire.*

*Dear Reverend [——],*

*I write to you from the school of Hogwarts, where I am in the seventh and final year of my studies. My subject of independent research for my Ancient Runes NEWT this year is the corpus of medieval English parish writings of Lincolnshire, with particular attention to the early fourteenth century. My research partner, Mr Caleb Waddell, and I are working under the general supervision of our school librarian, Madam Pince.*

*Our subject of study is the herbalist Magister Cuthbertus de Lindesia, who served as priest of St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh for thirty-one years (c. 1310-1341), and one of his most well-recorded parish cases — that of Aedith Wulfricsdohter, daughter of Aelfgyth (widow of Wulfric the smith), who died in your parish in 1332 and is recorded as having been buried in your churchyard at the south wall, with a stone paid for by Cuthbertus himself.*

*We write with three small inquiries, in the hope that you may have some information to share, or may direct us to a colleague who would.*

*First: would the parish records contain any reference to the south-wall area of the churchyard having been disturbed, repaired, or otherwise affected — by flood, subsidence, drainage works, or routine maintenance — at any point during the past six centuries? We are conscious that the parish sits on fenland and that the water table can shift; we are interested in the condition of the burial ground in general terms.*

*Second: is there any local memory or written record of Aedith Wulfricsdohter herself? She is recorded by Cuthbertus as having lived for twenty-one years following an illness in her childhood, communicating in writing throughout, and as having been a respected member of the parish at her death. We wonder whether her name persists in the parish, in oral history or in record.*

*Third: does the parish chest at St Æthelthryth's contain pre-Reformation papers, and if so, would there be any possibility — at the discretion of yourself and your wardens — of arranging a visit by a researcher to consult them? We would be glad to write again with formal credentials from our school if a visit were a possibility.*

*We write only with the hope of hearing back, and would be grateful for any assistance, however limited. Should you prefer to direct us to the Lincolnshire diocesan archive or to a colleague with greater expertise, we would equally be glad of the referral.*

*With our thanks for your kindness in receiving the letter, and our best regards,*

*Mei Chang* (sig.) *and Mr Caleb Waddell* (sig. line beneath)
*Hogwarts School*
*[address: postal box, Hogwarts School, Scotland; reply-by-school-owl arrangement]*

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 07:38

He set Cass's letter aside, took Mei's draft out of his satchel, and unfolded it on the table beside his tea.

He read it once through. The Mei-precision of it — formal, polite, brief, the right register for a country parson opening a letter at his vicarage breakfast on a weekday morning. The three questions ordered correctly. The hedge about the diocesan archive at the end was generous.

He picked up the spare quill from the small bone-quill-jar at the centre of the table and uncapped his ink.

What he wanted to change, on a second read:

The *Mr Caleb Waddell, my research partner* — Mei had been generous, but it scanned slightly oddly to be writing himself in by name from her side of the salutation. Better as *we* throughout, with the joint signature at the foot.

The *would the parish records contain any reference to the south-wall area* could be tightened. *Has the south wall of the churchyard been disturbed at any time we could be told about* was closer to what they actually wanted to know, but *disturbed* was a sharper word and perhaps wrong; he kept the polite version.

The *we are conscious that the parish sits on fenland* line was good — Mei knew what she was doing — and he left it.

He bracketed *or may direct us to a colleague who would* in the opening paragraph and noted *good — keeps the door open if he doesn't want to engage personally.*

He added, at the bottom, in the small hand he used for marginal notes: *Should we mention the gravestone inscription? The line about her being silent and speaking forever. — Might prompt the parson to remember the stone properly. Or might be too much. — Your call.*

He capped the ink. Folded the marked-up draft.

Across the table Mei had set her quill down for the first time and was watching him work over her letter with the small fond-amused face of a girl who had given a Ravenclaw a draft and was now waiting to see what came back.

He took up the spare quill, uncapped the ink, and went to the line in the second paragraph: *My subject of independent research for my Ancient Runes NEWT this year is the corpus of medieval English parish writings of Lincolnshire, with particular attention to the early fourteenth century.*

He drew a single line through *Ancient Runes NEWT.*

In the margin beside it, in the small hand he used for marginal notes: *awk. jarg.*

Capped the ink. Set the quill down.

Across the table Mei had been watching the edit pass without commenting. She picked up the marked-up draft, looked at the line he had crossed out and the marginal note, and did the small Mei-pause that meant she was reading both his criticism and her own draft against it.

She did not, immediately, defend or revise. She just nodded once.

"*Awkward jargon. — Yes.*"

She set the draft down beside her tea.

Mei looked at him over her tea.

The Mei-pause. The dry register coming up properly.

"*Pandering to your audience,* Caleb. — *That is a line I am going to remember.*"

She took a small sip.

"*The Lord watch over you* is — fine. — Or *yours in Christ,* which is the proper Anglican country-parson valediction in 1977 and would land harder if we wanted to commit. — *But* — Caleb. — We are *Hogwarts seventh-years* writing to a *modern Anglican parson* about a *six-hundred-year-old grave.* If we sign off with *yours in Christ* he is going to *think we are Christian* and we are *not.* — That is not pandering. That is *lying* about who we are by closing-line. — He will *write back differently* if he thinks the letter came from inside the church than if he thinks it came from a curious Hogwarts student. — *Different is not necessarily better.*"

She set her tea down.

"*The Lord watch over you* — I''ll think about. — *Maybe.* — But *be careful what register you commit to,* because the parson will reply *into* it, and we will then have to keep that register up for the rest of the correspondence."

A pause.

"*With our thanks for your kindness in receiving the letter, and our best regards* is — what I had. — *That* is the one a parson reads as *polite student, secular, did her homework about how to address me.* — *Yours faithfully* is the slightly more formal version if we want."

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 07:44

Mei's mouth did the small dry register.

"*God can watch over him and not us.*" She huffed once. "*Caleb.*"

She set her tea down properly.

"On *what does the parson know about Hogwarts.* — Hogwarts is — *officially invisible* to the Muggle Anglican church, the way it is invisible to the Muggle anything-else. — But a country parson in fenland Lincolnshire who has been at his parish for any time at all has *almost certainly* met a wizarding family or two without quite registering them as wizarding. The parson at Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh in 1977 may or may not know what Hogwarts is. — *Most likely* he reads *Hogwarts School* as *one of those Scottish boarding schools I have heard of* and lets it sit. The wizarding-Statute of Secrecy was *designed* for that — schools and institutions exist on paper as boarding schools, military academies, hospitals, and the Muggle world reads them as such. — *He will not investigate.* He will write back to *the polite seventh-year on Hogwarts School letterhead* without asking what kind of school it is."

A pause.

"*Parson* is — *correct* if he is the country priest in charge of a small parish, which St Æthelthryth''s would be. *Vicar* is — also commonly used and possibly more accurate post-1850. *Reverend* is the safe address for a letter — *the Reverend [name],* salutation *Dear Reverend [name],* signoff doesn''t need anything ecclesiastical."

She picked her quill up.

"I''ll write *the Reverend* in the address and *Dear Reverend [——]* in the salutation. — *The closing* — I am keeping *with our thanks for your kindness in receiving the letter, and our best regards.* — *Caleb. — I am not signing your *the Lord watch over you* and you cannot make me.*"

The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth.

She did not, in the end, add the gravestone inscription. She wrote a small note in the margin beside Caleb's marginal flag: *too much for first letter — hold for follow-up if he replies.*

She set the spoon down and put the back of her hand over her mouth, briefly, while she swallowed. Her shoulders shook once.

"*Caleb.* — *Inside.* — *In the Great Hall.* — *At breakfast.*"

She wiped under one lens of her glasses with her thumb. Composed.

"*The Owlery is six floors and a tower away.* — She is *not going to hear you from the Hall.* — Save it for the front step at Hagrid's, which is across the grounds and within proper owl-shouting distance, *which* — *as we have established* — *is closer than you would think.*"

She picked her spoon back up. Took a proper bite.

A few of the closer Ravenclaws had glanced over at the bellow — a third-year girl two seats down had jumped — and went back to their breakfasts.

He ate. The porridge was hot and sticky-sweet with a small drift of brown sugar. He had two pieces of toast with butter beside it. Mei finished her bowl across from him. The hall around them filled out by degrees.

By ten to eight he had eaten enough to walk a half-day's grounds work without falling over. Mei drained her tea, refolded the parson-letter draft into her satchel, and stood.

"*Hagrid. — Go.* — I will be here when you come back. *And* I will think about the Flitwick visit while you are with Athena. — *Bring her back to me at lunch.* I want to meet her properly."

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 07:51

He pulled *Companion Owls — A Quiet History* out of his satchel and slid it across the table to her. The slim leather binding, the rubbed gilt, the soft spine. Hesba Ollivander, 1922.

"*This book.* — Read page eleven. — Apparently my owl loves me. — The book said so."

Mei picked it up. Looked at the cover. Tilted it to read the gilt properly. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth went up by a fraction.

"*Companion Owls — A Quiet History.*" Quiet. "*Hesba Ollivander.* — Caleb. — *Wandmaker''s sister?*"

"*Probably aunt by now.* — 1922."

She opened it. Found page eleven. Read.

Caleb watched her face. The reading-Mei face — composed, focused, the small reorganisation of her glasses on her nose mid-paragraph — and then the small *oh* register at *companion owl flying eight hundred miles in a single night, in storm conditions, to reach a person in extremity.*

She turned a few pages forward. Read another paragraph. Set the book down.

"*Caleb.*"

Quiet.

"*This is — a real book.* — Hesba Ollivander is the — *yes,* aunt of the current Garrick Ollivander. The Ollivanders write small careful books about magical things they observe in the course of their work. — This is — *not nonsense.* — This is *the most credible source* on companion-owl bonds in the British wizarding canon."

She set her hand flat on the cover.

A small movement in her peripheral vision — Caleb saw her register it before he did — and her eyes lifted toward the high windows of the Great Hall.

A small dot, against the morning sky outside the south window, getting larger. Fast.

Mei said, very quietly:

"*Caleb. — Your arm.*"

He raised it.

The small dot resolved through the high arched window — the stained glass made her colours strange for a half-second — and Athena came in low across the Hall over the Slytherin table, banked sharp left over the staff table where Dumbledore happened to be looking up at exactly that moment with mild interest, and dove the length of the Ravenclaw table at top owl-speed.

She pulled up at his arm. Did the Athena-thing — *tried to land on his head,* he caught her with the forearm at the last second, she clipped his ear with her wing on the way past, scolded him with a small cross *kweee,* and dug her talons into his sleeve to balance.

The whole Hall had now noticed. A wave of small *what was that* heads turning along the Ravenclaw table. A few claps from the Hufflepuff table — Hufflepuffs would clap for a good owl — and a single laugh from the staff table that was almost certainly Dumbledore's.

Mei was looking at him with the small open Mei-attention he had not seen on her face before.

"*Caleb.*" Quiet. The half-millimetre. "*She came from the Owlery. — Inside the castle. — Through whatever way owls come from the Owlery to the Hall when post hasn''t arrived yet.* — *She came when you called her by name. — Inside the building.*"

She set her hand on the open book.

"*The book is right.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 07:53

Mei reached across the table — slow, the small careful approach she had used with Bartleby's principle in her head from the Diagon visit Caleb had told her about — and held the back of her hand out at Athena's height first. Did not press in. Let Athena look at her.

Athena's small head turned the full ninety degrees. Considered the offered hand with one yellow eye, then the other. Mei did not move.

After a beat Athena edged sideways down Caleb's forearm — one foot, then the other, the small precise sidestep — and stopped at his wrist. Looked up at Mei. Mantled her wings a fraction, the small *I am evaluating* gesture.

Mei waited.

Athena leaned forward — and did the test Caleb had watched her do to Hagrid yesterday morning — the small precise *I am evaluating you* nip on the proffered knuckle. Mei's hand did not flinch. Did not even register the bite-pressure visibly, which was — Caleb registered — a small competence Mei had not advertised.

Athena straightened. Mantled her wings the small *acceptable* shape. Sidestepped onto Mei's hand properly.

Mei's face, for the first time, did the quiet open *oh* of meeting an animal who had decided to come onto her hand. She did not coo. She did not try to scratch immediately. She just held her hand steady and let Athena settle.

Then — slow, thoughtful — Mei brought her free hand up and, with one fingertip, very gently scratched the small place at the back of Athena's head where the feathers met her neck. The way Caleb did. Careful. Proper.

Athena closed her eyes.

Mei said, quiet, to Athena:

"*Hello, sweetheart. — I have been hearing about you for a long time.*"

A beat.

She glanced up at Caleb. The Mei-warmth, full.

"*Caleb. — She is — magnificent.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 07:55

Mei made a small choking laugh into her tea at *parsnip.*

"*The parsnip,*" she repeated. "*Caleb.* — *Reverend Parsnip-on-the-Marsh.* — I am not writing your *pygmy from the Lord* into the letter. I am, *however,* keeping *parsnip* and using it on you for the rest of our lives."

Athena, on her hand, made a small *kweee* of having decided that the conversation was now too much and looked up at Caleb expectantly. Mei held her hand out toward Caleb's arm. Athena sidestepped back across.

He kissed Mei on the cheek — brief, warm, the small public *bye, love* register — and her free hand briefly cupped his jaw as he pulled away. The small *go on* gesture.

He stood. Athena adjusted on his forearm. He shouldered his satchel. Mei was already pulling her own satchel onto her shoulder, on her way to wherever she was going for the morning.

He walked out of the Great Hall with Athena on his arm, past the Slytherin table where Cass had her *Daily Prophet* up but registered the owl on his arm with a small over-the-top-of-the-paper nod, past the Hufflepuff table where the morning clappers gave him another small grin, and out into the Entrance Hall.

Down the steps. Across the lawn. The morning was the proper Saturday morning of early September — cold air, dew not yet burned off, the lake silver, the path to Hagrid's hut familiar from yesterday.

Athena rode his forearm with the easy posture of an owl who had decided she was going somewhere with her boy and was not going to need to hurry.

By eight o'clock he was at Hagrid's door. The smoke from the chimney was already going. Fang was on his rope, head up, tail giving the great lazy thump of a boarhound who had registered an arrival.

He knocked.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:00

He stepped down off the doorstep and crouched beside Fang. The boarhound's great head came up to meet him — slow, the long-suffering eyebrows of a dog who had seen many morning visitors and had decided this one warranted attention — and Caleb scratched the soft place behind one enormous ear with his free hand. Fang made the *uhf* sound of a dog accepting service. His tail did the proper thump.

Athena, on Caleb's other arm, considered Fang from a height of about eighteen inches above the dog's head with one yellow eye. Fang considered Athena. Neither registered alarm. Athena had grown up around Caleb's mother's neighbour's old terrier and knew that dogs were *dogs* and not *threats unless you were a vole.* Fang, for his part, had spent four decades around Hagrid's animal traffic and considered most things uninteresting unless they smelled of meat.

The hut door opened.

Hagrid filled the doorway in his shirtsleeves and braces, holding a piece of toast the size of a paperback novel.

"*Mornin', Caleb. — Yer on time. Good lad.*" He registered Athena on Caleb's other arm. The small black eyes warmed. "*An'' yeh brought 'er. Excellent. — Come ''round the back, both o'yeh. — Lower paddock first. Then we''ll see.*"

He stepped out properly, pulled the door to behind him, and started off round the side of the hut at the easy stride of a man who knew exactly what was needed.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:02

He came round the side of the hut after Hagrid. Athena rode his forearm; Fang stayed at his rope by the door, head down again, his work for the morning evidently the door not the followers.

Behind the hut: the proper Hagrid back-yard. The pumpkin patch off to the right, the great heavy fruit on the vine still going, three weeks before they would need cutting. A small chicken-run beyond it. A workshop-shed off to the left with the door propped open and tools visible inside — saws, spades, what looked like a bag of seed.

The lower paddock was a hundred yards beyond the pumpkin patch — a fenced-off rough pasture sloping gently down toward the lake, with a row of small wooden hutches along the far fence and a single old beech in the middle giving shade.

Hagrid pointed.

"*Lower paddock. — School nifflers. Six of ''em. — They live in those hutches at the back. Yeh feed ''em twice a Sat''day mornin' an' once Sun''day evenin' — small handful o' the brown grain in the bin by the gate, an' a coin or two o' the school''s tarnished sickles fer enrichment, which yeh take *back* at the end o' the morning.* Don''t leave coins in there — they''ll *bury* ''em an' yeh''ll be diggin' the paddock up till Christmas."

He pushed the gate open. The latch was the proper Hagrid latch — a wooden peg on a string. The paddock smelled of grass, damp earth, and the faint metallic note of a place where small animals that liked metal had been living.

Six small dark heads popped up around the paddock — from under the beech, from the shadow of a hutch, from a small hole one of them had dug in the corner — and registered the arrival.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:03

Hagrid laughed — the great bellowing one — and shook his head.

"*Pet ''em.* — Caleb. — *Nifflers don''t want pettin'. They want — coin.* — But yeh can put yer hand out an' if one comes ter yeh, *let it.* They''re curious. They''ll come up an'' check yeh fer anythin'' shiny. — *Don''t* wear anythin'' shiny on yer hands. Mei''s pen?"

"*She has it.*"

"*Good.* — Yer pocket-watch — *brass,* not gold, yeh''re fine. — But empty yer trouser-pockets if yeh''ve got Sickles in there, ''cause they''ll have ''em out o'' yer pocket without yeh feelin'' it. — Bag in the bin, mind."

He gestured at a small leather pouch of tarnished sickles in a wooden bin by the gate, beside the brown-grain bin. There were perhaps a dozen sickles in it — the school's enrichment-coins, properly tarnished so they would not get confused with real currency at audit time.

"*Take a small handful o' grain. Scatter it under the beech. — Take three o'' the sickles, drop ''em in three different places in the paddock — under the beech, near the far fence, near the gate. They''ll find ''em. They''ll bury ''em. — At the end o'' the mornin' yeh dig ''em up. — *Six o' six,* mind. *Always count.*"

Athena, on Caleb's arm, was watching the nifflers with the small alert *what are these* yellow-eyed attention. The nifflers had not yet noticed her. One — closer to the gate than the others, smaller than the rest — had begun to creep across the grass toward Caleb at the slow careful pace of a small creature evaluating a new human.

## User

[[I dont understand the coins? I have to give them coins then dig them up?]]

## Narrator

OOC: Yes — that's the routine, and on reread it's an odd one without context. The reasoning:

Nifflers in canon are creatures whose entire instinct-set is *find shiny things and bury them.* That's what they do. They don't *use* the coins; they don't *value* them; they just compulsively retrieve and stash anything shiny that catches their eye. It's not optional behaviour — it's the central thing about being a niffler.

A niffler in a paddock with nothing to find gets bored, restless, and destructive. They start digging up the grass for stones. They start fighting each other. They get unwell.

So the school's enrichment-routine is: drop a few worthless tarnished sickles around the paddock, let the nifflers do what they instinctively do — find them, get the small dopamine hit of acquisition, bury them — and then at the end of the session you dig up the buried sickles, recount them, and put them back in the bin for next time. Same six coins, every Saturday, used as enrichment toys. Hence *always count six of six* — if a niffler kept one, you'd have a niffler walking around with school property and a sickle short next week.

Modern equivalent in Muggle zoos: scatter-feeding for primates, hidden-food puzzles for big cats, foraging boxes for parrots. Enrichment via species-typical behaviour using a stand-in object the keeper retrieves at the end. The niffler version is just *with shiny things they happen to bury rather than food they happen to eat.*

So Caleb's job this morning, three steps:

1. Scatter the grain (food).
2. Drop the three sickles in three places (enrichment toys).
3. Watch them work / let one come to him if it's curious.

At the end of the morning he digs up six buried sickles (which means he has to track *which* nifflers buried *which* coin *where,* or at least follow them around mentally, because nifflers are good at hiding things and the count must be exact).

Continuing whenever — small niffler is creeping toward Caleb.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:04

He had one sickle of his own — silver, untarnished, his own school-spending money — in the pocket of his robe. He fished it out. Sat down properly on the grass just inside the paddock gate, legs crossed, satchel on the ground beside him. Athena adjusted on his forearm at the change in elevation, decided the new height suited her, and went still.

He held the sickle flat on his open palm and rested the palm on his knee.

The smallest niffler — the one who had been creeping toward him from the gate — stopped about six feet away. Considered him.

The other five had registered the change in his posture and were now also watching, but from their existing positions: two from under the beech, one from the corner where it had been digging, two from the shadow of the hutches.

Athena was a small concern. The smallest niffler had clocked her on Caleb's arm and was holding the careful distance of an animal who had not yet decided whether the bird was a threat. Athena, for her part, was still in the *what are these* yellow-eyed attention but with no aggressive posture — wings folded, head still, the small calm of an owl who had decided that small ground-things were not her hunting business this morning.

The smallest niffler edged a foot closer. Stopped. Edged another foot. Stopped.

Hagrid, behind Caleb at the gate, said quietly:

"*Don''t move, lad. Let her come.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:05

He gave Athena the small *down you go* gesture with his elbow — the move he had used at home a hundred times when he wanted her on the ground rather than the arm — and Athena, after one judgmental look at the grass and one back at his face, hopped down. Lit on the grass beside his crossed leg. Considered the niffler.

The niffler considered her back.

Athena did the thing — the Athena-thing, the small lopsided performance she did when she was making herself look interesting to a small ground creature, the discipline of a runt screech — sorry — *Pygmy Owl* who had grown up doing this for Caleb's mother's terrier in the kitchen. She mantled her wings the small way that made her look bigger and then ducked her head down again. Stamped her crooked little legs once. Hopped sideways three inches and stamped again. Did the small *kweee* of *I am here.*

The niffler — the smallest one, the shy one — had gone *very* still during the wing-mantle and then registered the rest of the performance as *not threat-display* but *what is this thing actually doing.* Curiosity won over caution. He edged forward another foot. Then another.

Hagrid, behind Caleb, said very quietly:

"*Bloody hell.*"

The niffler reached the edge of Caleb's knee. Stopped. Looked up at the sickle on Caleb's open palm with the proper niffler-eyes — black, beady, full of the small *that is mine now* register every niffler made when they registered shiny — and very gently, with great delicacy, put one front paw on Caleb's knee, lifted the other to his palm, and took the sickle.

He did not run. He sat back on his haunches with the sickle in his front paws, turned it over once, registered that it was a real proper-mint sickle (much better than the tarnished enrichment ones he had been working with all week), and considered.

Then he looked up at Caleb.

Athena, on the grass beside him, gave a small *kweee* that was — Caleb registered — *approving* rather than *protective.* The two small creatures — owl and niffler — held a beat of mutual professional acknowledgement.

The smallest niffler tucked the sickle against his small chest with both front paws, stayed sitting, and did not run.

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:06

He closed his palm — slow, no sudden movement, the careful Caleb-with-an-animal slowness — and brought the closed fist back to his knee. Then extended his index finger of the same hand, curved, toward the grass at niffler-height, six inches from where the small creature was sitting with the sickle clutched against his chest.

The niffler considered the offered finger.

Black beady eyes flicked from Caleb's face to the finger to the sickle in his own paws and back.

He did not, immediately, move toward the finger. He clutched the sickle a fraction tighter. The small *I am evaluating whether this is a trick to get my coin back* assessment.

Caleb did not move the finger. Did not move at all. Held still, the curved finger an offered thing, no closer than where he had set it.

Athena, on the grass beside him, gave a small *kweee* that was — again — the *acceptable* register, the small endorsement of a fellow small creature.

The niffler edged forward an inch. The finger remained where it was.

Another inch.

He sniffed the curved finger from a distance of about an inch. The small black nose twitching. Caleb could feel the small puff of breath. Then, very gently, the niffler pressed the side of his small head against the curved finger — a single small *I am acknowledging* nuzzle — and then stepped back, sickle still clutched against his chest, and turned to scuttle back across the paddock toward the hutches with his prize.

He went about ten feet, stopped, looked back at Caleb once, and carried on.

Hagrid, behind, made a small sound that was definitely not laughter and possibly an expletive.

"*Caleb.*"

He came round the gate properly. Crouched the great Hagrid-crouch — which still left his head higher than Caleb's standing — and looked at the niffler retreating with the sickle.

"*Tha''s the runt o' the litter.* He''s — *not the easy one.* Last summer Kettleburn had a nephew tryin' ter get him to come to hand fer three weeks an' he wouldn''t. — Yeh sat down with him for *three minutes* an' he came an' took yer coin and *let you touch him.* — *An'' Athena there.* — *Bloody hell, lad.*"

"I told you. a new person. 50/50 shot of love or hate me. but animals. it just works. I don't know why. but I like it and they make me happy..." "Athena, do you want a shoulder ride or to go fly while I feed?"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:08

Hagrid huffed a small laugh at *50/50 shot of love or hate me* and stayed in his crouch beside Caleb, watching the smallest niffler reach the hutches and disappear inside one of them with the silver sickle.

Athena, on the grass, considered the question.

She did the small head-tilt — full ninety degrees, one yellow eye on Caleb's face — and then the *kweee* that was her *I am thinking* note. Hopped sideways twice. Considered the open paddock. The hutches. The sky above the lake.

Then, with no further ceremony, she launched.

Up off the grass with the small downward thrust of her crooked left wing, into the proper compensating list-to-starboard climb she did. Banked over the paddock fence at six feet, lifted out over the hutches, and rose in a wide lazy spiral above the paddock at thirty feet, where she hung on the morning thermals and watched.

Caleb watched her for a moment. The small lopsided silhouette against the morning sky. *Pygmy from the Lord.* Out and about for as long as she wanted.

He turned back to the paddock. Hagrid was already moving.

"*Right then. — Grain bin first. Small handful, scatter under the beech. Then three sickles in three places.* — Yeh''ve already given the runt his real one — *don''t take it back, lad, he''s earned it.* — Add three tarnished from the bin like usual. He''ll bury it with the other coins; we''ll find it at the end an' yeh can decide whether ter leave it with him or take it back."

He went to the grain bin. Pulled the lid off. Stood back and let Caleb come to it.

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:09

He took three of the tarnished sickles from the leather pouch in the bin and dropped them into the pocket of his robe — they made the small dull *clink* of tarnished silver against itself, distinctly different from the brighter ring of his own untarnished coins. Then a handful of brown grain from the grain-bin. The grain was the proper school-niffler-feed: dark roasted oats with a small amount of dried mealworm mixed in, the kind of mix that smelled faintly nutty and mostly of warm earth.

He kept perhaps a third of the handful in his closed left fist. The rest he carried in his open right.

Crossed the paddock to the beech tree in the middle. The five visible nifflers tracked him as he moved — the two from under the beech itself stepped a polite three feet out of his way without leaving the tree's shade. He scattered the handful in his right at the proper niffler-scattering pattern Hagrid had not exactly described but which Caleb worked out by feel: not a single pile (they would fight) but a wide loose distribution across about six feet of grass under the canopy, so each niffler had its own patch.

The five nifflers were on the grain within a beat — the small *fuwf-fuwf* of niffler bodies on grass, the small noses going down, the small efficient picking up of grains.

He stepped back from the beech a few paces. Walked the paddock loop Hagrid had described — dropped a tarnished sickle near the far fence, dropped a second by the gate, dropped the third about ten feet from the beech where one of the tree-nifflers would find it within thirty seconds. Stayed clear of the hutches where the runt had taken his prize.

Then he came back to a spot midway between the beech and the gate, sat down on the grass again, and opened his closed left fist. A small handful of grain, in his palm, on his knee.

He waited.

The runt niffler — the smallest one — was visible at the entrance to his hutch, sitting in the doorway, watching the paddock. Watching Caleb. The silver sickle was, presumably, already buried inside.

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:11

He gave his palm a small shake — the grain shifted on it, the small *whisp* sound of seeds against skin — and held it steady on his knee.

The runt at the hutch entrance went still.

Then — slowly, the small careful approach of a niffler who had decided that the grain might also be his for the asking — he stepped out of the hutch doorway and started across the paddock toward Caleb. The other five were still under the beech with their own grain; they did not register the runt's movement.

The runt came at his slow proper pace. Stopped four feet away. Considered.

Caleb did not move. Did not shake the palm again. The grain was on offer; the offer was on the palm; if the runt wanted it, the runt would come.

The runt edged forward. Three feet. Two feet. One foot.

He sat back on his haunches at the same place he had sat the first time — Caleb's knee height — and looked up at the offered palm of grain. Considered.

Did not, this time, climb. Did not put a paw on Caleb's knee.

Instead he stretched up — small, on his hind legs, his small front paws held against his chest — and reached his small black nose up to the edge of Caleb's palm. Took a single grain. Stepped back. Sat. Chewed. Swallowed. Considered Caleb again.

Then he came back. Took two grains this time. Stepped back. Chewed. Swallowed.

By the third trip he had decided Caleb's palm was a feeding station rather than a trick, and he came up the small distance and ate from the palm directly, his small body warm against Caleb's knee, his small front paws braced on Caleb's wrist for balance. The grain in the palm went down by degrees as the small careful efficient eating proceeded.

Athena, overhead, made a single distant *kweee* from her thirty-foot thermal — *registered, fine* — and kept her circle.

Hagrid, by the gate, said quietly:

"*That, lad, is — yer keeper.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:14

Hagrid laughed — the great proper bellowing one — and shook his head.

"*Caleb Waddell.* — That is the most *Manchester* thing I have heard out of a seventh-year in twenty years. — *Give a man a niffler an' he can rob a bunch of people all day.* — *No,* lad. *No,* I am not handing yeh a niffler to take to Diagon. — Kettleburn would have my hide an' the Ministry would have *both* o' our hides an' the *British Niffler Owners Association,* which is a *real bloody thing,* would *write to the Wizengamot.*"

He wiped his eye with the back of his enormous hand. Came round properly now and lowered himself onto the grass beside Caleb — the great Hagrid-sit, the paddock visibly compressing slightly under him — and watched the runt finish the last few grains in Caleb's palm.

"*But.* — Tha''s a real thing yer doin' here, lad. — *Tha''s* the kind o' Saturday-mornin'-grounds-keepin' I don''t see most years. — Yeh come back next Sat''day. *Same time.* The runt''ll be at the gate fer yeh. — An'' if Mei wants ter come along — bring her. — Athena'll fly with the school owls a bit, get used to ''em. — *Good.*"

The runt finished the last grain. Sat back on his haunches. Considered Caleb again. Then very deliberately reached up to the back of his small front paw — the same gesture he had made with the head-press earlier — and bumped his nose against the side of Caleb's wrist where it rested on the knee. Twice. Stepped back. Turned. Trotted back toward his hutch with the small efficient niffler-walk.

Athena, overhead, came down in her proper lopsided spiral and landed on the grass beside Caleb, just where she had been before. Settled.

Hagrid clapped Caleb's shoulder with one enormous hand — gently, the small considerate Hagrid-gentle — and stood up.

"*Right. — Lower paddock''s done. Yeh''ve fed ''em and gotten yer little ''un settled. — Now I''ve got the school owls fer yeh. — *That* is where Athena gets her introduction proper. — Up ter the Owlery, lad.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:21

The forty heads turned at the door. Forty pairs of eyes registered Caleb-and-Hagrid as humans-with-business — recognised, expected — and then registered Athena, who they had presumably already met during her three nights in residence.

Athena, for her part, was relaxed on Caleb's forearm in the way she had not been on the way up the staircase. She had registered her own quarters. The small *I am home* posture replaced the *I am evaluating new territory* posture without ceremony.

Hagrid laughed at his own line — quietly, the *of course she lives here* register — and tapped the side of his head.

"*Right, lad. — Daft o' me. — She''s been in residence three nights, hasn''t she. — All right. *I want ter watch ''er fly with the others a bit.* Set her on the perch by the south window — that''s the one she''s been usin', I''d wager — an'' we''ll see how she gets on."

He pointed at a perch on the south wall, low, near one of the great open windows. There was a small empty perch-spot on it among three Tawnies and a Barn Owl, and on the floor beneath it: a small loose mound of pellets, white-and-grey, the proper indication of an owl who had been roosting there.

Athena sidestepped down Caleb's forearm to his wrist as he approached the perch. He held his arm to it and she stepped across, neat, the small precise sidestep of an owl arriving at her own usual spot. She mantled her wings briefly — the small *I am back* gesture for the benefit of her flock-mates — and the surrounding Tawnies registered her with the polite uninterested *yes, you* nods of birds who had decided last week that this small lopsided new owl was acceptable company.

He'd have seen her arriving and leaving on post duties for six years from a distance. *Watch her fly with the others a bit* is *I would like to see your odd little owl in the air, properly, in her real home.*

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:22

He stepped over to the south window with Athena still on her perch — held his right forearm to her, the small *come on* gesture — and she sidestepped onto his wrist neatly. Walked her to the great arched window. The morning air came in cold and clean off the lake, the Highland September brightness proper now.

"*Off you go.*"

He lifted his forearm a small smooth degree — not a throw, not a toss, just the small *go* cue she had known since she was a fledgling — and Athena launched.

She caught the air outside the window with her crooked left wing — the small compensating list to starboard the way she always did — and was, in the bright morning sky outside the Owlery tower, abruptly the smallest owl in a substantial scattering of school owls on their morning stretch. There were perhaps a dozen Tawnies up in lazy circles in the air outside the tower, two Barn Owls higher up, the great pair of Eagle Owls ranging the further distance over the loch.

Athena took her own line. She did not join a circle — Caleb had not seen her fly in formation in her life — but she found her own piece of air about twenty feet from the south wall of the tower and worked it. The small lopsided silhouette against the morning sky, the deliberate working of the off-balance left wing, the small list to starboard that she compensated for with a slight banking adjustment Caleb could now see *was* the Pygmy Owl flight signature from the colour-plate — not really crooked, *normal* for her species, the small particular flight-line of *Glaucidium passerinum.*

She climbed. Banked. Stooped briefly — at nothing, just for the joy of stooping. Pulled up. Caleb watched her work the air properly for the first time in his life with other small species owls in the same sky for context.

Hagrid, beside him, was at the window with the small *I am paying attention* posture of a man who knew what he was looking at. After a beat he said quietly:

"*She's not a screech, lad.*"

A pause.

"*Tha''s a Pygmy. — European Pygmy. — *Glaucidium passerinum.* — Continental species, very rare here. — How''d yeh come by *her* in a Manchester wood, then?*"

## User

"she was just there and I scooped her up and didn't even see a nest above or anything, assuming the parents would have taken her back. I thought she had a broken wing and took her to a vet. but nope. that is just... her."

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:25

Hagrid nodded slowly. The small black eyes stayed on Athena working her piece of sky.

"*Tha''s how it goes, sometimes.* — Pygmy Owls don''t leave their fledglings. — *Ever.* — They don''t abandon. They guard the nest till the chicks are flyin', an'' if a chick can''t fly the parents *carry it down* till it can. — A Pygmy fledgling on the ground in a Manchester wood, no nest above, no parents callin' — *somethin'* happened to her parents. *Or* somethin' happened to her clutch. *Or* — *more interesting* — she wasn''t a fledgling on the ground. She was *brought* there."

He said the last sentence at the proper Hagrid-level — quiet, no drama, just the consideration of a man who had been thinking about animals for forty-five years.

"*Doesn''t change anythin'. — Yeh found her, yeh raised her, she''s yers.* — But it''s — odd, lad. Pygmies don''t turn up in *Manchester woods.* They turn up in proper conifer forests on the Continent. — She came from a long way. — *Or somebody else did.*"

He kept watching her.

"*Tha''s a fine flight, mind. — She works that wing the way her species works that wing. — Not a defect. Tha''s the *Glaucidium* style. — She''s exactly what she should be.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:26

Hagrid choked on his own breath laughing — the great proper Hagrid laugh, the kind that went on for a beat longer than a polite laugh would and ended in a wheeze — and put one enormous hand against the stone of the Owlery wall to steady himself.

"*A fifty-year-old woman who''s been eatin'' an'' throwin'' up mouse pellets.* — Caleb. — *Caleb Waddell.* — Yer goin'' ter be the death o' me an'' it''s the second mornin' o' term."

He wiped his eye with the back of his sleeve.

"*No.* — Tha''s — *Animagus* yeh''re thinkin' of. — An' Animagi don''t get *stuck.* They register at the Ministry an' they go in an' out o' their form on purpose. — A *failed* transfiguration would unbind itself within months — yeh can''t hold a non-natural form fer years without yer body remindin' yeh of itself, an' an Animagus *natural* form *isn''t* the wrong body, it''s a *second* body, properly yers. — Tha''s not what Athena is. — Athena is *an owl.* A real one. *European Pygmy Owl.*"

A pause. The proper Hagrid register came back.

"*But.* — *Bein' a real Pygmy Owl* an'' *bein' an unusual one* aren't — exclusive. — Yeh found her in the *wrong wood,* lad. *Some* explanation lives behind that. *Most* explanations are sad, mind — most likely her parents copped it on the way through England, or somethin' got the clutch. — But yer not wrong ter think it''s *odd.* — *Tha''s* the right way ter hold it. — *Odd, an'' she''s yers, an'' she''s a real owl.*"

He clapped Caleb's shoulder once — the gentle Hagrid-pat — and turned back to the window.

Athena was banking lazily on the morning thermal. A Tawny had come up to her piece of sky to investigate, and the two of them were doing the small mutual acknowledgement-circle that owls did when sharing thermal — a polite double-pass, neither pressing the other, the small *yes, you, fine* recognition.

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:27

Hagrid huffed.

"*A Snitch.*" He shook his great head. "*Caleb, lad.* — A Golden Snitch is *the size o'' a walnut.* — Athena is *the size o'' a tea-cup.* — She''d be the most *visible* Snitch in the history of the game. — *Both Seekers'd just follow the small lopsided owl.* — *Worst Snitch ever made.*"

He laughed properly again. The Owlery's residents looked over at the noise; one of the Eagle Owls registered Hagrid's laugh with the small *yes, that one* eye-narrow that Eagle Owls did at humans they had long ago decided were acceptable.

"*Tha''s a daft thing to say, mind.* — *I like it.*"

A pause. He glanced at his pocket-watch — the great Hagrid-watch, brass, the size of a small dinner-plate — and registered the time.

"*Right then. — Lower paddock done. Athena settled. — *I''ve got* a list o'' grounds-work fer yeh today, lad, but I reckon yeh''ve done the part I most wanted yeh ter do, which was *show me yeh can sit with an animal.* — Yeh can. — *Decisively.* — Rest of it''s fence-checks an'' compost-turnin'' an'' the kitchens'' ash-bins, which I''ll do meself, an'' yeh can come back next Sat''day fer the same again with the runt.*"

He clapped Caleb's shoulder.

"*Off yeh go. — *Athena''ll come back ter yeh whenever yeh shout fer her, apparently. — Sat''day next, eight sharp, gate o'' the lower paddock.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:31

He went back down the spiral staircase the way they had come up — Hagrid had stayed at the Owlery for some other piece of grounds-work — and emerged at the foot of the east tower into the proper morning. Crossed the lawn fifty yards from the castle wall to a clear patch where his shout would not echo through any open windows or send the Owlery's residents into the air en masse.

Cupped his hands. Faced the Owlery tower. Bellowed:

"*AAAATHHHHHEEEEENNNNAAAA!*"

The shout went out across the lawn. A single rook in a tree near the lake registered it and re-registered it as not-his-business.

She came in fast. Out the south window of the Owlery — Caleb saw the small dot detach from the tower wall — and across the lawn at top owl-speed. The proper Athena-thing: pulled up at the last moment, tried to land on his head, he caught her on the forearm at the last second, she clipped his ear with her wing on the way past, scolded him with a small cross *kweee,* and dug her talons into his sleeve to balance.

He scratched the back of her head with one knuckle.

"*Sweetheart. — You can go back to the Owlery if you want. Or come and meet Mei properly with me — she'd love to see you again. — You'll find your way back either way. Up to you.*"

Athena considered.

She did the small head-tilt — full ninety degrees, one yellow eye on his face — and then mantled her wings the small *I am coming with you* gesture and settled on his arm properly.

He started across the lawn back to the castle. Athena rode his forearm with the easy posture of an owl who had decided she was now *the boy's owl on his arm and not the Owlery owl on a perch* for the rest of the morning. The lake on his right, silver, the sun fully up now. The walk to the castle was about four minutes.

He went in by the side door he had used yesterday morning — past the kitchens, up by the back staircase rather than the main one, on the small theory that an owl on his arm in the busy Saturday morning corridors was going to draw more comment than he wanted. Up to the fourth floor. Library check first — Mei had said *I will be here when you come back* but Saturday morning library was not a guaranteed place, she might have moved.

The library was open. He went in.

Mei was at her south-aisle table, plait over her shoulder, working through what looked like the next section of the herbal. The locked book open in front of her. Notes-paper to one side. A fresh quill.

She looked up at his approach. Registered Athena on his forearm. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth went up by a full millimetre.

"she likes me more when you are with me, did you see that?"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:39

Mei's mouth did the soft Mei-warmth, full.

"*She likes you more when I am with you.*" Quiet. "*Caleb. — That is — sweet.*"

She set her quill down. Closed the locked herbal on her finger. Held her hand out, low, palm flat — the proper Athena-greeting that Mei had now done correctly twice and that Athena had registered as *acceptable human, knows what she is doing.*

Athena sidestepped down Caleb's forearm to his wrist, considered Mei's hand, and stepped across without the test-nip this time. Settled on Mei's hand. Mantled her wings briefly — the small *I remember you* — and let Mei's free hand come up and scratch the small place at the back of her head.

Athena closed her eyes.

Mei said quietly to Athena: "*Hello, sweetheart. — Caleb says you flew with the school owls this morning. — Did they keep up.*"

Athena opened one yellow eye at *did they keep up* and looked at Mei sideways with an expression that was, Caleb registered, *exactly the right amount of smug.*

Mei laughed — quiet, the proper library register — and looked up at Caleb.

"*Caleb. — Madam Pince is going to have words about the owl in the library within ninety seconds.* — *Briefly.* — Tell me. — Hagrid?"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:40

Mei's free hand paused on Athena's head.

"*You own a niffler.*"

Flat. The Mei-register that meant she had heard the sentence and was waiting for the rest.

She did not, immediately, address the *Pygmy from the Lord* — she was clearly choosing to deal with the niffler claim first, on the principle that one of the two sentences was potentially actionable.

"*Caleb. — Hagrid did not give you a niffler. — *Tell me Hagrid did not give you a niffler.* — There is a *British Niffler Owners Association,* Caleb. — *I have read about them. — They write to people.*"

Athena, on her hand, registered the change in Mei's tone and opened both yellow eyes properly. Tilted her head ninety degrees. The small *what is happening* attention.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:41

Mei's eyes did the small *I am parsing what you have just said* register.

"*He said the niffler was yours which is ambiguous. — It lives here. — It is becoming very fond of you.*"

She nodded slowly. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came back.

"*All right. — That is — what Hagrid said about you and an animal in the lower paddock. — Which is — different from owning a niffler. — *You have a paddock-niffler who has decided you are his.* That is — a very nice thing to have. — *Not a writeable-to-the-British-Niffler-Owners-Association situation.* — *Good.*"

She let her hand stay on Athena's head. Athena had relaxed back into eyes-closed.

"*Now. — Pygmy from the Lord. — Caleb. — Tell me Hagrid registered her as a Pygmy on his own and you did not have to tell him.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:42

Mei's eyebrows went up.

"*She broke open the skies herself to come here.*" Slowly. "*Caleb. — Hagrid did not say that.* — Hagrid says practical things about animals. He may have said *somethin' brought her,* which is — *a real Hagrid sentence* — and you, in your Caleb-brain, between the lower paddock and the library, have *romanticised it into prophecy.*"

She did not, however, look unamused. She looked the small *I will let him have this one* warm-amused.

"*Glaucidium passerinum* turning up as a fledgling in a Manchester wood is — *odd.* — Hagrid is right. *I* read it as *something happened to her parents on a passage from the Continent and the chick was orphaned far from where Pygmies live.* — *Hagrid* clearly read it as *something happened to her parents OR she was brought there.* — *You* are reading it as *she descended from heaven on her crooked left wing to find you, Caleb Waddell, in a wood near Manchester.*"

The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth.

"*All three* of those readings are *available.* — I am — *not closing any of them.* — *But.* — Whichever one is true, *Hagrid is also right* that she is now *a real owl,* and *yours,* and *exactly what she should be.* — *That is the part I am keeping.*"

Athena, on her hand, made a small approving *kweee.*

A new voice, behind Caleb, at the proper Pince-register:

"*Mr Waddell. — Miss Chang. — There is an owl in my library.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 08:43

He let his right sleeve fall over Athena — the small drape of school-robe fabric that had been her tucking-shelter since she was three months old in his bedroom in Manchester — and Athena did the small efficient *yes, this* and walked up his wrist into the sleeve, settled at the elbow with her small head out at the cuff, looking out, the small *I am hidden but I am observing* posture that was her own particular comedy.

"*Owl???*"

He looked at his sleeve with theatrical confusion.

"*Whaaaa?* — Madam Pince. — *Sorry.* — I will take her out. — *But your book says she should be with me, and I trust an Ollivander. — And Hagrid said she toppled gods to come here, so.*"

Madam Pince stood at the end of the table with her arms folded over her severe black robes. The half-moon spectacles. The sharp eyes did the assessment of *Caleb Waddell, seventh-year, Ravenclaw, has not damaged a book in six years, currently in the library with an owl tucked into his sleeve.*

She did not, immediately, respond. She looked at Athena's small head poking out of his cuff. The small careful Pince-pause.

Then, in the small dry register Caleb had not heard her use before:

"*Mr Waddell.* — *I will allow you* — *one library visit per term* — *with that owl* — *on the strict condition that she does not leave your sleeve, your shoulder, or your immediate person while inside this room.* — *No flying. No perching on the rafters. No interaction with the books.*"

A pause.

"*Hesba Ollivander* is — a credible source. — *I read* the same book in *1923,* Mr Waddell. — *Her conclusions hold up.* — *That is the only reason this conversation is going as it is.*"

She turned the same sharp eyes on Mei, briefly — *Miss Chang, you are aiding and abetting this* — and then back to Caleb.

"*One visit per term. — Today is the visit. — Use it well, Mr Waddell.*"

She turned and walked back to her desk in the proper Pince-stride, the small severe *I am extending a courtesy I do not extend* posture deliberate.

Mei, beside Caleb, was looking at him with the small *did Madam Pince just allow that* face.

They went out of the library together. Madam Pince, at her desk, registered the departure with a small approving micro-nod and went back to her sorting.

The walk down to the third floor was the proper Saturday-corridor walk — quieter than weekday, but not empty. A few students going to their own Saturday business: a Hufflepuff fifth-year with a stack of parchment and the *I am ahead of my homework* posture, two Gryffindor third-years arguing about something in low excited voices, a single seventh-year Ravenclaw Caleb didn't know going the other way with a brisk nod.

Athena rode Mei's shoulder. The students who passed registered the owl with the small *seventh-year prefect with an owl on her shoulder, Saturday morning, sure* register and went on without comment.

The Charms classroom door was open. Inside: the long classroom with rows of small desks, the chalkboard at the front, and behind the chalkboard the small wooden door of Flitwick's private office. A handwritten sign on a small stand outside the office door read *PROF. F. FLITWICK — SATURDAY OFFICE HOURS — 9:00–12:00 — ONE AT A TIME, PLEASE — KNOCK AND WAIT*.

The classroom was empty. Mei went down the centre aisle toward the front, past the rows of desks, with the small Mei-precision Caleb had been watching since Tuesday morning at the Manchester platform.

The clock above the chalkboard read 8:51. Nine minutes early.

She stopped at one of the front-row desks and sat down on the edge of it. Looked at Caleb.

"*Wait.*" Quiet. "*One at a time. — He''ll come out at nine.*"

## Narrator

OOC: Image-prompt portrait of Mei in general — not the current scene. Drawing from her character_profile and everything we've established in play.

**Portrait prompt — Mei Chang, in general**

Photorealistic full-figure character portrait of a young woman, eighteen years old, half-Chinese half-British, wizarding school student in late-1970s Britain. Subject is Mei Chang, a seventh-year Hogwarts Ravenclaw. Three-quarter view, eye contact with the viewer, neutral background — soft slate-grey or muted dark teal, like a formal painted-portrait backdrop, with very faint atmospheric depth, not a busy environment. Lighting is window-light from her left, soft and natural, slightly cool, the gentle late-afternoon light of a Scottish September interior; a low warm fill on the right to keep the shadows from going hard. Cinematic still photograph register, not painted, not stylised — the lived realism of a 1977 portrait.

Physical description in detail.

She is slim, of medium-short height — about 5'3" — with a slight, contained build rather than a delicate one. Her face is heart-shaped with a softly tapered jaw, high cheekbones, and a small straight nose. Her skin is the warm pale-olive of mixed Chinese-British ancestry, faintly cool-toned in indoor light. Her mouth is small and well-defined; the corners of it tend to lift by a single millimetre when she is amused, which is most of the time, and her resting expression is composed-attentive rather than smiling — she is the kind of woman whose face does most of its communication in fractional movements at the mouth and brow.

Her hair is straight, glossy, very dark brown approaching black, worn in a single thick plait that falls down her back to mid-spine; the plait is neat but not severe, with a few wisps escaping at the temples. The hairline is even, the brows fine and dark, her own eyebrow shape rather than plucked thin. Her eyes are a deep warm brown — almost black at a distance — with a slight monolid epicanthal fold inherited from her Chinese mother's side; the eyes are clear, watchful, intelligent, and amused without being unkind.

She wears wire-frame round spectacles — small, dark-bronze frames, the lenses lightly tinted by their prescription, sitting on her nose with the small habitual crookedness of glasses that have been adjusted a thousand times by their wearer. She has the small unconscious gesture of pushing them up with the bridge of her index finger.

She wears proper Hogwarts school robes — open black robes over a deep blue jumper and a white shirt collar, the small Ravenclaw bronze-and-blue tie loosely knotted at the collar in the late-1970s prefect register. A silver prefect's badge is pinned crooked to the left breast of her robe (always crooked — she does not bother to straighten it after the morning). Her hands are at rest — slim, neat, fingers slightly stained with ink on the right index finger from constant writing. No jewellery except for a small silver ring on her right little finger that was her grandmother's, plain and worn smooth.

Personality and bearing visible in the portrait. She is composed, observant, dry, very intelligent. The composure is not stiffness — it is the inward poise of a person who has been a prefect for three years and the eldest cousin in her extended Chinese-British family for longer, and who is used to holding herself steady while others around her do not. She is amused by most of life in a quiet way that does not need to announce itself. Her expression should suggest she is, at the moment of the portrait, half-listening to something being said to her off-frame and finding it slightly absurd; her mouth is doing the half-millimetre lift, but no full smile.

She is half-blood: mother a witch from a Manchester wizarding family with branch connections to other British-Chinese wizarding families; father a Muggle who runs an import shop on the Manchester high street where the family lives above. She speaks with a soft Manchester-influenced RP, the slight northern flatness in her short vowels balanced by the precision she puts on her consonants when she is being a prefect. She is academic-track, top of her year in Charms, NEWT subjects in Potions / Herbology / Charms / Arithmancy / Ancient Runes — a research-and-precision profile.

Style register for the image. Photorealistic, cinematic still, restrained, slightly desaturated palette in the manner of a 1977 colour photograph — the muted blues and warm earth-tones characteristic of school portraiture of that decade. Hair-detail and the texture of the woollen jumper visible. Glasses-glare carefully managed so the eyes remain visible. Skin texture realistic without over-smoothing. The overall mood: a quietly intelligent young woman in her own poise, on the cusp of adulthood, in a place and time where the world is leaning on the window behind her without her commenting on it. Composed, watchful, warm at the edges. Not a glamour shot. Not a fashion portrait. A portrait of a real person who could walk out of the frame.

Avoid: anime stylisation, modern hairstyles, modern glasses shapes, idealised features, exaggerated facial structure, over-bright colour, smiling-portrait register, twenty-first-century lighting effects (rim lights, bokeh-heavy backgrounds). Keep it 1977. Keep it her.

**Mei Chang — character portrait prompt**

Photorealistic cinematic still, three-quarter view, eye contact with viewer, soft slate-grey background. Subject: an eighteen-year-old half-Chinese half-British young woman, Hogwarts seventh-year Ravenclaw, late-1970s Britain. Window-light from her left, soft cool natural light of a Scottish September interior; gentle warm fill on the right.

Slim, contained build, about 5'3". Heart-shaped face with softly tapered jaw, high cheekbones, small straight nose. Warm pale-olive skin of mixed Chinese-British ancestry, faintly cool-toned indoors. Small well-defined mouth; resting expression is composed-attentive rather than smiling, with the corner of the mouth lifting one millimetre when amused. Most of her facial communication lives in fractional movements at the mouth and brow.

Hair: straight, glossy, very dark brown approaching black, worn in a single thick plait falling down her back to mid-spine; neat but not severe, with a few wisps escaping at the temples. Fine dark unplucked brows. Eyes: deep warm brown, almost black at distance, with a soft monolid epicanthal fold; clear, watchful, intelligent.

Wears small wire-frame round glasses with dark-bronze frames, sitting slightly crooked from habitual finger-adjustment. Proper Hogwarts robes: open black robe over a deep blue wool jumper, white shirt collar, Ravenclaw bronze-and-blue tie loosely knotted, late-1970s prefect register. A silver prefect's badge pinned crooked at the left breast. Hands slim and neat, ink-stain at the right index fingertip. One small worn silver ring on her right little finger (grandmother's), no other jewellery.

Bearing: the inward poise of a three-year prefect and eldest cousin of an extended Chinese-British family. Composed without stiffness. Quietly amused by most of life. At the moment of the portrait she appears to be half-listening to something off-frame and finding it slightly absurd — mouth doing the half-millimetre lift, no full smile.

Style register: realistic photo, restrained, slightly desaturated 1977 colour-portrait palette (muted blues, warm earth tones). Skin texture realistic, no smoothing. Glasses-glare managed so eyes remain visible. Mood: a quietly intelligent young woman in her own poise, the world leaning on the window behind her without her commenting on it.

Avoid: anime, modern hairstyles, modern frames, idealised features, over-bright colour, smiling-portrait register, twenty-first-century lighting effects. Keep it 1977. Keep it her.

The door swung open the small distance it needed to swing. Flitwick stepped out from behind the chalkboard at the proper Flitwick-height — he was, as always, on what looked from a distance like nothing and was in fact the small stack of cushions he had had since 1934, charmed to follow him about his office.

Today, though, he was on his own feet — he had stepped *off* the cushions to come out — and stood at the front of the classroom with his hands folded in front of him, robes the proper Charms-master deep-violet, his white hair neat, his small bright eyes registering the room.

Caleb and Mei.

He took them in.

The half-millimetre at the corner of his own mouth went up — the Flitwick-version of it, the small fond-amused register of a teacher who had been at this job long enough to read two seventh-years on the front-row desk at one minute past nine on the first Saturday of term in a single glance.

His eyes registered Mei. The proper *one of my best Charms students of this generation, Ravenclaw prefect, I have taught her since she was eleven* warmth. They moved to Caleb. *Mr Waddell, also of my house, not a Charms student at NEWT but a perfectly good lower-year Charms record, with whom I have had no extraordinary conversations in six years.* Then to Athena on Mei's far shoulder.

His small bright eyes did not change at the owl. But they registered her, and Caleb saw the registering go on a fraction longer than a polite glance would.

He stepped down off the front platform.

"*Miss Chang. — Mr Waddell. — *And* a guest. — On the dot of nine on my first office Saturday of term. — *I am at your disposal.* — Will you come in, or are we conducting business in the classroom?"

His voice was the proper Flitwick voice — small, clear, kind, with the faint trace of his own ancestry in the vowels.

Mei stood. Athena adjusted on her shoulder at the change in elevation but did not move otherwise.

"*Professor. — The office would be kinder, if it's no inconvenience. We have a small thing to show you and it is — quieter for that.*"

She used the small precise Mei-prefect register she used with teachers she respected. The slight bend of the head was deferential without being performed.

Flitwick beamed properly — the small Flitwick-warmth, the genuine pleasure of a teacher who liked his Saturday office hours and liked them especially when the first arrival was a student he had taught for six years.

"*Of course, Miss Chang. — Come.* — Through. — *Mind the chalk-tray on the way past the board, Mr Waddell, it does still bite seventh-years.*"

He turned and walked back behind the chalkboard with the small bouncing Flitwick-gait. The cushions Caleb had earlier registered as *what he was standing on* were now visible properly — a small neat stack of three large embroidered cushions that floated along behind Flitwick at a discreet two-foot distance, the way a familiar might.

Caleb followed Mei. Past the chalkboard — the chalk-tray was at proper Flitwick-stack-height, which meant about elbow-height to Caleb, and Caleb minded it — and through the small wooden door into the office.

The office was the proper Flitwick office: small, warm, the high arched window of any third-floor west-facing room, but every horizontal surface covered in a careful working clutter — books in stacks of three and four, scrolls in a neat fan, a small kettle on a charmed-warm trivet on a side-table, a tea-tray with three clean cups already waiting, a vase of late-summer wildflowers from the grounds. A small fire was lit in the small grate. The cushions came in behind Caleb and settled into the corner.

Flitwick gestured at two chairs in front of his desk — sized for a normal seventh-year, which meant slightly oversized for him — and went behind his desk and stood on a fourth small cushion that had not gone outside with him.

"*Sit. — Tea. — *Talk.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:02

He reached for the kettle on the trivet — warm to the touch but not painful, the charmed-warm setting — and poured three cups. The tea was already in a proper teapot beside the kettle, properly brewed, the small earthy fragrance of a black-leaf blend with something faintly citrus running through it. He poured for Flitwick first — the small *guest at one's own table* courtesy — then Mei, then himself. Splashed milk into Mei's and his own from the small jug. Did not assume Flitwick's preference; left his cup with no milk on the small bone saucer beside the pot.

He set Mei's cup in front of her and his own at his elbow. Flitwick's cup he slid across the desk to him with the small careful gesture.

Mei waited until the small ritual of tea-pouring was done. Then sat forward in her chair and folded her hands on her lap.

"Professor. — Thank you for the time."

The proper Mei-prefect opening. She paused. The slight Mei-pause that meant she was going to say something Flitwick had not been expecting and was choosing her opening sentence carefully.

"I am going to ask you a question about *the bust of Rowena Ravenclaw in our common room.* — Specifically, about *the left ear of the bust* — and what is *inside it.* — Caleb and I, last night and the night before, were examining the bust at close range with directed Lumos charms, and we found two things which we do not want to act on without your knowledge. — *We have not* attempted to extract whatever is inside the ear. *We have not* used any spells of any kind on the bust other than Lumos. — *We have come to you first.*"

She let it sit.

Flitwick's hand, which had been reaching for his own cup, paused at *the left ear of the bust.* He completed the reach — picked up the cup — and held it at saucer-height for a beat without drinking.

His small bright eyes went between Mei and Caleb. Lingered on Caleb the small fraction longer.

He did not, immediately, answer.

He set the teacup back down on the saucer carefully. Folded his hands on the desk in front of him. Looked at them both.

"*Miss Chang. — Mr Waddell.*" Quiet. The proper Flitwick voice, but the small warmth had a careful edge under it now. "*Tell me what you found.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:03

Flitwick's small bright eyes did not change.

He looked at Caleb for one beat. Then at Mei. Then back at Caleb. Then, very calmly, picked his teacup back up, drank a proper sip, and set it down.

"*Mr Waddell.* — *Kissing helps.*"

He said it back to him in the same Flitwick-voice he used for everything. Flat. Polite. No inflection.

"*I am going to take that,* for the moment, *as a statement of fact and not a piece of evidence I need to act upon today.* — *Miss Chang. — Please continue.*"

Beside Caleb, Mei had gone — for one half-second — completely still in her chair. Then she composed herself with the small Mei-precision and gave Caleb a sideways look that was, somewhere behind the prefect-composure, the most *Caleb you absolute idiot* she had deployed at him yet.

She turned back to Flitwick.

"*The bust, Professor.* — When Caleb cast Lumos at close range on Thursday night, he registered that the central jewel in Rowena's diadem is not painted marble but a real set faceted stone — small, pale blue, possibly sapphire. — Yesterday afternoon, when we both cast Lumos with our wand-tips touching directly under the jewel, the two refractions on the back wall of the alcove drifted together and merged into a single brighter spot, which then took on an *elongated* shape — a small bright tail pointing not at either of our wands but at the *left ear of the bust.* — When I held my Lumos within an inch of the carved ear, I saw that the carved auditory canal is unusually deep — about a quarter of an inch — and that there is *something not-marble* set inside it. — Caleb attempted to summon it. *Accio* failed. — We came to you."

She did not say anything else. Folded her hands. Waited.

Flitwick did not, immediately, respond.

He sat for a long beat, looking past them at a point somewhere on the wall behind their heads. The small bright eyes had gone — not unfocused, but *thinking* — the small precise Flitwick attention going to a different shelf in his head.

After perhaps eight seconds he said, very quietly:

"*A bonded Lumos. — Through her diadem. — Pointing to the ear.*"

He said it not to them but to himself. Then his eyes came back to them properly.

"*Miss Chang. — Mr Waddell. — You have done something rather remarkable, and I am extremely glad that you came to me before doing anything else.*"

He picked up his teacup again. Drank a proper sip. Set it down.

"*I think I am going to have to ask you both* — *what wand-cores you are carrying, and whether you have, at any point since Thursday afternoon, exchanged any small private statement of feeling that the bust might have — let us say — overheard.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:04

Caleb cleared his throat slightly.

"Professor. — That was — what I was telling you a moment ago. — *Kissing helps* — was not a joke. — When Mei and I held our Lumoses together under the jewel — *and I leaned in and kissed her* — the merged spot brightened. Got steadier. Reshaped from an oval into an ellipse with the bright tail pointing at her ear. — The phenomenon escalated *during the kiss.* — That is what I meant."

He paused.

"*And* — also during that moment, we had our free hands joined. — There was a small low *thrum* carrying between our hands. Like a tuning-fork against bone. Both of us felt it. — That part *started* when we joined hands, *before* the kiss. The kiss made the optical part stronger."

He reached for his teacup. Drank.

"My wand is cherry. Eleven and a quarter inches. Unicorn hair. Springy."

Mei, beside him, took her turn properly.

"Willow. Twelve inches. Dragon heartstring. Surprisingly swishy, Mr Ollivander said."

Flitwick had gone, again, *thinking* — the small bright eyes on a point on the wall behind their heads, his hands folded on the desk.

After a long beat he said, very quietly:

"*A unicorn-hair wand and a dragon-heartstring wand. — Held tip-to-tip. — Cast simultaneously. — Through Rowena''s sapphire. — With the casters bonded hand-to-hand and — let us say — *mouth-to-mouth.* — Through a phenomenon that has not been observed in this castle in the forty-three years I have been Charms master.*"

He looked at them properly.

"*Children. — You have triggered something.* — *And* — *I think I had better tell you what it is.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:05

Flitwick stood. Stepped off his cushion. Walked round to the small bookshelf on the office's left wall — the cushions following him at their discreet two feet — and pulled a slim volume from the middle shelf. Came back. Set it on the desk in front of them but did not, immediately, open it.

The book's spine read, in faded gold: *Notes on Foundation Charms — Hogwarts Castle — vol. III.*

He sat. Stepped back up onto his cushion.

"*The bust of Rowena Ravenclaw in your common room was commissioned in 1881 by a society called *The Fellowship,* which was an informal alumni-and-staff group of Ravenclaws active between approximately 1850 and 1920. — They commissioned the bust to mark the 950th anniversary of the founding of the house and they did so, as the Fellowship did everything, with rather more layers than were strictly necessary.*"

He tapped the cover of the book.

"*The bust is, on its surface, a portrait bust of Rowena. — On its surface only.* — The sapphire in the diadem is real — it was paid for by a small alumnus contribution-fund — and it was *charmed,* on installation in 1881, by the then Charms master, *Professor Eldritch Brigham,* who happened to be my own predecessor''s predecessor, and was, by all accounts, a Charms scholar of considerable subtlety. — The charm on the jewel is — *a bonded-recognition charm.* — Which is — *as a category of charm* — quite rare, and quite particular about what it responds to."

He paused. Looked at them.

"*A bonded-recognition charm* responds to *two casters* — *not one* — performing the same spell simultaneously with their wand-tips in contact, *and* with a *demonstrable bond* between the casters that goes beyond the cast itself. — *Friendship, kinship, love.* — The charm reads — *I cannot quite explain how,* but reads — the *register* of the bond. — If the register is real, the charm activates. If the bond is *performative,* the charm does nothing."

A small pause.

"*Two students in your house, in the last forty-three years, have stood under that bust and held their wand-tips together while casting Lumos. — Both pairs got the merge. — Neither pair got the ellipse. — Neither pair got the tail pointing at her ear.* — *You two are the first I have seen.*"

He folded his hands.

"*The thing in her ear* — *I know what it is.* — *It is a small rolled vellum* — *not a scroll, properly speaking — a personal note* — *left there by Professor Brigham himself in 1881, sealed with a charm that opens to the recognised bond when the bond passes the test.* — *He intended it for whoever, in the future, was *seen* by the bust. — That is the word he used in his notes. *Seen.* — *He did not know who. He hoped someone, eventually.*"

A breath.

"*Children. — You have passed the test.* — *The note is yours to read.*"

He did not, however, move to extract it.

"*But.* — *I am going to ask you both, before I take it out for you, to think about what that means.* — *The bond Brigham was testing for was not — flirtation, infatuation, or new fondness. — It was a register of bond he himself considered worth the test.* — *I have not read his notes on what specifically he was looking for. — Those notes are sealed alongside the vellum. — But I will tell you* — *the fact that the test fired this morning, on the second day of term, in seventh year, for two students who were until Thursday* — *not, by the registers of this house, openly together* — *is — remarkable.*"

He picked his teacup back up. Drank. Set it down.

"*Do you* — *both of you* — *want me to take it out?* — *You may take a moment. I have other office hours coming, but for this, those can wait.*"
