"*Caleb.*"

Quiet. From behind the hand.

Flitwick, behind the desk, did the small Flitwick-pause that meant he was choosing to take a sentence with the dignity it warranted and not the dignity it requested.

"*Wasian babies,*" he said. Mildly. "*Mr Waddell. — That is a word I have not previously heard in this office. — I shall, I think, not ask.*"

A small pause. The half-millimetre at the corner of his mouth.

"*If you are both telling me* — *in your way* — *that the bond is real and you would both like the note brought out* — *I take that as your answer.* — *Miss Chang.* — *Yes?*"

He looked at Mei specifically. The small Flitwick-precision: she was the prefect, the daughter of a long-standing Manchester wizarding family, the student he had taught since she was eleven; he was asking her in her own register because Caleb had answered in his.

Mei lowered her hand. Composed. Looked at Flitwick.

"*Yes, Professor.* — *Please bring it out.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:09

Flitwick's eyes did the small *good question, properly asked* register.

"*Mr Waddell.* — *No. You do not have to tell me that the bond is real. — The jewel told me that.* — *Brigham's charm was, among other things, designed to spare any future Charms master the indignity of having to assess his own students' affections by interview. — The phenomenon you reported is the assessment. — I am satisfied.*"

He picked up the slim *Notes on Foundation Charms* book. Opened it to a page near the back marked with a faded ribbon. Read for a beat. Closed the book again.

"*Right.* — *Stay seated.*"

He stepped down off his cushion. Walked round the desk and past their chairs to the office door. Opened it. Walked through the Charms classroom to the alcove — *no,* Caleb registered, *he was not going to the alcove* — he was crossing the classroom to the chalkboard, where a small wooden cabinet was set into the wall beside it that Caleb had not previously registered. The cabinet had a tiny brass plaque on it: *Charms-Master's Reserve.*

Flitwick tapped the cabinet with his wand. Murmured something. The door swung open. He reached inside, found something by feel, and brought out a slim long wooden case — about the size of a wand-box, but longer and shallower.

He came back to the office. Set the case on the desk between Caleb and Mei. Did not, immediately, open it.

"*The case holds an instrument we keep for this exact purpose.* — *Brigham did not, of course, intend the note to be physically extracted from the bust by *hand.* — He intended it to be drawn out *by* a Charms master *in the presence of the recognised pair.* — That is why *Accio* did not work, Mr Waddell. The charm refuses summoning. — It permits *invitation,* which is the instrument''s function."

He opened the case.

Inside, on a velvet bed, was a thin tapered rod of dark wood, about eight inches long, with a small carved hollow at one end the size of a thimble. Not a wand. Some kind of specialist tool.

"*This goes into the ear,* — *gently,* — *and the rolled vellum, recognising it, comes out. — That is what it does. — Watch.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:11

Flitwick picked up the case with both hands. Stepped down off his cushion.

"*Yes. — Come along. — *To the alcove.* — *No need to bring tea.*"

He bounced past the chair Mei was sitting in at the proper Flitwick-pace and out through the small office door, the cushions following him at their discreet distance. The case was held carefully in front of him with both hands.

Caleb and Mei stood. Mei was careful with Athena on her shoulder. Athena had registered the change in posture and the new movement and was awake now, both yellow eyes open, the small *what is happening* attention.

Out of the office, through the Charms classroom — past the chalk-tray which Caleb again minded — out into the third-floor corridor. The Saturday-morning corridor was its proper self: thinly populated, the long west light coming in through the high arched windows, a few students going their own way. A pair of fourth-years coming toward them stepped aside for Flitwick automatically.

Up the great central staircase. Five flights. Flitwick at the head of the small procession, the cushions trailing him, Caleb and Mei behind, Athena on Mei's shoulder.

At the foot of the spiral stair to the Ravenclaw tower, Flitwick paused.

"*Mr Waddell. — Miss Chang. — When we reach the eagle, I will ask its riddle for the four of us. — I am Charms-master to the house. — The eagle will recognise my voice and pass us all through together. — *No clever answers, Mr Waddell.*"

The half-millimetre at the corner of Flitwick's mouth. He had — Caleb registered — already heard about *I love you, eagle.* From whom, Caleb did not know. The Hogwarts staff communications system was its own thing.

He went up the spiral stair. They followed.

The eagle door, at the top. Flitwick stepped up. The eagle considered him — recognised him — and the small bronze head tilted in the polite *Professor* register. It did not ask a riddle. It simply opened.

The common room beyond was its Saturday-morning self. A handful of students at the tables — two fifth-years working at the window, a seventh-year girl Caleb did not recognise asleep face-down in a book on a sofa, Davina at her usual prefect-table doing something with a clipboard. They registered Flitwick's entry with the small *the Charms master is in the common room with two seventh-years and an owl* attention and went deliberately back to their own work.

Flitwick led them straight to the Rowena alcove.

The bust was where it had been. The slight not-quite-smile. The pedestal cool. The small set sapphire in the diadem catching the ambient lamplight.

Flitwick set the case on the small reading-shelf inside the alcove. Opened it. Lifted out the tapered dark-wood rod. Held it carefully.

"*Stand on my left and right.* — *Wands ready,* — *but not lit yet.* — *I will speak the recognition. — At the word *now,* you cast Lumos, tips together, exactly as you did yesterday. — I will perform the extraction in the same instant.* — *Are we ready.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:18

The fifth-years at the window registered *entangling our souls with Rowena Ravenclaw's,* registered Flitwick standing beside Caleb with a wooden case open, and went very deliberately back to their work without comment. Davina, at her prefect-table, raised her eyes the small fraction. Held them on Caleb for one beat. Looked back at her clipboard.

Flitwick did the small Flitwick-pause that meant he was again choosing to take the sentence with the dignity it warranted and not the dignity it requested.

"*Mr Waddell.* — *Souls are not, strictly speaking, the unit of measure for Brigham's charm. — But* — *carry on.*"

He took up his position between them. Caleb on his left, Mei on his right. The cushions had settled in the corner of the alcove out of the way. Flitwick held the tapered dark-wood rod in his right hand, the carved thimble-end forward.

Athena, on Mei's shoulder, registered the new careful posture and went very still.

Flitwick raised his free hand to the marble of Rowena's left ear. Did not touch it. Held the palm flat about three inches from the carved surface. Closed his small bright eyes.

He spoke. Not loudly. The proper Charms-master register, every syllable distinct.

"*Magister Brigham, Magistra Ravenclaw, this is your charm and these are your seen ones. — Receive them and yield.*"

A small *click* registered in the bust — not audible, exactly, but the alcove felt it. The marble *softened* by the smallest degree, the way stone could not soften.

Flitwick opened his eyes. Looked at them.

"*Now.*"

Caleb's wand-tip came up to Mei's. The two of them spoke it together.

"*Lumos.*"

The two lights lit. Touched. Merged on the back wall in the small bright spot — and *this time,* with the recognition in place, the spot bloomed *brighter* than yesterday's had been by visibly several degrees, the ellipse re-forming with the bright tail pointing at Rowena's left ear, the angle sharp, the rotation paused into a single steady line.

Flitwick, in the same motion, slipped the tapered rod gently into the carved canal of the ear.

The rod went in to the carved depth — about a quarter-inch, exactly — and stopped.

A small fluttering inside. The proper rustle of something rolled-vellum responding.

Flitwick drew the rod back, slow, careful. As it came out the thimble-end of it had a small dark cylinder fastened to it — Caleb saw it emerge as the rod did, the rolled vellum about the size of a pencil-stub, dark-aged with six centuries — no, *one* century — of being inside marble.

The rod cleared the canal. The cylinder was free.

Flitwick set the rod down on the reading-shelf with the small careful gesture of a man who had done a clean job. The rolled vellum sat on the velvet of the case where the rod had been.

He took a step back.

"*Lights off, children.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:19

Caleb's *Nox.* Mei's *Nox.* The two wand-tips went out together. The alcove returned to its ambient lamplight.

Flitwick exhaled — small, quiet, the proper *that worked* exhale of a Charms master who had performed a complicated piece of inherited work cleanly — and stepped forward to the case on the reading-shelf.

He did not pick up the rolled vellum.

"*Children. — The rest of this is yours. — I am not going to read it for you, and I am not going to read it with you. — Brigham left it for the recognised pair, and the recognised pair is — you. — Take the case. Take it somewhere quiet. Read it together.*"

A pause.

"*I do, however, have one request.* — *When you have read it, and when you have decided what you wish to do with it — come back and see me.* — *Not because I am owed anything. — Because Brigham left a second sealed note for the Charms master of whichever generation finally saw the first one extracted, and I would like to read it knowing what you read. — At your pace. — There is no rush.*"

He closed the case carefully — the rolled vellum inside on the velvet, the wood rod beside it — and handed it to Mei rather than to Caleb, the small Flitwick-precision of giving the senior-Charms-student the responsibility of the carrying.

Mei took it with both hands. Bowed her head a fraction. The small *thank you, Professor* register.

"*Thank you, Professor.*"

Flitwick beamed properly. The small Flitwick-warmth fully on now.

"*Children. — That was — extraordinary. — Go.* — *Read.* — *Come back when you are ready.*"

He gave them the small *off you go* gesture. The cushions rose with him.

He left the alcove and went back across the common room toward the eagle door, the cushions trailing. The fifth-years at the window watched him go. Davina did not look up.

Mei looked at Caleb. The case in her hands. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth.

"*Caleb.*" Quiet. "*Where do we read it.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:21

Mei stayed where she was, the case in her hands, and looked at him.

"*The Room of Requirement,*" she said quietly. "*Henshaw mentioned it to you yesterday. — Most students go through Hogwarts and never find it. — Some do. — It is on the seventh floor, in the corridor opposite the tapestry of Barnabas the Barmy teaching trolls to dance ballet. — You walk past the blank stretch of wall opposite the tapestry three times, thinking *clearly* about what you need the room to be. — On the third pass, if it has decided to be there for you, a door appears.*"

A small pause.

"*That is — what I know. — I have not used it. — I have never tried.*"

She looked at the case in her hands. Looked at Caleb.

"*Caleb. — Today might not be the day to test it.* — *We have a vellum on velvet that has been in a marble ear for ninety-six years and the staff of this house just witnessed us being recognised by a founder''s charm.* — *Reading the vellum in our common room would be — fine. Reading it in the library would be fine. Reading it in your dormitory if Lin is out would be — also fine.* — *Reading it in a magical room that may or may not exist on the seventh floor, when neither of us has set foot in it before, is — possibly a thing for a less complicated morning.*"

A breath. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth.

"*But — Caleb. — If you want to find it, today, *for the dueling reason Henshaw gave you,* — *I will go with you.* — *That is a different thing from reading the vellum in it.*"

## Narrator

OOC: Quick clarification — Mei isn't *opposed* to reading the vellum in private. Her suggestion was the opposite: read it somewhere familiar and grounded (common room, library, dormitory), not in the Room of Requirement on the first attempt because the Room is new-untested territory for both of you. Privacy isn't the variable she was hedging on — *unknown space + freshly-extracted founder's note* was.

But Caleb's read of "you want to go somewhere private to read it" is a fine in-character read, and you've answered cleanly: go find the Room now for the dueling reason, then read the vellum somewhere else. Running.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:23

Mei's mouth did the small *Caleb you are not quite hearing me but I am going to roll with it* register.

"*Caleb. — I was not opposed to reading it in private. — I was opposed to the Room being where we read it. — The two are different.* — But — *yes. Let us go find it.* — *For the dueling reason.* — *Now.*"

She tucked the case carefully under her left arm — the one with Athena on the shoulder, so the owl was on her right and her left hand was free to steady the case — and went out of the alcove. Athena adjusted. Davina, at her prefect-table, registered the departure with the small *I notice but I do not need to know* nod.

Out through the eagle door. The eagle did not ask anything of them on the way out.

Up. The Ravenclaw tower was on the fifth floor; the seventh floor was two flights up and two corridors over. They took the moving staircases — one obliged them on the second flight, swinging into a different position halfway through their climb and depositing them in a corridor Caleb did not initially recognise.

By the time they reached the seventh floor proper, the corridor on the east side of the castle, the Saturday quiet was deeper. Fewer students this high. The corridor was wide and long, with the proper high arched windows on the right and a stretch of mostly-blank stone wall on the left.

About a third of the way down, a tapestry. Caleb had passed it a hundred times in seven years without ever properly looking at it. He looked at it now: a wide hanging in faded yellows and greens, the central figure a wizard in a yellow robe attempting, with visible patience, to instruct three trolls in what was unmistakably ballet — the trolls in awkward fourth position, one of them visibly resentful, the wizard's stick raised as a baton.

The small embroidered Latin tag at the bottom: *BARNABAS QUI VOCATUR BARMUS, MAGISTER TRIBUS TROLLIS.*

Caleb's mouth twitched.

Opposite the tapestry, the wall was blank stone. A long unfeatured stretch, no door, no portrait, no notice. About fifteen feet of it.

Mei stopped in front of the blank wall. Set the case carefully on the floor against her foot so she could free her hands.

"*Three passes,* Caleb. — *Thinking clearly* about what you need the room to be. — *Together?* — Or you go alone, and I watch?"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:31

Mei was about to do the first pass and paused.

"*Caleb. — Yes.*"

She turned a fraction toward him, the small Mei-precision.

"*That is — actually worth pausing on. — In a Slytherin common room a Charms master walking in with two students and a wooden case and pulling a scroll out of the founder's bust would have been *the* story for the term. — In a Hufflepuff common room there would have been a small spontaneous round of applause and a girl bringing us biscuits. — In a Gryffindor common room there would have been *three offers of help* and at least one person attempting to climb on the pedestal for a better look.*"

A small breath. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth was on but tired.

"*Ravenclaws — do not interrupt working magic.* — *That is the house rule.* — *Flitwick walked in with the case. The fifth-years at the window registered what was about to happen and did not interfere. Davina registered it from her prefect-table and did not interfere. The seventh-year on the sofa slept through it because she is a seventh-year and slept through it. — That is — what we are.*"

She tucked a wisp of hair behind her ear.

"*They are extremely interested.* — *Davina will ask me about it the next time we are alone together as prefects, which is — at the seven-o'clock meeting tonight.* — *The fifth-years will whisper about it for a fortnight.* — *But the *house rule* says you do not break a working spell with your curiosity. — *And* — *thank goodness for that, because if they had crowded the alcove I do not know whether Brigham''s charm would have fired through them.*"

She picked the case back up.

"*All right. — Three passes. — Together. — Thinking about a small private room we can use for dueling practice and other practical work over the year, that will be there when we need it and not be discovered by anyone else.*"

She took his free hand. Started walking.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:32

He held Mei's hand and started the first pass. Thought about it.

A small private room with a heart-shaped couch in the middle of it for making out on. Plush. Velvet. The proper red.

First pass: along the blank wall, fifteen feet, back. He kept his head straight and looked at the stone and thought *heart-shaped couch* with the proper deliberate focus of a person trying not to be distracted by the absurdity of what he was doing.

Mei, at his side, was — Caleb could feel through her hand — *trying* to think clearly about the small private practice-and-work room, the way she had described it before they started. Her own request. Her own intention.

Second pass: back along the wall. He thought *heart-shaped couch* again. *Plush velvet. Red. Mei.*

He stole a glance at her. Mei's face was the composed thinking-Mei face, eyes on the stone wall, focused.

Third pass: along the wall, fifteen feet, end.

A door appeared.

Not, however, the kind of door Mei had been thinking of. The door that materialised in the blank stone was a small dark-stained-wood door with a *heart-shaped* brass plate where the doorknob would be, and the plate itself bore the small embossed legend *FOR THE CALEB INTENT.* The door was about five and a half feet tall and slightly too narrow to walk through with dignity.

Mei stopped walking.

She looked at the door. Looked at Caleb. Looked at the door again.

"*Caleb.*" Quiet. Flat. The Mei-register that meant she had registered exactly what had happened.

"*What — exactly — were you thinking about while we walked.*"

She looked at him. Her hand came back into his.

"*But we are not staying long. — And we are not making out in there for an hour. — We have a vellum to read.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:34

Mei made a small *oh* sound at the back of her throat at *wanting to love you a little physically has never made me sad* — not a laugh, the warm soft one — and did not push back further.

He turned the heart-shaped brass plate. The door swung inward.

The room beyond was — exactly what he had been thinking, and more.

It was small. About twelve feet square. The walls were the proper deep velvet red of a small private parlour in a country house, not garish — the dark red of a 1930s drawing room, with brass picture-rail and a low ceiling. A small fire was lit in a tiny grate in one wall. A standing lamp at one corner gave warm yellow light. The floor was deep dark wood with a long oval rug in muted tones.

In the centre of the room, taking up most of it: a heart-shaped couch.

The proper Caleb-imagined heart-shaped couch. Deep red velvet upholstery, the back rising in the curve of a heart, two seated indentations gently sloping toward the centre point, where any two people sitting on it would end up against each other at the seam. The upholstery was the proper plush. The cushioning was generous. Two small embroidered throw-pillows sat on it — one with a small *C,* one with a small *M.*

A small side-table beside the couch held two glasses and a decanter of something dark. The decanter was, Caleb registered, half-full of what looked very much like elf-made wine.

A small bookshelf along one wall held — Caleb's eyes scanned it — *one* book. The slim leather-bound *Companion Owls — A Quiet History* by Hesba Ollivander, 1922. The same edition Caleb had in his satchel.

Above the fireplace, in a small ornate frame, a single hand-lettered card. The lettering on it read:

> *FOR THE CALEB INTENT.*
> *Maintenance fee: one (1) act of dignified self-awareness on exit.*

Mei followed him in. Stopped just inside the door. Took in the room.

She did not, immediately, react. She read the small framed card above the fireplace. Read it again. Looked at the heart-shaped couch. Looked at the *C* and *M* cushions. Looked at the wine.

She closed her eyes briefly. Opened them. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth was on, full, with the small *I am going to remember this for the rest of my life* register under it.

"*Caleb Waddell.*"

Quiet. From the doorway.

"*This is — the most aggressively Caleb room I have ever been in.* — *The Room of Requirement made you a — *date room.* — *On the seventh floor of Hogwarts.* — *With a copy of your owl book on the shelf.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 09:35

Mei did not, immediately, answer.

She stayed where she was, in the doorway, with the case in her hands. Her face did the thing it did when something landed that she was not ready for — the small composed Mei-prefect register *softened* by exactly the amount Caleb's sentence had asked her to soften, no more, no less. Her eyes did not leave his face.

After a long beat she stepped properly into the room. Set the case carefully on the side-table beside the decanter. Pushed the door shut behind her — the small *click* of the heart-shaped brass plate engaging.

The small framed card above the fireplace flickered once. The lettering rearranged itself into a different line:

> *MAINTENANCE FEE: PAID.*

The card flickered again and went blank.

Caleb registered: *the act of dignified self-awareness on exit* the Room had been asking for — Caleb's sentence to Mei, said inside the room without performance, before any kissing or wine — had been *paid in.* The Room had taken the sentence as currency.

Mei walked to the heart-shaped couch. Sat down on the *M* side. Held out her hand.

She did not, immediately, speak. The composed face was on, but tireder around the eyes than it had been a moment ago — not tired-tired, the *I am being met more than I expected to be met this morning* tired.

When she did speak, it was quiet.

"*Caleb.*" A breath. "*I have loved you back for almost as long. — Two years. — Possibly longer. — I lost count.*"

A pause.

"*Come sit.*"

He worked the soft places at the sides of her stomach, the small inward curve at her waist, the warm flat of her belly. Squeezed once at her sides — the deliberate small *I have you* squeeze — and slid both hands around to her back.

Her back was warm under the cotton. He bent his head and kissed her once through the cotton of her shirt — the small warm press over her sternum, between her collarbones — and worked up. Up the line of her throat. Slow. The small mouth-press at the soft hollow at the base. Up.

She lifted herself off his lap with the small Mei-precision — careful, slow, the deliberate disengagement that did not break the moment so much as set it down properly for later. ## Narrator

OOC: Before I run this — I need to actually write Brigham's note so Mei has something to read. Let me draft it and commit it, then run the scene.

The note has constraints: written 1881 by Professor Eldritch Brigham, Charms master, in the wake of commissioning the bust for The Fellowship's 950th anniversary observance of the founding of Ravenclaw house. Sealed by a bonded-recognition charm responding to *two casters with a real bond,* with the activation phenomenon producing the bright tail pointing at the ear. Brigham wrote *for whoever, in the future, was seen by the bust* — the word he himself used was *seen.* Flitwick has flagged that the kind of bond Brigham was testing for was not flirtation/infatuation/new fondness — *a register of bond he himself considered worth the test.* This means the note's content should be addressed to a *pair* who have something real between them, and it should reward that with — not power, not a weapon, not a war-secret — something more like a recognition-and-blessing, a small piece of the founders' work passing forward.

What I want the note to NOT be: a war-thread, a Path-thread, a horcrux-thread, a Marauders-thread. Brigham was 1881 — six decades before Voldemort. His charm was personal, not political. The note should be a *character* artefact, not a *plot* artefact. It should land as moving rather than as informational.

What it CAN be: an account of what Brigham himself had seen in his own life that made him want to leave this charm; an invitation to do something with it; a small piece of inherited Ravenclaw practice (a charm, a phrase, a place-keeping); something that lives in the heart of the campaign rather than its mechanics.

Drafting.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 10:08

Mei opened the case. Lifted the rolled vellum out with both hands — slow, careful, the small precision of a girl who had handled medieval documents in the library and knew how to handle inked vellum. Set the wooden rod aside on the side-table. Unfastened the small dark cord that held the roll closed and gently uncurled it on her lap.

The vellum sat open on her knees. Brown iron-gall ink in a clean Victorian copperplate. The first line was visible from where Caleb sat.

Mei looked at him.

He closed his eyes. Set his head back against the heart-shaped couch.

"*Please.*"

Mei did not, immediately, begin. She read the opening to herself first — the small Mei-precision of pre-reading before delivering — and after a beat, in her clear quiet voice, with the small adjustment of register that meant she was reading aloud something written by hand a long time ago, she began.

"*To whomever is now reading this. — I write in the summer of 1881, in the fiftieth year of my own marriage and the thirty-second year of my Charms-mastery of this house.*"

She read on. Her voice was steady and quiet, and the Room of Requirement was very still around them. The fire did its small *crack.* Athena, on the couch-back, did not stir. Caleb sat with his eyes closed and his head back and let her voice carry the Brighams' words into the room.

*My wife, Helena Brigham — born Helena Crouch of the Cambridgeshire Crouches, but always herself first — sits across from me at the desk as I write...*

*That is my disclosure. I am leaving this note in the ear of Rowena's bust because my wife and I, working together, have made a charm that recognises what we have...*

*The charm reads the steady kind — the k

## Narrator

OOC: Need to write the vellum first before Mei reads it aloud. This is Eldritch Brigham's 1881 personal note to whoever, in the future, the bust *sees.* I'll write it in keeping with what Flitwick told us: Brigham was the then-Charms-master, a man of considerable subtlety, who left the note specifically for the recognised pair. He didn't know who. He hoped someone, eventually.

The bond he was testing for was *real* — not flirtation, not new fondness — and the fact that the ellipse-with-tail fired is what makes Caleb and Mei the first pair to be properly seen.

Let me draft it and commit to the database.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 10:08

Mei opened the lid of the case carefully. The rolled vellum on its velvet bed. The small dark cylinder of it, faintly aged, the ink visible at the rolled outer edge as the proper iron-gall brown of late-Victorian script.

She lifted it out with both hands. Set the case back on the side-table. Unrolled the vellum slowly on her lap — about five inches by eight when flat, a single sheet — and read the first line silently to herself, to make sure she could read the hand.

Then she set the unrolled vellum on her thigh so it would not curl. Settled herself against the *M* cushion. Looked at Caleb.

"*Close your eyes.* — *I will read it.*"

He closed his eyes. Settled his head back against the curve of the heart-shaped couch-back. One of his hands found hers on the cushion between them. He let his other arm lie at his side.

Mei's voice — quiet, the proper reading register, careful with the unfamiliar copperplate — began.

*"To the pair whom the diadem has finally seen — and to no one else, for the charm has refused, by my design, all others — greetings."*

A small pause. She was checking the next line.

*"My name is Eldritch Brigham, Charms-master of this house, and on this day, the seventeenth of June in the year 1881, I have set the small sapphire above the brow of our founder''s likeness to do a particular thing."*

She read at the pace of careful reading — proper full stops where the late-Victorian sentences ended, the small Mei-precision of an Ancient Runes student handling a primary source.

*"The thing it does is to recognise. It looks, when two wands are brought to it together, for a bond between the casters. There are many bonds the charm could have been set to recognise. I chose one in particular, after long thought."*

Caleb let her voice come. Did not open his eyes. Her hand was warm in his.

*"I did not choose to recognise infatuation, which I have observed comes and goes in seventh-years and elder students like the weather of the lake. I did not choose to recognise affection, which is too common in this house to be remarkable. I did not choose to recognise even friendship, which is the bedrock of much that happens in this castle but is, by its nature, not always known to the friends themselves."*

A pause. Mei's voice had gone — Caleb could hear it without seeing her face — a fraction quieter.

*"I chose to recognise what I will call, for want of a better word, the *yoked mind* — the bond between two persons who have decided, without telling each other, that they will think the same thoughts about hard problems for the rest of their working lives, and who have begun to do so before they realised they had."*

She stopped there. The small Mei-pause. Caleb felt her hand tighten on his by a fraction.

She kept reading.

*"I am told this bond is rare. I am told it is rarer still that the two persons share affection of the more conventional kind. I am told the combination of the two — the yoked mind and the affection — is rarer still, and when it occurs, produces — I will say only — *durable* work."*

A breath.

*"If you are reading this, you are such a pair. The charm does not lie."*

Mei's voice paused again. Caleb did not open his eyes. He could hear her breathe.

She kept going.

*"It has refused, in the years it has been here, to fire for any pair who did not meet its threshold; I am told by my successor in this office, whoever you may be, that no pair has been recognised by it before yours. (If a pair has, I am wrong about the charm''s rarity, and I will be pleased to be wrong.)"*

*"I have left this small note in our founder''s ear for two reasons. The first reason is congratulations. You have found, in each other, a thing most people do not find. Do not waste it. Do not, in particular, waste it by failing to do the work the bond exists to do. The yoked mind is for *work,* young people. Not exclusively, but principally. If you have not yet decided what you will work on together — decide. The world has need of what two yoked minds can produce that one cannot, and the world will not benefit from a yoked mind which spent its years on small things."*

A pause. Mei's voice had not quite cracked but had got quieter.

*"The second reason is a gift."*

She read the rest. The room. The south-west turret stair on the seventh floor. The portrait of Solomon. The phrase — *we have come from the bust.* The room's contents: the desk for two, the fire, the self-filling shelf, the settable window, the cupboard with his own Charms notes. The durability — that the room was theirs *after* school as well as during, by a key Flitwick would offer them on graduation day. The ask: a page in the cupboard, after his death, describing any work the bond produced.

She read the closing.

*"May the work be good. — Eldritch Brigham. Charms-master, Ravenclaw House. 17 June 1881. Hogwarts School."*

Quiet.

The fire in the grate did its small *crack.*

Mei did not, immediately, say anything. Caleb did not open his eyes.

After a long beat she set the vellum carefully back on the velvet of the case in her lap. Took her glasses off. Set them in her own lap beside the vellum. Pressed the heels of both her hands against her closed eyes for one long beat.

Then put the glasses back on. Looked at Caleb.

"*Caleb. — You can open your eyes.*"

He reached over. Took the vellum gently — careful with the ninety-six-year-old paper — and set it back on the velvet of the case. Closed the case with both hands. Set the case on the side-table beside the decanter, well clear.

Picked up her glasses. Folded them properly along the hinge — Mei's small finickiness about her glasses required folding them along the hinge, not setting them open — and set them on top of the closed case.

Then he turned to her.

"*Come here.*"

She came. The small careful Mei-motion of a girl with no glasses on, getting up off the *M* cushion and turning to face him. He set his hands at her hips and lifted her — not heavily, the small bit of help — and she settled into his lap, both knees on either side of his thighs the way she had ten minutes ago. Her hands found his shoulders again. Her forehead came to his.

He kissed her.

Not slow this time. Not the careful experimental kissing of an hour ago. The proper kiss of two people who had just been told, in a Charms master's late-Victorian hand, that they were a *yoked mind* — that the small private *I have been thinking about you for two years and did not know I was thinking about you for two years* of their separate September-to-Junes had been the visible signature of something the charm in a founder's diadem had been waiting since 1881 to recognise — and that they had been *seen.*

Mei made a small sound at the back of her throat. Her hand at the back of his neck. The kiss went deep. Her hips shifted forward against his — the small involuntary press of a girl who had been told her bond was *durable* and was now properly leaning into the durability part. He brought one hand up under her jumper again, palm flat on the warm of her stomach over the cotton. His other hand at the small of her back, fingers spread, pulling her closer.

The room held them.

The fire did its small *crack.* Athena, on the couch-back, did not open her eyes — the small *whatever the humans are doing they are doing it again* register of an owl who had decided to sleep through the second kissing-event of the morning.

After a long beat Mei broke for air, briefly, forehead against his.

"*Caleb.*" Quiet. Her breath was faster than corridor breath. "*Yoked mind.*"

She kissed him again.

He stood up off the couch. The Room held its small warm self around him — the fire crackling, the lamp glow, the heart-shaped silhouette of the couch behind him.

The small framed card above the fireplace flickered.

The blank face came back. New lettering appeared on it, slowly, in the same hand the original card had used:

> *FOR THE CALEB INTENT.*
> *Acknowledged.*
> *Return when needed.*

The lettering held for a beat. Then faded back to blank.

Mei stood beside him. Picked up the closed case carefully — case, vellum, extraction rod, glasses now back on her face — and tucked it under her left arm. Athena, on the couch-back, opened both eyes, registered the *we are leaving* posture, and hopped sideways down the carved back of the couch to Mei's shoulder with the small precise three-step.

Mei adjusted for the owl's weight.

"*Owl perch noted for next visit.*" Quiet. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth on properly. "*Solomon next. — South-west turret. — Seventh floor. — *We have come from the bust.* — Let''s go pick up our key."

She took Caleb's hand.

The door opened by itself when they approached it — the small heart-shaped brass plate turning of its own accord — and they stepped back out into the seventh-floor corridor opposite the Barnabas tapestry. The door swung quietly shut behind them. The wall reformed itself within a beat — no door, no heart-shaped plate, just the blank stretch of stone that had been there before.

The corridor was its proper Saturday self. Empty.

The south-west turret was the other end of the seventh floor — Caleb knew the geography in the broad strokes — about three hundred feet away along the curving corridors. Mei led. Athena rode her shoulder. The case under her arm.

## Narrator

OOC: Re-reading the vellum — Brigham says the *graduation-day key* is offered by the Charms master *on the day you graduate.* That's a graduation gift, not something Caleb and Mei can pick up now. It's for *after* they leave school in June 1978, to allow them to return to the room from anywhere in Britain at any future time.

The room itself, here-and-now, opens to Solomon the cat with the phrase *we have come from the bust.* No key needed at school. The graduation key is for *post-Hogwarts* access only.

So the order is: go to the room now via Solomon. Flitwick gets reminded that the recognised pair has been found, and the second sealed note for the Charms master gets read by him. The graduation key sits with Flitwick (or with his successor) and is offered to Caleb and Mei on the day they graduate next June.

What I should have said in Mei's last line was *Solomon next, then Flitwick — not the other way.* Caleb's read of *key first* was based on a slight misreading of the vellum (mine and his). Adjusting.

She looked up.

"*Caleb. — Re-reading.* — *The key is for after we leave.* — *Flitwick will offer it to us on the day we graduate, next June.* — *Not now.* — *Here at school we use Solomon and the phrase.* — *The key is for post-Hogwarts access — letting us return from anywhere in Britain, any time in our future lives.*"

She rolled the vellum back up. Slid it back into the case. Tucked the case under her arm again.

"*So.* — *Solomon first.* — *Then back to Flitwick to tell him what the note said and let him read the second sealed note he was meant to read.* — *He doesn''t need to give us anything today. — He just needs to know.*"

She looked at the corridor ahead.

"*South-west turret. — Let''s go.*"

A small breath.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 10:38

They reached the south-west end of the seventh floor.

The corridor narrowed here — older masonry, less travelled by students, the long west-facing windows giving onto the lake side. The light through them was the late-morning gold-on-water of a September Saturday. The corridor ended at a small alcove with a tight spiral staircase going up — narrow, the kind of turret-stair that suggested the tower above it had been built for a single small chamber rather than a teaching space.

At the foot of the staircase, at about chest height, mounted on the stone wall: a small portrait. The frame was modest — dark wood, the gilt worn to a dull bronze — and the canvas inside it was perhaps eighteen inches by twelve. The painted scene was a small Persian rug on a stone floor, and asleep on the rug, curled tightly with his tail over his nose, a small grey tabby cat.

The cat was painted properly — not a cartoon, the proper Victorian-realist style with the small careful brushwork of fur. His sides rose and fell visibly with painted breathing.

Mei stopped two feet from the portrait.

"*Caleb. — He''s yours.*"

She did not approach further. Stepped back the small polite half-step. Athena, on her shoulder, registered the portrait with the small *that is a cat but it is also a painting* attention.

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 10:40

He stepped up to the portrait.

"*King Solomon. — Meow meoooww meeow. — We come from the bust.*"

The small grey tabby's painted ear flicked.

The other ear flicked. Then, slowly, with the considered dignity of a portrait-cat who had been disturbed at his nap, Solomon raised his small painted head off the cushion of his tail. Opened his eyes — they were the proper amber-yellow of a real tabby, the brushwork on the irises remarkable — and looked at Caleb.

He looked at Caleb for a long beat.

Then his small painted mouth opened.

"*Hello,*" he said. The voice was the proper portrait-voice — a fraction tinny, the way painted voices were, but otherwise the warm dry voice of an older cat who had been asleep on a rug for ninety-six years and was not sorry about it. "*You have indeed come from the bust. — *Both* of you. — Hello, Miss. — Hello, Sir. — Hello, small Pygmy.*"

He stood up on the rug. Stretched the proper cat-stretch — front legs forward, back arched up, tail high — and shook himself.

"*A moment.*"

He turned and walked off the canvas — out of the right-hand edge of his portrait, the painted rug now empty — and was gone.

For perhaps three seconds Caleb registered nothing.

Then, behind the portrait, a *click.* The whole portrait — frame and all — swung outward on a hinge Caleb had not seen. The painted Solomon was on the inside of the frame now; the stone wall behind where the portrait had been had a small dark opening in it about three feet square.

A short narrow passage. Inside, at the far end, perhaps eight feet away, a small wooden door with a brass handle. Beyond the door — Caleb could not see, but the light coming through the narrow passage was the warm yellow of a fire and a lit lamp.

Solomon's painted voice came from inside the frame, slightly muffled by the angle:

"*Mind your heads.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 10:41

Solomon's painted face reappeared at the left edge of the canvas — he had walked back across from wherever he had gone — and considered Caleb's question with the small dignified head-tilt of a cat who had been asked a fair question and was deciding how to answer.

"*Was a cat. — Once. — Many years ago, when there was a painter and a sitting and a small grey tabby on a rug in a Charms-master''s study who would not stop being painted. — That cat lived eleven years, ate well, slept warm, was loved. — I am his portrait. — I have his memories of being him, and I have ninety-six years of being a portrait of him, which is a different thing again.*"

A small pause. The painted ear flicked.

"*You are welcome, Sir. — Welcome to *both* of you. — Brigham would be very pleased. — Pass through. — Mind your heads. — *Solomon stays here on his rug.*"

He padded back across the canvas to his rug, did the proper three-turns-in-place, and curled up again with his tail over his nose. Within a beat his painted breathing had resumed the sleeping rhythm it had had when they arrived.

Mei looked at Caleb. The small *after you* gesture.

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 10:42

He bent his head — the passage was three feet square, the lintel low — and stepped through. Mei followed close behind, Athena ducking her own head against Mei's shoulder for the doorway, the case still tucked under Mei's arm.

Eight feet of stone passage. Their footsteps were quiet. The light at the far end was warm.

The small wooden door at the end. He turned the brass handle. It opened without resistance — no charm-check, no second password. Brigham had built the wards into Solomon, not the door.

Inside.

The room was small. About ten feet square. Stone walls, dark wood floor, a low vaulted ceiling that matched the turret above it. A single tall narrow window on the west wall — currently showing what Caleb registered, after a beat, as not the view *outside* the south-west turret but a view of *open Lincolnshire fen at dawn,* low mist over reed-grass, a single heron in the middle distance. The window had set itself, on their entry, to what their working most needed to look at.

In the centre of the room: a working desk. Two chairs. The desk was the proper Victorian-Charms-master desk — dark wood, two inkwells, two penholders, the surface broad enough for two people to spread papers and a book or two between them without bumping elbows. A green-shaded brass lamp at the centre, lit.

To the right: a small grate, fire lit, low and steady.

To the left: the bookshelf Brigham had described. Currently empty — *self-filling,* Caleb registered — and the shelves were the right size for working-volumes rather than display. Brigham had built it to fill with whatever they needed for whatever they were working on. It was, at this moment, waiting.

Against the back wall: a small dark cabinet, the size of a wardrobe but shallower. The proper *recognition* cupboard. Brigham's own working notes inside, opening on a recognition phrase.

The room smelled faintly of old paper, beeswax polish on the desk, and the small clean note of burning oak from the grate.

Mei came in behind him. Stopped a step inside the door. Took in the room.

"*Caleb.*"

Quiet. The composed Mei-precision in the proper register of *I am in a room a Charms-master made in 1872 and left for whoever passed his test* — there was no half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth this time. The room was serious.

"*This is — extraordinary.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 10:44

Mei's mouth did the small *Caleb you absolute idiot* register at *zoom and enhance on the parsnip.*

"*Caleb. — The window is not a — *surveillance window.* — It is — *what we need to see for the work.* — *And* — *what we need to see this morning, apparently, is Lincolnshire fen at dawn,* — *which is — actually about right for the parson-letter we are writing. — The Room is — agreeing with our research thread. — Not — letting us spy on the parsnip in his vicarage.*"

She took a step toward the back wall and looked at the small dark cabinet — the recognition cupboard — that Caleb had pointed at.

There was no visible lock. No keyhole. No latch. The doors were a smooth pair of dark wood without handles. But on the right-hand door, at chest-height, was a single small carved word in raised script. The lettering was the late-Victorian script of the rest of the room. The word was:

*COGITATIO.*

Mei read it. Tilted her head.

"*Cogitatio.* — *Latin.* — *Thought,* or *the act of thinking,* or *meditation on a problem.* — That is — *cognate with the English word that follows from it.* — *Not* what the second note is for, I think. — *This* is the recognition-phrase Brigham named in the vellum: the cupboard opens to *recognition* spoken aloud. — *This is the prompt.*"

A small pause.

"*Caleb. — If we say it correctly, the cupboard gives us Brigham''s Charms notes. — If we say it wrongly, I imagine it stays shut.*"

She looked at him with the half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth — back on properly now, the Mei-warm.

"*Together? — Or separately?*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 10:45

He took her hand. Stepped up to the cupboard with her. The dark wood doors close enough to touch. The carved word *COGITATIO* at chest-height on the right-hand door.

"*Three. Two. One.*"

They spoke it together.

"*Cogitatio.*"

A small *click* registered in the wood — not loud, the proper Brigham *click* the bust had made an hour ago when Flitwick had spoken the address. The doors of the cupboard parted by an inch, then swung the rest of the way open on their own.

Inside: shelves. Five of them, narrow, full.

The shelves held papers and slim books. The papers were rolled and tied with faded cotton tape — perhaps thirty rolls. The books were leather-bound, slim, with no titles on the spines. Beside the rolls on the top shelf was a small leather-bound notebook with a single faded gilt word on its cover: *BRIGHAM.*

A second, smaller object on the top shelf beside the notebook: a sealed envelope. The wax on it was the deep red of late-Victorian sealing-wax. The envelope was addressed in the same hand as the vellum, in pencil rather than ink:

*To the Charms-master of the generation in which the bust first sees a pair. — From Eldritch Brigham.*

Mei did not, immediately, touch anything. She let her eyes go across the shelves. Took in the scope of what they were looking at.

"*Caleb.*" Quiet. "*This is — Brigham''s working library on the charm he made. — Notes. — Probably failed drafts. — His own thinking on bonded recognition and durable-room construction. — *Decades* of work.*"

She pointed to the sealed envelope.

"*And — that — is the second note. — For Flitwick. — Brigham left it here, not in his own office, because he knew that whoever opened this cupboard would, by definition, be the recognised pair, and that — *they* would be the ones who took the second note to the Charms-master.* — *We take that to Flitwick.* — *We do not open it.*"

She let her hand stay at her side.

"*The rest of this — *is ours to read.* — Eventually. — *Caleb. — That is — a year''s reading.* — *Maybe more.*"

*I get a sheet of paper and calligraphy 'The Bone Zone', heat some wax onto the back and stick it above the exit*

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 10:47

He went to the working desk. The proper Brigham desk — broad surface, two inkwells, two penholders. He found a clean sheet of vellum-quality paper from a small neat pile in a tray on one side of the desk — the Room had stocked it — and pulled one of the penholders toward him.

The pen was a proper calligraphy quill, ready-cut, the nib clean. He uncapped the right-hand inkwell.

*THE BONE ZONE.*

He did it properly, the way his father had taught him to draw the shop's signage when he was thirteen. Black ink, full strokes for the verticals, the proper serifs at the feet of the letters, the small flourish on the *Z.* Six inches across, neat across the centre of the sheet.

He blew on it. Set the quill down.

The room had no wax-pot visible, but on the corner of the desk — Caleb registered now — a small brass holder held three sticks of sealing-wax in three colours: deep red (the proper Brigham wax), black, and a faded green. Below them a small enchanted candle-flame in a glass dome, ready to soften the wax. The desk had everything.

He picked up the deep red stick. Held it over the flame. Dripped four neat blobs of wax onto the back of the sheet, one in each corner.

Walked the sheet to the small wooden door of the room — the one they had come in through, eight feet down the stone passage from Solomon — and pressed the four wax-corners against the lintel above the door, on the inside. The wax cooled and held.

*THE BONE ZONE,* in his calligraphy, was now mounted above the exit of the working room Eldritch Brigham had built in 1872 for the use of a particular kind of student which he had not, in 1872, expected would ever be found.

He stepped back. Looked at his work. Nodded once, satisfied.

Mei, by the cupboard, had been watching the whole performance in silence with the small composed Mei-face on but doing visible *I am not laughing at this* work behind her eyes.

She did not, immediately, say anything.

Then, quietly:

"*Caleb. — The man has been dead for at least seventy years, and you have just put — *THE BONE ZONE* — above the door of his working room. — In calligraphy. — In red sealing-wax. — *Brigham would have been quite confused.*"

The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth was on, fully.

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 10:49

Mei looked at *THE BONE ZONE* on the lintel.

The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth went up the small fond fraction.

"*Caleb. — That is — *our* mark. — Yes.*" Quiet. "*Brigham would be confused for about thirty seconds and then he would write something dry about it in his notebook and move on. — He left the room for *whoever was recognised,* not for *whoever was reverent.* — *We are who we are.* — *Mission accomplished.*"

She picked up the sealed envelope from the top shelf of the cupboard — careful, the proper Mei-precision with a 96-year-old document — and tucked it under her arm beside Brigham's wooden case. Did not open the case to put the envelope inside; kept the two separate so Flitwick could see the envelope was untouched. The cupboard door she pushed shut by hand. It closed with the same small *click* it had opened with, and the *COGITATIO* on the right-hand door went back to looking like a carving in wood with no working hinge behind it.

"*Flitwick.*"

She turned to the door. Stepped under *THE BONE ZONE* lintel. Caleb followed her — through the short stone passage, out past Solomon (the painted cat opening one yellow eye at their passage, registering, closing it again) — and the portrait swung shut behind them on its hinge.

The SW seventh-floor corridor. Saturday-morning quiet. The window light gold on stone.

Mei adjusted Athena on her shoulder. Looked at Caleb.

"*Charms office. — He''s still in office hours till noon. — *We give him the envelope. — He reads it. — We hear what Brigham left for him.* — Then — *lunch.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 10:52

They went down. Down two flights of moving staircases — one obliged them on the second flight, swinging back to its previous position halfway through their climb the other way — and along the third-floor corridor to the Charms classroom.

The door was open. Inside: the classroom in its proper Saturday office-hours state. A single fifth-year was in the front row at one of the small desks, waiting. He looked up at their entry, registered Mei's prefect badge, gave a small *you two were first, I came later* nod, and went back to his book.

The office door behind the chalkboard was closed. A small notice on the corner of Flitwick's desk at the front read: *Currently with a student. Wait quietly.*

Mei sat on the edge of one of the front-row desks. Caleb sat beside her. Athena on Mei's shoulder, the case and the sealed envelope under her arm. The fifth-year went back to his book.

They waited.

The Charms-office door opened after perhaps four minutes. A seventh-year girl Caleb did not know came out — Hufflepuff tie — with the small relieved face of a student who had just had a difficult conversation about a NEWT project and was glad it had gone better than she had feared. She crossed the classroom and went out. Flitwick stepped out behind her on his cushion stack.

He registered Caleb and Mei. The small bright eyes went immediately to the sealed envelope under Mei's arm. Held there for a beat.

His face did the proper Flitwick-thing it had done in his office: the small careful warmth, an under-edge of *something has happened* attention. The fifth-year in the front row had stood up and was waiting to be called.

Flitwick looked at the fifth-year. At Mei and Caleb. At the envelope.

"*Mr Ackerley.* — *Two minutes, please. — Wait here. — Mr Waddell, Miss Chang — into my office.*"

The fifth-year sat back down without complaint.

He stood up off the front-row desk and stepped across the centre aisle to her — the Hufflepuff seventh-year — before she reached the door of the classroom.

She paused. Pleasant face, mid-brown hair pinned back, the proper Hufflepuff yellow-and-black tie. The small *yes?* register of a girl who had been about to leave and had been intercepted.

"*Caleb Waddell. Ravenclaw. — Welcome back. — How''s your year starting?*"

He stuck his hand out.

She took it. Smile — the small genuine Hufflepuff one, warm without being performative.

"*Imogen. — Imogen Vale. — Pleased to meet you. — Year''s starting — *all right.* — Just sorted out a NEWT-project worry with Flitwick. — I''m better than I was twenty minutes ago.*" She huffed a small relieved laugh. "*Yours?*"

"*Started.* — *Slughorn approved my Folly.* — *Henshaw rated my wand-pause at 0.9 seconds yesterday.* — *Now my girlfriend and I are about to give Flitwick a sealed envelope from 1881.* — *So — fairly normal Saturday morning.*"

Imogen looked at the envelope under Mei's arm at the desk behind him. Her eyebrows went up.

"*That is — *not* a normal Saturday morning.* — *Good luck with — whatever that is.*"

She gave his hand a small final shake, gave Mei a friendly nod across the room — *Mei. Hi* — and went out the classroom door.

Flitwick, by the chalkboard, had registered the whole exchange with the small Flitwick-face that meant *Mr Waddell is doing Mr-Waddell-things and I have decided not to remark on it for the second time today.* He gestured at the office door.

"*In, please.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 10:58

Flitwick's office. Tea-tray still on the side-table; cups from the morning rinsed and reset. The small fire still going in the grate. The kettle on its charmed-warm trivet.

Flitwick stepped up onto his cushion behind the desk. Mei and Caleb in the two chairs facing him. Athena on Mei's shoulder. The case under Mei's left arm, the sealed envelope held separately in her right hand.

Flitwick's eyes did the small *go on, but I noticed* register at *Dumbledore said to lift people up.*

"*Mr Waddell.*" Mild. "*That was a kind thing to do. — Miss Vale was indeed having a worried morning and is now having a relieved one, and a hand-shake from a fellow seventh-year in another house is — exactly the kind of small thing Headmaster Dumbledore would approve of. — Whether she also needed* — *Mr Waddell* — *to know that you are giving me an 1881 sealed envelope this morning* — *that is a separate question. — But the introduction was a good instinct.*"

The half-millimetre at the corner of his mouth. Then he sobered.

"*The envelope.*"

Mei held it out to him across the desk.

Flitwick took it carefully in both small hands. Looked at the front — *To the Charms-master of the generation in which the bust first sees a pair. — From Eldritch Brigham* — held the wax seal up to the lamp for a beat to check it was intact. It was.

He set it on the desk in front of him. Did not, immediately, break the seal.

"*I am going to ask you both, before I read this. — *What did the first note say?* — Not the literal text. — *What did Brigham give you.*"

He looked at them with the small Flitwick-attention.

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 11:00

Flitwick was quiet for a long beat.

He had been looking at Caleb while Caleb spoke. The small bright eyes had not left him. The composed Charms-master face had gone, by small degrees, *softer* — not visibly emotional, but the small careful Flitwick-warmth had deepened in a way Caleb registered without being able to name.

When Caleb stopped speaking Flitwick did not, immediately, respond.

He looked at Mei. The same long beat.

Then back at Caleb.

"*Mr Waddell.*" Quiet. "*Miss Chang.* — *That is — almost word for word what Brigham wrote about his own marriage in his second published volume of Charms theory. — Which he did not publish under his own name, but under his wife''s. — *Helena Brigham — On Bound Cognition* — 1879. — Two years before he set this charm. — I have read it three times in my career.*"

A pause. He picked up the sealed envelope from the desk. Looked at the wax seal again. Set it back down.

"*He set the charm to find — *more of us.*" — That is what the work was for. — *He did not expect to find any.* — He wrote, in the preface to that volume, that he and Helena were *almost certainly statistically alone in their century in this respect,* and that he was content to be so. — *He set the charm anyway.* — Because *Helena asked him to.* — She — *Mrs Brigham* — believed, against his evidence, that another pair would come along. — *She was right.*"

He looked at them with the small Flitwick-attention, more focused now.

"*Children.* — *I have been Charms-master in this castle for forty-three years.* — *I have known about Brigham''s charm for forty-three years.* — *I have not, in all that time, known what was on the inside of the sealed note he left for whichever Charms-master would have to hand over the second key.* — *I am about to read it.* — *With your permission.*"

He picked up the envelope. Held it carefully. His small hands shook by exactly the smallest degree it was possible to register and still be a steady-handed Charms master. He set it back on the desk.

"*Either of you. — Both of you. — Mr Waddell, you first.*"

He waited.

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 11:01

Flitwick nodded slowly.

"*Thank you, Mr Waddell. — I — *would* like to see the vellum. — Not today. — Today belongs to you and Miss Chang. — But — *when you are ready,* in a week or a month or a year, *I would like to read it properly.* — Bring it to one of my office hours. — *That is all.*"

He picked up the sealed envelope. Held it for one more beat — the small steadying breath of a man who had been a Charms-master in this castle for forty-three years and was about to read a note left for the holder of his position by his second predecessor.

Broke the wax seal.

Inside: a folded sheet of vellum, smaller than the one Caleb and Mei had received. He unfolded it. The same precise late-Victorian copperplate. He read it silently — the small Flitwick-eyes moving steadily across the lines — and his face did not change visibly for the first paragraph.

Then, partway through what looked like the second paragraph, his small hand came up to his mouth briefly.

He kept reading.

When he finished, he set the note flat on the desk and did not, immediately, speak.

After a long beat he picked the note back up. Folded it carefully. Set it inside the envelope. Set the envelope inside the small breast pocket of his Charms-master robe, where it disappeared with the small *fwf* of robe-fabric.

He looked at them.

"*Children.*"

Quiet.

"*Brigham — has — left me — some instructions which I am to follow. — *And* — *a personal note* — *which I am to keep.* — *Neither of these things concerns you operationally.* — *The room is yours under the same terms he gave you in your own note. — The key — and I will tell you now, Mr Waddell, *because you asked the practical question this morning* — is — *two keys.* — *One per recognised pair-member.* — *Brigham was clear about that in his instructions to me. — Each of you will have your own.* — *Which means that even if one of you should be travelling and the other should be in Britain at need, either of you can return to the room.* — *That is the operational answer.*"

A pause.

"*The non-operational thing I will say is this.* — *Brigham wrote, in the personal portion of the note, that he was — *grateful.* — *To whichever Charms-master held this office at the moment the first pair was found.* — *For having lived to see it.* — *He did not know it would be me.* — *He could not have. — But — *I am.* — *And — I will say to you both — privately, in this office, without your needing to register it as a sentimental moment — *thank you. — For triggering the charm. — For coming to me first. — For doing the work properly.*"

He looked at his desk. At his folded hands. At the side of the room.

"*That is all I will say. — Miss Chang. Mr Waddell. — *Go and have your lunch.* — Mr Ackerley is waiting in the classroom. — *I will see you in this office again when you are ready to show me what Brigham wrote you.*"

He gave them the small *off you go* gesture.

The small Flitwick-warmth was full on, with a register under it Caleb had not previously seen — the proper *I am moved* register, quiet, contained, in the way a 43-year Charms-master allowed himself such a register: by allowing it to be visible for exactly one minute and then putting it away.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 11:04

He stood. Held his hand out across the desk. Flitwick took it — the small precise Flitwick-handshake, his hand smaller than Caleb's but the grip surprisingly firm — and held it for one proper beat.

"*Mr Waddell.*"

"*Professor.*"

Mei stood beside him. Did the small precise prefect-bow of the head — chin to chest, half a second, back up — the proper Mei-acknowledgement of a teacher she respected and a moment that mattered.

"*Professor.*"

"*Miss Chang.*"

They went out. Through the small office door, past the chalkboard, through the classroom where Mr Ackerley looked up hopefully — Flitwick's voice from behind them called *Mr Ackerley, come in please* — and out into the third-floor corridor.

The corridor was its proper Saturday-late-morning self. A small clutch of fourth-years going somewhere with broomsticks. A single Hufflepuff first-year apparently lost, looking at a piece of paper with the proper *I should not be on this floor* face.

Mei did not, immediately, say anything. The case under her left arm, Athena on her left shoulder, her right hand free and finding his without comment as they walked.

They went past the staircase to lunch. The Hall would not start serving until twelve — they had an hour. The corridor light long and gold through the high arched windows.

After perhaps thirty paces Mei said, very quietly, beside him:

"*Caleb.* — *I do not have words for this morning yet. — Give me — until lunch.* — *Until I have eaten something. — Then I will.*"

She squeezed his hand once. Kept walking.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 11:07

Mei's hand tightened on his.

She did not look at him. She did not break stride. The composed Mei-prefect walking-pace continued the way it had been, the proper public register of a girl with an owl on her shoulder and a 96-year-old case under her arm crossing the third-floor corridor of Hogwarts at quarter past eleven on a Saturday morning.

But her hand, in his, tightened — and held — and did not let go.

After about ten paces she said, very quietly, without turning:

"*I know.*"

The two syllables were the warmest Caleb had heard her say in three days.

They kept walking.

"You Soloed me? A film which I can reference because it is out at this time and full of British actors opening and closing doors on spaceships? God, you are perfect."

## Narrator

OOC: Quick check — Caleb's "You Soloed me?" is referencing *Star Wars* (1977), which came out on 25 May in the US and 27 December in the UK. We're at 3 September 1977. In Britain, *Star Wars* has not been released yet — it's about four months from a UK theatrical opening. American audiences are deep into it, but a Manchester half-blood who's been at Hogwarts since the start of June would not have seen it. The Muggle-paper buzz from American previews may have reached Britain, but the film itself hasn't.

So Caleb saying *which I can reference because it is out at this time* is — slightly — running ahead of the actual UK calendar. He could plausibly know *about* Star Wars (American papers, magazines, maybe a cousin's letter), but he wouldn't have *seen* it yet and the reference would not necessarily land for a British wizarding-half-Chinese girl who has been at Hogwarts since June.

Two options:

1. **Run it with Mei not getting the reference,** because it's American and not yet released in Britain — she'd ask what *Soloed* means.
2. **Treat it as Caleb the Muggle-adjacent Manchester boy having heard about *Star Wars* via Muggle American magazines or cousins,** and have Mei roll her eyes at *Caleb being into the American film thing* without quite getting it.

Either way, the Han Solo reference (closing doors on spaceships) isn't going to land for Mei the way it would land for an American teen in September '77. She'd be quietly amused but lost.

Running option 2.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 11:08

Mei's mouth did the small *Caleb you are referencing something I do not know* register.

"*You Soloed me.*" She repeated it back at him. "*Caleb. — That is — *not in any context I recognise.* — *Solo* as a verb is — performing alone? — And — *a film with British actors opening and closing doors on spaceships* is — *what.*"

She glanced sideways at him. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth.

"*Caleb. — Is this the American thing your cousin in Reading wrote you about in July? — The one with the *robots*? — That came out in America in May and is not coming to British cinemas until *Christmas?*"

She did not, however, stop holding his hand. Or stop walking. She had registered that the line was *meant* as affectionate even if she could not unpack the reference.

"*You are — telling me I am perfect* — *in a language I do not yet have.* — *That is — fine, Caleb.* — *I''ll see the film when it comes out. — Then you can tell me again.*"

She glanced at Athena on her shoulder. The owl had registered her name twice in the conversation and had opened one yellow eye in the *I am being discussed* register. The other eye stayed half-closed.

She slowed her pace as the corridor opened toward the main staircase. Stood with him at the top of the flight. Athena adjusted on her shoulder.

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 11:11

Mei's face did the proper *yes that is the right idea* register.

"*Caleb. — *Yes.* — Our office. — That is — *exactly* what it''s for.*"

She turned on her heel without further discussion and started back the way they had come. Athena adjusted on her shoulder at the new direction. The case under Mei's arm came along.

Up the staircases. Two flights, the proper Hogwarts moving-staircase tax — one of them obliging them by going the wrong way for half a flight and then righting — and along the seventh-floor corridor past the Barnabas tapestry — Caleb registered it on the way past, the wizard in yellow with the trolls in awkward fourth position — to the south-west end and the foot of the turret stair.

Solomon was asleep on his rug in his portrait. The painted breathing rose and fell.

Caleb stepped up to the portrait this time without Mei needing to prompt him.

"*King Solomon. — Meow meoooww meeow. — We come from the bust.*"

Solomon's ear flicked. He raised his small head, opened the amber-yellow eyes, and considered Caleb with the same dignified attention as before. Then, after the proper one-beat assessment:

"*Hello, Sir. — Hello, Miss. — Hello, small Pygmy. — *Welcome back so soon.*"

He stood, stretched, padded off the right edge of the canvas. The portrait swung outward on its hinge. The short stone passage. *THE BONE ZONE* in Caleb's calligraphy on the inside of the lintel — visible as Mei stepped through, who registered it again with the small *Caleb you absolute idiot* fold of her mouth.

Inside the room: exactly as they had left it twenty-five minutes ago. The fire in the grate, lower now but still burning. The green-shaded brass lamp on the desk. The window currently showing — Caleb registered, with a small *huh* — *not* Lincolnshire fen at dawn but a soft-lit early-evening view of a small Cotswold-stone village from a distance, with the spire of a small church visible against a soft mauve sky.

Mei stopped in front of the window. Looked at it. Looked at Caleb.

"*The window has — *moved.* — *To Cirencester.* — *Possibly.* — *That is — *what we are working on now.*"

She set the wooden case down on the working desk. Carefully, in the centre, where it would not be jostled. Then sat down in one of the two chairs at the desk — the right-hand one, the *Mei* chair as it had been by accident — and looked at the empty self-filling bookshelf for a moment.

A single small movement registered on the shelf. A book *appeared* on the middle shelf — soft *thump* — and a moment later, a second book *appeared* beside it. Caleb stepped closer. Read the spines.

*Magickal Wiltshire: a Topographical and Historical Survey* — slim, late-Victorian.

*The Talbot Family of Lacock: A Wizarding Genealogy 1539-1944* — slimmer still, more recent, perhaps 1950s.

The shelf was filling itself with what they would need for the next part of the work.

Mei made a small sound that was almost a laugh.

"*Caleb.* — *The room is — extremely helpful.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 11:14

The room did not, immediately, respond.

The fire in the grate did its small *crack.* The lamp on the desk continued its warm yellow glow. The Cotswold-village window held its mauve evening sky.

Nothing on the bookshelf moved.

Mei, in the *Mei* chair, looked at Caleb with the small dry register.

"*Caleb. — The room is — *not* sentient.* — *It is a working-space charm of considerable subtlety, set by Brigham, and it responds to *what we are working on as a yoked pair.* — *It does not — *take dessert requests.*"

A pause. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth.

"*Also — Caleb — it would not need to. — *Your girlfriend* — *is sitting at the desk.* — *And* — *Caleb. — Just ask me what dessert I like, and I will tell you.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 11:15

Mei looked at him for a beat. The composed Mei-face on, but the half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth doing the soft warm thing.

"*A recipe book.* — *Caleb.* — *All right. — Fair. — Brigham''s shelf was probably not going to give you one anyway, but — fair attempt.*"

A small pause. She thought properly.

"*My favourite dessert.* — *Caleb. — That is — actually a difficult question.* — *I love a lot of desserts. — But — if you are asking what *one thing* I would want a man to bake me to impress me. — Not bought. *Baked.*"

She tapped her finger once on the desk.

"*My grandmother — my mother''s mother, in Manchester — makes a *Chinese-style egg tart.* — Hong Kong egg tart, properly. *Daan taat.* — Small, the size of a saucer, flaky pastry, the custard set but still soft, slightly browned on top from the oven. — *I have not had hers since June.* — *That is — what I would want.*"

A breath. The Mei-warmth.

"*Caleb. — I will warn you. — *It is — a hard bake.* — The pastry is — *not* a normal British shortcrust. It is a *laminated* pastry — *water dough* and *oil dough,* folded together — and the custard has to be eggy enough to set but not so eggy it scrambles in the oven. — *Bakers spend years on it.* — *But.* — *If you tried,* — *and brought me one,* — *even if it was — *not perfect* — *I would — *love it.*"

She did not, however, push the recipe further. Looked at him properly.

"*Why are you thinking about baking me a dessert.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 11:17

Mei went still.

The composed Mei-face held — but her eyes did the small *I heard that* register, the one that had no half-millimetre at the corner of the mouth attached to it, the one that meant she was actually listening.

She did not, immediately, respond. The Mei-pause that meant she was honouring her own *we are not talking about this until lunch* boundary and also recognising that Caleb had just said something that did not, exactly, *talk about it* — said something *adjacent to* it — said something that had been allowed under the literal terms of her ask.

The fire did its small *crack.*

After a long beat she stood up from the *Mei* chair. Crossed the small distance to where he was standing by the bookshelf. Stood in front of him. Set both her hands lightly on the front of his robe at his chest. Looked up at him.

"*Caleb.*"

Quiet. The Mei-warm at full. The composed face *softening* by the specific amount she allowed it to soften when she was choosing to be soft.

"*That sentence — *interwoven requirement couple of property holders* — *is — *the most Caleb sentence you have ever produced about me.* — *And I am — *keeping it.* — *Forever.*"

She did not, however, kiss him. She rested her forehead briefly against his chest — the small *I am here* gesture — and then stepped back.

"*Lunch.* — *Then I will have words. — Until then, Caleb. — *We are.* — *That is the whole sentence for now.*"

She picked her satchel up from where she had set it beside the *Mei* chair, settled it on her free shoulder. Athena adjusted. The case stayed on the desk — left in the room, the proper safekeeping Caleb had suggested.

"*Now. — Out. — Athena, back to the corridor with us. — *Caleb. — Lunch.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 11:19

He gestured Mei toward the door with both hands. The proper *go on, out, lunch* gesture.

Mei made a small huff. Went. Through the small wooden door, down the eight feet of stone passage, out past Solomon's portrait — Solomon registered them passing with one open amber eye and a small dignified *Sir, Miss, small Pygmy* — and the portrait swung quietly shut behind them on its hinge. The wall closed up. The SW seventh-floor corridor was its quiet self again.

They went down. Down to the third floor, down to the second, down to the Entrance Hall. The Great Hall had begun its lunch-set — the tables filling with proper Saturday-lunch fare, the smell of soup and bread coming up the corridor — and a thicker stream of students than at breakfast was heading in. By eleven forty-five the Hall would be properly populated.

Mei led them to the Ravenclaw table. The same spot as breakfast, third-down on Caleb's side, opposite each other. Athena rode Mei's shoulder. She set her satchel on the bench beside her.

She poured herself tea first. Took a long sip. Closed her eyes briefly. Opened them.

"*Right.*" Quiet. "*Lunch. — Then I will have words.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 11:31

Mei made a small *snrk* into her tea at *what sex is to the physical aspect of a relationship, our brains have been at the mental version of that so hardcore that a charm from ~100 years ago recognized it.*

She set the tea down. Wiped under one lens of her glasses with her thumb. Composed.

"*Caleb. — That is — *yes, that is what happened.* — *And I am — *not* going to phrase it that way to the next person who asks how my day is going.* — *You can if you want. — I am going to say — *we had a good morning, thank you, how was yours.*"

She picked the tea back up. Took another sip.

"*Also — Caleb — *next person is on me* — *fair.* — *I will handle them.* — *But — be aware* — *Lin* will be the next person, *almost certainly,* and *Lin will know* from one look at us that something has happened. — *He saw us nap together yesterday in your bed.* — *He knew before this morning that we were a thing.* — *He''ll know that whatever happened this morning is — *more* than yesterday.* — *I do not, particularly, want to keep it from Lin. — But — I do not want to *give him the whole thing* across a lunch table either. — *Caleb. — Read my register and match it.*"

A breath.

"*And — yes. — Owl on shoulder. — *Bonus.*"

Athena, on her left shoulder, made the small *kweee* of being mentioned and being content.

Around them the Hall was filling out properly now. A few faces Caleb knew were already at tables. Lin was visible coming in through the great oak doors at the far end, satchel slung, freshly-showered hair still damp at the ends — he had presumably been at Quidditch practice this morning, the Ravenclaw team did Saturday early-morning trials in early September.

Lin spotted Caleb and Mei at the table. Started walking toward them down the length of the Hall.

A small breath. She had visibly steered the conversation onto baking-mechanics as a way of giving them both a settled register before Lin arrived — Caleb registered it as the choice it was.
