## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 14:36

Esme had stood up from her chair and slung her satchel. Was a half-step toward the cafe door when Caleb stood too — came round the corner of the patio table — and hugged her.

Not big. The proper *I am here, you did the hard thing* hug — both arms loosely around her shoulders, a beat of contact, a small squeeze at the top of her back.

Esme did not, immediately, return it.

Then she did. One arm came around his side. The composed-Esme held for one beat longer, and then her shoulders dropped — properly dropped, the way they had been holding all afternoon — and she pressed her forehead briefly against his collarbone. The small *I am taking this for a beat* shape of a fourth-year being held by an older student who was, for the moment, family-adjacent.

She did not cry. Esme did not cry. But she breathed properly — a small full breath in, a small full breath out — for the first time since Caleb and Mei had intercepted her at the dormitory stair.

After perhaps five seconds she stepped back. Adjusted her satchel. Adjusted her glasses. The composed-Esme was visibly back in place, but with the small *that helped more than I had room to admit* register at the corners of her eyes.

"*Caleb.*" Quiet. "*Thank you.*"

She turned to Mei. Mei stood. Held her arms out — the small *if you want one* offer — and Esme stepped into it. Mei's hug was different from Caleb's: properly contained, one arm round Esme's shoulders, the other at the back of her head, the prefect-and-younger-girl shape of a Mei who had been the older cousin in her own family for ten years. Held for the proper beat. Released.

Esme stepped back. Picked up her last slice of bread from the table where she had set it down to be hugged.

"*Sunday or Monday. — *I''ll find one of you.*"

She turned and went out through the cafe interior. The pale-blue door opened, she stepped through, and it closed quietly behind her.

The patio held its quiet.

Mei sat back down. Caleb sat back down. They were alone at the table with the half-eaten bread, three empty coffee cups, and the warm September Paris air around them.

Mei reached across the table and took Caleb's hand. The small *we did the thing properly* squeeze.

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 14:39

He went back through the cafe door from the patio into the warm yellow interior. The glass case at the bar held the three pastries that had been there when they came in: a small almond croissant, a *pain au chocolat,* and a *canelé* — the small dark caramelised Bordeaux pastry with the soft rum-vanilla custard centre.

He took a plate from the stack behind the bar. Slid all three onto it. Set the plate on the bar.

The espresso machine.

It was a proper Italian lever machine — La Pavoni, brass and chrome, the long pull-lever, the small portafilter, the steaming wand. The kind of machine Muggle cafes were just starting to get serious about in 1977. Caleb had seen one once, in a Manchester coffee bar his mother had taken him to when he was fifteen, and had watched the barista pull a shot.

He had never operated one.

But the Room had made it run itself for the three coffees they had drunk on arrival, which meant the milk-frothing apparatus was already hot and the espresso group was up to temperature.

He found two wide-brimmed white ceramic cups under the bar — the proper *latte bowl* shape, broad-mouthed and shallow, the kind a barista needed to do art on top of — and set them on the drip tray. Found a small steel jug for the milk. Pulled the lid off a small jug of milk that had also appeared at the bar — cold whole milk, the proper fat content for foam.

He worked it out by feel.

Filled the steel jug halfway. Held it under the steaming wand. Found the lever. Opened it — the wand hissed, the milk went into the proper *whirlpool* spiral that Caleb had watched the Manchester barista produce, the small *thwip-thwip-thwip* of milk being aerated by the steam jet — and he held it for the count of about fifteen seconds the way the man in Manchester had. The milk doubled in volume. The jug got warmer in his hand. Closed the lever.

The milk inside was, somehow, properly textured. The Room of Requirement was, evidently, going to make the milk work whether his technique merited it or not.

He set the jug on the bar. Pulled the portafilter, knocked the spent grounds out into a small bin — the Room had already made him a shot for the two cups; the grounds were warm — and pulled a fresh dose with the small precise *click-tamp* he had also watched the Manchester man do. Locked the portafilter into the group head. Pulled the lever.

Two streams of espresso came out into the two small cups he had set under the spouts. The small honey-coloured *crema* on top.

He set the cups onto the wide-brimmed latte bowls. The espresso went into the bottom; the latte bowl was for the milk.

He picked up the steel jug. Tilted it. Started pouring from a height the way he had seen the Manchester barista pour. The milk went into the cup in the small even stream, and as the cup filled and Caleb brought the jug closer to the surface, the white foam began to surface on top of the brown.

He tried the small wiggle that produced a heart.

It produced — *not* a heart.

What appeared on the surface of the first latte was a small lopsided shape that resembled, more than anything else, a Pygmy Owl in flight, off-balance left wing extended.

Caleb stared at it.

He had not been *thinking* about Athena while pouring. But the Room was — the Room had presumably picked up the pattern from his head, or the milk had decided on its own, or the cosmos of small Saturday-afternoon coincidences had decided for him.

The second cup he poured more deliberately. *Heart, please.* The milk produced — a proper recognisable, if slightly lopsided, heart shape.

He carried both wide-brimmed cups and the plate of three pastries back out to the patio.

Set them on the table.

Mei had been watching through the back-door windows of the cafe with the small composed Mei-amused register on the whole time. When Caleb set the cups in front of her, she registered the latte art and her face did the proper *oh* register.

"*Caleb. — *Did you just — *make a Pygmy Owl in foam in the Room of Requirement.*"

She picked up the cup. Held it carefully so the foam pattern stayed visible. The small lopsided Athena in flight, slightly lopsided heart-foam beside it.

"*Caleb. — *That is — *one of the most absurd and lovely things you have ever done.*"

"get used to it because I do them all the time... you may not always be amused but you will never be bored. that is my promise." *kiss on her cheek*

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 14:44

Mei made the small *snrk* into the latte foam at *you may not always be amused but you will never be bored* and tilted her head a fraction toward him for the kiss. He pressed it to her cheek — warm, brief, the proper soft-public register, his lips at the small soft place just below her cheekbone.

She did not move away after. Held her face there for a beat. Her free hand had come up and lightly squeezed his wrist where it rested on the table.

"*Caleb. — *I am keeping that promise.* — *And I am — *holding you to it.*"

She picked up her latte bowl with both hands. Took a careful sip from the side that did not destroy the small lopsided heart on the surface. The proper post-coffee Mei-exhale of contentment.

"*Caleb. — *The Pygmy-owl one is yours. — *I get the heart.*"

A pause. Her eyes went briefly to the cafe interior — to the empty cafe, the espresso machine, the small Paris-September warmth of the room.

"*This room — *for Esme — *was — *perfect, Caleb. — *Not just because it was private. — *Because — *the small cafe shape made the conversation possible.* — *We could not have had that conversation in our common room. — *We could not have had it in the Bone Zone, the other one. — *We could have had it in the working room behind Solomon, but — *the working room would have made it feel like — *we were solving a problem.* — *Which we are. — *But — *Esme needed the conversation first, and the problem-solving second. — *The cafe gave her — *coffee and a basket of bread and a chair to sit in and people across from her. — *That was — *what she needed.*"

She took another sip. The Mei-warmth, full.

"*Caleb. — *You are getting very good at this.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 14:46

Mei lowered her cup. Looked at him across the table with the dry-warm register that meant she had heard the prompt and was deciding how much of it to give him.

"*Caleb.*"

A pause. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came on.

"*You are going to be a good — *Caleb.* — *That is the sentence.* — *And — *you are. — *Already.* — *I am not going to say — *good dad* — *or — *good husband* — *or — *good anything-else you are fishing for.* — *Caleb. — *I am not feeding the prompt.* — *You are going to be a good Caleb, which is — *the only thing I am qualified to assess you on at eighteen, and which is — *the assessment I am making.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 15:20

Mei made a small huff at *I have you trapped* — *Caleb, you do not trap a Ravenclaw with a logical syllogism, you flatter yourself* — and pointedly went back to her book.

He pulled his satchel onto his lap. Drew *Companion Owls — A Quiet History* out. Opened it at the page where he had left off in the library yesterday — somewhere around page eighteen, the chapter on the bond developing without training.

The west-window table held its quiet. Mei across from him reading her *Charms Theory NEWT Companion.* The afternoon settled into the proper Saturday Ravenclaw common-room afternoon: low conversation at other tables, a fire at the south end going low, the occasional rustle of a page, the long slanting late-afternoon September light through the high arched windows.

He read.

Hesba Ollivander's prose had the small careful Victorian-clinical voice of a woman who had been observing companion owls for forty years and was being precise about what she did and did not know. Page nineteen — on the *aging* of companion-owl bonds: *the bond, once formed, does not weaken with the years; the literature contains no recorded case of a companion owl who, having bonded, later detached. Death of the person, in every case the writer has been able to trace, is followed by the owl''s departure into the wild, and the owl is not, by any subsequent reckoning, observed to attach again to another person.*

Caleb set the book down on his thigh briefly. Looked at it. Picked it back up. Kept reading.

Page twenty — on the *flight-range* of the bond: *the writer has documented, by correspondence with surviving owners, eight cases in which a companion owl flew from her person''s established residence to a place the person had never previously been, simply because the person was there. In one of those eight cases the distance was 380 miles. In another, 800. The owl, in every case, arrived in the manner of a working post-owl arriving at an addressed recipient — directly, without searching.*

A pause. He looked up briefly. Mei was still reading her Charms book.

Page twenty-one — on what happens during periods of separation: *the writer notes, with the caveat that this is anecdotal across a small sample, that the companion owl during periods of separation appears to retain — at distance — some awareness of her person''s state. There are accounts, repeated across multiple owners, of owls becoming agitated at the precise hour of bad news affecting their person, even when the owl is in the Owlery and the person is hundreds of miles away.*

The clock on the eagle-door mantel ticked over from 15:32 to 15:33. The afternoon settled deeper.

Marriage.* — *That tradition is — *useful for thinking but maybe not — *true about the actual experience.* — *From inside it — *maybe the bond and the love are — *the same thing seen from two angles.* — *Or — *two things that grew up together so completely that they cannot be separated in practice.* — *Caleb. — *I — *do not know which version is true.*"

She set the cup down. Did not, however, drink yet.

"*And — *the *we just felt like boyfriend and girlfriend from the moment you said yes to one date* part — *Caleb. — *Yes. — *That.* — *Because — *we had been *acting as* a yoked pair for years before we put words on it.* — *The yes-to-one-date was — *the public acknowledgement of a thing that had been already happening.* — *It did not have to develop. — *It already had.* — *That is — *probably the answer.*"

She picked up the water. Drank a proper sip. Set it down.

"*Caleb. — *That is a — *very Caleb question to ask.* — *And I am — *going to think about it.* — *Possibly for years.* — *We may have an answer when we are forty.*"

She set the book back on the table. Did not open it.

"*All right. — *Yes. — *It is. — *Caleb. — *Thank you for that one.*"

The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth was on, soft warm.

The west-window light had begun the small shift from gold to amber as the afternoon went toward early evening. The common room held its proper Saturday post-tea quiet. Davina was still at her duty-rosters but had visibly slowed — the last roster was almost done.

## Narrator

OOC: Right — *we can also leave on non-Hogsmeade weekends* is a real point. Hogsmeade weekends are *officially-sanctioned* trips off-grounds for third-years and up; they're not the only way Caleb and Mei get out of the castle.

Other ways off-grounds for seventh-years:
- **Medical leave with a professor's note** (the Henshaw thing for Wilfred on Wednesday).
- **Permission from Head of House / Deputy / Headmaster** for family reasons (visits home, illness, funerals, weddings).
- **Floo from Hogsmeade** on a Hogsmeade weekend, technically — *or* the lesser-known fact that **the Hogwarts Floo network has nodes the staff can authorise** (Flitwick's office has one, McGonagall's office has one, Dumbledore's office has one). Students don't normally use them but they can be granted on request for legitimate reasons.
- **Seventh-year Apparition test** — Caleb and Mei would both have passed their Apparition test in sixth year, giving them the legal right to Apparate from anywhere unwarded once they're outside the castle grounds. They can Apparate from Hogsmeade on any day off, not just Hogsmeade weekends, *if* they can get to Hogsmeade.

So Mei's calendar above was overly Hogsmeade-anchored. Caleb's right — they have more flexibility than that. Running.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 15:59

Mei looked at him.

The composed Mei-precision held for a beat. Then the half-millimetre came on, full soft.

"*Caleb. — *Two hours.* — *Yes. — *Working room.* — *Brigham''s. — *Not the Bone Zone.* — *Caleb. — *I want to start reading.*"

She picked up her satchel. Slung it. Caleb stood with her. Picked up his own.

She glanced once across the common room. Davina at her prefect-table had finished her duty-rosters and was packing her clipboard away — properly done for the afternoon, the Saturday-prefect-work was over. The west-window light had gone amber. A few students at other tables looking up at the gathering-up movement.

Davina caught Mei's eye across the room. Did the small *I see you both leaving* nod. Did not, however, raise an eyebrow.

Mei nodded back.

She took Caleb's hand. They went across the common room toward the eagle door. The students who registered them registered them; the proper Saturday-common-room non-comment held. Out through the eagle door — Mei answered the riddle (*if you have me, you want to share me; if you share me, you haven''t got me — what am I?* — *a secret*) — and into the seventh-floor corridor.

The walk to the south-west turret took about four minutes. Past the Barnabas tapestry. Around the corner. Down the narrowing older corridor. To Solomon at his small Persian-rug portrait at the foot of the turret stair.

Caleb stepped up.

"*King Solomon. — *Meow meow meow. — *We have come from the bust.*"

Solomon's painted ear flicked. He raised his head. The proper amber-yellow eyes opened. Considered Caleb. Considered Mei. The small *I have seen these two before* register.

"*Hello, Sir. — *Hello, Miss. — *I see the small Pygmy is — *elsewhere this afternoon.* — *Come through.*"

He walked off the right edge of the canvas. The portrait swung outward on its hinge. The short stone passage. *THE BONE ZONE* on the inside of the lintel above the door at the end. Mei snorted once at the calligraphy as she passed under it.

Inside the room.

The window had changed itself again. Not the Lincolnshire fen at dawn. Not the Cotswold-stone village. This time it was a *small wood at twilight* — proper Northern-European mixed conifer-and-beech, the kind of woodland a Pygmy Owl would nest in, with low evening light through the trunks and a sense of long quiet. The window had taken in *the companion-owl thread* Caleb had been reading.

The fire in the grate was already lit, low and steady — the room was anticipating their use.

The desk was as they had left it: Brigham's wooden case sitting in the middle, vellum and extraction rod safe inside the velvet, the green-shaded brass lamp on. The cupboard at the back wall closed, *COGITATIO* in its carved place.

The self-filling bookshelf had two more books on it than this morning. Caleb stepped close and read the spines.

*Companion Owls — A Quiet History* (Hesba Ollivander, 1922) — a second copy, the one from the library, evidently the Room had decided one was for him to carry and one was for the room.

And — beside it — a slim leather volume Caleb did not recognise, with no title on the spine, just a small embossed *EB* at the bottom. Eldritch Brigham.

The Room had brought one of Brigham's own notebooks out of the cupboard for them.

Mei stopped beside Caleb at the shelf. Registered the EB volume. Her face did the small *oh, the room is — helpful* register.

"*Caleb. — *The room is — *anticipating.* — *It has — *pulled one of Brigham''s personal notebooks for us without us asking.*"

She picked up the small leather volume. Held it carefully. Did not, immediately, open it.

"*Caleb. — *I am going to take this chair.*" She pointed to the right-hand of the two — the *Mei* chair from this morning. "*You — *Brigham''s case. — *Open the vellum properly and re-read it once.* — *Then take a fresh sheet from the desk pile and start making the notes on Aedith and the parishes and the Path that we have so far, so we have a *summary of our own research* in this room as a starting point.* — *We are — *building the file.* — *Caleb. — *Hour and a half. — *Then dinner.*"

She sat. Set the *EB* volume on the desk in front of her. Did not, however, open it. Folded her hands on top of it. Looked at Caleb.

"*Are we doing the work.*"

## User

"yes"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 16:08

He sat down in the *Caleb* chair across the desk from her. Pulled Brigham's wooden case toward him. Opened it carefully. Lifted the rolled vellum out — the dark-aged cylinder of it now familiar to his fingers — and unrolled it on the desk. Set the small green-shaded brass lamp closer so the late-Victorian copperplate caught the light.

He read it again. Properly. The whole thing this time — *to the pair whom the diadem has finally seen* — through *may the work be good. — Eldritch Brigham.*

The second read landed differently than the first. This morning Mei had read it aloud to him on the heart-shaped couch and he had heard it as *we have been told a thing about ourselves.* Now, alone with the vellum at the working desk, the second read landed as *here is the task.* Brigham had not just told them they were a pair. He had told them *the world has need of what two yoked minds can produce that one cannot, and the world will not benefit from a yoked mind which spent its years on small things.*

He set the vellum down. Took a fresh sheet from the small pile in the tray on his side of the desk. Uncapped the right-hand inkwell. Picked up a clean quill.

Across the desk, Mei had opened the *EB* notebook. She was reading the first page silently, her finger moving slowly down the line of script. Her face had gone into the proper reading-Mei attention. She did not look up.

He wrote at the top of his sheet:

> *AEDITH WULFRICSDOHTER and the PATH — research file as of Sat 3 Sept 1977*

Underlined it. Started.

He worked. Slow at first — finding the right shape for the file — then faster as the structure settled. He wrote the *young woman* section first: Aedith's name, parents, dates, visions, silentium, the twenty-one years of writing, the gravestone, the inscription. Then *Cuthbert:* born Sleaford, son of Aelfric the wheelwright, priest of St Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh for thirty-one years, herbal 1314, parish casebook presumed lost. Then *the four petitioners:* Stainford (parson's register burned 1493), Kirkham (Lancashire, continuous register from 1287), Margery of Lacock (commonplace book at Cirencester or Talbot archive), Iohannes Capellanus de Sleaforde — named twice in the parish register, not in the Compendium, *the most interesting one.*

Then *document targets:* Cirencester / Cotswold Branch of Magical Records Office; the Talbot family private archive; the parish chest at Æthelthryth-on-the-Marsh; the Lincolnshire Calendar entries (1311 bog fever, 1312 four petitioners named); Hesba Ollivander 1922 *Companion Owls* read by Madam Pince in 1923 (anomaly noted).

Then *open hypotheses,* in a numbered list. Visions and Path may be separate phenomena. Cuthbert may have reverse-engineered the Path from Aedith's visions. Aedith's silence may have been her own actions going wrong. Three readings on whether Cuthbert hid her, had a child with her, or marked her writings not her body. *Did Cuthbert walk it himself, or only record.* The *bog conditions / parish-chest* question for the parson letter.

The quill moved steadily. Mei across the desk was reading her EB notebook in proper silence. The fire did its small *crack.*

By 17:15 he had filled three sheets and was on a fourth. The structure had solidified — it was a proper working file now, the kind he and Mei could both read and add to.

He set the quill down. Stretched his back. Looked up.

Mei was watching him.

She had set the EB notebook open on the desk with a finger marking her place. The composed Mei-face was on, but with the small *I have been quietly observing for a while* register.

"*Caleb.*" Quiet. "*You have been writing for an hour and ten minutes straight. — *That is — *not your usual pace.* — *The room is — *helping you.*"

A breath.

"*Brigham''s notebook is — *what I expected.* — *Charms theory, Victorian register, dense.* — *Not for a first afternoon. — *I have read about eight pages and have understood — *six.* — *He writes the way Hesba Ollivander writes, but about charms instead of owls. — *Careful, slow, precise.* — *It will take time.* — *Which we have.*"

She looked at the window — the small Northern-European twilight wood — and at the fire.

"*Caleb. — *Dinner in forty-five minutes.* — *We pack up at quarter to six and walk down.* — *Are you in the middle of something or at a place to stop.*"

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 17:15

"*Caleb. — *No.* — *I was asking* — *whether you were in the middle of something or at a place to stop.* — *Not — *please stop.* — *If you are in flow — *keep going. — *I''ll keep reading.* — *I''ll tap you at quarter to six.*"

She picked up the EB notebook again. Found her place. The composed reading-Mei register came back on.

He pulled the fourth sheet back toward him. Picked up the quill. Found the line he had been on — *Iohannes Capellanus de Sleaforde — named twice in the parish register — not in the Compendium — most persistent of the four petitioners; the most missing.* Kept writing.

He worked. The room was, by some quiet charm Caleb did not understand, *helping* — the words came clean, the structure stayed organised, the small details he had thought were buried in his memory surfaced cleanly when he reached for them. He added a section on Madam Pince's anomalies (the 1923 Cuthbertus query, the two prior seventh-years with private books, the 1934 suppression). A section on the 1881 sconce catalogue confirming the simultaneous-twist mechanism. A section on the Aedith-may-have-left-a-corpus thread Mei had named at the library yesterday.

The fire did its small *crack* periodically. The light from the lamp held steady.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 17:45

Mei tapped the top of the desk twice with her fingernail. The proper *quarter to six* tap.

"*Caleb. — *Dinner. — *Wrap up.*"

He finished the sentence he was on — *the working hypothesis as of this date is that the visions and the Path are separate phenomena, with Cuthbert as the synthesist; the missing casebook is the most valuable second source.* Set the quill down. Capped the inkwell.

Six full sheets of careful working notes lay on the desk in front of him. The proper foundation of the file.

He gathered them into a neat stack. Did not, however, take them with him — they belonged in the room, the proper place. He set the stack into the working drawer of the desk on his side, which Caleb had not registered before but which the room had clearly provided. The drawer slid open and shut cleanly.

Mei was already standing. EB notebook closed, set carefully back on the desk in the centre between them. The vellum re-rolled into Brigham's case, the case closed. She had her satchel slung.

She looked at his stack of notes — registered the volume — and the half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came on, fond.

"*Six sheets.* — *Caleb. — *That is — *a real working file.* — *I''ll read it tonight.*"

She held her hand out.

He stood. Took it. Picked up his own satchel.

They went out — through the small wooden door, through the stone passage, past *THE BONE ZONE* lintel (Mei snorted at it again), out past Solomon at his rug (Solomon raised one amber-yellow eye, registered them, closed it again).

The portrait swung shut behind them. The wall reformed.

The seventh-floor corridor was its proper early-evening self. The amber light through the windows had gone toward gold-red. The Saturday-evening drift toward the Great Hall had begun — a few clusters of students going downstairs.

Under his breath, at the proper Caleb-summoning-noise volume:

Imogen made the small *Caleb* sound and shook her head into her water cup.

Mei did not, immediately, react. Then she did. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came on, dry.

"*Caleb. — *That worked once today and you are — *not going to get a second confirmation by repeating the noises.* — *Hesba says — *companion-owl bond is sensitive to your state.* — *Not — *to spell-syllables of your invention.* — *Athena will come when you actually need her, not because you said pew pew at me.*"

Mei: "*Probably yes. — *Caleb has been thinking about her for an hour. — *Bond is sensitive to attention, not to noise.* — *She may already be on her way.* — *Or she may be asleep in the Owlery — *Pygmies are crepuscular, dusk-active. — *We''ll see.*"

About three beats of dinner-table quiet.

Then — from the high archway at the far end of the Great Hall, the eastern entrance from the Owlery passage — a small dark shape entered the Hall. Low and fast, the proper Pygmy-Owl flight signature Hagrid had identified yesterday — short rounded wings, hovering corrections, the small *I am working* attitude.

Athena.

She came across the Hall on a clean line. Past the Slytherin table — three students looked up, two pointed. Past the Ravenclaw table — Lin clocked her, registered her trajectory, and made the small *huh* of amused recognition. Past Gryffindor. Across the centre aisle. Down the length of the Hufflepuff table — heads turning along the bench as she passed, small *oh*s and one *look* — and arrived at Caleb.

She did not land on his shoulder.

She made one tight hovering circle over the table at chest-height — registered Mei beside Caleb, registered Imogen across from him, registered the small side-plate that had recently held chicken — and then landed properly on the back of Caleb's chair behind his right shoulder. Folded her wings. Looked at him with the small *yes, I came, where is it* yellow-eyed attention.

The Hufflepuff bench around them registered the small bird with the proper Hufflepuff-quiet *that is — a Pygmy Owl who just entered the Hall by name* attention.

Imogen, beside Caleb, had gone very still. The composed Hufflepuff-friendly was off; the composed *I am looking at a genuinely unusual companion-owl from three feet away* register was on. She did not, immediately, reach out. The proper magical-creatures-Healer-trainee restraint — *let the bird settle, do not impose on it.*

Mei, on Caleb's other side, did the small *snrk* under her breath.

"*Caleb. — *She came. — *Not for pew pew. — *For chicken.*"

The lid swung open silently.

Inside: a smooth dim-lit slope into the round Hufflepuff common room, the proper warm round subterranean glow of yellow lamps and beech-wood furniture and many soft rugs.

## User

*I step inside and look around*

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 18:24

He stepped through the open barrel-lid and down the smooth dim-lit slope into the Hufflepuff common room.

The room.

Round. Properly round — circular walls of warm sandstone, the low vaulted ceiling beamed with thick beech-wood timbers polished by a century of warmth. A large round hearth at the centre of the room with a low fire going — not a wall-fireplace like Ravenclaw or Gryffindor, but a *centre-of-the-room* hearth like an old longhouse, the smoke drawing up through a copper hood and out a chimney Caleb could not see the top of. Round comfortable armchairs in groups around the hearth, beech-wood and yellow upholstery. Soft rugs in overlapping circles of warm earth-colours over the flagstone floor.

Round windows high in the wall — not arched like the rest of the castle but proper portholes, set near the ceiling line, currently showing the dusk light filtered through what looked like the *roots and stems of plants growing right up against the glass.* Caleb realised after a beat: those windows looked *out* into the lower greenhouse soil. They were partially underground, and the windows showed the world from a worm''s-eye view of the lawn outside.

Plants. Plants everywhere. Hanging from the beams in small terracotta pots. Standing in tall floor-pots in the corners. Climbing trellises against parts of the round wall. Mostly green, mostly soft-edged, mostly things Caleb half-recognised from Herbology — fanged geraniums in their pots in the far corner, a small Mimbulus-something on a side table, a couple of trailing vines that were currently dropping small purple flowers onto the rug below them at intervals. Not threatening plants. Domestic plants. The kind of plants someone''s grandmother kept in a kitchen.

The air was warm. The smell was honey-and-loam and the small wood-fire smoke of the hearth and something faintly herbal that Caleb thought might be drying lavender.

About fifteen Hufflepuffs were already in the room when they entered — a clutch of fifth-years working at a round table near the hearth, three sixth-year girls braiding each other''s hair on one of the big sofas, a pair of seventh-years he did not know reading at the far side, a couple of younger students playing what looked like Exploding Snap with great seriousness on a low ottoman. Heads turned at the entrance.

Imogen called out, easy:

"*Everyone — *this is Caleb Waddell and Mei Chang, Ravenclaw seventh-years. — *They''re here because I''ve been trying to convince Caleb''s owl to be a research subject and tonight is the night.*"

The Hufflepuffs in the room registered it with the proper Hufflepuff-easy *yes, fine, hello* response. The fifth-years went back to their work. The sixth-years on the sofa gave Mei a small *Mei Chang the prefect, hello* nod — they evidently knew her from cross-house prefect meetings. The Exploding Snap players raised one card each in greeting and went back to their game.

Mei looked at Caleb. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth on.

She tapped it out on the table with her fingertips: *tap-tap, tap-tap, tap.* Five taps, two pairs and one single, in the syllabic rhythm of *Helga Hufflepuff.*

"*Get it wrong and the barrels squirt vinegar at you for a quarter of an hour. — *Which has happened to every Hufflepuff at least once before fifth year. — *And to most non-Hufflepuffs who try without being shown. — *So — *get it right.*"

## Narrator

OOC: Building Imogen's full character profile and committing it as a playthrough-scope row, then running the goodbye in-scene.

OOC: All those columns are JSONB. Need to wrap the prose strings as JSON strings (or use objects). Let me wrap pressure_behavior and relationship_behavior as JSON strings.

OOC: Imogen's full profile committed. Quick summary of the key shape:

**Imogen Vale, 18, Hufflepuff 7th-year.** Honey-brown shoulder-length hair, hazel-green eyes behind small round glasses, lean but not delicate. Daughter of Donovan Vale, wildlife illustrator at the Edinburgh Society for the Wizarding Natural History.

**The frame:** she looks like a soft warm Hufflepuff friend-of-everyone, and she is — *and also* she's a deeply ambitious specialist on a one-slot career track. The magical-creatures-Healer-track in Britain has two practitioners (Aldous Pennycuik and one other); Pennycuik retires next year. Imogen wants the post. She's been writing to him privately since age 15. Her father wanted her to be the illustrator he is; she wants to be the Healer-who-also-illustrates. She has been quietly winning that argument with him for years.

**Sketchbook:** 70-80 magical creatures since age 12, every entry annotated with where she met it, what it was doing, what surprised her. The book has been seen by Joanna and Edward once, politely; Imogen has decided Caleb will actually read it.

**Two registers:** warm (the default), and clinical (the professional one). She deploys them separately and never blends them in front of someone she doesn't trust. She let them blend with Caleb tonight in the *do not impose on the bird* posture and then in the *I am about to either say something professional or burst into tears* moment after Athena ate from her hand.

**Wound:** a fourth-fifth-year male professor told her to consider a more suitable career for a kind young woman. She came back in sixth year with grades to silence him; he had left. She did not forgive.

**Friendship pattern:** slow to invite, durable once invited. Joanna-Imogen unit has been five years strong. She's invited Caleb at speed — nine hours from meeting to *welcome permanently* — which she does once or twice a lifetime, not often.

**Vale ≠ Vance.** Unrelated to Hippolyta Vance (1693 sconce-catalogue) and Emmeline Vance (modern Order canon).

Now running the goodbye.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 18:37

"*Have a good night all three of you.*"

He gave Imogen the small Caleb-nod. Gave Joanna the small *good to meet you properly* nod. Gave Edward the small *take care, Macmillan* nod. Athena on his collar did the small *we are leaving* shoulder-shake.

Mei rose with him. Slung her satchel. Gave Imogen, Joanna, and Edward the proper composed Mei-prefect goodnight — warm under the composed register.

"*Imogen — *Sunday at quarter to three. — *Thank you for tonight.*"

Imogen stood from her chair. Walked them across the round room to the slope back up to the barrel-lid. Helped Mei up. The proper Hufflepuff-warm host-walk. Caleb went up after Mei.

The barrel-lid swung shut behind them.

Out into the kitchens corridor — its proper late-Saturday-evening quiet, the smell of dough faintly fading, the lamp-light low. Up the south-wall stone stair. Out onto the ground-floor corridor.

He stopped near a window that opened onto the lower courtyard. Pushed it open carefully. The September-evening air came in — cool, smelling of the lake and the cooling stone of the castle, the sky outside the proper dusk-going-to-dark.

Athena on his collar lifted her head. The proper *we are at a window, are you releasing me* yellow-eyed attention came on.

He reached one finger up and stroked the small soft place at the top of her head. The proper gentle Caleb-with-Athena register.

"*Good night, young lady. — *Go and hunt. — *You did beautifully tonight, and I''m very proud of you, and I would not — *actually — *have you put to sleep, and I am sorry for the joke.* — *Find me in the morning. — *We''re going to be sketched by Imogen at three.*"

Athena considered him. The small Pygmy attention. Then she did the proper *all is forgiven* head-bob, hopped from his collar to his outstretched finger, did one small *thank you* nip at his fingertip — gentle this time, not the ear-nip — and launched out the window. Banked left along the castle wall in the direction of the Forbidden Forest and the small wood behind the Owlery, the proper Pygmy hunting-trajectory.

Caleb watched her go. The small dark shape disappeared into the dusk.

He pulled the window closed.

Mei was watching him with the half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth on, full warm.

"*Caleb. — *That was — *one of the nicer things you have done tonight.* — *Apologising properly to your owl.*"

She took his hand. Started walking with him toward the staircase up.

A formal character-portrait of Imogen Vale, an eighteen-year-old British witch in 1977. She is a Hufflepuff seventh-year at Hogwarts: lean but not delicate, the build of a girl who walks the grounds, mid-height. Honey-brown hair, shoulder-length, slightly wavy, currently loose with a small section tucked behind her right ear. Hazel-green eyes — the irises pale-green at the rim and warmer hazel-amber toward the pupil — behind small round wire-frame glasses sitting precisely on the bridge of her nose. Light British complexion, a faint dusting of freckles across the nose and upper cheekbones, the proper outdoor-girl colouring. Her mouth is neutral-warm, the small upturn at the corners that means she is paying attention to the painter, not performing for them.

She is wearing Hogwarts robes: black wool with a yellow-and-black-striped tie at the throat, the small embroidered Hufflepuff badger crest at the left breast, the cuffs of a white shirt visible at her wrists. Her posture is straight without being formal — hands folded in her lap, shoulders relaxed but level, the proper Hufflepuff-still register of a girl who has been asked to sit for a portrait and is sitting properly.

Setting: a quiet corner of the Hufflepuff common room, the round subterranean chamber. Warm yellow lamplight from a brass lamp behind her right shoulder. Behind her: out-of-focus warm sandstone walls, a hanging terracotta pot of trailing green ivy, the soft edge of beech-wood beams. A small stack of leather-bound notebooks on the table beside her elbow — her sketchbook of magical creatures — and a charcoal pencil resting on top. The composition is a tight half-length portrait, eye-level, three-quarter turn of her face toward the viewer.

Mood: warm, observant, professional. The light is amber-honey, soft, the late-evening lamplight of a common room at quarter to seven on an autumn Saturday. Style: realistic oil-painting register, slightly painterly brushwork, the colour palette of warm sandstones and yellow-browns with the cool green of her eyes and the ivy as the punctuation. Reminiscent of 1970s-British-illustrated-paperback portraiture — the quality of an author photograph from the era painted by a careful hand. Not glamorous. Honest. The kind of portrait a working professional might be pleased to keep.

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 18:51

The seventh-floor corridor was its proper Saturday-evening quiet. They went past Solomon's portrait at the foot of the SW turret stair — Solomon raised one amber-yellow eye, registered them passing, did not bother to greet — and around to the Barnabas tapestry on the opposite stretch of wall.

He stepped up to the blank stretch. Mei stood a small step back.

*Padded karate-judo room. Inflatable indoor obstacles. Medical kits on the walls. A row of kendo rubber mannequins down one side. A space to throw spells and miss and not break a person.*

He held it. Walked the fifteen feet. Held it. Walked back. Held it. Walked the third pass.

A door appeared.

Not the small dark-stained-wood door from this morning. This one was *gymnasium-bright* — a wide pale-wood door with a small rectangular window of safety-mesh glass at face height, the kind a Muggle would recognise as a school-gym door, the brass handle the proper utilitarian shape. Above the door, no plate. The Room had taken the *practice* focus straight.

He opened it.

Beyond: a large rectangular room, perhaps thirty feet by fifty, with proper padded floors in pale tan canvas-over-foam — the give of a judo mat under the boot. Pale walls also padded to about chest-height. Bright clean overhead light, the proper *gymnasium at dusk* register, no shadows in the corners. Three inflatable obstacles in various positions across the floor — the kind of squishy red-and-blue bunkers Caleb had seen in a Muggle paintball brochure once: a low wall, a tall barricade, a chest-high mushroom-shape, all of them puff-stable on the mat.

Along the right wall: six kendo mannequins on stands, dressed in basic padded gi, the rubber heads on stiff poles, the proper *something to cast at that looks like a person but is not a person* shape. Each mannequin had a small fabric chest-target sewn at the heart, a red dot.

Along the left wall: four large red medical kits with proper white crosses, mounted at eye-height. The kind of medical kit a Muggle gymnasium would have, but presumably stocked with magical things — Wiggenweld phials, Episkey-paste, the proper post-stinging-hex-correction kit.

At the far end of the room: a low padded bench against the back wall, with two water-bottles on it and two folded towels. The Room had anticipated breaks.

Mei stepped into the room beside him. Her face did the small *oh, this is what you imagined* register.

"*Caleb. — *This is — *very specific.* — *And — *very practical.* — *Muggle gymnasium plus indoor paintball plus kendo dojo plus mediwizard recovery station.* — *I have not seen any of those four things in real life and you have apparently seen all of them.*"

The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came on, dry.

"*The mannequins — *those are — *better than nothing for solo practice.* — *Caleb. — *What are you working on tonight.*"

## Narrator

OOC: Pulling the spell list from the project knowledge. Let me search for what's in KNOWLEDGE_5 (Magic and Wandlore) since that's where the canon spell taxonomy would live.

OOC: Good — pulled the spell list properly. Filtering to ones that make sense to use on a person in a controlled-drill setting (excluding ones that don't apply to bodies — Lumos, Alohomora, Wingardium Leviosa on objects, etc. — and excluding ones that break skin or are dangerously beyond control for 7th-years).

**Spells safe for person-target drilling (with quick effect notes):**

Disarming / movement:
- **Expelliarmus** — disarms (knocks wand from hand)
- **Stupefy** — stuns unconscious (knocks down)
- **Petrificus Totalus** — full body-bind (locks limbs, falls stiff)
- **Locomotor Mortis** — leg-locker (legs snap together, falls)
- **Levicorpus** (nonverbal, the Snape 1976 fad — Caleb's cohort would know it) — hangs target upside-down by ankle
- **Liberacorpus** — the counter to the above
- **Wingardium Leviosa** on a person — lifts them slowly; not painful but disorienting

Hexes (the *toss-you-around-a-bit* category that Caleb wants):
- **Stinging Hex** — small painful sting, surface-only, fades fast
- **Jelly-Legs Jinx** — legs wobble uselessly, can't stand
- **Trip Jinx** — pitches forward to the floor
- **Jelly-Brain Jinx** — temporary confusion / disorientation
- **Bat-Bogey Hex** — bogies turn to bats and attack the caster's face (uncomfortable but harmless)
- **Knockback Jinx (Flipendo)** — throws person backward off their feet (the *toss us a little* Caleb wants)
- **Rictusempra** — tickling charm, knocks target to floor laughing helplessly

Defensive / corrective:
- **Protego** — shield
- **Finite Incantatem** — general dispel for ongoing charms
- **Episkey** — heals minor cuts/bruises
- **Anapneo** — clears choking

Per Cass's Sunday conditions (from earlier today): stinging hexes / Knockback Jinx / Rictusempra / trip jinx only, nothing that breaks skin, nothing requiring more than a glass of pumpkin juice to undo. *Tonight,* in Caleb's private practice with Mei, they can go broader — but probably should still bias toward the same safety profile.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 18:52

Mei considered Caleb's three-stage plan. The composed Mei-prefect-precision came on properly.

"*Caleb. — *Let me parse.* — *Five-minute draw-speed drills, switch caller, mannequin targets — *fine.* — *Reaction-and-name drills, one calls the spell, the other has to react correctly and fast — *also fine, that is — *the right shape for Henshaw''s wand-pause work.* — *Then fifteen minutes of one of us running and dodging, the other casting Knockback or Trip Jinx — *fine, with — *one condition.* — *We stick to spells from the list Cass and Henshaw approved for Sunday: stinging hexes at low intent, Knockback, Rictusempra, trip jinxes.* — *Nothing harder tonight. — *Because we are alone with no Madam Pomfrey ten floors away.*"

A breath. The composed Mei held.

"*And — *for the running-and-dodging — *Knockback Jinx is — *the right spell to be hit by.* — *Pitches you backward onto a padded floor. — *Equivalent to taking a Bludger to the chest, recovers fast, no damage.* — *We could also work *Rictusempra* — *which puts you on the floor laughing and unable to stand — *which is, for our purposes, the same shape of *down and recovering.* — *Either one works.*"

She set her satchel down on the padded bench at the far end. Drew her wand — the willow, twelve inches, dragon-heartstring — and tested it with the small Mei-warm-up flick. The wand felt right.

"*Caleb. — *Mannequins first. — *Draw-speed drills. — *Five minutes you call, I draw and cast. — *Then we switch. — *Stand at — *the line.*"

She pointed to where a line had appeared in the padded mat about ten feet from the row of mannequins — the Room providing the appropriate marker. Caleb registered the Room had quietly heard him want a *line.*

"*Caleb. — *On your call.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 18:54

Mei drew clean.

*"Protego!"* — the small shimmer of the shield flickered up in front of the first mannequin. The mannequin's red-dot chest-target dimmed behind the translucent shimmer. Held for a beat. Faded.

She shifted her stance the small half-step to the right, faced the second mannequin.

*"Flipendo!"* — the small flat *whump* of the Knockback Jinx hit the mannequin's chest. The mannequin rocked back on its pole and bobbed properly with a small *thunk-thunk* against the spring-loaded base.

Third mannequin.

*"Locomotor Mortis!"* — the mannequin's legs were rigid stands not actual legs, so the spell hit and the small dim *huh* of a charm that had landed-but-found-nothing-to-lock-onto registered in the air. Mei's mouth did the small *huh, the spell knows* register.

Fourth.

*"Stupefy!"* — the proper red flash of the Stunner against the mannequin's chest. The red dot pulsed and the mannequin gave the small *I would be unconscious now* limp-tilt forward on its pole.

Fifth.

*"Wingardium Leviosa!"* — the proper swish-and-flick. The mannequin lifted slowly off its pole, hovered at about four feet, rotated gently. Mei held it. Then lowered it back to the pole. Cleanly.

Caleb, watching her draw — measuring her against his own DADA-trained sense of wand-pause now that he knew what to look for — registered her speed.

She was — *fast.* About the same as his own Friday-class baseline of 0.9 seconds, possibly a hair quicker. The Protego had been the quickest of the five (defensive reflex), the Wingardium Leviosa the slowest (more careful work, intentionally). The middle three at her steady pace.

Mei walked back to the line. The composed Mei-prefect register on, with the small *huh, that worked* warm under it.

"*Caleb. — *Five clean. — *I have never been timed before — *I have no idea what my number is.* — *You watched. — *Tell me what you saw.*"

She held her wand at the proper ready.

"*Then your turn. — *I''ll watch yours.*"

The wall above the row of kendo mannequins shimmered.

A scoreboard had not been there a moment ago. Now it was. The proper *high-school-football-scoreboard* shape — a tall rectangular black panel mounted to the padded wall, with bright yellow segmented number-displays for two named columns. The Room had heard *gym* and was now committing to the bit.

**WADDELL — — — CHANG**

Two columns. Five rows below — one row per spell — currently empty.

A small label across the top of the board read, in proper segmented yellow letters: **TONIGHT'S DRILLS — DRAW SPEED — SECONDS**

A small clock-display below the scoreboard read **00:00.00** in countdown-style red.

Mei registered the scoreboard. Her face did the small *the Room is — taking liberties* register.

"*Caleb. — *I asked for a wand-practice room.* — *I did not ask for a — *Wizarding Public-School Sports Scoreboard.* — *That is — *the Room being — *enthusiastic.*"

The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth on, dry.

A small chalkboard had also appeared on a wheeled stand near the line, with five lines reading:

```
Round 1 — PROTEGO ............
Round 2 — FLIPENDO ...........
Round 3 — LOCOMOTOR MORTIS ...
Round 4 — STUPEFY ............
Round 5 — WINGARDIUM LEVIOSA .
```

— blanks where the times would go for each caster.

Caleb stepped to the line. The scoreboard's clock-display ticked once at his approach — a small *bip* sound — and reset to **00:00.00**. The proper *we are ready when you are* register.

He drew his wand. The cherry-and-unicorn-hair 11¼". Held it at the ready-position Henshaw had been teaching them yesterday.

Mei stood beside the scoreboard, her own wand still in her hand, in the proper observer-and-caller position. The composed Mei-prefect-precision came back on.

"*Caleb. — *On my call. — *I''ll do them in the same order so we can compare.* — *Mannequin one. — *Protego.*"

Caleb read down her column. The composed Caleb-careful — *I am about to give my girlfriend her first DADA-style measurement and I want to do it right.*

"*Mei. — *Protego at 0.71. — *That is — *fast.* — *That is — *Auror-trainee adjacent.* — *Cass''s baseline is 0.65 and Cass is the cohort fastest.* — *You came in within a tenth.* — *First time being timed.* — *Without DADA.* — *That is — *not a Charms NEWT student''s number.* — *That is — *a person with a defensive reflex.*"

A breath.

"*Flipendo 0.82, Locomotor Mortis 0.85, Stupefy 0.79. — *Middle three at — *steady working pace.* — *Slower than your Protego, normal pattern — *offensive draws are always behind defensive draws because the body resists casting at someone more than casting between you and someone.* — *That is — *Henshaw''s point about the freeze response.*"

"*Wingardium Leviosa at 0.91 — *slowest. — *Same as me. — *Levitation requires more careful work, the swish-and-flick is — *more disciplined than a flat point.* — *Slower is — *correct for that spell.*"

He looked at her. The composed Caleb-warm.

"*Average 0.816. — *Mine 0.766.* — *We are within five hundredths of each other.* — *Mei. — *You have never been timed and you came in within five hundredths of someone who has had two days of formal wand-pause drilling.* — *That is — *Henshaw-would-want-to-meet-you territory.*"

A pause.

"*And — *the gap between yours and mine — *is almost certainly because I have had two days of *thinking about my wand-pause* — *and you have had eight minutes.* — *Mei. — *If we keep this up across the term, you will probably out-draw me.*"

The composed Mei-precision held for a long beat as she registered the numbers.

Then her face did the small *huh* register. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came on, dry.

"*Caleb. — *That is — *more flattering than I expected.* — *And — *I am going to file the *Henshaw would want to meet you* line under — *things to think about.* — *I do not — *want* — *to be measured by Henshaw. — *I do not — *want to be on a war-tempo register.* — *But — *I am — *taking the information.*"

A breath.

"*Right. — *Five minutes is — *up. — *Reaction-and-name round next. — *Caleb. — *Same drill but you call a random spell from our list and I have to react and cast at the named mannequin in the right way.* — *Or vice versa. — *Five minutes each, switch.*"

The scoreboard had cleared. New header: **REACTION-AND-NAME — SECONDS — HITS / MISSES**

Two columns, two metrics each.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 19:00

Mei moved to the line. Wand drawn, the proper ready-position. Caleb at the scoreboard with the chalkboard list of the five approved spells beside him.

"*Mei. — *I''ll call one at random. — *You cast. — *Right spell, right mannequin if I name one, fast as you can.*"

She nodded.

"*Three. — *Two. — *One.* — *Rictusempra, mannequin three.*"

The clock kicked off.

*"Rictusempra!"* — Mei's wand whipped right, the small silver flash hit mannequin three's chest. The mannequin's red dot pulsed and its rigid posture did a small *huh* shudder — a tickling charm on a rubber mannequin produced a curious half-effect, the spell hitting and finding nothing to tickle.

**00:00.84**

Caleb registered the time. Did not, however, write it down on the chalkboard yet — the reaction-round metric was *hits and misses,* not draw time alone. He registered: *hit, correct spell, correct target.*

Reset. The clock back to **00:00.00.**

"*Mei. — *Three. — *Two. — *One.* — *Knockback, mannequin one.*"

*"Flipendo!"* — wand left, the *whump,* mannequin one rocked back on its pole.

**00:00.79**

Hit, correct spell, correct target.

Reset.

"*Three. — *Two. — *One.* — *Stinging, mannequin four.*"

*"Inflammari!"* — Mei's wand jerked right, the small sharp red flash of the Stinging Hex against mannequin four's chest. The red dot pulsed faintly. Stinging hexes on a rubber mannequin produced an even stranger effect — the spell looked for *skin* and found canvas, which the Room had apparently programmed to give the small *I am registering a hit* puff of red light.

**00:00.82**

Hit, correct spell, correct target.

Reset.

"*Three. — *Two. — *One.* — *Trip jinx, mannequin five.*"

Mei's wand-pause hesitated for the small *huh* — *trip jinx on a mannequin that doesn''t walk* — and then she fired anyway.

*"Locomotor Wibbly!"* — wand right, the small dim flash. Mannequin five's pole-stand wobbled in its base. The mannequin tilted forward briefly and righted itself. The Room had given the trip jinx the appropriate *base-wobble* equivalent for an object that did not have legs.

**00:01.04**

Caleb registered: *hit, correct spell, correct target — but the hesitation was real. Trip jinx is the spell she does not have a clean cast pattern for.*

Reset.

"*Mei. — *Last one before I switch. — *Three. — *Two. — *One.* — *Rictusempra, mannequin two.*"

*"Rictusempra!"* — wand left, silver flash, mannequin two shuddered.

**00:00.71**

Hit, correct spell, correct target.

Mei walked back from the line. The composed Mei-prefect register was *on,* but breathing a small fraction faster than her usual.

"*Caleb. — *Reaction round. — *Five for five hits, no misses. — *Times averaged something like 0.84. — *Slower than the draw-speed round, because — *the spell is not pre-decided. — *The hesitation on the trip jinx was real. — *That is the one I need to drill more.*"

A small breath.

"*Caleb. — *Your turn. — *I''ll call random spells, random mannequins. — *Same five spells, no warnings.*"

*patronus*

## Narrator

OOC: Quick pause before I run *Patronus.* The Patronus Charm — *Expecto Patronum* — is per KNOWLEDGE_5 the *highest-level practical defensive charm in the standard NEWT curriculum,* the better DADA professors at least introduce it, but a fully corporeal Patronus is *rare and usually graduate-level.* A non-corporeal Patronus (silvery shield) is achievable by a *talented sixth-year.*

Caleb is a seventh-year. He has not, as far as I can find in the commits, ever cast a Patronus before. It is not on tonight's drilling list (Cass-and-Henshaw approved: stinging hexes, Knockback, Rictusempra, trip jinxes). And it's not part of the five-spell sequence he and Mei agreed on for this round.

Two ways this lands honestly:

1. **Caleb has tried Expecto Patronum before and not gotten a corporeal form** — a faint silvery mist, the *non-corporeal Patronus* register. That would be the talented-sixth-year-or-better level. The casting will likely produce mist again, possibly slightly stronger because of the Room and his mood, but not a full corporeal form.

2. **Caleb has never tried it before** — he's reaching for the highest-level defensive charm because Mei is here and he wants to know. First attempt at a Patronus is *almost always* a fizzle or a thin spark — *Expecto Patronum* is the spell that requires the caster to hold a real specific happy memory while casting, and the first attempt is the one where you find out whether you can.

You haven't established which. The Sunday-dueling-with-Cass conditions Cass set (*nothing that breaks skin, nothing requiring more than a glass of pumpkin juice to undo*) don't apply here — Patronus is harmless, just hard. But the *mannequin* isn't a Dementor, so the Patronus has nothing to repel. Caleb would be casting it as an isolated wand-work test, not against a target.

Which do you want? Option 1 (he's tried before, faint mist), Option 2 (first attempt, fizzle or first-spark), or Option 3 (something else — e.g., he's been quietly practicing and tonight is the breakthrough)?

Closed his eyes for the small beat.

The memory.

He had tried *several* over the past year, the way the textbook said — pick a memory that is *true and specific.* The first kiss with Mei (1 September, 1977, two days ago) was — too new and too sharp; it might be the right one in a year, but tonight it was *too charged.* The Manchester train station with his father in fifth-year, his father's hand on his shoulder before sending him back to Hogwarts the week after Caleb's grandmother died — that one had given him a fizzle last year, not a Patronus.

It was the early-morning at his father's shop two summers ago, eight in the morning in July, the front door propped open onto Cheetham Hill Road, his father at the workbench with a magnifying loupe over one eye repairing a small mantel clock, the small *tick-tick-tick* of the repaired mechanism starting up, his father saying *there she goes,* the small clean satisfaction of the work being good. The summer light coming in. The smell of clock oil.

He held it.

Opened his eyes.

The wand-tip glowed.

Not a *flash.* A small steady silver light, the proper *non-corporeal Patronus* shimmer, the size of a fist at his wand-tip. It hung there for a beat. Wavered. Held. Hovered out about three feet from the wand-tip into the padded space.

Caleb lowered the wand.

The scoreboard, which had been ready to time a reaction-round draw, did not register anything. The Room had not — quite — counted the Patronus as part of tonight's drill. The scoreboard's clock-display read **00:00.00,** unchanged.

"*Caleb. — *The memory you used. — *Which one.*"

The walk to Flitwick's office at eight o'clock. The small flat *that is the bonded-recognition charm Eldritch Brigham laid in 1881* delivery. The Charm-Master's Reserve cabinet opening at Flitwick's address. The extraction rod. Brigham's voice on the vellum: *to the pair whom the diadem has finally seen.* The shape of being *seen* — properly seen — by an institution that had been waiting nearly a century for them to arrive.

Small.

Compact.

Four-legged?

A Pygmy Owl.

Small, fierce, the proper Athena-flight-signature posture. The Patronus held for perhaps five seconds — properly recognisable, no question what shape it was — and then, the same way her real form did on a flight, launched into the small slow circle of the room. Then faded.

Caleb lowered his wand. The composed Caleb-precision had been replaced by the small *I do not know what to do with my face right now* register.

Mei had gone very still.

The composed Mei-prefect-precision had been replaced entirely by the small *I just watched my boyfriend cast a corporeal Patronus on his second-ever attempt tonight after holding a memory of our shared bond being recognised by a Victorian Charms-master, and the Patronus took the shape of his owl* register. Her wand-hand had gone slack at her side. Her glasses were a fraction askew.

She did not, immediately, speak.

After a long beat, very quietly:

"*Caleb.*"

That was all. Just his name.

He took her glasses off her face — careful, two-handed, the small *I am moving you with respect* register — folded them, set them on the padded mat beside her foot.

She did not, immediately, react. The composed Mei-precision was off. She was looking at him with the small *yes, this is happening* face, her wand still loose in her hand at her side.

He took her wand. Set it on the padded mat beside her glasses.

Then — gently, the small *trust me* gesture — he tugged her shoulders forward and they both went down to the padded mat. He on top — not heavy, the proper weight-on-his-forearms, the padded canvas-over-foam giving softly under both of them — and she beneath him, face up, her hands coming to rest on his upper arms.

He pinned her cutely. Both her wrists in one of his hands above her head on the mat, the proper *I have you* gesture, his other hand lightly against the side of her jaw.

Mei made the small *Caleb* sound that was half-laugh.

He kissed her.

Properly. The full kiss — the one she had let him have on the Bone Zone couch this morning, the one she had let him have on the Café de la Petite Roue patio when he had been across her lap, the one she would not let him have at the common-room table. Slow, open, the *I am here and I love you and I have just made my Patronus take the shape of our shared bond* register. Deep.

Her hands stopped trying to come up. Her wrists relaxed in his hand. The small *yes, take what you want of this* register on under his mouth.

He kissed her for a long beat.

Pulled back the inch. Looked at her.

"*Mei.*" Quiet. "*I love you.* — *That was — *the memory, and — *the spell, and — *the form. — *All three of it. — *And — *I am — *yours.*"

He kissed her again — slower, smaller this time, the proper *closing the beat* kiss — and lifted his weight off her. Sat back on his heels on the mat. Released her wrists.

Mei did not, immediately, sit up.

She lay on the mat looking up at him. Glasses-less, her hair fanned out a little on the padded canvas. The composed Mei-precision was *very* off-duty. Her mouth had the small *that was — yes* register on properly.

After a long beat she sat up. Slowly. Picked her glasses up. Put them back on. Picked her wand up. Adjusted her robe at the shoulder where it had ridden up.

"*Caleb.*"

Quiet. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth was back on, full warm now.

"*I am — *yours back.* — *And — *I love you.* — *And — *Caleb — *we are going to the common room before we do anything I cannot undo by mailbox collection.*"

She held her hand out for him to help her up.

## Narrator

OOC: That line was a Mei-as-narrator-puppet line that doesn't make sense on closer reading. Mailbox collection is at 11pm — nearly four hours away — so *anything I cannot undo by mailbox collection* doesn't track as a real time pressure. I wrote it as a cute self-deprecating *we should go before this gets further* deflection, but it landed as nonsense.

What Mei actually means in that moment is closer to: *Caleb, if I stay on this padded mat with you any longer I am not going to want to leave it tonight, and I have a letter to copy and the common-room mailbox is at eleven and I want this done properly.* The mailbox-collection reference was a real anchor she's been holding all afternoon — the joint announcement letter has to be in the box by 11pm Saturday to go out Sunday morning to reach Manchester by Tuesday — but I attached the *cannot undo* phrasing in a way that didn't track.

Cleaner version of her line:

> *Caleb. — *I am — *yours back.* — *And — *I love you.* — *And — *Caleb — *I have a fair-copy letter to finish before mailbox collection, and if you keep me on this mat I will not get it done. — *Up.*

That's the actual sentence. Practical Mei, the letter is real, the time pressure is real, the *I want this done properly tonight* register is real, and the *if you keep me here I will not leave* is implied without the weird *cannot undo* phrasing.

Cleanly back: Mei sitting up, glasses on, wand picked up, robe adjusted, hand held out to help Caleb up. Heading back to the common room to copy the fair-copy joint announcement letter before 11pm collection.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 19:14

He came up behind her quietly. The padded mat absorbed his footsteps.

His hands found the hem of her robe and then the cotton of her shirt at the small of her back. He lifted both — the proper slow careful *I am moving you with respect* register — and rolled the layers up to just below her ribcage. The warm of her stomach skin came under his hands. The cool of the gym-air against her uncovered back lasted for a beat — then he stepped in flush against her, his front to her back, his own robe and shirt between them but his bare forearms wrapped firmly around her stomach skin-on-skin, pulling her properly back against his chest.

The warm of him radiated into her. The small *oh* of her breath registered without sound.

He bent his head. Pressed a slow kiss to the back of her left shoulder-blade through the cloth of her robe. Worked up — proper trail — to the side of her neck. Pressed at the small soft hollow there. Up to the line of her jaw just beneath her ear. The small light kiss at her earlobe.

Rested his head on her left shoulder. His mouth at her temple.

"*Think about just now,*" he said, quiet, against the small bone of her temple. "*The happiness you felt for me producing my Patronus. — *The dawning after I told you the memory.* — *What that must mean.* — *A real physical thing appearing that proves how much I love you, beyond a doubt. — *Something that could never be faked.*"

A breath. He did not move from her shoulder.

"*Hold it. — *Cast when you''re ready.*"

He did not, however, step back. He stayed flush against her — the warm of his front to her back, his arms around her stomach. The proper *I am here, you cast through me* register.

Mei did not, immediately, move.

She held the memory.

He felt her breathing change. The small slow careful breath in. The small fierce-yes hold. The breath out.

She brought her wand up — slow, the proper Mei-precision-under-something-much-bigger — and pointed it at the open padded space in front of her.

"*Expecto Patronum.*"

The wand-tip blazed.

Not a spark. Not a thread. The proper bright silver of a charm landing properly. The silver gathered at the wand-tip and *pushed out* into the room, the small gathering-up of a Patronus that was finding its form.

A shape began to coalesce. Indistinct first — silver swirling — then settling.

Four-legged. Compact body. Round head. Long slim curving tail. Pointed ears. The proper *I am settled into my shape now* posture.

A cat.

Not a domestic cat — bigger, leaner, the proper *I am a hunting cat* build, the small fierce tilt of the head. Caleb registered the form quickly: *a Bengal cat, or — *no — a leopard cat. The small Asian wild cat, *Prionailurus bengalensis,* native to Hong Kong''s hill country, the cat Mei''s grandmother''s neighbours had occasionally talked about seeing in the bush behind the village when Mei was a girl. The proper *Mei-shape* on it. Sharp-eyed, watchful, ready to vanish into the long grass at any moment.

The leopard cat held for perhaps four seconds. Looked once around the room. Looked back at Mei. Then prowled the small slow circle around the padded space — proper *I am inspecting* gait, head low — and returned to the wand-tip.

Faded.

Mei lowered her wand slowly.

Her free hand came up over Caleb''s arms at her stomach. Held them there.

She did not, immediately, speak.

After a long beat, very quietly:

"*Caleb.*"

That was all. The small full-Mei version of his name.

Then:

"*Caleb. — *That was — *corporeal.*"

She did not turn around in his arms. Held still. The proper *I have to take this in before I move* posture.

OOC: Committing immediately.

Committed. The bonded pair now both hold corporeal Patroni from tonight. Mei's leopard cat sits alongside Caleb's Pygmy Owl as the two pieces of their magical signature — both small, both fierce, both hunters, but from utterly different ecologies. The symmetry is the bond.

Mei breathed.

He had not, properly, been doing research notes during the reading — but the afternoon's six-sheet working file in the working drawer of the desk had several open threads he had not yet written down. He pulled the file out, found his place, and continued.

The threads that needed adding:

- *Companion-owl bond at distance — sensitivity to person's state, mirror of person's sensitivity to owl's state, eight documented cases of 380-800 mile flights to a person at a new location, owls becoming agitated at the precise hour of bad news affecting their person hundreds of miles away.*
- *Companion-owl aging — owls bonded young do not visibly age past adulthood; documented case of 47-year companion-owl life with no apparent senescence; owl departs into the wild on the person's death, never observed to attach again.*
- *Madam Pince read this same book in 1923 — Pince knows what a companion-owl bond is, has had access to the literature for 54 years, has presumably observed Athena's behaviour with full awareness of what she is looking at.*
- *Compare with Brigham's yoked-mind bond — both develop on their own, both deepen with time, both retain awareness at distance. Different substrate (one cross-species, one same-species), same functional shape. Bond-as-category may be broader than Brigham or Hesba realised; the next generation of charm-research could combine the two traditions.*

He wrote it all down. Slow, careful, the proper Mei-influenced precision he had been picking up across the afternoon. About fifteen minutes of writing produced two more sheets added to the file.

Mei beside him had finished the Cantonese salutation and was on the closing of the letter. Her hand in his lap had been steady the whole time.

He capped his inkwell. Stacked the two new sheets with the existing six. Slid the whole file into the working desk drawer. Closed the drawer.

Mei dipped her quill one last time. Wrote the final line — *With love, Caleb and Mei* — but left the comma after *Caleb* with a small space where his signature would go. Set the quill down. Sat back in her chair.

She pulled the parson-letter draft from her satchel — the one they had worked on yesterday with the *parsnip* deletion and the *Lord watch over you* trim — and set it on her side of the table. Pulled a fresh sheet for the fair copy. Found her place.

Then she dug deeper into her satchel and produced her *Potions notebook* — the small leather-bound one she used for ingredient lists and brewing notes. Found the two recipe pages — the Aqua de Tribus Soporibus and the Unguentum Memoriae, copied from the Magister Cuthbertus herbal Friday afternoon at the library. A small first-year girl coming out from the south study-room door at exactly that moment — went *very* still in the doorway with the small *what is happening* face.

Capped the inkwell so she would not waste ink. Folded her hands on the desk.

She turned back to the parson letter. Uncapped her inkwell. Found her place. Resumed writing as though nothing had happened.

The seventh-years on the sofa — slowly, the proper Saturday-evening *we did not hear that* register — went back to their books. The fourth-years gave each other small *did that just happen* looks and resumed packing up. The first-year girl in the south doorway visibly debated turning around for a beat, then committed and crossed the common room toward the dorm-stair with her eyes on her own feet.

He kept his eyes on the Cassia-recipes sheet. The first recipe — *Aqua de Tribus Soporibus* — had advanced to the third ingredient under his right hand.

Mei's quill resumed on the parson letter.

The fire in the common-room grate at the south end did its small *crack.* The two seventh-years on the sofa had gone properly back to their books. The last fourth-year from Bea Sutton's cluster had gone up to the dorms. The first-year girl who had crossed through had reached the spiral stair and disappeared. ▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 20:51

He held Mei's hands at the window. Did not, however, take her back to the desk yet. The third minute was up; she had not yet asked to move.

He held the thought instead.

*Athena. — Young lady. — Mei needs you. — Mei is overwhelmed and a small bird against her cheek would do what nothing else can right now. — Come if you can. — Mei is your person too tonight. — Be soft with her. — She is — not yet used to being loved the way you and I love her.*

Held it for the small careful beat. Did not say anything aloud.

For perhaps thirty seconds: nothing. The lake outside. The Forbidden Forest dark line. The small Pipistrelle had not come back.

Then.

A small dark shape — proper Pygmy-flight signature, the short rounded wings, the working hovering corrections — came across the lawn below at speed, banked up the tower wall, and arrived at the leaded sill from the outside.

The window latches were closed. Athena hovered at the glass for half a beat. Then performed the small precise *I am tapping at your window* gesture with her beak against the pane — *click, click, click* — at the level audible to Caleb and Mei.

Mei registered the tap. The composed Mei-precision came on properly — the *what is happening* register — and her eyes found Athena at the glass.

The small *oh* face. Full warm.

Caleb let go of one of her hands and reached up to the small latch of the window. Lifted it. The leaded pane swung inward on its hinge.

Athena hopped through the gap. Landed properly on the inner sill.

Caleb closed the window behind her against the September air.

Athena turned. Folded her wings. Looked at Mei with the small *yes, I came* yellow-eyed register. Then walked the four small precise steps along the sill — past Caleb's hand on the latch — and stopped directly in front of Mei.

Looked up.

Mei did not, immediately, move. The composed Mei-precision was — completely off-duty. The small *Athena has come to the window unbidden* face was on. She lifted her free hand slowly to the level of Athena's chest. Open palm. The proper magical-creatures-trainee posture she had picked up from Imogen at the Hufflepuff table.

Athena considered. Then hopped onto Mei's hand. Walked the small three steps up Mei's forearm to her shoulder. Stopped there.

Tilted her head.

Then — proper, deliberate, the small *I am here for this specifically* gesture — Athena leaned the small full Pygmy-Owl head-rub against the side of Mei's cheek. Pressed. The small soft *I am loving on you* contact. Her feathered crown rubbed against Mei's cheekbone properly.

Held for perhaps three seconds.

Then nuzzled in toward Mei's ear. The small *I am here, you are loved* press.

Mei's free hand came up slowly and her fingertip stroked the soft place at the top of Athena's head.

The composed Mei-precision did not come back.

After a long beat, very quietly, into Athena's feathers:

"*Hello, young lady. — *I do not know what to say to you.*"

## Narrator

OOC: Quick logistics check before I run. Caleb and Mei are seventh-year Ravenclaws in separate dorms (boys' dorm and girls' dorm). The dorm staircases at Hogwarts are charmed: the girls' staircase is famously inaccessible to boys (the stair turns into a slide). Boys' dorms historically *don't* have the same charm in canon, so a girl can technically go up the boys' staircase — but that's a custom in Gryffindor's tower specifically that gets noted; whether Ravenclaw has the same architecture isn't fully canon. The general Hogwarts rule across all houses is *students sleep in their own dorms.*

So *here* (the common room) isn't where they sleep, and *in your dorm* would be either: (a) Mei sneaks up to Caleb's boys' dorm, possible if Ravenclaw boys' staircase lets girls up — which is unestablished in our campaign but plausible; (b) Caleb attempts the girls' staircase, gets repelled by the slide-charm; (c) they go somewhere else.

The somewhere else: the Bone Zone (Room of Requirement) is a real option. Caleb's *less heart-shaped Bone Zone* phrasing reads as *somewhere private and not the heart-couch register specifically — just a place to lie down and be held.* The Room can shape itself into a quiet bedroom with a normal bed if that's what he requires. That works.

The other real option is the office — Brigham's working room — which is not a sleeping space but is private, has a couch, and is theirs.

Two cleanest paths:

1. **The Room of Requirement, opened with the intent of *a quiet bedroom with one bed where Mei and I can just sleep, no other register.*** Mei comes with him. They sleep. They're back to their dorms by morning.

2. **Mei sneaks up to Caleb's seventh-year boys' dorm.** Establishes that Ravenclaw boys' stair lets girls up (or establishes that it doesn't and they have to go to option 1). They have to be quiet — Caleb's dorm-mates Lin and presumably others are present and asleep. Less private. Workable but logistically harder.

My read is option 1 is what Caleb is reaching for with *less heart-shaped Bone Zone* — he wants a quiet private space that is theirs, not the heart-couch register but the *just sleep* register. The Room can do that.

Running option 1.

The composed Mei-prefect register held for the small beat it took her to register that Caleb had asked her — plainly, the way she had asked him for plain instructions earlier — what she most wanted to be asked.

Quiet. The full soft warm.

"*Less heart-shaped Bone Zone. — *A quiet room with one bed. — *I want — *no register. — *No conversation about what I am and am not allowed. — *Just — *you holding me. — *Sleep.*"

A breath. The composed Mei-precision began to come back — but only the practical-logistics part of it.
