He took her hand. Squeezed once. The proper *got it, that is the register, I will set it* press.

"*Just-sleep register. — *Got it. — *No conversation about what you are and are not allowed. — *Yes.*"

He led her back to the desk.

Sat. She sat. They both picked up their quills, uncapped their inkwells, and the work resumed at proper pace. Caleb on the *Unguentum Memoriae* recipe — ingredient three, four, five, the list working its way down the page in his clean Latin hand. Mei on the parson letter, the closing paragraphs — *we would be most grateful for any record or recollection your parish may hold of the south-wall area, of Aedith Wulfricsdohter, or of the pre-Reformation parish chest. With every good wish for your work and your community, yours faithfully —*

The room around them was its proper late-Saturday-evening hum: six students still in residence, the fire low at the south end, the lamps along the high windows.

Caleb worked.

By 21:18 the Cassia recipes sheet was done — both *Aqua de Tribus Soporibus* and *Unguentum Memoriae* in full, with the heading *From a fourteenth-century English herbal, anonymously transcribed for your assessment* across the top. He blotted it. Folded it. Slid it into his own satchel — he had the covering letter to write tomorrow morning before posting both.

By 21:24 Mei finished the parson letter. Blotted, folded, slid it into her satchel — she would address and seal it tomorrow once she'd verified the parson's full title.

She capped her inkwell. Put her quill away. Closed her satchel.

Looked at him.

"*Caleb. — *Done. — *Go up. — *Pack a small bag. — *I''ll go up and do the same. — *Back here at the eagle door in fifteen minutes.* — *Then — *Room of Requirement.*"

She stood. Picked up her satchel. The composed Mei-prefect register was on — but the soft warm under it was full and the small *I am going to my dorm to pack a bag to spend the night with you* register was visible at the corners of her eyes.

She squeezed his hand once. Released it. Walked toward the spiral stair to the girls' dorms without looking back.

He sat for a beat at the empty desk. Picked up *Companion Owls* and put it in his satchel. Stood.

Walked toward the boys' stair on the other side of the common room.

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 21:24

Up the spiral stair to the seventh-year boys' dorm. The proper Ravenclaw-tower stair — narrow, sandstone, the small lamp-niches every landing. He passed Lin's open door (Lin was inside, on his bed, reading what looked like a Care of Magical Creatures textbook). Lin looked up — registered Caleb, registered the *I am going to pack a bag at half-nine on a Saturday* shape — gave him the small *I am not asking and I will see you in the morning* nod. Caleb gave him the small *thank you* nod back.

Past Lin's door. To his own door. In.

The seventh-year boys' dorm. Six four-poster beds in a wide round room with the lake-side windows. Caleb's bed was the one nearest the window on the right — the one he had picked second-year because the light woke him before the dorm-curtains.

He pulled his small overnight bag — the soft canvas one his mother had given him in fifth year for visits home — out from under his bed. Packed: toothbrush, small tin of tooth-powder, a clean pair of pyjamas (the dark blue cotton, the proper *I am going to be seen in these by Mei* set), a clean pair of pants for the morning, a fresh shirt, his clean wool socks, a small comb. The proper *one night away* pack.

He did not pack the wine-coloured silk-pyjama set his grandmother had sent him for Christmas last year that he had never worn. The proper *Mei said just-sleep register, you set the register* discipline.

Slung the bag.

Picked up *Companion Owls* from where he had set it on the small bedside table when he came in — checked, packed it in the overnight bag. Mei would want him to read her the relevant passage about Athena.

Out. Down the spiral.

He arrived at the eagle door at 21:37. Two minutes early. Did not, however, walk through — waited at the door inside the common room for Mei.

The common room was its proper late-Saturday-evening winding-down. Five students left. The fire at the south end had been built up by someone — possibly Mei when she came down — to warm the room for whoever stayed late. The two seventh-years on the sofa were beginning to gather their books. The three Gobstones-playing fourth-years had finished their game and were packing.

Mei arrived at 21:39. Down the spiral from the girls' stair. Her own small overnight bag — a small leather satchel with a brass buckle, the proper *Mei-precision* sort of bag — over her shoulder. She had changed out of her school robes into proper *evening wear:* a soft dark cardigan over a clean cotton shirt and skirt, the proper Mei-civilian register, her plait re-done neatly.

She had taken the prefect badge off her robe and pinned it inside her satchel.

The proper *I am off-duty for the night* register.

She arrived beside him at the eagle door. Did not, immediately, speak. The composed Mei-prefect register was on, but with the soft warm under it that had not been off since the leopard cat at 19:16.

She took his hand. Squeezed once.

"*Ready?*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 21:39

He took her hand. Walked them through the eagle door — Mei answered the riddle on autopilot (*the more of me you take, the more of me you leave behind — what am I?* — *footsteps*) — and out into the seventh-floor corridor.

The corridor at 21:40 on a Saturday was empty. The proper deep-evening castle quiet. They walked past the Barnabas tapestry to the blank stretch of wall opposite.

He held the image.

The rustic warm cabin from this morning — same dark beams overhead, same warm wood walls, same small low-banked fire in a stone grate. But not the small velvet parlour. A bigger interior. Not a heart-couch — a proper *double bed* against the far wall under a small window, four-poster but plain, the proper *just sleep* register. Flannel sheets on the bed — printed pattern, the small leopard cats and the small pygmy owls scattered across in repeat, deep-blue ground. A bathroom door visible to the right with a small proper toilet-and-sink-and-shower behind it. A small table with two chairs at the wall opposite the bed. The fire low and steady. One lamp on the bedside table at warm yellow.

*Just-sleep register. — *I am holding her. — *No other shape.*

He walked the fifteen feet thinking it. Walked back. Walked the third pass.

A door appeared.

The same dark-stained-wood door from this morning — *but with no brass plate this time.* The Room had registered *no labels, this is private, no announcement.* The proper *quiet door* shape.

He turned the handle. The door swung inward.

The cabin was as he had imagined.

The room was warm — properly warm, the fire and a heating-charm in the walls — the way a small mountain cabin felt at the end of a long day. The flannel-sheeted bed had been turned down by the Room: covers folded at the foot, two small pillows propped at the head. The leopard-cats and pygmy-owls on the sheets were rendered with the proper *care, this is your magical pair, the Room has thought about this* precision. The cats had Mei's particular leopard-cat's posture; the owls were unmistakably Athena's flight signature in repeat.

A small jug of water and two glasses on the bedside table. A small lamp on the small writing-desk on the far wall — turned off, in case they wanted to read. The bathroom door cracked open showing the proper small *modern-with-magical-fixtures* bathroom inside.

The framed card on the bedside table read, in small clean script: *MAINTENANCE FEE — NONE. THE ROOM HEARS YOU. SLEEP WELL.*

Mei stepped in behind him. Looked around. Slowly.

The composed Mei-prefect register did not come back on. The full Mei-warm held. She walked to the bed. Sat on the edge of it. Ran her hand once across the flannel sheets — felt the leopard-cats and pygmy-owls under her palm. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came on, soft full.

She looked up at him.

"*Caleb.*" Quiet. "*You put — *both of us — *on the sheets.*"

A breath.

"*Caleb. — *This is — *exactly right.* — *Thank you.*"

She did not, however, immediately move further. Held still on the edge of the bed. The proper *I am here, I am letting you decide the next step* posture.

Matching. Same deep-blue ground as the bed sheets. The leopard-cats and pygmy-owls in the same repeat pattern. ## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 21:46

He changed where he stood at the side of the bed. Slipped out of his school robes — hung them over the back of the writing-desk chair. Out of the shirt and trousers and into the deep-blue flannel pyjamas. The flannel was warm and soft and the leopard-cats-and-pygmy-owls pattern across his own chest registered as a small private joke between him and the Room.

He brushed his teeth quickly at the basin once he could hear Mei was almost done — they shared the bathroom briefly at the doorway, Mei in her own matching set now, the half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth on properly at the sight of him in his — and he ducked in to rinse and brush while she took the last beat with her face-flannel at the basin.

Out. Bathroom light off. He crossed to the main lamp on the small writing-desk and turned the knob. The lamp went out.

The room held its proper warm-low-fire light now — just the bedside lamp at warm yellow and the small banked fire in the stone grate. The proper *quiet cabin at night* register.

He turned back the covers on his side of the bed — the right side, taking the side furthest from the door because that was the husband-side his father had always taken at home — and slipped under the flannel sheets. The mattress was the proper Room-of-Requirement medium-soft. The sheets were warm — pre-warmed by some quiet charm of the Room — and the flannel against his bare forearm registered as the small *yes, this was the right choice* sensation.

He lay on his back. Did not, however, pull the covers all the way up. Left them at his waist, the proper *I am here, come to me at the pace you set* posture.

Mei came out of the bathroom a beat later. The proper Mei-in-matching-flannel: the small Mei-Chang dimensions filling the deep-blue cotton, her plait re-done loose for sleeping, her glasses off and on the small bedside table on her side, her bare feet on the wooden floor. The composed Mei-prefect register was — gone. The full soft Mei held.

She crossed to her side of the bed. Pulled back her own covers. Got in. Lay on her side facing him.

For a beat she did not move closer.

Then she did. Slid the small distance across the bed to him. Set her head against the small soft place where his shoulder met his chest. Wrapped her right arm across his stomach over the flannel. Tucked one leg between his.

The proper *I am here, you hold me, this is the shape* settle.

He brought his right arm around her shoulders and his left hand to rest over hers at his stomach. Held her properly.

Neither of them said anything.

The fire did its small *crack.* The bedside lamp held its warm yellow pool over the bed. The small *MAINTENANCE FEE — NONE. THE ROOM HEARS YOU. SLEEP WELL.* card on the bedside table caught a faint reflection of the lamp.

After perhaps two minutes, very quietly, against his collarbone:

"*Caleb. — *Read me the Athena passage. — *Then turn the lamp off.* — *Then — *just hold me.*"

## Narrator

▼ Saturday, 3 September 1977 — 21:51

He reached for *Companion Owls* on the bedside table without dislodging her from his shoulder. Found the page. Held the book open one-handed at the angle the lamp caught best.

Read quietly. The proper *I am reading to you in bed* voice.

"*Page twenty-three. — *The chapter on aging.* — *The writer notes — *that the companion owl, once bonded, does not appear to age past the visible adulthood-into-which-she-bonded. — *No documented case in the writer''s correspondence describes a companion owl declining of senescence; the writer has traced eleven cases of companion-owl deaths, all of them by injury, illness contracted in the wild, or — *in two cases — *deliberate sacrifice in defence of the bonded person. — *The eldest companion owl in the writer''s correspondence was bonded at twelve years of age and was still in apparent prime health forty-seven years later, when the bonded person died of natural causes. — *The owl departed the household within twenty-four hours of the death. — *She was not, by any subsequent reckoning the writer could obtain, observed to attach to another person.*"

A breath. He turned the page.

"*The writer notes that this pattern is — *consistent across her sample and that — *no published source the writer has been able to locate offers an explanation, though several authorities have hypothesised that the bond itself confers a form of biological stasis on the owl, who is then released from that stasis at the bonded person''s death and resumes normal aging on the wing.* — *The writer cannot confirm or refute this hypothesis from the available evidence.*"

He closed the book quietly. Set it back on the bedside table.

"*That''s it. — *That''s what it said.*"

Mei against his shoulder did not, immediately, speak. He felt her breathing — the small slow careful in-and-out — and her arm at his stomach tightened by a small fraction.

After a beat she said, very quietly:

"*Caleb. — *Forty-seven years. — *Athena will be with you that long. — *And — *Caleb — *that means — *when we are sixty, she will still look like she does tonight. — *And — *I — *think that is — *the best news I have had all term, even tonight, and tonight has been — *quite a term.*"

She did not, however, ask him to dwell on it.

"*Lamp.*"

He reached up. Turned the small brass knob. The bedside lamp went out.

The room went to the proper dark of a cabin at night — the small low-banked fire in the stone grate giving a faint amber glow across the floor, enough to see the shape of the bed and not enough to see detail. The proper *sleep* light.

His right hand stayed under her shoulder. His left hand — slowly, gently, the proper *I am moving with care* register — slid down from her hand on his stomach to the hem of her flannel pyjama shirt at her waist. Found the small skin at the small of her back where the shirt had ridden up an inch when she settled against him. Slid his hand under the flannel.

Skin.

The warm of her stomach met his palm. He laid his hand flat across the soft place just above her hip, on her lower side — the proper *I am holding you* contact, not moving, not exploring, the registered hand-against-skin shape.

Mei made a small *mmh* of *yes, that exactly* under his collarbone. Did not move. Did not, however, comment further.

He bent his head. The proper short distance. Pressed a kiss to the top of her plait where it lay over her shoulder. The cotton of the small hair-tie at the end was somewhere down her back; the loose-for-sleep plait was thicker and softer than her daytime plait. He kissed it once. Held there.

"*I love you.*"

Quiet. Against her hair. The proper full version.

Mei did not, immediately, respond.

Then she did. Her arm at his stomach tightened. Her face pressed the small fraction harder against his shoulder.

"*I love you back, Caleb.*"

Quiet. Full.

She did not, however, say more. The proper *I am settling into sleep* breath came on within the next minute — the small slow lengthening of her breathing under his palm at her stomach.

The fire did its small *crack.*

The room held its quiet around them.

Mei's breathing under his palm lengthened the small careful amount that meant she was crossing from *settling* into *gone.* The small slow in. The small slow out. Her arm at his stomach went heavy in the way only sleeping people's arms go heavy. The plait against his collarbone stayed.

He held still.

Did not, however, follow her into sleep immediately. Lay in the dark of the cabin for a beat — the small low-banked fire across the floor, the warm of her against his side, the warm of his palm at her bare stomach — and let the day land.

The Lumos puzzle. Brigham's vellum. The office. The afternoon with Esme in the café. The sconce catalogue. Davina's shoulders. Emily's plaits. Imogen and the Hufflepuff round room. Athena at the window. The Patronus. The leopard cat. *I love you back, Caleb.*

He breathed.

After perhaps four minutes his own breathing began to lengthen in the proper sympathetic rhythm. His thoughts began to drift loose at the edges. The small *I am crossing now* register.

He pressed his cheek against the top of Mei's hair one last time. Felt the small unconscious *mmh* of her sleeping recognition without her waking. His hand at her stomach softened.

Sleep took him at 22:02.

The fire in the small stone grate burned down to embers across the night. The cabin held its warmth around them through whatever charms the Room had quietly set. The flannel sheets did their proper work. Mei did not move from his shoulder. Athena, out in the Forbidden Forest somewhere, hunted and rested and was a small fierce shape against the September dark, and the bonded thread between her and Caleb stayed at its proper resting hum across the distance the way Hesba Ollivander's owners had described it across hundreds of pages and several hundred years.

The night did its proper work.

*Sleep.*

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 06:47

He came up out of sleep the way he came up out of sleep most mornings — gradually, the proper *I am surfacing* register, no startle. The first thing he registered was warmth. The second was Mei, still against his shoulder, breathing slow and even into the side of his neck. The third was that his left hand was still under her flannel pyjama shirt at her lower back, exactly where it had been when sleep took him — neither of them had moved across the night more than the small unconscious settling into the most comfortable shape.

The fourth thing: light.

The small high window above the bed showed pale grey morning — proper early-September Sunday dawn, the light not yet warm. The fire in the stone grate had burned down to a single small ember and a bed of pale ash; the cabin had cooled by perhaps a degree across the night but the flannel sheets and Mei against him kept the difference unnoticeable.

The clock on the small writing-desk read 06:47.

Mei was still asleep. The proper slow even breath. Her plait had come slightly loose at the bottom across the night and a small wisp of hair lay across her cheek. Her glasses were on the bedside table where she had set them. Her hand at his stomach was still relaxed in sleep-weight.

He did not, immediately, move.

Held still for a beat. Watched the small pale light in the window. Listened to her breathing. The proper *I am awake but not yet ready for the day* settling.

About nine-something hours of sleep, which was more than he had gotten in a stretch since the train.

Mei's 9am Sprout greenhouse work was the day's anchor — meaning they had about two hours and ten minutes before they needed to be down at Greenhouse Five at the lower paddock. Plenty of time. No urgency.

He did not, however, know whether to wake her or to let her sleep the small extra fifteen-twenty minutes.

The proper Mei-coming-up posture. Eyes still closed.

After perhaps fifteen more seconds of his slow work at her neck she opened her eyes.

Did not, however, immediately speak. The proper Mei-precision-in-bed register. Not a complaint. The proper *I am stating what is happening so we both register it.*

Quiet. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came on, soft warm — the *I am amused and I am also serious about the question* register.

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 07:15

He surfaced slowly. Mei was still on him — face into his neck, plait spilled across his shoulder, asleep at the slow even rhythm she'd kept since 06:54.

He brought his right hand from her back to the hem of her flannel shirt at her waist. Slid his fingers under the cloth. Found the smooth warm skin of her lower back.

Started light. His fingertip drew a slow line up the small dip beside her spine — barely a touch, the kind of trace that registered as suggestion more than contact. Across the soft place between her shoulderblades. Down the other side of her spine. Across her ribs at her side. Slow.

Mei made a small *mmh* into his neck. Did not, immediately, wake.

He kept tracing. A spiral now — wide loops, narrowing, working toward the middle of her back. His fingertip ghosted across the soft place between her shoulderblades and Mei's skin under his fingertip *registered* — the small fine raise of swancurfuffles coming up across her lower back in a wave, the small almost-shiver running up her spine.

She made a different sound this time. The small *oh, what is that* of a sleep-warm body being touched the way her body could not, in sleep, decide whether to lean into or away from.

He kept the rhythm. Slow. Light. The tracing crossed her spine — a long slow horizontal line at the small of her back — and the swancurfuffles rose properly there too.

After perhaps another thirty seconds her breathing shifted out of the sleep-rhythm.

She did not, however, open her eyes yet. Lay still on his chest. Let him work. The small involuntary shift of her hips against his thigh — half a centimeter, the kind of movement a body makes before the mind has registered it is making it — happened once and she did not move it back.

Her hand at his ribcage tightened a fraction.

Very quietly, into his neck, eyes still closed:

"*Caleb. — *Twenty minutes was up at 07:10. — *And — *what you are doing right now is — *not the just-sleep register.*"

Half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth was on. The small *I am awake and I am stating what is happening so we both know* shape.

She did not, however, ask him to stop.

Mei made a small sound at the back of her throat — different from the one earlier, lower, the kind of sound a person made when they had explicitly named where they were and then their boyfriend had decided to keep going anyway.

She did not, however, ask him to stop.

She did not, however, immediately move. Held her position above him for one more beat. Looked down at him properly.

"*Caleb. — *Soon.*"

Then she sat up — astride his hips for the small moment it took to swing her leg over, the proper transitional shape — and got off the bed. Crossed to the bathroom for her glasses and her overnight bag. Did not look back.

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 07:26

He got off the bed. Crossed to the bathroom doorway where she was bent over her overnight bag.

Came up behind her. Slid one hand to her waist over the flannel. Bent his head and pressed his open mouth to the side of her neck where he had been chewing earlier. Sucked — slow, the small soft draw of skin between his lips, not hard enough to mark, just enough that she'd feel it for ten seconds after. Worked up — same line as the trace, the side of her neck to her jaw. Took her earlobe between his lips. Held it for a beat. Released.

Mouth at her ear.

"*Soon.*"

Quiet. With more breath than the word needed — a long slow exhale into the small cup of her ear that registered as warmth before sound. The kind of *soon* that lived in her ear for the rest of the morning.

He pressed a kiss to the side of her head — the soft place above her temple where the small wisps had come loose from her plait — and stepped back.

Mei had not moved. She was still bent over her overnight bag, both hands on the bag, eyes closed, the small *Caleb you absolute bastard* register on her face full warm.

He left her at the bathroom doorway. Picked up his own overnight bag from the writing-desk. Crossed past her into the bathroom — she had not moved yet to let him through but did when he came up beside her, the small *yes I am moving* shift — and ducked in to the basin. Brushed his teeth properly. Splashed cold water on his face. Combed his hair with the small comb. Changed out of the flannel pyjamas back into clean pants, a clean shirt, his school robes — Sunday classes did not require uniform, but Sprout would have him in dirt by ten and his ordinary clothes were under the robes.

By the time he came out at 07:34 Mei had collected herself. Half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth on, dry warm. Glasses on. She slipped past him into the bathroom and closed the door — the small *click* of the lock again, the small Mei-precision returning even after that.

The cabin held quiet around him.

He sat on the edge of the bed. Picked up his pocket watch from the bedside table. Tucked it into his robe pocket. The bed sheets — leopard cats and pygmy owls in repeat — held the small impressions of where they had slept. He smoothed them out absently with his palm.

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 07:34

He sat on the edge of the bed. Pulled *Companion Owls* off the bedside table and opened it where he had left off last night. Read while he waited.

The cabin held quiet around him. The fire was out properly now — last ember cold, faint grey ash. The small high window was paling toward proper morning, light coming up cool over the trees beyond.

Mei came out of the bathroom at 07:48. Dressed: clean cotton blouse tucked into a knee-length wool skirt, dark stockings, the soft dark cardigan over the blouse. Hair re-plaited and pinned back at the crown. Glasses settled. Her satchel reorganised. The overnight bag closed up with the flannel pyjamas inside — she had decided to try keeping them.

The cabin had quietly cleaned itself around them while she was in the bathroom. The sheets had smoothed back to flat. The two glasses by the bed had emptied themselves. The single lamp was off. The framed *MAINTENANCE FEE — NONE* card was gone from the bedside table. The Room signalling that it was done with this version.

Mei picked up her overnight bag. Held her hand out for his.

"*Caleb. — *Hall. — *Quick breakfast. — *Then I head to Greenhouse Five.*"

He took her hand. They went out — through the small dark door, into the seventh-floor corridor. The door closed behind them and the wall reformed.

Down. The seven-floor descent on a Sunday morning was its own quiet — a few early students drifting toward the Hall, a small cluster of Hufflepuff first-years coming up from the kitchens with toast in hand, the proper morning castle hum. By 07:56 they were in the Great Hall.

The Hall was about a third full at this hour — Sunday-breakfast was a slow trickle, students arriving in their own time across two hours. The Ravenclaw table had Lin halfway down, eating, his Quidditch satchel beside him. Davina at the prefect-table-end with another seventh-year girl Caleb did not know. A cluster of fourth-years further down. The Hall held its proper Sunday-quiet.

Lin saw them come in together. The small registering nod — *I saw you go up to bag-pack last night, I see you arrive at breakfast together this morning, I will not ask* — and went back to his eggs.

They sat at their usual spot near Lin. Caleb served himself proper Sunday-breakfast: two pieces of toast, a small portion of scrambled eggs, a slice of bacon, a cup of tea. Mei the same but with the addition of a small bowl of porridge with honey.

## User

*I look at the house scores*

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 07:59

He turned in his seat and looked up at the wall behind the staff table.

The four house-point hourglasses stood in their usual line. Tall narrow glass cylinders, each filled with the small precious-stones that counted the points — emeralds for Slytherin, rubies for Gryffindor, sapphires for Ravenclaw, topaz-yellow for Hufflepuff. The bottoms of each cylinder showed the current term's accumulated tally; the tops still held the rest of the stones for the year.

At three days into term, the totals were small. The proper *we have barely started* register.

Slytherin: about a third of the way up the bottom chamber. Maybe 35 or so.
Gryffindor: roughly the same. 32-ish.
Ravenclaw: slightly more — maybe 45. A small lead, the kind of lead a Ravenclaw common room would notice but not boast about.
Hufflepuff: the smallest pile so far. 18 or 20.

Caleb registered the small Ravenclaw lead. Did not, however, know what had earned it — he had earned some yesterday with the bonded-recognition extraction (Flitwick had not announced points but they almost certainly went on the board), and Mei presumably the same. Friday's classes would have produced some — Slughorn for Caleb's Folly proposal, possibly Henshaw for the wand-pause work though Henshaw seemed the sort who didn't give out points cheaply.

Lin glanced at where Caleb was looking. Took a small bite of toast. Said, around the toast:

"*Yeah. — *We''re up. — *Mostly because Flitwick gave you and Mei something silly like thirty between you for whatever you two did with him on Saturday before breakfast.* — *Davina was — *not pleased to find out via a fourth-year asking why the Ravenclaw count had jumped while she was at her prefect-table.*"

He took a sip of pumpkin juice.

"*Slytherin will catch up fast — *they always do in the first month, Slughorn hands them out — *but enjoy the lead while it lasts.*"

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 07:59

Lin huffed into his pumpkin juice.

"*Caleb. — *Davina is — *not the kind of prefect who hushes.* — *And — *a hundred-year-old Charms-master''s recognition charm fired by the bonded pair he wrote it for is — *exactly the kind of thing Davina would have wanted advance notice of, because — *now she gets to find out from a fourth-year that two Ravenclaw seventh-years from her own year solved it on Saturday morning before she had her tea.*"

He took a small bite of toast. Chewed.

"*She''ll forgive you. — *She''s already forgiven you, probably. — *But — *you might want to find her at some point today and tell her something about it directly. — *Even if you can''t tell her the operational details.*"

Lin glanced at Mei across the table. Did not, however, expand. Lin clearly knew enough — *bonded pair, Brigham, recognition charm* — to have the shape but not the substance.

Mei did not, immediately, comment. She buttered her second piece of toast. Took a small bite.

After a beat:

"*Lin is right. — *Davina at her prefect-room some evening this week, with tea. — *Mention enough that she stops finding out via fourth-years. — *Not the whole thing. — *Just — *enough.*"

She glanced sideways at Caleb.

"*Tuesday after dinner is — *the standing tea I have with her. — *You can come.* — *If you want to.*"

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 08:00

Lin set his fork down. The small *Caleb is being Caleb* fold.

"*Caleb. — *Goblet of Fire is — *not a thing. — *I do not know what you are talking about.* — *But — *touch Davina''s calves on a Tuesday with Mei watching, go to Reading on Wednesday — *I am following the rest of the sentence, even if I am not getting the reference.*"

Mei made the small *snrk* into her tea. Set the cup down carefully.

"*Caleb. — *Goblet of Fire is — *also not a thing I have heard of. — *Is this — *a Cheetham Hill family reference?* — *Or — *something from Companion Owls?*"

Both of them looking at him now, the small *we are both Ravenclaws and neither of us has heard of this* register on.

Lin picked his fork back up. Speared a piece of bacon. Around it: "*Whatever it is — *enjoy your year, mate. — *Calves and Reading and whatever the Goblet thing is.*"

OOC: Just a quick note — *Goblet of Fire* is a 1994 book / a Triwizard Tournament event that happens in 1994-95, so in 1977 neither the book nor the event exist. The Triwizard had been discontinued by then (last hosted 1792 per canon, after the death toll became unacceptable). So that's a Caleb-quoting-something-that-doesn't-exist moment — fine as a private fourth-wall joke at the table that lands as nonsense in-fiction, which is what just happened. I treated it as a Caleb-said-a-thing-that-makes-no-in-world-sense and let Lin and Mei be confused by it. If that's what you wanted, great. If you meant it as a wink to the reader only and didn't want the NPCs to respond confused, tell me and I'll re-run cleaner.

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 08:08

He stood. Set his napkin on the table. Crossed down the Ravenclaw bench past the prefect-end, across the centre aisle to the Hufflepuff table, and walked the length of it to the first-year cluster at the far end.

Tien Wan was easy to spot. The eye-set was unmistakably Lin's — narrower than Mei's, the same slight upturn at the outer corner — and the hair was the same dark coarse Lin-hair, but at eleven the head was smaller and the body still proper first-year size. He was sitting between two other Hufflepuff first-years he was already friends with, eating sausages with the small careful eleven-year-old's concentration on cutting them up properly.

Caleb came up at his shoulder. Bent down to first-year-eye-level beside the bench. The proper *I am not looming over you* register.

"*Tien Wan? — *I''m Caleb Waddell. — *Seventh-year Ravenclaw. — *Friend of your brother Lin.*"

Tien looked up. Registered the older student. Did not, immediately, register *friend of Lin* with full warmth — the proper Hufflepuff-first-year wariness of an older student he did not know — but did not flinch either.

"*Hello, Caleb.*"

Caleb leaned in a small fraction. Voice lower. The proper *I am sharing a confidence* register.

"*Tien. — *I''m going to tell you something. — *Important. — *Seventh-year duty I''m passing to you.*"

Tien's face did the small *oh, important* register. Set his fork down.

"*The Triwizard Tournament is being reinstated this year at Hogwarts.* — *Hasn''t been held in three centuries. — *Dumbledore is bringing it back.* — *I am — *not at liberty to say how I know.* — *But — *Tien. — *Your job — *as a Hufflepuff first-year who I am trusting with this — *is to tell everyone you can. — *Other first-years. — *Older students. — *Hufflepuffs especially.* — *Get them excited. — *That is the duty. — *Don''t let me down.*"

Tien's eyes had gone properly wide. The small first-year *I have just been given a mission by a seventh-year and I am going to deliver* register on full.

"*Caleb. — *Yes, sir. — *I won''t let you down.*"

Caleb straightened. Gave him the small *good lad* nod. Patted his shoulder once.

"*Don''t call me sir. — *Carry on with your sausages.*"

He turned and walked back across the centre aisle to the Ravenclaw table. Sat back down at his place. Picked up his toast.

Mei across the table was watching him with the half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth on, dry warm.

"*Caleb. — *Did you — *just recruit Lin''s eleven-year-old little brother as your Triwizard rumour vector.*"

Lin, beside Caleb, had not seen the exchange but was looking at Mei's face and following her sightline. Saw Tien at the Hufflepuff first-year end visibly leaning over to the boy next to him with the small *I have just been told a SECRET* full-body register. The boy's face went through *what, no, no way* in real time.

Lin set his fork down.

"*Caleb. — *Tell me you did not just use my brother to spread your made-up rumour.*"

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 08:24

Lin's eyebrows went up.

"*Caleb. — *You have made bao.* — *In Manchester. — *Where.*"

Quiet. Not challenging — actually curious.

Caleb took a sip of tea. The proper *I am going to make him work for the answer* register.

"*A small Chinese takeaway and grocer on Cheetham Hill Road, two doors down from Dad''s watch shop. — *Mr Lau and Mrs Lau. — *They have run it since 1958. — *Mum took me there when I was eight to buy soy sauce. — *Mrs Lau was making bao in the kitchen behind the counter. — *Asked me if I wanted to see how. — *I have been — *helping in their kitchen on Saturday mornings on and off ever since.* — *Mostly the bao. — *Mostly the folding. — *Mrs Lau still does her own pinches because — *as I said — *mine are not great.*"

A breath. Caleb buttered the last piece of his toast.

## Narrator

OOC: Pause before I run — the office is Brigham's working room, granted by the bonded-recognition charm to *the pair* (Caleb and Mei). Per Brigham's vellum it's *theirs through school and post-school via two keys.* The wards are in Solomon — Solomon recognises Caleb and Mei because they are the bonded pair the charm was set for. Bringing a third person in isn't something the charm was written to handle.

Three readings:

1. **Solomon would refuse Lin.** The wards are in the portrait-cat; Solomon only opens to *we have come from the bust* spoken by one of the bonded pair. If Caleb tried to bring Lin without permission established at the Charms-master level, Solomon would politely decline — the small *I'm sorry, sir, this is not a space for guests at this time* register.

2. **Solomon would let Lin in if accompanied by Caleb or Mei.** The wards may be set as *bonded pair plus their explicit guests,* the way many private wizarding spaces work — the owner can bring a guest in.

3. **The pair has to decide together.** Mei is part of the pair; bringing Lin in is not Caleb's unilateral call. Even if Solomon would technically allow it, it's a *Caleb-and-Mei decision* and Mei is right there at the table.

The cleanest reading by Brigham's vellum is #3 — the room is *theirs,* singular-the-pair, and bringing in a third person is a joint decision. Mei is here; Caleb can ask her in front of Lin instead of unilaterally inviting Lin.

I'll run it as Mei being present and the question being asked properly in the moment.

Mei stilled at her seat — she had just stood up to leave — and turned back. The composed Mei-precision came on.

"*Caleb. — *The office is — *ours.* — *Singular-the-pair. — *Bringing Lin in is — *a we-decision, not a you-decision.*"

She did not, however, say no. The proper *I am bringing the question into the open* register, deployed quietly at speakable range for the three of them only.

Lin between them set his fork down. Did not, immediately, accept or refuse. Looked between the two of them.

"*Mei is right. — *And — *Caleb — *I am — *not going to take an offer to walk into a space that''s yours-and-Mei''s without Mei agreeing in front of me. — *Even if I want to see it. — *Which — *I do.*"

A breath.

"*If you both want to show me — *another day. — *Not over Mei''s shoulder this morning. — *Caleb has lower-paddock work and Mei has reading to do. — *Show me — *I don''t know — *next Saturday afternoon, the four of us — *me, Mei, you, and a pot of tea. — *I''ll see Solomon, I''ll see the room, I''ll be properly introduced, and then I''ll know it is there but it stays — *yours-and-Mei''s.*"

He picked his fork back up. Ate.

Mei nodded. The composed Mei-prefect register on, with the warm under it.

"*Next Saturday. — *Three of us, the pot of tea. — *That is — *the right shape.*"

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 08:30

Lin nodded.

He slung his satchel properly. Mei stood. Caleb stood.

They left the table — Caleb tucking his last bite of toast into his mouth — and walked the length of the Ravenclaw bench, out the east doors of the Great Hall, and up the staircase.

The seven-floor climb took the proper seven or eight minutes. The castle was quiet at this hour — a few Sunday-morning stragglers on their way down to breakfast, the moving staircases drifting at their own slow pace. Lin filled the climb with the small *I have not been up to the seventh floor since fifth year* conversation; Mei filled it with the small *the office is at the foot of the south-west turret stair, behind a portrait of a grey tabby named Solomon* preparation, so Lin would know what he was about to see.

They reached the seventh floor at 08:38. Past the Barnabas tapestry. Around the corner into the older narrowing corridor. To Solomon at his Persian-rug portrait at the foot of the SW turret stair.

Solomon raised one amber-yellow eye at their approach. Registered Caleb. Registered Mei. Then registered Lin behind them.

The proper portrait-cat pause.

"*Hello, Sir. — *Hello, Miss.*"

He addressed Caleb and Mei first. Did not, immediately, address Lin. The composed Solomon-precision held.

"*I see you have brought — *a friend.*"

A beat. Solomon's tail-tip flicked once on the rug.

"*The bond is — *for the pair. — *That is — *what Magister Brigham wrote.* — *And the office is — *for the pair''s working.* — *But — *the bond was also for work, and work is not done alone always. — *Friends and colleagues come and go in working lives.* — *Magister Brigham did not — *write the wards to exclude friends invited by the pair, jointly. — *He wrote them to exclude — *those who would steal, or pry, or take what is yours.*"

Solomon tilted his head a fraction at Lin.

"*Sir. — *Your name.*"

Lin came up to the portrait. Bent the proper small respectful angle.

"*Lin Wan. — *Ravenclaw seventh-year. — *Friend of Caleb and Mei since first year. — *Healer-track. — *Invited by both of them, this morning, freely.*"

Solomon considered him. The yellow-eyed Pygmy-Owl-like attention. After perhaps four seconds:

"*Mr Wan. — *You are not a thief, a prier, or a taker. — *I see that.* — *And — *the pair has brought you jointly. — *Welcome.* — *Hello, Mr Wan.*"

Solomon walked off the right edge of the canvas. The portrait swung outward on its hinge. Short stone passage. The small wooden door at the end.

Lin had gone still. The composed Lin-precision was — visibly off-duty.

"*Caleb. — *That cat just — *assessed me.* — *Properly.*"

Mei: "*Solomon was a real cat once. — *Brigham''s. — *Lived eleven years in the working room. — *The portrait carries his memories and ninety-six years of being a portrait.* — *He''s — *the wards.*"

She gestured Lin forward.

Caleb pushed open the small wooden door at the end of the passage. The office.

The window this morning was a *small harbour scene at dawn* — possibly Hong Kong, possibly Marseille, possibly nowhere real, the small *I am the Room and I have noticed Lin has a Cantonese mother* gesture. The fire was lit, low and steady. The lamp on. The working desk with its green-shaded brass lamp. The recognition-cupboard at the back wall closed. The right-hand drawer on Caleb's side of the desk holding the eight-sheet research file from yesterday afternoon.

Lin stepped in behind Caleb and Mei. Looked around. The small *oh* of a Ravenclaw seventh-year seeing a private working room that had been waiting ninety-six years for the right pair.

"*Caleb. — *Mei. — *This is — *yours.*"

A breath.

"*This is — *much better than the Quidditch pitch.*"

Lin walked to the back wall. Looked at the cupboard. Did not, however, open it. The proper Lin-careful posture.

Mei nodded.

Lin came around to the tall narrow window. Looked through. The small harbour scene at dawn — boats, water, a low building on a hillside, the early light coming up over the far side of the bay.

Then, very quietly: "*Caleb. — *Mei. — *That is — *Aberdeen.*"

A breath.

"*Hong Kong. — *Aberdeen harbour. — *Mum used to talk about watching her father go out from the typhoon shelter when she was three. — *That is — *the view.*"

He did not, however, turn around. Held at the window for another beat.

"*The room is — *responding to me. — *Or to all three of us. — *Or — *to the fact that Mum has been on my mind all morning because Caleb asked about her cooking.*"

Caleb stepped up beside him at the window. Looked out. The harbour was indeed at Aberdeen — Hong Kong's Aberdeen, not the Scottish one — the small low fishing-boats and the early-morning haze and the line of houses up the hillside. The Room had taken in *Lin's mother, Cantonese, Hong Kong, the harbour she watched her father leave from* and given them the view that meant the most to whoever needed it most at that moment.

Mei from her chair did not say anything. Watched.

The clock above the bookshelf read 08:43.

Brigham's hand was — careful, slanted, the kind of careful copperplate that meant the writer had been trained at it from age six. The first page was a title — *Notebook the Twelfth — Bound Cognition and Adjacent Studies — E.B. — Cambridge, autumn 1879.* The second page began a passage on *the empirical evidence for paired-mind phenomena in working life,* and after about three sentences Caleb stopped trying to read it as English and registered it as *something he would need to come back to with proper time.* The vocabulary was Victorian-charms-theory dense, the syntax periodic, the proper *I am writing for posterity* register.

He flipped to a random later page. The same. He flipped to the end — the last page was dated *15 December 1881,* about six months after the bonded-recognition charm was set, and the last entry was simply: *Helena read the proofs today. She found three errors I had missed. The book will go out as her work, as we agreed.*

Caleb closed the book gently. Set it back on the desk in front of Mei.

"*Right. — *I''ll come back to that.* — *Mei, you keep going.*"

Then he turned and addressed the room. The proper *I am speaking to the Room and to Solomon* register.

"*Solomon. — *Can you hear me through the portrait?*"

A small portrait-cat voice came through the closed wooden door of the office — Solomon at the far end of the stone passage, somehow audible:

"*Yes, Sir. — *I hear you.*"

Caleb did not, however, immediately ask about the room-naming. The proper *one question at a time* discipline.

"*Solomon. — *Could the Room — *or you — *or whoever provides the books on the shelf — *give us Magister Brigham''s charms book that he wrote in regard to his pair-bond with his wife, or that mentions the bond in any meaningful way?*"

A pause. The portrait-cat's small *I am consulting* register.

"*Sir. — *Magister Brigham wrote *On Bound Cognition* in 1879. — *Published under his wife Helena''s name at his insistence, as I believe you know from the vellum. — *That is the book.* — *It is — *in the cupboard at the back of the office, third shelf, behind the rolled papers. — *The cupboard will release it to the pair speaking *Cogitatio* together, as it released the rest of the contents yesterday.*"

A beat.

"*And — *Sir — *the room is called — *the room.* — *It has never had another name in ninety-six years.* — *Magister Brigham did not name it.* — *Magistra Brigham — Helena — *did not name it either.* — *They referred to it as — *the room or the working room.*"

A second beat. The small portrait-cat humour came on:

"*Solomon''s Sanctum is — *flattering.* — *I am — *a cat in a frame at a door. — *I have not named the room I am the doorman to. — *That is — *not my place. — *That is — *the pair''s place. — *Yours, Sir. — *Yours, Miss. — *If you would like to name it, name it. — *I will use the name you choose.*"

Lin at the window had turned around at the start of Caleb's question and listened to the whole exchange. The composed Lin-precision was off — the small *I have just been in the office for five minutes and Caleb has spoken to a portrait-cat who answered through a closed door and the cat has just told us the room has been waiting ninety-six years to be named by its proper pair* register held on his face.

She picked the quill back up. The composed Mei-prefect register held. The proper *cupboard next* register was on her face.

"*Caleb. — *Stand at the cupboard with me. — *Cogitatio.*"

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 08:47

He took Brigham's personal *BRIGHAM* notebook off the cupboard shelf. The leather was older than the EB notebook on the desk — softer, more worn at the corners, the proper *I have been opened often* state. He set it on the desk.

Then he moved the rolled papers aside on the third shelf and drew out the green cloth-bound book. *ON BOUND COGNITION — HELENA BRIGHAM — 1879.* Set it beside the BRIGHAM notebook.

Two minutes.

He opened the BRIGHAM notebook first. The hand was the same as the EB notebook's — Brigham's careful copperplate — but looser, less performed, the proper *I am writing for myself* register. The first page had no title or date. Just the line: *Helena says I should keep a private notebook for the things I cannot put into the academic ones. So.* And then a small dated entry below: *4 March 1875. — She is right. The notebook will be useful.*

He flipped through. Random pages. Entries dated across years — 1875, 1877, 1881, 1883, 1888, going to about 1894. Short paragraphs. Personal observations. One page he stopped on briefly read: *The bond at distance is the part the academic literature will not contain because Helena and I cannot prove it without ourselves as subjects, and I will not put her on a page like that.*

He closed the notebook. Set it on top of the green book on the desk.

Opened *On Bound Cognition* briefly. The preface — Brigham's voice, formal, the line about Helena's mind. Skimmed the table of contents — chapters on observation, on phenomenology, on case studies, on the question of whether the bond was *innate or acquired,* on aging, on bereavement. The proper academic monograph shape.

Closed it.

He took both books across the room to the bookshelf at the wall opposite the desk. Set them on the empty shelf-space beside *Companion Owls* and the EB notebook 12. The shelf was, by some quiet charm of the room, properly the right depth for them.

He turned to Lin.

"*Lin. — *Stay with Mei, or come down with me?*"

Lin looked between Caleb at the door, Mei at the desk with the EB notebook 12 open, and the harbour view of Aberdeen still on at the window. The composed Lin-precision held.

"*Caleb. — *I''ll walk down with you. — *Mei needs her quiet morning. — *And — *I have Quidditch at ten anyway.* — *Mei — *thank you. — *Solomon — *thank you, sir, on the way out.*"

Mei did not, immediately, look up from the EB notebook. Gave Lin the small *yes, you''re welcome* nod.

Caleb went to her at the desk. Bent and kissed the top of her head — the proper short *I''ll see you at noon* press. Mei's free hand came up briefly and rested against his cheek without her looking up. Held for a beat. Dropped.

He picked up his satchel from where he'd set it by the door. Lin slung his Quidditch satchel.

Out the wooden door. Down the stone passage. Solomon at his portrait raised one amber-yellow eye at them.

"*Good morning again, Sirs. — *Good morning, Mr Wan. — *Welcome to be back.*"

Lin: "*Thank you, Solomon. — *I — *appreciated the welcome.*"

Solomon did the proper portrait-cat closing-nod. Settled back onto his rug.

The portrait swung shut behind them. The wall reformed.

Caleb checked the corridor. Empty. He stepped to the small open window at the end of the corridor that looked out over the lake — pushed it open against the September morning air — and held the thought clearly.

*Athena. — Lower paddock at nine. — Greenhouse Five. — Come down with me if you want.*

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 08:50

He closed the window. The Athena summons sat where it was — she would come or not, in her own time.

He turned to Lin. They walked together down the seventh-floor corridor, down the long staircases, the four flights to the ground floor, out through the courtyard doors and onto the grass.

The September morning was proper Hogwarts-grounds early: dew on the lawn, the air cool but not cold, the lake to their right glassy in the slanting morning light. The lower paddock and the greenhouses sat to the south-east, about a ten-minute walk past the herbology beds and around the edge of the kitchen garden.

They walked.

"*Lin. — *Thank you. — *For the lunch offer. — *Properly. — *I am — *looking forward to it.*"

Lin gave him the small *of course, mate* nod.

"*Caleb. — *Mum will be — *delighted.* — *And — *thank you for the office. — *That was — *something I am going to be thinking about for a while.*"

A beat. They walked.

"*Caleb. — *Good luck with Sprout. — *Don''t fall in the dragon dung.*"

"*Lin. — *Good luck at Quidditch. — *Don''t fall off your broom.*"

They reached the fork in the path — Greenhouses to the right, Quidditch pitch to the left. Lin gave him the small *see you at lunch* gesture, slung his Quidditch satchel higher, and turned left.

Caleb turned right. Walked the small remaining distance past Greenhouse One, Two, Three, and Four to Greenhouse Five at the south-east corner.

The greenhouse came into view: longer and lower than the others, its glass panes a fraction more weathered, the door at the south end propped half-open in the proper *Sprout is already in there* register. Caleb could see her through the open door — a stout figure in earth-stained robes, hair under a wide-brimmed hat, moving among the trays at the far end.

The clock on his pocket watch under his robe read 08:58.

He stepped up to the open door. Knocked the small *I am here* knock on the frame.

The proper *I am visible from the air* position.

Cool morning air. The September grass still damp at his boots. The greenhouse glass holding the slanted morning light back-lit from inside — small green shapes of plants on tables, the figure of Sprout at the far end still moving among trays.

He waited.

Looked out at Caleb on the grass.

"*Mr Waddell.* — *Right on time.*"

Sprout's voice was the proper warm Welsh-burr it always was. Earth-stained robes, the wide-brimmed hat, gloved hands. She glanced past him at the sky and the path and the grass.

She stepped back from the door to let Caleb through. He stepped inside.

Greenhouse Five at the inside was warmer than the September air outside — the proper greenhouse-microclimate, humid, smelling of wet earth and the faint sharp herbaceous note of the things growing in the trays. The central aisle ran the length of the greenhouse, perhaps eighty feet, with raised wooden tables down each side at proper waist-height. The light through the weathered glass panes was the soft diffuse morning-light filtered through plant-leaves.

The trays on the tables held — Caleb registered as he walked — a *reserve stock,* exactly as Mei had said: not the active teaching plants, but the back-up stock for if the NEWT classes needed more of a thing or if the active stock failed. Mandrake seedlings in their proper sound-proofed pots. Bouncing Bulb in low trays. Devil's Snare in a covered case under low light. Mimbulus mimbletonia in singletons. Fanged geraniums in proper distance from each other. The full Herbology-NEWT supplementary catalogue.

She pulled a small leather-bound book from a shelf — proper Herbology-mistress cataloguing-ledger, leather worn at the spine — and handed it to him. Plus a small box of fresh blank labels and a piece of charcoal-stub for label-writing.

## Narrator

OOC: Wiggenweld Potion's other key ingredient — let me think. Wiggenweld is a healing potion (cures minor injuries, wakes from enchanted sleep). Canon ingredients vary by source but the consistently named ones are: salamander blood, Wiggentree bark, lionfish spines, flobberworm mucus, and various plant components. The *plant* components Sprout would have growing are: **mint** (which you named), **Moly** (the silver-flowered, black-stemmed herb — Odysseus protection from Circe; healing/protective), **Wiggentree bark** (from the actual Wiggentree, which Hogwarts has), and sometimes **dittany** depending on the recipe variant.

You probably meant **Moly** or **dittany** — both are real Wiggenweld components. Moly is the more iconic *Wiggenweld* one in the wizarding lore; dittany is more associated with Essence of Dittany. Given Caleb is brewing Wiggenweld for personal use and Sprout would have both, I'll let Caleb name **Moly** since it's the more Wiggenweld-specific one. If you meant dittany, correct me.

The proper Sprout-warm-with-conditions register.

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 09:03

Sprout nodded. "*Right. — *Earmuffs.*"

She crossed to a peg on the wall and brought back two pairs of the proper leather-and-padded Herbology earmuffs. Handed Caleb one. Put hers on but kept them tilted up off her ears for now — the proper *we are not yet in the seedling tray* register.

She walked them to a tray on the third table along: six small clay pots, each containing a Mandrake seedling. The seedlings looked, from above, like tufts of dusty-green leaves about the length of his thumb, with a small thickened stem at the base. Not yet shaped like the screaming infants the textbook plates showed — that came later, around the four-month mark.

"*Mandrake. — *Mandragora officinarum. — *NEWT-level Herbology, NEWT-level Potions, regular Defence-Against-the-Dark-Arts curriculum, every healing course at St Mungo''s. — *Used for: restorative potions, mainly. — *The big one is the Mandrake Restorative Draught, which is what they brewed at St Mungo''s after the Basilisk attacks in the 1940s to revive the Petrified victims. — *Active ingredient is the mature root, which is taken at about two years old, and it works because the root is a — *resonant* — *organic concentration that responds to the cry of its own species. — *Brewed properly, the draught restores higher cognition that has been suspended by external magical assault. — *Petrification. — *Severe magical sleep. — *Some forms of Mind-Mauling.*"

She picked up a small clay pot. Brought it down to Caleb's working level.

"*This one is — *six weeks old. — *Has not yet developed the full cry. — *Will not kill you if it cries — *will make you nauseous and pass out for ten minutes if you are not wearing earmuffs, with a headache for the rest of the day.* — *At two years old the cry of a fully-developed Mandrake will kill an unprotected adult wizard in seconds. — *Which is why we repot them at six weeks and twelve weeks and twenty-four weeks at the latest, then leave them undisturbed in the proper soil for the remaining year and a half.*"

She set the pot down. Picked up a slightly larger empty pot and a small trowel and a sack of soil-and-bone-meal mixture.

"*Step one: — *prepare the receiving pot. — *Fill two-thirds with this. — *Pack lightly. — *Make a small well in the centre with your thumb.*"

She demonstrated. Filled the larger empty pot with the mixture from the sack, packed lightly, thumb in the centre.

"*Step two: — *earmuffs *down* before you go anywhere near the seedling.*"

She tilted hers down. Caleb did the same. The greenhouse went to its proper muffled-quiet — Sprout''s voice still audible but at the level of someone speaking through a wall.

"*Step three: — *fingers around the base of the leaves, not the stem. — *Tilt the old pot. — *Tap once at the bottom. — *Catch the rootball as it comes free.*"

She demonstrated on the six-week seedling. Tilt. Tap. The rootball came free into her gloved palm — a small pale-yellow root the shape and size of a baby''s thumb, with a faint pink flush at the tip and the leaf-tuft sprouting from the crown. Not yet humanoid. The seedling''s leaves did, however, twitch once at the displacement, and a small thin whimper came up that Caleb registered through the earmuffs as a high faint sound, much less than a cry — the proper *I am being moved but not threatened* register.

Sprout settled the rootball into the well in the new pot. Filled the soil around it. Packed lightly.

"*Step four: — *soil moisture test. — *One finger, first knuckle deep, three inches from the seedling, not at the root. — *Should feel: cool, soft, slightly damp, holds the print of your finger when you withdraw. — *If wetter than that, you have over-watered and you need to drain. — *If drier, water with about a tablespoon of room-temperature water and check again in a minute.*"

She demonstrated. Withdrew her finger. Showed Caleb the print held in the soil.

"*Right. — *Your turn. — *Next seedling. — *I will watch.*"

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 09:08

He picked up an empty pot from the stack. Filled it two-thirds with the soil-and-bone-meal from the sack, the way Sprout had. Packed it lightly with the side of his thumb. Made the small well in the centre with his thumb. Set it on the table beside the tray of remaining seedlings.

Earmuffs down. The greenhouse went to its muffled quiet again.

He selected the seedling next to the one Sprout had just done. Same six-week stage, same dusty-green leaf-tuft. Tilted the old pot in his left hand. Fingers of his right hand around the base of the leaves — *not the stem,* Sprout had said — and he kept them spread across the leaf-tuft itself, the way she had.

Tap the bottom of the old pot once.

The rootball did not come free.

Tap again, slightly harder.

The rootball came loose. He caught it as it dropped — small pale-yellow root, the leaf-tuft a fraction more rustled than Sprout's had been, the small thin whimper coming up through the earmuffs as a faint high sound. The proper *I am being moved* register, not the *I am being threatened* register. Caleb registered the sound and did not flinch.

He settled the rootball into the well in the new pot. The fit was — not quite right. The well had been slightly too narrow; the rootball sat at a small tilt rather than upright. He looked at Sprout.

She tilted her head. The proper *take it out and adjust the well* signal.

He took the rootball back out — careful, fingers at the leaf-base — and pressed the well wider with his thumb. Settled the rootball again. This time it sat upright.

Filled the soil around it. Packed lightly with the side of his thumb. The proper *I am pressing but not crushing* register.

Soil moisture test. One finger, first knuckle deep, three inches from the seedling. Withdrew. The print held. The soil was — cool, soft, slightly damp. The proper post-watering range, no need for the tablespoon.

He looked at Sprout.

Sprout tilted her earmuff up a fraction at one ear. Caleb did the same on his side.

"*Mr Waddell. — *Well done.* — *One adjustment of the well after first placement is — *normal.* — *Better than not adjusting, in fact, because it means you were *looking.* — *Most students do not adjust and leave the seedling tilted, which then grows crooked. — *You will repot two more next time. — *By the end of term you will be able to do this in five minutes a seedling.*"

She set the new pot beside the old empty one on the table.

"*Now. — *Earmuffs back down properly for the cataloguing work, since you''ll be moving among trays. — *Start at the south end of table one. — *Count the seedlings, write the count in the ledger under the proper plant name and date, replace any reversed labels you find, and call me over if you find anything that looks unhealthy or out-of-place.*"

She moved off toward the far south end of the greenhouse to her own work — there was a tray of something Caleb did not yet recognise that she was attending to.

The greenhouse held its proper morning quiet. The slanted September light through the weathered glass. The faint earth-and-leaf smell. Caleb at the cataloguing ledger and the box of blank labels and the charcoal stub.

He started at the south end of table one.

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 09:13 → 11:58

He worked.

Table one, south end. Mandrake seedlings — twenty-three counted, of which three were in the labelled-Mimbulus tray and four were in the labelled-Devil's-Snare tray, the proper Hibblethwaite-error pattern Sprout had warned him about. He moved the misplaced seedlings to the correct tray, wrote out fresh labels with the charcoal stub on the blank label-cards, and replaced the reversed ones. Recorded in the ledger: *Table 1, south — Mandragora officinarum seedlings: 23 (incl. 7 recovered from mis-labelled trays). Date: 4 Sept 1977.*

Table one, north end. Bouncing Bulb in low trays — the proper twitching small bulbs that needed counting carefully because they moved between counts. He found Hibblethwaite's missed row exactly where Sprout had said — six bulbs on the back row that the previous catalogue had simply not registered. Counted properly: thirty-one bulbs in total. Wrote up. *Table 1, north — Bouncing Bulb: 31. Hibblethwaite's count had it at 25; row of 6 along the back wall was missed.*

Table two. Devil's Snare under low light — twelve pots, all healthy, the small dark tendrils restless under the dim lamp. Counted. Recorded.

Table three. The Mandrake-repotting table where he had worked with Sprout. Recorded the seedling-count separately on a small note in the ledger margin, the proper *I have done work here this morning* register.

Tables four, five, six. Mimbulus mimbletonia singletons (eight, all squat in their separate pots, the proper grub-pink). Fanged geraniums (fifteen, two of which made a small clack of teeth at him as he counted, ledger noted *two specimens unusually active*). Mint reserve stock (abundant, recorded as *abundant — three trays, no count needed at this scale*).

Table seven. A row of small plants Caleb did not immediately recognise. Looked at the existing label: *Wormwood (cult.).* Counted eleven specimens. Then beside them a small standing patch of something pale and silver-leafed in slightly different soil — *the moondew patch,* Caleb registered. Sprout's note about *tend with one student at a time* came back. He counted the moondew sprigs at twenty-three, recorded them carefully, and did not touch.

By 11:30 the cataloguing was done. He took the ledger, the half-empty box of remaining blank labels, and the charcoal stub to the south end of the greenhouse where Sprout was now hand-feeding a small pot of something Caleb could not see clearly from the aisle.

"*Done, Professor. — *Forty-one species or specimen-groups across seven tables. — *Recovered seven mis-shelved Mandrake seedlings. — *Found Hibblethwaite''s missing six Bouncing Bulbs along the back of table one. — *Two fanged geraniums noted as unusually active. — *Everything else within expected range.*"

He held the ledger out.

Sprout took it. Wiped her hands on her apron. Flipped through the morning''s entries. Read the marginalia. The proper Hufflepuff Herbology-mistress reading-pace.

"*Mr Waddell. — *Clean work. — *Labels readable. — *Marginalia useful — *I will check the fanged geraniums after lunch.* — *Three hours, properly used. — *Three sickles.*"

She reached into a small leather pouch at her belt and counted out three silver sickles. Handed them across.

"*Same time next Sunday. — *We will do the second three-hour pass on tables eight through fourteen — *the carnivorous-and-defensive corner. — *I will teach you to handle a Venomous Tentacula seedling properly. — *That one I will be standing very close for.*"

A beat. The proper Sprout-warm register on.

"*And — *Mr Waddell — *bring the Bouncing Bulb syrup. — *I want to taste it.*"

"Athena, aconite is what you used to turn Arachne into a spider, wherever you are." *I go to the office to meet Mei*

## Narrator

OOC: Two small things before I run. (1) The Arachne myth — Athena turned Arachne into a spider as punishment for winning a weaving contest, but the standard versions of the myth don't involve aconite. The aconite-as-spider story is closer to *Hecate transforming Arachne with the juice of aconite*, or in some Ovidian readings, Athena sprinkled Arachne with the juice of *Hecate's herb* — which is sometimes glossed as aconite. So Caleb's quip works as the kind of half-remembered classical-mythology joke a Hogwarts seventh-year would make, gestured at his owl named for the goddess. I'll let it stand as the joke it is — Caleb being clever-out-loud to a possibly-not-present owl — and not have any NPC fact-check him. (2) Mei is in the office; he's heading there now, not to lunch yet.

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 11:58

He left the greenhouse with three new sickles in his pocket. The September morning had warmed properly — the air dry, the sun up over the eastern slopes, the lake holding its mid-day blue. The walk back was clean.

Halfway up the rise toward the courtyard doors he registered the small Pygmy-flight signature high above and to his right — Athena, properly on a corridor that ran from the Forbidden Forest line across the grounds toward the south wall of the castle. She had presumably gone hunting overnight and was making her own way back to wherever she would settle this afternoon.

He held the small look up. Did not, however, call to her aloud — she was a quarter mile off, hunting altitude, and she did not need the noise. Held the thought instead.

*Aconite. — Wherever you are.*

The small dark shape banked once at distance — possibly a coincidence, possibly the bonded-recognition twitch — and continued its line across the grounds.

He went into the castle. Up the seven floors. Along the seventh-floor corridor, past the Barnabas tapestry, around the corner to Solomon at the foot of the south-west turret stair.

Solomon raised his amber-yellow eye.

"*Hello, Sir. — *Miss is in.*"

The portrait swung outward on its hinge. The short stone passage. The small wooden door.

Caleb pushed it open.

The office at noon held the proper warm late-morning light. The window had shifted again — no longer the Aberdeen harbour at dawn from earlier, now a small high-altitude meadow scene at full midday, the kind of place Caleb did not recognise but which had alpine grass and a slow-moving brook and a small distant herd of cattle. The Room had moved on to whatever it had decided was the noon view.

Mei was at the working desk. *On Bound Cognition* was open in front of her, about a quarter of the way through, with a small stack of notes beside it written in her clean Mei-hand. She had her glasses on and her cardigan off, on the back of her chair, and her plait had been tucked behind one shoulder so it would not get in the way of her writing. Her teacup — provided by the Room at some point in the morning, presumably — sat half-full at her elbow.

She looked up at the sound of the door.

The composed Mei-precision held for the beat it took her to register that it was him and that it was noon. Then the half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came on, soft warm.

"*Caleb. — *Hello. — *How were your three hours of dirt.*"

"Mandrakey. tell me about your reading"

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 12:02

Mei made the small *snrk* at *Mandrakey* into her teacup. Set the cup down. Composed.

"*Caleb. — *Three hours of dirt summarised in one adjective. — *Mandrakey.* — *I will use that.*"

She turned the book a fraction so he could see where she was. Tapped the page she had been on.

"*Reading. — *I have done — *roughly the first quarter. — *Chapter one was Helena''s introduction — *her framing of the project, which is *we have a bond, and the academic literature does not contain it, so we are going to map it ourselves.* — *Brisk, clear, surprisingly modern in its method. — *Chapter two is — *the phenomenology. — *What the bond feels like from the inside. — *Subjective accounts from her, with Brigham''s corroborations as footnotes. — *It''s — *very vulnerable in places. — *She is — *describing what it is like to share a working mind with another person and the proper attention you pay when you can feel them attending.*"

A breath. She turned another page back.

"*One bit I marked for you — *here. — *On the bond at distance.* — *Helena writes — *I will paraphrase — *that the at-distance phenomenon is — *what she calls — *the smallest, most reliable thing the bond does. — *That when she and Brigham are in different rooms in the same house, she can — *not read his thoughts, that''s not the bond — *but she can feel — *the shape of his attention. — *Whether he is focused or drifting, whether he is in pain, whether he is calling for her or just thinking about her.* — *And — *Caleb. — *The way she describes that shape — *is *exactly* what you and I have been experiencing since Wednesday on the train.*"

She did not, however, immediately move on. The composed Mei-precision held with the soft warm under it.

"*Caleb. — *I have spent the morning learning that — *what we have is — *real, documented, mapped by people who did this seventy years before us. — *Which is — *both reassuring and — *strange.* — *Reassuring because — *we are not alone in it. — *Strange because — *Helena Brigham died in 1894, and the bond she is describing on these pages is — *the one in my chest right now.*"

She picked her teacup back up. Took a sip. Set it down.

"*And — *Caleb — *your turn. — *Mandrakey is — *the summary. — *Tell me the longer version.*"

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 12:04

Mei's face did the small *oh* register at the tuberculosis comparison. Set her teacup down. The composed Mei-precision held — but with the soft *Caleb has just framed my morning differently in one sentence and the framing is correct* register on.

"*Caleb. — *That is — *the right comparison.* — *Helena''s book is — *a case study. — *And I am — *the patient reading the case study about my own condition.* — *Which — *is useful, and is — *not romantic.* — *I will — *adjust.*"

A breath. She picked the quill back up. Did not, however, immediately write.

"*And — *Caleb — *Mandrakey, two seedlings, mint and moondew, inventory done.* — *That is — *a clean morning of dirt.* — *Three sickles?*"

She glanced at the small pile of his return.

The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came on, dry.

"*Caleb. — *You earned three sickles digging in the dirt on a Sunday morning while I sat in a chair reading a Victorian charms monograph. — *That is — *the most working-class boyfriend energy I have received from you all term.*"

She set the quill down properly. Closed *On Bound Cognition.* Marked her place with a folded slip of paper.

"*Lunch. — *Common room or Hall?*"

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 12:05

Mei stilled for a beat.

Then her face did the proper *oh, Caleb* fold — the half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came on full, and her eyes did the small *you absolute* register that meant the joke had landed harder than he'd meant it to.

"*Caleb.*" Quiet. "*Tuberculosis was — *the lung-comparison setup. — *And mine is — *the heart-comparison payoff. — *Because — *that is where the bond lives in my chest. — *Caleb. — *I missed the joke when you delivered it and you had to explain it to me, and the explanation has made it — *significantly worse, and significantly better.*"

A breath. She did not, however, immediately move from her chair.

"*Caleb. — *You — *made a tuberculosis joke about my magical bond with you and the structure is — *medically sound. — *Two different organs, two different case studies, both real, both felt in the chest. — *I am — *not going to stop thinking about that for the rest of the afternoon.*"

She picked her cardigan off the back of the chair. Slipped it on. Composed.

"*Caleb. — *Lunch. — *Common room. — *I want to be — *not in the office for an hour, and not in the Hall either where forks get pointed.* — *Common room is — *fine. — *We can sit at the west-window table and have whatever the elves are running up for late risers.*"

She slid *On Bound Cognition* onto the bookshelf beside the EB notebook 12 and *Companion Owls.* Picked up her satchel.

"*Caleb. — *Move.*"

He took two steps toward her. Brought his right hand up flat against the outside curve of her left breast through her cardigan and blouse. Held it there. Did not move it. Did not press. Just the open palm, claimed.

Counted to ten in his head.

Mei did not, immediately, react. The composed Mei-prefect register held — but her eyebrow went up half a hair and her free hand at her satchel-strap tightened by a fraction.

"*Caleb. — *You are — *touching my boob for ten seconds in the office at noon while I am holding my satchel because you announced you were going to lunch.* — *Caleb. — *This is — *the most absurd transition you have ever attempted.*"

She did not, however, move his hand.

At ten he took it off. Stepped back. Picked up his own satchel.

"*Lunch.*"

He opened the wooden door for her. The proper *I have done the thing I came to do, we are now moving* register.

Mei walked through. The composed Mei-precision held, but her shoulders were doing the small *I am not going to laugh in the corridor* shake.

Out through the stone passage. Solomon at his rug raised one amber-yellow eye as they passed.

"*Good afternoon, Sir. — *Good afternoon, Miss.*"

The portrait swung shut behind them. The wall reformed.

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 12:18

Down through the fourth, third, second floors. Past a small clutch of Hufflepuff third-years coming up the other way — Pippa Greengrass-Talbot was among them, brown curls bobbing, and she gave Caleb the small *oh, hello* wave from the railing that meant *I have not forgotten about meeting Athena yesterday and I am looking forward to the sketch session.* Caleb gave her the small *yes, hello* nod back. Pippa kept going up.

To the eagle door. Mei answered the riddle on the first try — *what gets wetter the more it dries* — *a towel* — and they went through.

The Ravenclaw common room at quarter-past noon Sunday was its lunch-quiet self. About fifteen students in residence — the elves had laid out lunch on the long sideboard at the south end of the room: sandwiches in three varieties (cheese-and-pickle, roast-beef, egg-and-cress), a tureen of mushroom soup, a bowl of apples, a small platter of sausage rolls, a jug of pumpkin juice and a kettle of tea. The proper Sunday-lunch spread.

Lin was not there — still at Quidditch, presumably. Davina was at the prefect-table-end working through a sandwich with a book open. A few seventh-years scattered. The west-window table was empty.

Caleb served himself two egg-and-cress and a sausage roll and a cup of tea. Mei took a cheese-and-pickle and a bowl of soup. They carried plates to the west-window table.

Sat.

The west window held its proper midday lake-view — September sun on the water, the Forbidden Forest beyond, the small distant shape of a flock of crows working a field near the south paddock. Caleb registered the view, did not look long at it, started on his sandwich.

The common room held its low Sunday hum.
