Davina set her sandwich down. The composed Davina-prefect register held for a long beat — the kind that meant she was registering the words but not yet committing to a response.

"*Chang.*" Quiet. "*Corporeal Patronus. — *On his second attempt. — *That is —*"

Caleb pulled his wand from his sleeve-pocket. Held it across the prefect-table at the proper angle — *not* at Davina, *not* at Mei, but at the empty space above the prefect-table — the small Caleb-precision of *I am about to produce something for the table to see.*

Held the memory. The same one. The Lumos puzzle. The two wands. The pale blue. The Charm-Master's Reserve cabinet opening. Brigham's vellum. *To the pair whom the diadem has finally seen.*

"*Expecto Patronum.*"

The silver came on at his wand-tip. Bright. The gathering. The small fierce Pygmy-Owl coalesced about three feet above the table — wings folded, round body, the yellow-eye absence-of-silver, the proper Athena-flight-signature posture. Held for perhaps four seconds, looked once around at Davina, then performed a small slow circuit of the prefect-table at table-height — past Davina's tea, past Mei's soup, past Caleb's plate — and returned to his wand-tip. Faded.

The common room had gone *very* quiet.

Caleb registered, peripherally, that *several* heads had turned. The seventh-years on the south sofa. Two fourth-years at the central table. A small clutch of fifth-years at the alcove. Bea Sutton standing near the sideboard with a bowl in her hand had stopped mid-step. The proper *what was that silver thing* register on perhaps eight faces.

Davina's face did not move for a full beat.

Then the composed Davina-prefect register came back on — but with the small *I have just been delivered a piece of information at my own prefect-table that I am going to have to think about for several days* shape under it.

"*Waddell.*"

Quiet. Very quiet.

"*You — *cast a corporeal Patronus — *in the Ravenclaw common room at Sunday lunch — *at the prefect-table — *in front of approximately fifteen students. — *And the form is — *an owl.*"

A breath. She did not, however, immediately move further. Her sandwich sat untouched in front of her.

"*Waddell. — *That is — *a graduate-level charm. — *In your second year of NEWT. — *And — *you have just demonstrated it casually in your house common room over lunch.*"

The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth did not come on. The composed Davina-prefect register held entirely.

Mei beside Caleb had gone still. The composed Mei-precision had come back on properly — and with the small *I just said the line that produced that* register on her face.

"*Caleb.*" Quiet. "*I — *said it. — *Out loud. — *At the prefect-table.* — *And — *you cast it. — *In front of the room.* — *That was — *both of us breaking a privacy we had agreed to keep, in one minute.*"

OOC: Committing the disclosure immediately.

Disclosure locked. The Patronus form is now public Ravenclaw knowledge and will spread across houses over the next 1-2 days.

She did not, however, take it back. Picked up her sandwich instead. Took a bite of it.

Caleb ate. The sausage roll was proper Hogwarts pastry — flaky, hot, the sausage seasoned with sage and pepper the way it always was. He chewed.

Around them the common room had mostly resumed its lunch hum, though Bea Sutton was visibly still telling the seventh-year on the south sofa about the silver owl, and a fourth-year coming in from the dorm stair had just stopped at the eagle-door threshold to ask the boy nearest her *what did I miss* in a stage whisper.

"*Caleb. — *XO sauce is — *a Cantonese condiment.* — *Origin: — *Hong Kong, late nineteen-sixties, named after the brandy because the chefs who invented it wanted to signal it was *premium* — *but there is no brandy in it.* — *Recipe: — *dried scallops, dried shrimp, Jinhua ham or Chinese cured ham, garlic, shallots, chillies, all chopped fine and slow-cooked in oil for hours until everything gives up its flavour and you have — *a chunky red-brown oil-and-bits paste that tastes like the sea concentrated.* — *Caleb. — *It is — *the most umami substance on the planet. — *Half a teaspoon transforms anything you put it on.*"

A breath.

"*Mum''s recipe is — *her mother''s, from before they left Hong Kong in nineteen-forty-six. — *She makes a small jar at Lunar New Year and refills as we run out. — *She uses the dried scallops Dad just brought back from Causeway Bay in August. — *Three weeks of slow work.* — *Davina has been getting a small jar a term since fifth year. — *It is — *the bribe currency of our friendship.*"

Mei did not, immediately, react. Her sleeve was still pressed against her mouth from the shaking.

Then her free hand came under the table, found Caleb's hand on her thigh, and very precisely lifted it off and set it back down on the bench between them.

She turned her head a small fraction toward him. The composed Mei-precision was on under the warm.

"*Caleb. — *Hand off the thigh at the prefect-table. — *Davina has already absorbed three gweilo-tier sentences from you in seven minutes. — *Adding a thigh-squeeze on the bench is — *not the move.*"

Davina across the table picked her tea up. Took a sip. Did not, however, comment. The proper *I am pretending I did not see the hand-redirection any more than I am pretending I did not see the boob-line earlier* register held.

A small breath. Davina set the tea down.

She picked up *Magical Theory: Advanced.* Tucked it under her arm. Stood.

"*Lunch — *enjoyed. — *Mostly.* — *Carry on.*"

She crossed to the prefect-stair and went up.

The west end of the prefect-table held its small post-Davina quiet. Mei set her sleeve down. Composed properly. Looked at Caleb.

The leopard cat coalesced about three feet in front of Mei — compact, lean, long curving tail, head low in the hunting posture.

Then they noticed each other.

The Pygmy Owl tilted its head — the small precise *I see you* gesture. The leopard cat went still, its head lifting from the hunting-low posture to a watchful upright. They considered each other for a beat.

Then the Pygmy Owl banked a small slow circle around the leopard cat at chest-height — proper *I am a small bird flying above a small wild cat* register — and the leopard cat watched it the whole way. The owl returned to hover beside the cat. The cat shifted its weight onto its front paws and rubbed its head once against the owl''s breast in the way a small wild cat marks something it has decided is its own.

The owl settled.

They held the configuration for another two seconds — bonded pair, in silver, side by side in the air of a Charms-Master's office on a Sunday afternoon.

Then both faded.

The room held very quiet.

Flitwick had not moved. The composed Charms-Master register was — entirely off-duty. His eyes were on the empty air where the two Patroni had been. After a long beat:

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 15:56

They climbed the rest of the way up. Fifth floor. Mei led him along the corridor toward the west wing where Magister Brigham's working room sat behind sconce #1.

The corridor was empty at this hour — Sunday afternoon, most of the castle out on the grounds or down at the lake while the September sun held. The proper hush of a stone corridor with no traffic.

At sconce #1 — the small brass wall sconce that did not appear in the seventeen-sconce common-room catalogue, because this one was elsewhere — Mei stopped. The door appeared on the wall opposite at its proper response to their joint approach: pale-grey oak, the small brass plaque reading *BRIGHAM* in unfussy capitals.

Solomon was painted on the small panel beside the door — a long-haired tortoiseshell cat in a gold frame, currently asleep with one paw over his eye. He cracked the eye open at their approach. The proper *yes, both of you, in you come* yellow-eyed acknowledgement.

Mei pushed the door open.

The office held its usual proper warm. The west window — currently showing the late-afternoon Manchester rooftops of Cheetham Hill, which had been the view since this morning when Caleb had set it — caught the light from a sun about three hours past its zenith. The shelves with the EB notebooks, the Brigham personal notebook, Helena's *On Bound Cognition.* The Cogitatio cupboard. The two armchairs by the fire. The proper writing-desk by the west window where Caleb had drafted the Cassia recipes letter yesterday. Helena and Brigham's portrait above the mantel, both presently looking at their visitors with the small *yes, welcome back* register.

The room smelled of old paper and the faint herbal of Brigham's working tobacco from a century ago that the wards still held in low concentration in the corners.

Mei went to the bookshelf. Took down *On Bound Cognition.* Crossed to the armchair on the left of the fire. Sat properly, the small *I am settling in to read* posture, tucked her feet up under her.

Caleb crossed to the writing-desk by the west window. Set his satchel down on the floor beside the chair. Took out his quill, a fresh sheet of parchment, an inkwell from the desk drawer. Set them out.

Sat down.

Looked at the blank parchment.

The Cure for Boils proposal. Three ingredients. Pure ethanol as solvent. Control protocol. Slughorn deadline Friday.

He uncapped the inkwell.

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 16:09

He dipped the quill. Wrote at the top of the parchment, in his proper Caleb hand:

*Proposal: Ethanol-Solvent Cure for Boils — Hypothesis Test*
*Caleb Waddell, NEWT Potions, 4 September 1977*

Underneath: *Hypothesis.*

Stopped. Sat with the quill above the page.

The hypothesis was — *can pure ethanol carry the binding of a three-ingredient potion in place of water.* He had said it to Slughorn on Friday. Saying it on parchment for a NEWT proposal needed — different shape. Wider. More formal. The proper academic register.

He wrote: *Standard potion brewing uses water as the universal solvent. This proposal asks whether a controlled alternative — pure ethanol — can carry the same binding work, using Cure for Boils as a simple three-ingredient test case.*

Set the quill down. Read it back. Frowned at the *universal solvent* phrase — too strong; water was not literally universal, just default. Crossed it out. Replaced with *unmarked default.* Read it back again. Better.

Mei across the room turned a page of *On Bound Cognition* with the soft paper sound. Did not, however, look up.

The fire did its small *crack.* Solomon outside the door slept on his frame. The late-afternoon Manchester light through the west window came in the proper diagonal warm across the writing-desk.

Caleb dipped the quill again. *Methodology.*

## User

.

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 16:11

He dipped the quill again. Wrote:

*Methodology. The same potion will be brewed twice in parallel under controlled conditions: once with water as solvent (control), once with pure ethanol (test). Both brews to use identical quantities, identical heat protocol, identical stirring patterns, identical timing. The only variable is the solvent.*

Set the quill down. Read it. The phrase *identical* four times in two sentences was — bad style — but it was, structurally, what the methodology *was.* The whole point of a control was that every variable except the one being tested was held identical. He let it stand.

Underneath: *Ingredients. Dried nettles (six leaves), crushed snake fangs (four), horned slugs (two).* He had brewed Cure for Boils twice in second year and could recite the ingredients in his sleep. The doses were the standard textbook quantities; he wrote them down without checking.

The clock on the office mantelpiece — a small mahogany thing of Brigham's, ticking away at the proper Charms-craftsman rate — read 16:14. Caleb had not registered the clock before today. He registered it now. It was tuned a half-second slow per minute against his own pocket watch — the proper old-instrument register. He noted the discrepancy mentally and went back to the page.

Underneath: *Equipment.*

Across the room Mei turned another page of *On Bound Cognition.* The fire did its small *crack.*

## User

.

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 16:14

*Equipment. Two pewter cauldrons, size 2, matched volume. Two brass scales for ingredient weighing. Two glass stirring rods. Two timekeepers calibrated to the same reference (Charms-classroom standard).*

He paused. Looked at the *Charms-classroom standard* phrase. Crossed it out — Slughorn would want *Potions-classroom standard* — corrected.

Underneath: *Solvent preparation.* Sat with the quill.

The water was easy: tap from the dungeons sink, filtered through Slughorn's standard charcoal filter, the way every cauldron in the castle was filled. The ethanol was the question. Pure ethanol — laboratory-grade, no congeners — was not something the school stockroom carried in bulk. He would need to either Vanishing-extract it from a high-proof spirit (Slughorn had mentioned this Friday) or order proper laboratory ethanol from a chemist's supply.

He wrote: *Pure ethanol may be obtained by Vanishing-extraction from a high-proof spirit (95% or greater, undiluted), separating the alcohol from congeners and water by selective Evanesco on the residual components. Alternative: laboratory-grade ethanol from a Muggle chemist''s supply.*

The Muggle chemist's-supply option was — interesting to him personally — but Slughorn would probably prefer the Vanishing-extraction version, on grounds of *we are wizards, we extract our own solvents.* He let both stand for now; the proposal could choose later.

The mantel clock — half a second slow per minute against his pocket watch — registered 16:18. Caleb dipped the quill again.

*Controls and observation protocol.*

## User

.

## Narrator

▼ Sunday, 4 September 1977 — 16:18

*Controls and observation protocol. Both cauldrons brewed simultaneously, on adjacent burners, by the same brewer. Witness present (Slughorn, Mei Chang, or Cassia Vance) to verify identical procedure. Observations at each stage recorded in writing at the moment of observation, not retrospectively: colour, consistency, surface activity, scent. Both cauldrons to be brought to completion regardless of outcome — even a failed test brew is data.*

He paused. The *witness present* line — Slughorn had named Mei, Cassia, or Slughorn himself. He had written *Mei Chang* on the parchment in proper formal academic register. The proper *I am working with my girlfriend at the level of academic record-keeping* shape. Looked across the room at her in the armchair properly for the first time in twenty minutes.

She was — head down over *On Bound Cognition,* the lamp on the small table beside the armchair lit against the late-afternoon dimming, her finger tracing a line on the page she was reading. Concentrating. Did not register him looking.

He went back to the page.

*Endpoint criteria.* Underneath: *The test brew may produce any of four outcomes. (1) Identical to control — ethanol carries the binding fully. (2) Failed brew — ethanol does not carry the binding. (3) Partial brew — some binding work succeeded, some failed (which suggests ethanol carries some kinds of magical bind but not others). (4) Unexpected outcome — the brew produces something other than Cure for Boils (which suggests ethanol introduces its own properties to the work and is not, in fact, a neutral substitute).*

Set the quill down. Read the whole sheet back. It was — a real proposal. The structure was sound. Slughorn would want more on the *what does "identical" mean operationally* question — but that was a Tuesday-evening-with-Mei refinement, not a Sunday-afternoon problem.

The mantel clock read 16:23.

*Risk Assessment. The principal risks of brewing with ethanol as solvent are (1) flammability of ethanol at brewing temperatures, (2) displacement of breathable air by ethanol vapour in confined work-stations, (3) unknown interaction of ethanol with the standard Cure for Boils ingredients given that the existing canon of brewing literature assumes water. Mitigation: brew at the workbench nearest the dungeon air-shaft; adult witness (Slughorn) present for ignition and throughout the active phase; small-volume initial brew (single-dose) to limit ethanol present at any moment; standard fire-suppression charm (Aguamenti) ready at the witness''s wand.*

He read it back. Nodded to himself. Underneath: *Timeline and Resources.*

*Phase one: ingredient sourcing and ethanol preparation. One week, sourcing standard ingredients from the school stockroom (no special order required) and obtaining laboratory-grade ethanol from a Muggle chemist''s supply via Hogsmeade weekend visit. Phase two: matched-conditions test brew. One brewing session, both cauldrons in parallel, fully witnessed. Phase three: solvent-adjusted test brew. If matched-conditions produces outcome 2 (failed) or outcome 5 (apparent failure), repeat with adjusted heat protocol per Methodology revision. Phase four: write-up. Two to three weeks per round.*

Joanna held the eye contact for a beat.

Then — properly, deliberately — she lifted her hand. Made a slow, precise gesture: thumb and forefinger forming a small circle, the other three fingers raised. Tilted her head. The proper Hufflepuff-prefect *I see you, and the answer is no* register, dry enough to land across eight tables of Sunday-evening Great Hall.

Plan was Wednesday assuming no Wilfred reply by Tuesday morning. Compressing Monday and Tuesday now.

▼ Monday, 5 September 1977 — 08:00

The morning compressed itself.

They left the Bone Zone at quarter to eight — Mei stopped at the writing-desk on the way out, picked up the framed photograph of Mei Ling, held it for a long beat, set it back down, the proper *I am leaving her here in the room that held us tonight* posture. The door dissolved behind them.

Down to the dormitories on the fifth and seventh floors to change into proper Monday robes. Breakfast at the Hall — Lin saved them seats again, the proper *I am not going to ask where you spent the night* register on Lin's face, dry. Cass beside Marcus across, the same composed non-comment. Mei finished her Hexfield notebook entry over toast: rules clean-copied with the *Patronus opening — legal* clause and Caleb's refining notes (auto-respawn timers, medic short-timer, revisit 30-sec flag-hold).

Caleb sent off the parson letter and the Cassia-Vance recipes sheet via school owl post before nine — Athena, fresh from the Owlery, agreed to carry both. The Reading-Evening-Post-subscription cover-letter went out separately to the *Reading Evening Post*''s Friar Street offices in Esme''s hand with Caleb''s name in the *postal subscription payment to be delivered in person Wednesday* line. A small *acknowledged* owl came back at lunchtime from the Post confirming the subscription would be activated upon payment.

Classes ran. Potions with Slughorn at ten — Caleb handed in his Cure for Boils proposal at the front of the classroom; Slughorn took it, set it on top of the small stack of NEWT-project submissions on his desk, said the brief *I will read it this evening, Mr Waddell* register. Cass passed Caleb a small folded note halfway through the brewing exercise that read *ethanol-extraction is a real method, not just Caleb-original; my dad does it for laboratory work in his apothecary in Edinburgh; ask me at dinner.* Caleb pocketed the note properly. Charms in the afternoon with Flitwick — the proper Monday class, no special bond-recognition follow-up but Flitwick gave Caleb the small *yes, see you Wednesday at four* nod at the end of the lesson.

Mei saw Davina at the standing prefect-table-end at dinner and gave her the proper *the body-thing conversation happened with Caleb last night and he handled it* short brief — Davina did not ask for content, just gave Mei a shoulder-press and the *I am glad* warm. Davina would get the longer version at the Tuesday standing tea.

Lin proper-sat with Caleb in the dorm common room at nine and asked, quiet, if Caleb was all right. The proper *I am your friend, you spent the night somewhere not your bed, I am checking* register. Caleb said yes, he was. Lin did not push. They played a game of Exploding Snap and went to bed at eleven.

▼ Tuesday, 6 September 1977 — 16:00

Davina's room. The standing tea. Mei brought one jar of XO sauce (in addition to Davina's standing-tea jar). Caleb on the small armchair beside the writing-desk for the proper Caleb-shoulder-massage register — five minutes, brief, professional, no commentary, the proper *I am here as the boyfriend who is part of the standing tea now* shape. Davina got the proper full account of Sunday: the patronus disclosure, Flitwick's two papers, Mei Ling, the photograph the Room produced, the brûléed egg-tart plan for the 18th. Davina listened without interruption. At the end: *Caleb. I want to be there on the 18th — not in the room, just in Manchester. Standing offer.* Caleb accepted.

Mei stayed at Davina''s through to dinner. Caleb left at 17:00 for the office to work on his Folly proposal refinements with Mei joining him at 18:00.

No Wilfred reply came at breakfast Tuesday or by Tuesday dinner. Esme caught Caleb in the corridor after Potions Tuesday — the proper *nothing yet; my parents wrote that Wilfred has not been to dinner for two Sundays but they have not asked him why* update.

The Reading plan was on.

Wednesday morning.

▼ Wednesday, 7 September 1977 — 07:30

Caleb at the Hall for early breakfast. Henshaw at the head table — Caleb caught his eye, the proper *prearranged medical-leave register* gesture. Henshaw nodded once, came down at the small post-breakfast pause, handed Caleb a small folded note that read: *Mr Waddell will be in the infirmary today with a minor potions-fume reaction sustained during private brewing-research practice. Madame Pomfrey has not been informed; the note is for the staff record only. — H.* Caleb pocketed it.

Esme at the Ravenclaw table, robes pressed properly, the proper *I am ready and trying to look like I am not* register. Caleb sat beside her briefly. Confirmed: Floo from the third-floor public hearth at 09:30. Tilbury''s bakery in Friar Street, Reading. Caleb drops the Reading Evening Post payment at the offices around the corner first (cover), Esme goes to the bakery door. Wilfred at the counter, hopefully.

Athena at the Owlery, fed. Caleb''s wand in his sleeve. The Wiggenweld phial in the inner robe pocket. A small leather purse with the Reading Post subscription payment — three Galleons converted into Muggle pounds at Gringotts last week via the small front-office exchange Slughorn had pointed Caleb to in fifth year.

He met Esme outside the Hall at quarter past nine.

"*Esme. — *Ready.*"

Esme looked at him properly. The Chelsea bun still in her hand.

"Wiggenweld?"

"Caleb, Wiggenweld is for physical wounds — cuts, broken skin, the sort of thing you'd use on a bruise to bring colour back. It's not — a mind-thing. It won't help Tom with whatever he's seeing in his head. It might help Wilfie's cheekbone heal faster, which is something, but it won't solve the actual problem."

A small breath.

"Also, if I dose a Muggle with a wizarding potion without explaining what it is, that's — Statute issues. Not the worst Statute issues anyone has ever had, but the kind of thing where Mum would have a word with me at Christmas. And — Tom doesn't know wizards exist. Wilfie does, because I'm his sister and he's a Squib, but Tom — to my knowledge — does not."

She looked at the bakery's upper-floor curtains across the road.

"What we have for mind-things is — Calming Draught, which I do not have. Dreamless Sleep, which I do not have. And neither would actually fix what's happening in his head — they'd just put him to sleep through it. The thing he needs is — a Muggle psychiatric ward, and probably medication, and rest, and probably weeks of it."

A breath. She looked back at Caleb.

"What Wiggenweld could do is — help Wilfie. If we can get up to him, we can heal the bruise. That's a small thing, but it would mean — when he writes to Mum and Dad next, he doesn't have a visible mark Tom gave him to hide. Which would give him space to think clearer about — what to do next. Without — the daily reminder."

"so.. what do we do about Tom? do we call for a visit? a surprise visit? or you go tell Tom you are doing it?"

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 7 September 1977 — 09:49

Esme thought about it properly. Held the Chelsea bun in her hand, looking at the bakery across the way.

"There are three real options, the way I see it.

"One. We go up to the flat right now. You wait in the bakery; I go up the side stairs and knock. I'm his sister. Tom would either let me in with Wilfie, or refuse. If he refuses, we know more than we do now. If he lets me in, I see Tom with my own eyes, I see Wilfie in his own home, and I decide from there.

"Two. We call a Muggle psychiatric ambulance from the public phone at the Forbury. We give them Caroline's number — she said she'd be a witness — and they come to the flat without Wilfie having to call. Tom gets sectioned, probably, against his will. Which is — the cleanest medical outcome, and the worst outcome for Tom's trust in Wilfie, which Tom might never forgive. And Wilfie might never forgive me for setting it in motion behind his back.

"Three. We go home empty-handed today. I write to Mum and Dad tonight. I tell them everything I just learned from Caroline. They come down from Bristol on the weekend, they go up to Wilfie's flat as his parents, and they handle Tom from there — either talking him into seeing somebody, or calling someone themselves. Adults handling adult things. I am eighteen, and this is — possibly bigger than I should be handling on a Wednesday morning."

A breath.

"Option three is the safest. Option one is the most direct. Option two is the most decisive, and the most likely to break things between Tom and Wilfie that won't unbreak."

She looked at Caleb.

"I think — I think I want option three. I want Mum. I want Dad. Tom hitting Wilfie is the kind of thing that needs Dad, not me. And the Aldermaston piece — if it's real — definitely needs adults. Not us."

"Yes. That's good. We stay. We watch. We have until — Henshaw's medical-leave note covers us until evening curfew, so we've got the full day. If we sit at different cafes on Friar Street through the morning, walk the side streets in the afternoon, switch positions a couple of times — we get a real read on whether anyone's watching the flat."

A breath.

"And if anyone is watching, we'd want to know before Mum and Dad walk in there at the weekend. If Tom is having an ordinary psychotic break, the parents go to Reading and handle it. If Tom is being watched by — whoever — because of Aldermaston, the parents going to Reading on the weekend is exactly the wrong move, and I need to know that before I write the letter tonight."

She looked at Caleb.

"You take the south end of Friar Street, I take the north end. We swap at noon. We meet at the Owl & Ledger for a sandwich at half twelve. Compare notes. Then afternoon, we sit at the Forbury and the church-square — anyone moving regularly between those points and the bakery would have to cross our sightlines. Floo back at — say five, in time to be at school for dinner."

A small breath.

"And I eat this bun now because I am suddenly very hungry."

She bit into the Chelsea bun properly. The proper *I have just decided and the calories are now permitted* shape.

▼ Wednesday, 7 September 1977 — 09:54

Esme made a small *snrk* into the last bite of her bun.

"Consensual physical intimacy partner. Caleb. That's a worse cover than just-friends-having-coffee. But fine."

She finished the bun. Brushed crumbs off her fingers. Took his hand properly — the proper *we are doing the couple-cover thing again because we have all day to sit in cafes and watch a bakery* shape.

They walked back up Friar Street. Past the bakery on the opposite side — Esme did not look at the upper windows directly, the proper *I am holding the discipline* shape. Down to the south end of Friar Street, where the road met the high street. On the corner, a small cafe — *Edie's Tea Rooms* in proper green-painted lettering on a white sash window, two small round tables on the pavement out front, the proper Wednesday-morning steam of a kettle visible through the glass.

Caleb pushed the door open. Small bell. The cafe inside was — proper Reading 1977, six tables on a chequered tile floor, a counter at the back, a small kitchen behind it, the smell of bacon-and-egg cooking and proper builder's tea brewing. Three customers in already — a builder at the counter with a mug, a woman in a navy coat reading a Mills & Boon at a window table, an older man in tweed at a back table with a folded *Times.*

Edie herself at the counter — sixties, white apron over flowered dress, the proper *Edie has run this cafe since 1962* shape.

"Morning, loves. Sit anywhere."

Caleb led Esme to the table by the front window — proper sightline to Friar Street running north, past the bakery shopfront and the upper-floor windows of the flat visible at a slight angle. The kind of table where you could see who came and went past the bakery without obviously watching it.

He pulled out the chair for Esme. The proper *cover-boyfriend* shape. She sat. Caleb sat opposite — meaning Esme had the proper Friar-Street view across his shoulder.

Esme reached across the table. Put her hand over his. The proper *we are a couple having coffee and tea* shape on her face for any passing eye.

"Two teas, please, Edie. And — what's the morning bun?"

Edie called back from the counter: "Iced bun or hot cross. The hot cross is still warm from the morning batch."

"Two hot cross, please."

The hot cross buns arrived within two minutes, the tea right after. Esme poured for both — proper milk in first, the proper Reading-Muggle order. Stirred. Sipped.

Across the room, the woman with the Mills & Boon turned a page. The builder finished his mug. The man in tweed had not moved from his paper in five minutes.

Out the window, Friar Street held its proper Wednesday-morning slow. A delivery van passing. Two women with shopping baskets. A boy with a bicycle.

The upper windows of Tilbury's — Esme could see them through Caleb's shoulder at the small angle — held their drawn curtains. Nobody moving at them.

The watch began.

Edie was at the next table over wiping down crumbs. She straightened up at the line.

She gave Esme the proper appraising smile — *young woman whose fiancé has brought her back to the place of their first date,* the kind of detail Edie's cafe ran on.

"Anniversaries are a lovely thing. A year today, is it? Well done, the both of you. I'll bring you two more on the house in a bit — anniversaries get the second round."

She moved off to the counter with the cloth.

Esme waited until Edie's back was turned. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came on, dry warm.

"Caleb. Clarinette is not a name. That is a French clarinet. You have just told Edie my name is the Female Clarinet."

A breath into her tea.

"And we are now getting free buns under false anniversary pretences, which is the kind of small Muggle fraud my mother would actually be cross with me about. Caleb. Why."

She did not, however, sound annoyed. The proper *I am twenty per cent amused and eighty per cent making sure the line is on the record* shape. She squeezed his hand across the table — the cover-couple gesture maintained.

"Also, if Edie comes back and asks how we met, I am taking the lead on the story because I do not trust you with — Edie's emotional investment in our anniversary."

Esme made a small sound at the back of her throat that was half-laugh and half-groan.

"Hot cross with you. Caleb. That is — a worse pun than I would have credited you with at ten in the morning, and I have known you a week."

A breath. The half-millimetre on, dry warm full.

"Clare. Fine. Pet name accepted. Better than Clarinette, which would have me typing my own death warrant every time you said it. And — fair on the tip; if you tip well enough that Edie comes out ahead on the anniversary, it is not fraud, it is — paying for the experience of being treated like a couple in love at ten in the morning. Which — for what it is worth — is — not a bad thing to be paying for."

She stirred her tea once. Looked out the window past Caleb's shoulder properly. Friar Street still slow — a postman on his round now, working the south side, the small canvas sack over his shoulder. A young woman pushing a pram. The delivery van had moved on.

The upper windows of Tilbury's: still curtained, no movement.

"Bacchanalia. Also — for the record — is the wrong word for what you meant to say, which was *Bacchanalian.* Or — more likely — *bacchanal,* which is the noun. Bacchanalia is the *festival of Bacchus,* which is — a real thing in classical Rome — orgies in honour of the wine god. Caleb. You — almost — told Edie our anniversary was at — a Roman orgy."

A small breath.

"Which — actually — would have been the cover story I would have remembered fondly. Edie's face, hearing it. Caleb. Maybe save that one for our second imaginary anniversary."

She took a proper sip of her tea. Glanced at his pocket-watch on the table, the proper *we have all day and watching is patient work* shape.

He turned north up Friar Street.

Toward Tilbury's.

Esme's hand on Caleb's tightened a fraction.

"Caleb. The man in tweed just left. Going north. Watch where he goes."

## Narrator

▼ Wednesday, 7 September 1977 — 17:42

The day compressed itself.

Edie's Tea Rooms till eleven — no movement at Tilbury's windows. Caleb and Esme walked the church-square and sat at the Forbury through the late morning, the small drizzle picking up and easing again. Back to Edie's for lunch — Edie greeted them like returning grandchildren, sat them at the same table, brought soup and bread without taking the order, refused payment for the lunch on the grounds that *anniversary couples don't pay twice in a day, my loves.* Caleb tipped generously into the small saucer by the counter when Edie wasn't looking — the proper *we are not stealing under false pretences* discipline held.

Afternoon on the bench across from Tilbury's. Esme worked through the two-week back-issues of the *Reading Evening Post* methodically, finger down each column: hospital admissions, missing-persons, court news, death notices, bakery accidents, factory injuries. Nothing involving Wilfred or Tom or anyone in their flat or any address on Friar Street. The thirteenth page of the 31st August edition carried a single-paragraph notice about *Atomic Weapons Research Establishment Aldermaston* — a routine staff-changes notice mentioning *three engineers retiring this quarter* with no names. Esme circled it lightly in pencil.

Tilbury's bakery closed at four. Wilfred came down to flip the *OPEN/CLOSED* sign with the proper Wednesday-afternoon weariness, did not look out at the street, went back inside. The lights on the ground floor went off ten minutes later. Lights came on at the second-floor flat window briefly — visible at the small seam where the curtain did not quite meet the frame — and went off again.

Five o'clock. Caleb and Esme walked back to the Owl & Ledger. The papers under Esme's arm. Mr Tobias Bryant still at his bar with a different newspaper now, not the *Daily Prophet* but a Muggle *Times.* Did not look up. Two sickles into the grate-jar; the green-flame Floo from the back room.

Back at Hogwarts. Out of Muggle clothes into proper robes from the alcove behind the suit of armour. Down the corridors to the Great Hall for the late edge of dinner.

Mei at the Ravenclaw table — saved seats, the proper *you have been gone all day and I have been keeping my plate warm* register. Lin beside her, the small *welcome back* nod. Cass and Marcus across.

Mei looked Caleb properly in the eye as he sat down.

"Caleb. Esme. Report."

Esme, beside Caleb, picked up a slice of bread properly and started buttering it with the deliberate care of someone who has been on her feet in a Reading cafe most of the day and is finally going to eat at a Hogwarts table.

"Hexfield tryouts. Yes. When and how."

Joanna said at the prefect meeting yesterday — quote — *I have had eleven Hufflepuffs ask me about Hexfield this week and I do not have answers.* Marcus got two notes from third-years on Monday. Marcus, quiet: "Saturday afternoon. Two till four. Barnabas tapestry. Room scales."

Caleb thought about it properly. The proper *I am the inventor and the planning is mine* shape.

"Open call. Anyone who shows up gets a cracker. Room makes the pitch big enough for whoever turns up — I think it can do that, it scaled for eight on Sunday. We run short matches, four minutes each, mixed-team scrambles so everyone plays with everyone. No score-keeping for tryouts. Idea is — let people feel the rules, see if they like the geometry, leave if it's not for them. End of the session — anyone still standing and asking *when's next match* gets on the proper roster."

A breath. He looked at Esme.

"You in?"

Esme: "I am Floo-tired and emotionally tired and I'd like to sit it out as a watcher this first round. Sketchbook with Imogen if she's willing. Next one I'll play."

Mei: "Reasonable. We also need — someone to draft the open-call notice for the four common rooms. Imogen probably best — she did the floor-plan, she has Hufflepuff credibility, and the notice from a non-Caleb hand will land more neutrally than from Caleb himself. *Hexfield tryouts, Saturday 10 September, 2pm, seventh-floor corridor opposite the Barnabas tapestry, all years welcome, bring your wand and nothing else.*"

Esme stood for a beat in the entryway and took in the room — proper. The west window showing the Cheetham Hill rooftops at evening, lamps lit in scattered windows across the Manchester slate. The fire low in the grate. The two armchairs. The writing-desk with Caleb's signed Cure for Boils proposal dried and weighted under brass. The bookshelf with the EB notebooks, the Brigham personal notebook, Helena's *On Bound Cognition*. The Cogitatio cupboard. The portrait of Helena and Magister Brigham above the mantel — both present, both composed, the small *welcome, third person ever* register on Helena's painted face.

"Caleb. Mei. This is — properly extraordinary."

A breath. Quiet. Esme set her satchel down beside the armchair on the right of the fire. The proper *I am being a guest who does not want to disturb the place* shape.

"Helena Ravenclaw on bound cognition on a shelf in your private warded room. Brigham's personal notebook beside it. A Manchester evening in your west window because the room remembers your father's shop. Caleb. You — built a research career in this room in eight days."

Caleb settled into the left armchair. Mei took the desk chair, turned it around to face them. Esme sat in the right armchair properly, the proper *I will sit and tell you what happened* posture on her face.

Mei: "Esme. From the beginning. Caleb has been here. I have not. Tell me everything."

Esme breathed in. Started talking.

She gave Mei the Reading day in clean sequence — Owl & Ledger Floo, Forbury walk, Friar Street geography, the Post offices and Mrs Brooks and the back-issue bundle, the bench, the bakery entrance, Caleb's *Tilde* gambit, Caroline at the counter, the *Wilfie* recognition between them, the bruise on Wilfred's cheekbone, *Tom is upstairs resting today*, Caleb's *attractive lady* recovery and the four-buns-and-a-walk that turned the encounter into the around-the-corner conversation, Caroline Pickwell at 47 Friar Street with her front-window view across the bakery, two weeks of psychotic-break symptoms, the broken bedroom window with the chair, the *Saturday-night Tom-hit-Wilfred* punch, the *Tom worked at Aldermaston for four years, quit six weeks ago, told Wilfie he was tired of the commute* sequence, the timeline lining up at six weeks. The three options laid out, the third chosen — letter to her parents in Bristol tonight, parents come down to Reading at the weekend. Caleb's poisoning hypothesis as the medical reframe that gives Tom a different treatment pathway. The day-long watch — Edie's Tea Rooms cover (Strumento family with the anniversary), the church-square, the Forbury, Tilbury's bench, the back-issues with the Aldermaston staff-changes notice (three engineers retiring this quarter, no names) circled in pencil. Mr Atkins in tweed who walked past the bakery without looking. Bakery closing at four. Wilfred flipping the sign without looking at the street. Lights briefly on at the second floor through a curtain-gap. Floo back at five.

Then — at the end — the things Caleb had said in cafes, fast and dry, only the lines worth telling: *consensual physical intimacy partner; the Strumento family with their daughter Piccola; the schoolteacher extra-credit memory; the Italian-instrument naming convention; the daddy line at dinner that Caleb has since clarified is no.*

Esme finished. Looked at Mei.

"That is the day. Wilfred has a bruise on his cheekbone from his partner who is in a psychotic break that may be poisoning-related from a Cold War nuclear facility, and I am writing to my parents tonight. The rest is — Caleb being Caleb in cafes for cover."

Mei sat with that. Did not, immediately, speak.

The fire did its small *crack.* The mantel clock at 18:34.

Then, very quietly:

"Esme. Thank you for telling me all of it. And — for the Caleb-being-Caleb piece — I am — going to laugh about the Strumento family later, because that is the only thing to do with it. The schoolteacher story is staying in this room. The daddy line — Caleb has already handled. I am — most worried about Wilfred and Tom, and we will help you with the letter tonight if you want."

A breath. The half-millimetre on, soft warm under it.

"Tell me how I can help. Now."

"The letter to Mum and Dad tonight is the actual thing. I want to write it here, with both of you, not alone in the dorm. Because — I have written the careful version of this letter to my parents in my head a dozen times since Saturday and every version has been wrong. "If I write it here, you can read each draft. Mei — you are better at proper structured prose than I am. A small breath. The half-millimetre on small now, soft.

"That is — what I want. The letter, here, tonight, with both of you. Then I will be — better tomorrow. Even if Wilfie is not."

Three plates from the small cupboard above the counter. Bacon two rashers each. Scrambled eggs portioned. Mushrooms split. Toast at the side.

Kettle finally hot at the proper boil — Caleb made tea in a small brown pot the Room had also provided, scooped the loose leaves from a tin on the counter. Three cups properly: milk first, two sugars in Mei's, two in Esme's, one in his own. Stirred each.

Loaded everything onto a small wooden tray — three plates, three mugs, three forks, three knives, a small dish of brown sauce because his dad always had brown sauce with bacon, and a small dish of butter for any more that might be wanted on the toast.

Lifted the tray properly. Crossed the cabin to the bed.

Mei was sitting up against the headboard now, hair half-out-of-plait, glasses on. The proper *I am awake and I am being served breakfast in bed* register on her face. Esme had pushed herself up beside her, hair properly mussed, the flannel collar gaping at her throat where she had not done it up — the proper *I have just woken up and I am going to be a guest now* shape.

Caleb set the tray on the foot of the bed. Climbed up properly on his own side, settled cross-legged at the foot. Distributed the plates — Mei's, Esme's, his own. The mugs the same.

Mei looked at her plate properly. Then at Caleb.

"Caleb. You — woke up at six and made bacon and scrambled eggs and mushrooms and toast and tea for the three of us. With your dad's brown sauce on the side. While swearing at hot grease. In a cabin the school built for us last night."

A breath. The half-millimetre on, soft warm full.

"Caleb. This is — the second morning in a row you have made me breakfast in bed in a Room of Requirement variant. Yesterday brûléed egg-tarts, today a full Cheetham Hill English. I am — going to expect this. Caleb. Just so you know."

Esme picked up her fork. The proper *I am being fed by my friend who I love and I am not going to ruin it with comment* register on her face. Cut a piece of bacon. Ate it.

"Caleb. The bacon is — properly rendered. Crisp without being burnt. You — paid attention when your dad taught you. Thank you."

She did not, however, look up. Continued eating with the proper *I am hungry and this is good and I am going to focus on it* shape.

Caleb picked up his own fork. The proper *I have just made breakfast for two people I love and they are eating it in matching leopard-cat pyjamas in a bed in a cabin the school made for us* shape on his face. Ate properly.

The fire was nothing. The candles low. The dawn light grey through the high window, properly Manchester-November-pre-sunrise even though it was September Hogwarts. The corkboard with the family photographs on the wall opposite. Wilfred at sixteen above where Esme was sitting now. Mei Ling with the kite. Caleb's father at the workbench.

Cass crossed to Henshaw's desk with the proper *I am one of his better students with an end-of-class small matter* register. Caleb followed her at a respectful one-step-behind distance.

"Sir. Quick mention before we go. The cohort's running a sport in the Room of Requirement on Saturday afternoon at two. It's something Caleb invented last weekend — we've played it once. Open call to all four houses on the notice we're putting up Friday. Thought you should know."

Henshaw set his quill down. Looked at Cass for a beat. Then at Caleb behind her.

"A sport. Invented by Mr Waddell. Played once. In the Room of Requirement. Open call to four houses on Saturday at two."

A breath. He picked up his coffee mug. Took a sip.

"Mr Waddell. Step up. Explain."

Caleb stepped beside Cass properly.

"It's called Hexfield, sir. Two teams of four. Capture the flag, basically — two flags, one at each end of an inflatable-soft pitch the Room builds. Object is to get the other team's flag to your own base. Legal spells: stinging hex, Rictusempra, Knockback, trip jinx. Cass set the original rule — nothing that breaks skin. Hit by any legal spell, you go down where you are. Revive by a teammate holding 30 seconds of contact, or by the downed player crawling back to their own base to self-reset. The Room scores it. Last Sunday we ran three matches with eight players, fifty minutes total, no injuries."

A beat.

"Teams are assigned by Christmas-cracker draw at the start, sir — first cracker pulled gives a random colour, and once a team has four the rest auto-assign to the other team. Keeps it even. The flags themselves are — magically waving banners. The Room produced them on its own Sunday and we kept them. They're — well, the Room rendered them with a reclining-figure pose. We did not request it; the Room appears to have made a small joke at my expense."

Henshaw's eyebrow went up the proper one millimetre.

"A reclining figure. Of."

A small breath. The proper *I am going to be honest about this* register on Caleb's face.

"Me, sir. The Kate Winslet pose from the Muggle film. Fully clothed — the Room declined to render anything else. Purple banner at one end, gold at the other. Magically waving."

Cass, beside Caleb, the proper composed Cass-cynical *I am going to vouch for this without softening it* register on her face:

"Sir, I can confirm. The flags are functional and the figure on them is fully dressed. Caleb did not ask for the pose. The Room of Requirement has a sense of humour, which most of us were not aware of until Sunday night."

Henshaw took another sip of his coffee. Set the mug down. Did not, immediately, laugh — but the corner of his mouth registered the proper *I am being told something genuinely funny by competent students and I am letting them carry on* shape.

"Mr Waddell. Two questions. One — what spells did students cast that you did not expect, in your first match? Two — what did the Room do that you did not request?"

Caleb thought about it properly.

"On one, sir — Patronus opening, by me. I cast a Patronus to distract the other team's flag-defender for ten seconds while I sprinted up the side. Cass called it the dirtiest opener she had ever seen and we added a rule clarification afterwards — *Patronus openings legal; psychological warfare is in scope.* Otherwise everyone stayed inside the agreed legal-spell list."

A breath.

"On two — the Room added random in-play effects we did not ask for. A four-foot square of grass briefly became ankle-deep pond at centre-left during round one. A column rotated forty-five degrees mid-round in round two, changing sightlines for both teams. A cold draft swept the centre in round three smelling of the Forbidden Forest. None of them affected casting, but they affected positioning. The Room — appears to be — collaborating with the design rather than just rendering it."

Henshaw nodded slowly. The proper *I am taking this seriously even as I am also amused* register held.

"Mr Waddell. The cap on the open call. Year-group."

"Hadn't set one, sir. Would welcome your view."

"Fourth year and above. Make it explicit on the notice. A first-year who turns up to be hit by a Knockback Jinx in an inflatable room will be a first-year who turns up to me on Monday morning. I do not want that conversation. Mr Waddell — fourth-year-and-up. Make the notice clear."

"Yes, sir. Will adjust."

Henshaw picked up his quill again. Then, without looking up:

"I will come Saturday at two. I will not interfere unless something is going wrong. I want to see your Patronus opener in person — I am told it was *the dirtiest thing Cass Vance has seen in a duelling room,* which is a small recommendation in itself. Carry on with your day, Mr Waddell. Cass — thank you for the courtesy."

## Narrator

OOC: Let me check what the Wiggenweld julep concept actually is on the record.

OOC: The Cure for Boils proposal exists but I don't have a commit with the actual *Wiggenweld julep* concept content. From the compaction summary at the top: *Cass passed note: "ethanol-extraction is a real method, my dad does it for laboratory work in his apothecary in Edinburgh; ask me at dinner."* So the proposal involves ethanol-extraction methodology. The summary calls it the *Cure for Boils proposal* and the *Wiggenweld julep idea* — the user calling it a julep is news to me. A julep is an ethanol-based medicinal preparation (Muggle medicine 18th-19th c. — alcoholic infusion). So the concept: ethanol-extracting some component to create a more shelf-stable / faster-acting Wiggenweld variant, framed as a "julep."

This is Caleb sharing nascent academic work with Liri at the bench. Soft pitch, not a sell. Liri is a scholar; she'd engage seriously.

▼ Thursday, 8 September 1977 — 11:11

Caleb glanced at the lead. Still doing nothing. The proper Alchemy-pace held.

"Penrose. Quick one. You take Potions?"

Liri made the small *I do not, you should know this* shape but answered properly.

"No. Five NEWTs is the cap and I dropped Potions for Alchemy in fifth-year because Slughorn was — politely — not letting me bench-run my own experiments. Sage does. Why."

"Because I have an idea I'd like a smart person's read on, and Mei is good at structure but she's elbow-deep in Charms theory this week and Cass is the one who pointed me at the method to begin with so I can't sense-check it back at her. So you're the third smart person on my list. Sorry to be third."

The corner of Liri's mouth came up the small fraction. The proper *I have just been told I am the third smart person on a list and I am amused rather than insulted* register on her face.

"Third on a Waddell list. I'll take it. Go on."

Caleb leaned properly. The proper *I am going to explain a half-formed idea to a stranger and see if she can break it* shape on his face.

"Right. Wiggenweld. The standard brew — heat the syrup of hellebore, add the moonstone shavings, the unicorn horn, the salamander blood at the right beat, ten or eleven minutes total under proper conditions. Comes out a pale green syrup. Shelf life about three months in a sealed phial. Hits a small cut and it heals the cut. Hits a bigger wound and it works slower but it works. Standard Healer kit, brewed by every apothecary in Britain, the workhorse healing potion."

A breath.

"My idea — and Cass pointed me at the method — is to ethanol-extract one of the active components. Specifically the hellebore-and-moonstone interaction product. You'd brew the standard syrup, then run it through a slow ethanol extraction at low heat — Cass's dad does this in his apothecary in Edinburgh for laboratory work, it's a real method in Muggle pharmacology too. Take the concentrate that comes off. You'd lose volume — maybe down to a tenth of the original. But the concentrate would be — faster acting because higher purity, and shelf-stable for years instead of months because alcohol-preserved. A *Wiggenweld julep,* working name. Few drops on a wound instead of a teaspoon. Suited to an Auror or a soldier's kit. Smaller phial, longer shelf, faster action."

A small breath.

"Sage said this morning I should write it up properly as an end-of-term project after Slughorn approves the Potions side. Slughorn has the proposal-draft from Wednesday. I have not heard back yet. The bit I want a third smart person's read on is — *is there a reason this would not work* that I have not thought of. Not — *would Slughorn approve.* Just — chemistry. Or alchemy. Whichever applies."

Liri set her quill down properly. Looked at the lead for a beat. Then at Caleb. The proper *I am thinking* register on her face — careful, slow, the small line at the bridge of her nose from concentration.

"Two questions, Waddell. One — the *interaction product* between hellebore and moonstone is — alchemically a kind of *trans-stable compound,* meaning the two reactant species rearrange under heat into a third species that is the actual active material. The salamander blood and unicorn horn are catalysts, not ingredients. If you ethanol-extract the trans-stable compound out of the syrup, you have to be sure your ethanol is — properly anhydrous. Water in the ethanol will start to back-react the trans-stable into its precursors and you'll lose the activity. Cass's dad — does his work with anhydrous ethanol?"

A breath.

"Two — the *shelf-stable for years* claim needs testing. Alcohol preserves most things. It does not necessarily preserve every alchemical product. Some trans-stable compounds are stable in syrup because the syrup is part of the equilibrium that keeps them stable. Strip the syrup and replace with ethanol, you might be in a new equilibrium where the trans-stable wants to decompose back to its precursors over time. Your years might be — weeks. Or months. Or actually years. You won't know without testing. You'd want to do a proper accelerated-degradation test — store samples at, say, room temperature, body temperature, and warm body temperature, test activity at one week, four weeks, twelve weeks, against a control of regular Wiggenweld. That's — six months of work, minimum, just to get the shelf-stability data."

A small breath. She picked the quill back up. Wrote in her notebook *11:14 — still no observable change.*

"Otherwise — I think it could work. The idea is — actually clever. A concentrated battle-Healer potion for Order-types and Aurors makes — sense in the current political register. And ethanol-extraction is — under-used in wizarding pharmacy. We rely on syrups and direct decoctions because that's what the medieval texts give us. You'd be — applying a Muggle pharmacology method to a wizarding ingredient. Sage would like that, by the way — she's a Muggle-method-importation enthusiast, it's part of why she likes me. Slughorn — I don't know. He might or might not."

She looked at him properly.

"And — Waddell — the *third on your list* line. I am — not, in fact, on most of these lists. So I am — flattered. Don't, however, leave Mei off your list because she has Charms this week. She would like to look at this one. She is properly good at structure and you are right about that."

A small breath.

She picked her quill back up. Wrote *11:17 — still no observable change* in the proper rhythm.

"Saturday, two o'clock, Room of Requirement, watching the first match. I will be there. Thank you for the in-person ask. It is — more considerate than the notice would have been."

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 8 September 1977 — 11:24

Liri made a small *hm* sound at the back of her throat. The proper *Caleb has just asked a properly Liri-flavoured linguistic question and I am going to answer it carefully* register on her face.

"Waddell. Genuinely good question. The answer is — both are correct, with a register-distinction. *Plait* is the noun for the finished thing — the rope of hair you end up with. *Plaiting* is the gerund — the act of doing it. *Braid* is — American, mostly, though it has crept into British speech in the last twenty years, and *to braid* as a verb is now broadly acceptable on both sides. Older British speakers — my aunt in Cardiff, my mother — would say *plaiting* in the act and *plait* for the result. Younger British speakers will sometimes say *braiding* without anyone correcting them."

A small breath.

"In this classroom, on a Thursday in 1977, with two Britons at a marble bench — *plaiting* is the proper word. If you say *I will plait your hair while you observe lead,* my aunt would smile. If you say *I will braid it,* she would understand you but make a small face."

The corner of her mouth came up the proper fraction.

"So — yes. You plait to make a plait. Plaiting is what you do. The verb and the noun share the root and the *-ing* is just the action of arriving at the object. Like — *building a building* or *cooking a cooked thing.* Caleb — would you like to be the kind of British person who says *plaiting* or the kind who says *braiding?* The answer is — properly your choice. I do not have a strong opinion. Mei may."

Care of Magical Creatures was the proper Thursday-afternoon Hagrid affair — bilberry tea in tin mugs from the kettle in his hut, the seven seventh-year Creatures students arranged around the lower paddock fence. Today's topic was Mooncalves — Hagrid had two on loan from a Scottish breeder for a week, and the lesson was reading their hoof-prints in damp earth to determine emotional state. Caleb did the proper *I am the Manchester boy who pays attention to small mammals because I have been raising one for years* register — got the prints right at first try (one calf was anxious about the warm afternoon; the other was settled), and Hagrid gave the proper *good lad, that's right* nod and ten points. Cass would update the ledger.

The proper show-off moment came at the back end of class when Hagrid asked if anyone wanted to try a Patronus to gauge the calves' response to it (Mooncalves are known to settle in the presence of a real Patronus, and Hagrid had not seen Caleb's Patronus on Sunday in person). Caleb did the proper *yes, thank you sir, can I send Athena to visit Nealon on Saturday morning at eight* recovery and Hagrid agreed.

End of class at half-four. Caleb walked properly up the lawn from the paddock, into the castle through the side entrance near the greenhouses, up the corridors to the fifth floor west wing. ▼ Thursday, 8 September 1977 — 16:38

The right foot bare in his lap. Small, pale, the proper Mei-foot — narrow, second toe a fraction longer than the first, a small old scar across the top of the arch from a Manchester-kitchen-childhood incident he did not yet know the story of.

He started rubbing properly. Thumb-presses along the arch, slow, the proper *I am going to do this for as long as you keep reading* shape. Did not look up at her face.

Mei made a small sound at the back of her throat — pleased, the proper *that is a new thing and it is — fine* register. Did not, however, comment further. Picked the book up.

She read aloud properly. The proper Mei reading voice — quiet, careful, the small Cantonese-precision-on-English consonants:

"*Of the True Object of the Bonded State.*

*It is by general observation that the bonded pair are mark'd by a Symmetry of Form — by which we mean, that their Patroni do recognise each the other, that their wands do produce sparks unbidden in the presence of one another, and that the Diadem of Magister Ravenclaw's making is said to glow upon the simultaneous presence of two so joined. These Symmetries are pretty to remark, and have furnish'd the matter of much Discourse in the literature of our Subject.*

*Yet I would submit that the Symmetries are not the Object of the Bonded State, but only its Sign. The Object is — properly — the Common Labour of the Hearth. By which I mean, that two persons so joined are given, by the Bond, the Capacity to do together the proper domestic Work of being two souls in one Household — to make tea for one another without ask, to feel the other's Sleeping Breath at distance, to share the Burden of the Grief that either one carries alone, and to maintain the small daily Kindnesses that sustain a long Companionship.*

*The Patroni are pretty. The Diadem is fine. But the Hearth is the Object. The Sign is given that the Hearth may be tended. To mistake the Sign for the Object is to misread the whole Bond.*"

She paused. Caleb's thumb was on her arch, slow proper pressure. He had not looked up.

"Helena Brigham. Seventeen-something — exact date not in the front-matter, the book was printed posthumously by her husband. *To mistake the Sign for the Object is to misread the whole Bond.*"

A breath.

"Caleb. You — at this morning's lunch — and just now in front of the Tai O window — described the bond as *two people doing the actual emotional work of being bonded.* Helena Brigham just called it *the Common Labour of the Hearth.* The same thesis. In your hand on my arch right now. Caleb — give me the left foot too."

She shifted in the chair the small fraction. The left foot offered.

A breath.

To put oneself in the small Way of the partner's daily small Errands, that they may not be alone in them. To feed the partner, to undress them when they are weary, to tend the small Hurts of the body without speech. The Hearth is fond as well as deep.*"

Another small breath. The proper Mei-precision held under the wet of her voice now.

"*The third instance is the inheritance. That what one has known, the other comes in time to know. What one has loved, the other in time loves also. What one has lost, the other in time mourns alongside. So that, at the End, two souls have become — not one, which is a Romantic Falsehood — but a Common Pair, which is a far richer Thing, and which constitutes the True Object of the Bond.*"

She closed the book on her finger for a beat. Looked down at Caleb on the rug at her feet.

"Caleb. *To undress them when they are weary, to tend the small Hurts of the body without speech.* Helena Brigham, seventeen-something. You are — at four-forty on a Thursday — doing what she described, on a rug in her husband's office. The room is — I think — properly pleased with us right now."

The fire's small *crack* underneath them. The Tai O dawn held its pink in the window. The jade frog on the mantel. The egg-tarts still steaming faintly on the desk. Caleb's thumb on her left arch, slow and proper.

"On the *does it change what it means to me* — *I am working it out as I tell you.* Properly. Stand still while I do."

A breath.

"*One.* It is — not a violation. You did not — know. You did not — pluck the name out of my sister's grave. You picked it because it scanned. The fact that it lands on my sister's name is — a coincidence, or — a quiet pull I am not going to interpret yet."

A small breath.

"*Two.* If we have a daughter — eventually, in the long version, as discussed — and we name her *Chloe Ling Waddell* or *Chloe Ling Chang-Waddell* or whatever order we settle — *Ling* would be — *Mei Ling's name carried forward.* Which is — *not the same as Mei Ling.* But — *would be a thing.* My mother — would notice. My mother — would *cry,* possibly. Which would be — *the right kind of crying.* It would land for her as — *Mei has carried her sister forward into the next generation.* Which — *is* — *something I would, in fact, want to do, if I had stopped to think about it.* *Which I had not.*"

A breath.

"*Three.* Which means — *Caleb you accidentally named our hypothetical daughter after my dead sister at four in the afternoon on a train you did not know yet that I had a dead sister.* That is — *bond-shaped,* or — *coincidence-shaped.* I do not know which. *Helena Brigham would have an opinion.* I will check chapter seven."

She wiped under one lens of her glasses with her thumb properly. Eyes wet — the tender-wet, not the laughing-wet of an hour ago.

"*Caleb.* — *More okay,* in fact. Properly more okay. Not less. The name was — *the right one to land on,* even though you did not know why. *Chloe Ling Waddell-Chang* — *yes.* Whenever she comes. *Yes.*"

A breath. She picked her satchel back up.

"Now — *dinner.* I have just — *had to think about my dead sister's name being one of our future children's names while standing in the doorway of Brigham's office, fully dressed for the first time in two and a half hours, sixty seconds before we are supposed to walk into the Great Hall.* Caleb. *That was — a lot.* Move."

She opened the door.

OOC: Locking this — naming-related thread tied to the dead sister is high-stakes.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 8 September 1977 — 18:44

Imogen looked up from her potatoes. The composed Hufflepuff-precision came on properly for a literature question — the small *yes, I have read this in three places and have seen one in person, here is what I know* shift.

"She was a yearling in July, so she's a yearling-going-on-two-year-old now. Hippogriffs reach what Aldous calls *flight-mature* at about three years — that's when they can carry a rider comfortably and have full adult plumage — and properly *full-grown* by five. So Dame Tryfannia is — at the start of her grown body. She's not a chick anymore but she's not at her full size either. She'll get bigger across the next three years."

A breath. She picked her fork up but did not eat yet.

"Full-grown female hippogriff — at the shoulder, about the height of a large draft horse. Five feet at the withers, give or take. Body length nose-to-tail-tip around nine to ten feet. Wingspan when fully extended — fifteen to eighteen feet, females tend toward the larger end. Weight — roughly the same as a heavy hunting horse, maybe seven hundred pounds. Males slightly smaller in body but with showier head-plumage."

She took a small bite. Swallowed.

"Dame Tryfannia in July — I'd say four feet at the shoulder, maybe twelve-foot wingspan. Already big. By half-term she'll be a hand taller and another foot or so of wing on each side. She is — Caleb — *very large.* If you are picturing a clever pony with feathers, you should picture instead an Irish wolfhound bred up to the size of a Clydesdale and then given the front half of an eagle. That is what you are walking up to in Snowdonia."

Lin paused his fork mid-air. "Babe. I am going to need to think about this for a minute."

Imogen set her fork down properly. The composed Hufflepuff-warm came on at the soft register — the proper Imogen-warm of someone whose actual subject Caleb had just gotten right on the first try.

"Caleb. *Yes.*"

A breath.

"That is — the correct response, and you have arrived at it on a *picture in a sketchbook* without seeing one. Aldous says the same thing every time he takes a new student into the field — *no drawing prepares you, no description prepares you, you are going to stand twenty feet away from one and forget what you came to do for the first thirty seconds.* He calls it — *the first-encounter pause.* Every Healer who works with hippogriffs has it. He still does, twenty-six years in. The pause never quite goes away. It gets shorter."

She picked her fork up again. Did not, however, eat yet.

"A flock — *yes.* Worse. Better. Both. A flock at rest in the late afternoon, with the light coming sideways through the wing-feathers when they preen — Aldous took me to see one in Beddgelert in July, fifteen birds in a hillside hollow with Tryfan behind them, late sun, and I — *sat down on the heather because my knees stopped working.* Properly. Aldous walked off and let me have the moment. Came back ten minutes later with two flasks of tea. Did not say a word until I drank mine. *That* is the field-experience. You will — *also* — likely sit down on the heather."

A small breath. The half-millimetre on, soft warm.

"Caleb. *Respectfully cautious, maybe slightly afraid* — is — exactly the right register to bring. Aldous will not take a student into the field who is *not* slightly afraid. Confident-not-afraid is — the register of someone who has not understood the creature and will get hurt. *Respectfully cautious* is — the register he trains for."

Lin, very quiet, looking at his fork: "Babe. I am — going to sit down on heather, in Snowdonia, in eight weeks, because of a name I made up over potatoes. Caleb."

Imogen considered properly. The composed Hufflepuff-precision held for the small *Caleb has asked a question I have not yet decided the answer to* beat.

"Honest answer — I do not know yet. I had been planning the trip in my head as — Aldous-led, because I have not done a Snowdonia field-find without him and would not propose it to my parents otherwise. *But* — if I write to him tonight and pitch it as *Imogen plus two friends, half-term week, find Dame Tryfannia's flock if you can give us a pointer to where they've moved* — he might say *yes, I'll join you for the find* or *yes, here's the pointer, off you go* or *no, this is not appropriate for students at your level, I will take Imogen alone in November.*"

A breath. She picked up her fork properly this time and took a bite.

"My guess — fifty-fifty between *he joins us for one day* and *he sends us with a pointer and an owl-arrangement to check in.* He likes me independent in the field. He also likes me alive. The half-term week is — long enough for him to do the pointer-and-check-in version with a one-day overlap on his end. That would be — the best version. I would have a day with him in the field as the working-Healer-track student, you and Mei would have the *find Dame Tryfannia* adventure shape, and the overlap day would be — Aldous meeting you and Mei and seeing how you handle a flock encounter, which he would absolutely use as data about whether I have brought sensible friends or daft ones."

She took another bite. Swallowed.

"So — *probably* — *us side adventure with Aldous as the safety-pointer and one overlap day.* Subject to him agreeing. I will know in a week."

Mei beside Lin, dry warm: "Caleb. *Us side adventure with a professional safety net and a one-day inspection.* That is — the proper shape of a half-term trip we propose to our parents. *Snowdonia hippogriff search* is — three steps of polite English away from *no, Mei, you may not.* *Snowdonia magical-creatures field exposure with the working Healer present part of the time* is — *yes, Mei, please write to us about how it went.*"

Politically — *cautious-grey,* not *grey-going-dark.* If I had to plot the two families on a chart, Vances are further from neutral than Bletchleys."

A breath.

"On *good influence for Cass* — that is a harder question. Marcus is — *quieter than Cass and slower than Cass and softer than Cass.* The cohort assumption has been — *he stabilises her.* The Mei-and-Lin reading has been — *he absorbs her without challenging her, which is what she has chosen because it does not threaten her armour.* Those are — *different things.* The first is a good-influence read; the second is a sustainable-but-not-growth read."

Lin, quietly: "Mate. Marcus is — *hard to get a handle on* by design. He is — *the Slytherin who is in the room and lets you forget he is in the room.* It is — *useful for him* socially because nobody notices what he sees, and *useful in the cohort* because he absorbs Cass without making a thing of it. But — *I have known him five years and I could not tell you what he wants out of life beyond Cass and a quiet job.* That is — *not a complete person to me yet.* He may be one. I have not seen it."

A small breath. Mei picked her fork up. Did not eat.

"Caleb. *Speech about a philanthropist* — no. Marcus is not — that. Marcus is — *fine in the cohort, fine for Cass on her current terms, possibly fine for the long run, possibly not.* Cass does not — *need* — a philanthropist. She needs — *someone who does not flinch.* Marcus does not flinch. Whether that is — *love* or — *a chosen stillness* is — *not for us to decide.*"

▼ Thursday, 8 September 1977 — 19:08

They came up off the marble staircase onto the seventh-floor corridor together. Caleb walked Cass past the Barnabas tapestry — *give me twenty seconds* — round the corner to Solomon's portrait. Cass leaned against the wall opposite the tapestry, watched.

Solomon raised one amber-yellow eye. "*Mr Waddell. — Solo again. Twenty seconds, sir.*"

"Just my ethanol notes, Sir. Thank you."

The portrait swung. Caleb stepped through, picked up the eight draft sheets, the sodium chloride table on top, a fresh quill from the well. Stepped back out. The portrait swung shut.

Walked back round into the corridor. Stopped halfway between Solomon and the Barnabas tapestry. Cupped his hands at his mouth — *Athena, with me* — and whistled the two-note call he had used on Saturday at the breakfast table.

Twenty seconds. The small dot.

Athena banked round the far end of the corridor — proper Pygmy speed — and touched down on his outstretched forearm. Walked the small few inches up the inside of his sleeve and tucked herself against his upper arm. Settled.

Cass had been watching the call from the tapestry. The composed Slytherin-evaluation register on properly — the *Caleb-summoned-his-owl-inside-the-castle-by-whistling thing has just happened in front of me and I am going to file it* shape.

"Waddell. — Filed. The owl came to a corridor whistle. We will — discuss that later."

He came up beside her. Paced three times in front of the wall. *The cabin variant. Three friends. Tea. Three matching sets of leopard-cat-and-Pygmy-Owl flannel pyjamas. King bed with three pillows, sheets in the same print. Perch for Athena with a window onto somewhere she can hunt. Cosy. Quiet.*

Door materialised. Brass plaque: *Three Friends and a Bidet Cabin.* Cass read the plaque, did not comment, the half-millimetre coming up the small fraction.

He held the door for her.

Inside: rustic cabin. Beech-wood beams, polished. Round wood-burning stove lit low orange behind the mica window. Three armchairs in a semicircle around the stove, three woollen throws over the arms. Low pine table in the centre with a teapot steaming and three mugs. Kitchen alcove with Mei's Bidet Cabin sink, gold tap. Three matching folded pyjama sets on a bench by the door — soft flannel cream ground, repeat-print of leopard cats and Pygmy Owls interleaving, three sizes (Mei's smallest, Caleb's middle, Cass's third). At the far end of the cabin behind a low partition: a king-size bed with three pillows, the same leopard-cat-and-Pygmy-Owl flannel sheets and a thick quilt. Wide windowsill on the back wall, mountain pass at dusk — pine, evening light, half a mile of open hunting-air. Below the window, a carved wooden perch with cushion and water-saucer, Pygmy-sized.

Cass stopped two paces inside the door. Looked round properly. Took in the matching-pyjamas, the matching sheets, the bed-for-three.

A long beat.

"Waddell. — The room has produced three matching sets of pyjamas with leopard cats and Pygmy Owls, and matching sheets on a king bed with three pillows. That is — a level of three-friends-cabin commitment I had not anticipated. *Filed.* I am — guessing Mei has the smallest set on most nights."

She set her satchel down beside the second armchair. Picked up the third pyjama set. Considered.

"You are — expecting me to change. For a twenty-minute homework review."

"Yes. The room knows what a cosy evening means. Pyjamas, tea, stove, Athena on her perch. Off-duty for twenty minutes. Marcus gets you back in pieces."

Cass did the small half-shrug. Went through the changing-door.

Caleb let Athena out of his sleeve onto the perch. She walked the small few inches to the water saucer, drank twice, settled. Looked out the window at the dusk and tracked the small movement of something in the far pines — proper Pygmy attention, the *I can be where I am supposed to be and also keep an eye on dinner outside* posture.

Cass came out in the third matching set, the leopard-cats-and-Pygmy-Owls print on her cream flannel. Hair down. Glasses still on.

"Waddell. — *Cream flannel.* My grandmother would approve. Pour the tea."
