▼ Thursday, 8 September 1977 — 19:14

Caleb walked to the perch first. Held one finger out properly to Athena. She side-stepped to it.

"Athena. — Off duty. Go hunt or sit by the fire or do what you want for an hour. We're in here, the cabin is warm, the mountain pass is yours. Find me when you find me."

She considered him. Did the small *yes, fine* head-bob. Walked back from his finger to the perch. Settled. Did not, however, leave through the window — chose the warm side, the proper *I will sit by the stove instead of hunting tonight* register. Tucked one foot up under her. Closed one eye. The other on the stove flame.

Caleb undressed by the door without theatre. Tie off, jumper off, blouse off, trousers off — folded the stack onto the bench beside his pyjama set. Stripped down to underwear. Pulled the leopard-cat-and-Pygmy-Owl flannel on. Bottoms. Top.

Did not, however, look to see whether Cass was looking. She was — by sound — in the kitchen alcove with the teapot, the small clink of porcelain on the gold-tap sink.

He walked over barefoot to the kitchen alcove. Took the teapot from her. Did the proper *I'm pouring for both of us* move — filled hers first into the mug she'd picked, then his. Lifted the milk jug, added the small dash for himself, set the jug toward her so she could choose.

Carried both mugs to the low pine table by the stove. Set them down. Sat in the third armchair, the one nearest Athena's perch and the window.

"Twenty minutes of homework, plus as long as you want to stay. Marcus knows you're with me, the cabin will provide whatever else, the room knows three friends."

A breath. The composed Caleb register held warm.

"And about Athena — the whistle is for show. She just knows when I want her. We also have a bondage thing."

Cass had been pouring her own milk. Stopped. Set the jug down.

A long beat.

Then she made a sound that was half-laugh half-*Waddell-no* and rubbed under one lens of her glasses with her thumb.

"Waddell. — *Bondage thing.* — That is — *not the technical term.* The technical term is — *companion-owl bond,* per Hesba Ollivander 1922, which Imogen has been reciting at the rest of the cohort for a week. *Bondage* is — *the Muggle leather-and-strap term* and you have just used it about your owl. Caleb. *Athena does not have a safe word.*"

A breath. The half-millimetre on, full warm dry.

"On the *she just knows when I want her* — that I had — half-suspected. The whistle on Saturday at breakfast — Mei told me the next day, at the Hexfield post-match — produced Athena from inside the castle in about thirty seconds. The corridor whistle tonight did the same. Either you are — broadcasting at her on a frequency the rest of us cannot hear, or — *she just knows.* Caleb. *Either way, that is a real thing,* and I am — *adding it to the bond-disclosure I am carrying around in my head about you tonight.*"

She picked her tea up. Took a sip. Settled into the second armchair, feet tucked under her, the proper Cass-off-duty posture deployed for the first time in front of Caleb.

"Right. Twenty minutes. *Show me the sodium chloride table. — But not before I tell you* — the *Mei has the smallest set* observation a minute ago — I owe you an apology for. That was — not necessary. The room knows the sizes. I do not need to say so."

Caleb made the small *snrk* into his tea at *I am not shapely.*

"Vance. — Acknowledged. Both of us over-explained. We're fine. The room knows three small-to-medium witches and a Caleb who isn't very wide. *Filed.*"

He set the mug down. Pulled the eight draft sheets toward him on the low pine table. Cass leaned forward in her armchair, feet still tucked, the proper *I am ready to read* shape.

"Right. Pitch in one paragraph. Slughorn's standing brief: anyone in seventh-year Potions who wants to propose an independent investigation, submit by week three with a clear hypothesis, methodology, expected results, and a control. Independent investigation gets you the equivalent of a small-essay grade if it lands, gets you Slughorn's attention if it goes further. I went to him Monday morning. Pitched: *Wiggenweld brews to standard with the canonical lavender-and-dittany suspension in water, but the active migrates unevenly across the phial over twenty-four hours — anyone who's used a phial more than three days after brewing knows the bottom is stronger than the top. The hypothesis is that part of the active wants a less-polar solvent and is settling out of water-suspension into the lavender-oil layer, and that brewing with a small ethanol fraction in the solvent would hold the active in true solution longer.* Slughorn's reply same morning: *interesting if you can show the migration is real, not just folklore — and if your ethanol fraction doesn't kill the dittany or the lavender. Draft by Friday morning.* That's what I'm holding."

He turned the first sheet round so Cass could read the abstract.

"Methodology — three batches. Control: standard Wiggenweld, water-only solvent, brewed to the textbook. Experimental A: ten percent ethanol-by-volume in the solvent. Experimental B: twenty percent. All three brewed in the same hour, same flame, same crystal stir. Test at day one, day three, day seven, day fourteen: assay potency by — *here is where I am working it out* — Slughorn's casual suggestion was *taste it on a Bouncing Bulb cut, see how fast it heals,* which is — *not* — a measurable assay. I have proposed instead the sodium chloride table — here — *the rate at which a calibrated drop closes a standardised cut on a peeled potato treated with a known saline solution.* The saline standardises the substrate so the comparison is between solvents, not between potato varieties."

He slid the sodium chloride sheet across. Cass leaned in properly.

"Liri Penrose, at Alchemy this morning — across the bench from me on slow-Calcination duty — said two things on it. *One,* the ethanol fraction would oxidise the dittany if it sat with the active for more than the brewing window, so I need to add the ethanol *after* the dittany has bonded with the lavender, not in the initial solvent, which changes the method. Liri's exact phrasing was *ethanol is too cheerful with dittany,* which I am — keeping in the cohort vocabulary. *Two,* she asked whether I had considered using *propanol* instead at the same fraction, because propanol is less reactive with dittany but holds the lavender active just as well. I had not. Slughorn might or might not let me change variables this late in the proposal week, but Liri's right that the choice is — non-trivial."

He set the quill down beside the sheets.

"Where I want your brain — the sodium chloride concentration. I have it at zero-point-nine percent — physiological saline — which is what the apothecary references for Wiggenweld assays. Liri thought that was right. But Vance — you do better chemistry than either of us. Is point-nine the right anchor? And — second — does the ethanol-fraction-after-bonding change my methodology enough that I should rewrite section two before tomorrow morning?"

▼ Thursday, 8 September 1977 — 19:15

Caleb made the small *snrk* into his tea at *I am not shapely.*

"Vance. — Both of us over-explained. We are fine. The room knows three small-to-medium witches and a Caleb who isn't very wide."

He set the mug down. Pulled the eight draft sheets toward him on the low pine table. Cass leaned forward in her armchair, feet still tucked, the proper *I am ready to read* shape.

"Right. Folly. — Started at Slug & Jiggers on 31 August with Cassia Burbage, who brewed Wiggenweld in front of me as the standard and let me have a phial as reference. I pitched her a julep version — bourbon, mint, sugar, ice — because Wiggenweld already has mint and bourbon is an alcohol I had in front of me at the Leaky an hour later. She countered fifty-fifty on terms: must work, must heal, written recipe with weights and times. Working name: *Caleb's Folly,* her coinage."

He turned the first sheet so Cass could read.

"That night at the Leaky I ran the first experiment. One drop of finished Wiggenweld into a fresh mint julep, stirred with the mint sprig. *Nothing visible* — no fizz, no colour shift, no separation. Faint herbal undertone in the taste, otherwise the julep was the julep. Conclusion: alcohol bound the moondew on contact, neutralised the healing component. *Cannot add finished Wiggenweld to alcohol.* Have to brew the second half of a Wiggenweld in a non-alcoholic carrier and join it to the alcohol *after* the bind."

He slid the next sheet across.

"Pitched it to Slughorn on Friday morning, first day of Potions. He took the bourbon-julep pitch, then reframed it as master's-track work and narrowed it down for NEWT feasibility — one spirit, one potion, for the year. *Pick one. Establish whether any alcohol can carry any bind.* The bourbon-versus-rum-versus-gin question is post-Easter. So I picked ethanol. Pure, clean, no congeners, no flavour, no second-order chemistry to argue with. Slughorn nodded. He wants the written proposal by Friday — *tomorrow* — in his pigeonhole before Charms. Controls, cost ledger, named witness for any tasting trial."

He set the quill down beside the sheets.

"Where I have got to in the draft — methodology section. Three batches. Control: standard Wiggenweld, water-only solvent, brewed to textbook. Experimental A: brew the bark-and-salamander half in water exactly as standard, brew the moondew-and-mint half in *pure ethanol* instead of water, join the two halves at the standard moment. Experimental B: same as A but the ethanol half is brought to brewing temperature and held there for ninety seconds before the join — testing whether the bind window matters when the carrier is different. Assay at day one, three, seven, fourteen on a peeled potato cut, standardised with sodium chloride at point-nine percent."

He looked across at her properly.

"Liri at Alchemy this morning gave me a thing I have not yet integrated into the draft. — She said *ethanol is a cleaner solvent for the moondew half than water is on first principle, because moondew is itself an alcohol-soluble, water-tolerant compound — the textbook uses water because water is the unmarked default, not because water is the best carrier for moondew.* Which means — if she is right — *the standard recipe may already be the inferior version* and the ethanol Folly may not just be a curiosity but an actual improvement. Cass — that is a much bigger claim than what I pitched Slughorn on Friday. And I do not know if it is right."

He set his palm flat on the draft.

"Where I want your brain. — *Do you believe Liri's compound-chemistry read on moondew?* Because if it holds, my proposal needs to say so, and the entire framing of the project changes from *can alcohol carry a bind* to *is the canonical Wiggenweld the wrong recipe.* And I have until tomorrow morning to decide which version goes in Slughorn's pigeonhole."

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 8 September 1977 — 19:21

Cass had been listening with her chin on her hand, glasses low on her nose, the proper Cass-reading-mode posture. She let the question sit for a beat.

Then she sat up a little. Reached for the first draft sheet. Pulled it toward her properly. Took a sip of her tea.

"Right. Let me read Liri's read before I answer."

She did not, however, take long. Skimmed the methodology. Tapped a fingernail against the moondew-and-mint half of Experimental A.

"Waddell. — Liri is half right. Moondew *is* more soluble in ethanol than in water at the cold-brew step — that bit is real, it's in the standard Compendium notes if you read the footnotes nobody reads. *But* the canonical Wiggenweld isn't using water as a carrier for the moondew. It is using water as a *coolant* and a *bind-bridge* between the warm bark-and-salamander side and the cold moondew side. The water is doing two jobs at once. Ethanol does the solvent job better and the coolant job *worse* — ethanol evaporates faster, holds heat differently, and the bind-bridge moment when the two halves meet is calibrated to water-on-water surface tension."

She set the sheet down.

"So the standard recipe isn't *wrong.* It is the *best compromise* between two competing demands when your carrier has to do both jobs. If you go to ethanol on the moondew side, you have a better solvent and a worse bridge. Whether the trade is net positive — *that's the actual experiment.*"

She picked her tea up. Sipped. Considered.

"Don't change your framing for the proposal. Slughorn signed off on *can alcohol carry a bind* — that is still the question. Liri's compound-chemistry point goes in the proposal's *expected results* section, not the hypothesis. You write: *prior literature suggests ethanol is the superior solvent for moondew at the cold-brew step; the standard recipe uses water as a compromise carrier serving both solvent and bind-bridge functions; the experiment will test whether the solvent gain offsets the bridge loss.* That gives Slughorn the master's-track framing he already saw and *predicts* the result you might get without overclaiming. If the ethanol Folly outperforms the control on day-fourteen potency, *then* you write the paper that says the canonical recipe is the wrong compromise. Not before."

She set the tea down. Looked at him properly.

"On your three-batch design — Experimental B is the right call. The ninety-second hold tests whether the bridge-loss can be mitigated by giving the ethanol half more time to settle into the bind window. If A fails and B succeeds, you have isolated the bridge problem as the variable. Add a fourth batch if you have the budget. Experimental C: ethanol on the moondew side, plus a five-percent water cosolvent in the ethanol half. That tests whether *adding back* a little of the bridge function recovers the bind without giving up the solvent gain. Three's the minimum, four is the actually-rigorous version."

She tapped the sodium chloride sheet.

"Point-nine percent is the right anchor. Standard physiological. Don't change it. — One thing I want flagged in the cost ledger: pure ethanol is *Ministry-controlled* for tax reasons. You can't just buy it. You need a school-research exemption, which means Slughorn has to sign off on the procurement, *not just the project.* Put that in the budget section explicitly. He will appreciate that you knew."

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 8 September 1977 — 19:25

Cass had been listening with her chin back on her hand. When he finished she did not, immediately, speak. Sat with it for a beat. Then took her glasses off, rubbed the bridge of her nose with two fingers, and put them back on. The proper Cass-thinking shape.

"Waddell."

A breath.

"That is — *a question I had not thought to ask in three years of Potions.* And — *now you have said it,* I cannot work out why we have not asked it. The textbook says *brew* and we brew. The textbook says *warm sugar-water* for the Bouncing Bulbs and we use warm sugar-water. *Nobody at Hogwarts brews potions cold,* and I have never read a footnote that *explains why.* The reason has always been *because that is how it is done.*"

She picked her tea up. Cold by now. Did not, however, complain. Drank what was left.

"Right. Your logic. — Heat is doing two things in standard brewing: it is *extracting* components from solid materials — bark, dried roots, dittany leaf — and it is *activating* certain reactions that need an energy input to start. Some potions need both. Some only need one. *Wiggenweld* — the bark-and-salamander side definitely needs heat for the extraction; the bark is fibrous, the salamander blood is viscous, and getting them to give up their actives without heat would take *days* at room temperature if it worked at all. So you cannot cold-brew the *whole* Wiggenweld. — But the moondew-and-mint side. *That* is different. Moondew is a liquid extract already, mint is leaf material that releases its oils into any decent solvent at any reasonable temperature, and the *bind chemistry* on that side is — Caleb, *I do not actually know* if it needs heat to bind, or whether the heat is there because the bark-and-salamander side needs it and the canonical recipe carries the heat through to the join."

A small breath.

"If — *if* — the moondew-and-mint bind happens cold in ethanol, then your join becomes: warm bark-and-salamander side from the first cauldron, cold ethanol-moondew-mint side from the second beaker, joined at the standard moment. The temperature differential at the join would be *larger* than the standard recipe, which may help or hurt the bind-bridge. — But you would have eliminated the *need* for the coolant function of the water on the moondew side, because the ethanol-cold solvent is *doing the same job* the water-cool solvent does at the standard moment."

She set her empty mug down.

"Caleb. — *That is your fourth batch.* Not the cosolvent one I suggested. *That* one. *Experimental D: pure ethanol on the moondew side, cold-brewed at ambient temperature, joined to standard hot bark-and-salamander side at the canonical moment.* If D works, you have proven that Wiggenweld's moondew side has been brewed warm for three hundred years for no reason other than the *bark side needed warm cookware in the same kitchen.* That is — *the actual master's paper.* Not a footnote about whether ethanol can carry a bind. *Whether half the potion has been heated unnecessarily since the recipe was written down.*"

A breath.

"You can still pitch the narrow version to Slughorn tomorrow. *Slughorn's project* is the three-batch ethanol-vs-water comparison he agreed to on Friday. Your *D batch* you can run in parallel as a *side experiment* — note it in the proposal as *exploratory, off the main grade,* and ask his permission. He will say yes. He likes a student who runs a side experiment for free."

She picked her empty mug up, looked at it, set it down again. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came up the small fraction.

"Waddell. *Pour the tea.*"

OOC: Locking — Cass's batch D suggestion and the cold-brew hypothesis is meaningful proposal-state.

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 8 September 1977 — 19:28

Caleb stood. Took the teapot to the kitchen alcove. Filled it from the kettle, set it back on its trivet for the hot pour. While the kettle came back to the boil he took a third empty mug from the shelf, filled it from the cold tap at the Bidet Cabin sink, and dropped a fresh pinch of loose-leaf tea into it. Set it on the low pine table beside the warm pot.

He poured Cass's mug hot first. Steam came up. Then his own. Then nudged the cold mug across the table the small inch toward her.

"Two of those. Demonstration."

He sat down. The half-millimetre at the corner of his mouth on the small fraction.

Cass watched him do it. Did not, immediately, drink. Picked up the cold mug, looked at it against the lamp — the tea leaves sitting in the cold water doing nothing visible, the colour barely changing — and then picked up the hot mug, which was already going amber.

She set both down. Considered him properly.

"Waddell. — *Possibly degraded by the heat.* That is — the *third* thing you have said tonight that I had not thought to ask. — Yes. The standard recipe assumes *active components survive the brew temperature.* It does not assume *active components are unaffected by the brew temperature.* That is — *a different assumption* — and the Wiggenweld bind moment is hot, and *moondew is light-sensitive and heat-volatile in its isolated form* — the apothecary jars are smoked glass for a reason. — If the canonical recipe is *cooking off* a fraction of the moondew''s actives at the join because the bark-and-salamander side comes in hot — then your cold-brew Folly is not just *as good as* the standard, it could be *measurably better.*"

A breath. She picked the hot mug up properly. Drank.

"That is the *thesis* your master''s paper makes. *Not* in your Slughorn pigeonhole tomorrow. *Tomorrow* you say *can ethanol carry the bind.* This — *the cooked-off-active hypothesis* — goes in the cover sheet of Folly Year Two, after Slughorn has approved Folly Year One and watched batch D succeed for fourteen days. — You do not bring three years of thesis to a NEWT proposal on the day it is due. — *You bring the question Slughorn signed off on,* and you let the data carry the rest."

She set the hot mug back down. Picked up the cold one. Looked into it. The tea leaves had begun to release the faintest brown plume into the cold water, very slowly, at room temperature, with no help from heat at all.

She tilted it the small fraction so Caleb could see.

"It is — *steeping.* Slowly. But it is. — *Point made, Waddell.*"

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 8 September 1977 — 19:30

Cass set the cold mug down. The composed Cass-evaluation register came off the small fraction. The proper *Caleb has just asked me how I am, in this room, in cream flannel pyjamas, after I gave him a master's-track read on his proposal and we are now ten minutes past the twenty-minute mark and I am still here* shape settled on her face.

A breath. She picked the hot mug up. Held it in both hands.

"Waddell."

Quiet.

"How am I. — That is — not a question I get asked at the Slytherin table without it meaning something else."

A small breath.

"Mostly tired. — First week of NEWTs is the proper first week, which I had forgotten over summer. Slughorn already wants me on a Master's-track Arithmancy paper for him, which is — flattering and also the sort of thing he says to four other students in the same week, so I am holding it lightly. McGonagall told me in the corridor on Tuesday that she expects me to put my name in for Head Girl in the spring, which is — *not happening,* because Davina will get it and should, and I do not want it. Marcus is — Marcus. *Fine.* Quieter than usual. I have not asked him why and he has not offered."

She took a sip. Set the mug down.

"My brothers wrote me. — Both of them. The same week. Which they have not done in three years. — *That* is the thing I am sitting with, and I will not tell you what they said. — Not because I do not trust you. Because I have not decided what I think about it yet, and I will not put your name in the same sentence as theirs until I have."

A breath. The half-millimetre at the corner of her mouth came up the small fraction — dry, not warm.

"That is — *how I am.* — Thank you for asking. — *Properly.*"

She picked the hot mug back up. Drank. Set it down.

"Now. — *Your turn.* You have had — *a Thursday.* Mei is downstairs, the cohort knows the yes-in-principle, Imogen has knighted a hippogriff, you have proposed three master's-track theses in this room before nineteen-thirty, and I am drinking your tea in pyjamas the room made for me. — *Caleb. How are you.*"

## Narrator

▼ Thursday, 8 September 1977 — 19:32

Caleb stood. Came round the low pine table to behind Cass's armchair. Put both hands on the tops of her shoulders — over the cream flannel, the proper *Caleb-shoulder-rub* hands, the same ones he had used on Lin when Lin had been crying over a Transfiguration practical in fifth year and on Mei the previous Saturday morning.

Cass made the small involuntary *huh* at first contact. Her shoulders were properly rigid — the kind of rigid that wasn't *today*, that was *weeks*. Caleb registered it without commenting. Started slow. Thumbs along the trapezius, the long sweep from neck-base out toward the points of the shoulders. Felt the knots come up under his thumbs the way they did. Worked them.

"I am going to come rub your shoulders now because you deserve it. You don't need to ruin it by pointing out that I'm not having sex with you because that is not the purpose. Although I just did that. Twice. Which is exciting and new for me."

Cass made a sound that was half-snort half-*Waddell.* Did not, however, move. Let her head tip the small inch forward to give him better access at the base of her neck.

"Waddell. — *Filed.* I will not — comment on the twice. Properly congratulations. Mei is — *very lucky.* Move your left thumb a half-inch up. — *There.*"

He worked. Found the worst knot at the right shoulder where she carried her satchel, dug into it with the heel of his thumb in the slow firm way that hurt before it stopped hurting. Cass exhaled properly. Let her shoulders drop the small inch.

"I told you about my bondage things, which are new."

"The Athena ones."

"Yes. — Imogen is drawing Athena for me, which is adorable. Half-page sketch, going to bind it into her field-notes section on Pygmy Owls. She showed me at dinner. The little dot of white above Athena's right eye is in it properly."

He moved to the left shoulder. Found a different knot pattern there — more diffuse, less *I carry a satchel*, more *I sit at a desk and grip a quill too hard*.

"Esme. — The first-year Hufflepuff girl with the bowl haircut and the wave at me from the Hufflepuff bench tonight. — Her younger brother. Wilfred Tom — five — at her parents' house in Reading. They send the *Reading Evening Post* every week so she can see his weather drawings in the kids' section. He is mad for them. I subscribed her to twelve back-issues last Sunday — twelve weeks of brother in newsprint — and she is now properly on the standing-subscription so Mrs Mowbray at the post office sends her the new one every Wednesday."

A small breath.

"Esme cried when she told me about him. Not theatre. The kind of crying you do when you have been holding something for three weeks. — She is one of mine now. — I tell you because you should know."

Cass had gone still under his hands. Not the *stop* still. The *I am listening properly* still.

"Waddell."

Quiet.

"Esme Mowbray is — the small-fair-haired Ravenclaw fourth-year you let dance with you on the train last year, and again this year. — Yes. I know who she is. *I did not know about the brother.* — Adding to file. — *Properly one of yours,* the way Lin is one of yours and Mei is one of yours, in the senior-cohort sense, not the romantic sense. — Caleb. — *Good.* A fourth-year girl with a five-year-old brother she misses ought to have a Caleb who subscribes her to twelve weeks of weather drawings. — That is the right shape."

A breath. Her head tipped the small fraction further forward. The shoulders had come down two full inches under his hands now.

"Keep going. — Both sides. Slowly. *Twenty more minutes, Waddell, and then I will let you go back to Mei and bring her the gossip from this room which is that you give an excellent shoulder rub and your fiancée should be informed.*"
